target_output.workflows: Valuation Submission Workflow: appraiser submits POST /valuations with address and comp_radius_miles; API normalizes address, creates valuation_request (status=pending), enqueues SQS message with valuation_request_id; background worker dequeues message, runs ValuationOrchestrator: (1) fetch 6 nearest comp_sales from provider API (cached 24h by address), (2) fetch tax_records from county data feed, (3) call ML AVM model returning avm_estimate + confidence_low/confidence_high, (4) check Redis cache for LLM narrative by property fingerprint - on miss, build structured prompt with comp table + tax fields + AVM estimate, call GPT-4o via PrivateLink, parse narrative_body and confidence_statement, store model_runs record, cache response; (5) in parallel, ShadowModeRunner calls legacy AVM and inserts shadow_runs record with legacy_estimate and delta_pct; (6) update valuation_request status=complete. Appraiser polls GET /valuations/:id until status=complete, then loads GET /valuations/:id/report. Appraiser Certification Workflow: appraiser reviews report including AVM estimate, confidence interval, LLM narrative, and comp sales table; if satisfied, calls POST /valuations/:id/certify which sets appraisal_reports.certified_by and certified_at; system generates PDF via S3 and returns download URL. Override Workflow: review_appraiser calls POST /valuations/:id/override with override_estimate and reason; appraisal_reports.overridden set to true; audit_events record written with original and override values.
target_output.data_model: properties (id UUID PK, address_normalized TEXT UNIQUE, latitude NUMERIC, longitude NUMERIC, property_type ENUM residential/commercial/multi_family, sqft INT, bedrooms SMALLINT, bathrooms NUMERIC, year_built SMALLINT). comparable_sales (id UUID PK, subject_property_id UUID FK, sale_price NUMERIC(12,2), sale_date DATE, distance_miles NUMERIC, sqft INT, bedrooms SMALLINT, address TEXT, source TEXT, fetched_at TIMESTAMPTZ). tax_records (id UUID PK, property_id UUID FK, assessed_value NUMERIC(12,2), last_sale_date DATE, last_sale_price NUMERIC(12,2), tax_year SMALLINT, annual_tax NUMERIC, source TEXT). valuation_requests (id UUID PK, property_id UUID FK, appraiser_id UUID FK, status ENUM pending/processing/complete/failed, comp_radius_miles NUMERIC, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). model_runs (id UUID PK, valuation_request_id UUID FK, avm_estimate NUMERIC(12,2), confidence_low NUMERIC(12,2), confidence_high NUMERIC(12,2), llm_narrative TEXT, llm_model_version TEXT, llm_prompt_template_version INT, llm_cost_usd NUMERIC(6,4), run_duration_ms INT, cache_hit BOOLEAN, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). shadow_runs (id UUID PK, valuation_request_id UUID FK, legacy_estimate NUMERIC(12,2), new_estimate NUMERIC(12,2), delta_pct NUMERIC(5,2), created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). appraisal_reports (id UUID PK, valuation_request_id UUID FK UNIQUE, final_estimate NUMERIC(12,2), overridden BOOLEAN DEFAULT false, override_reason TEXT, certified_by UUID FK, certified_at TIMESTAMPTZ, pdf_s3_key TEXT). audit_events (id UUID PK, event_type TEXT, actor_id UUID, resource_id UUID, payload_json JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). llm_prompt_templates (id UUID PK, version INT UNIQUE, template_text TEXT, active BOOLEAN, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). Property valuation financial entities: valuation_accounts (id UUID PK, property_id FK, valuation_date DATE, avm_value_cents BIGINT, confidence_score NUMERIC, valuation_method ENUM hedonic/comps/hybrid, currency CHAR(3), model_version VARCHAR), transaction_history (id UUID PK, property_id FK, sale_date DATE, sale_price_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), buyer_id VARCHAR, seller_id VARCHAR, transaction_type ENUM arms_length/distressed/foreclosure, recorded_at DATE), mortgage_ledger (id UUID PK, property_id FK, lender_id VARCHAR, origination_date DATE, loan_amount_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), outstanding_balance_cents BIGINT, lien_position INT).
target_output.api_design: POST /v1/valuations body {address, comp_radius_miles} returns {valuation_request_id, status: 'pending'}. GET /v1/valuations/:id returns {status, pipeline_stage, progress_pct}. GET /v1/valuations/:id/report returns full report JSON including avm_estimate, confidence_low, confidence_high, llm_narrative, comp_sales array, shadow_run legacy_estimate. POST /v1/valuations/:id/certify (appraiser role) sets certified_by and triggers PDF generation. POST /v1/valuations/:id/override body {override_estimate, reason} (review_appraiser role only). GET /v1/shadow-runs/comparison?since=2026-01-01 returns aggregate accuracy stats (mean_delta_pct, p95_delta_pct, count) for shadow mode validation. GET /v1/admin/llm-costs?period=30d returns total LLM spend by day and cache_hit_rate. All endpoints require Auth0 JWT; RBAC enforced per endpoint.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth0 MFA enforced for all appraiser and review_appraiser accounts; 15-minute JWT TTL with silent refresh. RBAC: appraiser role restricted to own valuation_requests; review_appraiser can access all reports and shadow comparison. Financial data (avm_estimate, sale_price, assessed_value) stored with AES-256 encryption at rest via KMS. LLM API calls via AWS PrivateLink endpoint - GPT-4o prompt data never traverses public internet. Prompt injection mitigation: comp sales and tax record fields sanitized before prompt assembly; LLM output validated for required sections (narrative_body, confidence_statement) before storage. audit_events table captures every valuation submission, LLM call (with prompt_hash for reproducibility, not full prompt text), and appraiser override for USPAP and Fair Housing compliance evidence. USPAP compliance: appraisal_reports.certified_by and certified_at required fields enforce human appraiser certification before report delivery; AI outputs never delivered as final values without certification. Fair Housing: shadow_runs delta_pct analyzed monthly by neighborhood demographics for bias detection.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI: pytest with mocked LLM responses (recorded fixtures) and synthetic comp/tax data, 80% coverage gate, docker build multi-stage, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with health check gate. Three ECS Fargate task definitions: api (2-4 tasks, autoscale CPU 70%), frontend (2 tasks fixed), background-worker (1-3 tasks autoscale on SQS ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible). RDS PostgreSQL multi-AZ with automated backups. Secrets in AWS Secrets Manager: Auth0 client secret, OpenAI API key, comp data provider API key, 90-day rotation. LLM prompt template changes deployed via DB migration (llm_prompt_templates insert with new version, flip active flag) with no code deployment required. Environments: dev (single tasks, RDS t3.medium), staging (2 tasks, RDS r6g.large, production comp data snapshot for shadow-mode accuracy testing), production (autoscaling, RDS r6g.xlarge multi-AZ).
target_output.observability: Datadog APM tags every request span with appraiser_id, valuation_request_id, and pipeline_stage. Custom metrics: valuation_pipeline_duration_seconds histogram by stage (comp_fetch, avm_model, llm_call, pdf_gen), llm_call_cost_usd counter per model_run, llm_cache_hit_rate gauge (target > 30% to validate caching strategy), shadow_mode_delta_pct histogram (tracks accuracy convergence during 90-day period), appraiser_override_rate gauge (high override rate signals LLM narrative quality issues). Datadog dashboard: daily valuation volume, pipeline stage latency breakdown, LLM cumulative cost by week, shadow-mode delta distribution chart. PagerDuty: SQS queue depth > 50 with no worker consumption (P2), LLM API error rate > 5% (P2), RDS connection count > 80% (P3).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GPT-4o cost: ~$0.03 per valuation (prompt ~800 tokens + response ~400 tokens). At 100 valuations/day: ~$3/day LLM costs, offset to ~$2/day with 30% cache hit rate target. ECS Fargate: ~$150/month baseline for 3 services. RDS r6g.xlarge multi-AZ: ~$400/month. ElastiCache Redis: ~$50/month. Total estimated ~$700/month at 100 valuations/day. LLM cost spikes monitored via Datadog alert if daily_llm_cost_usd exceeds $20. Cache TTL tuning: reduce to 12 hours in volatile markets to improve narrative freshness at higher API cost.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): properties and comp_sales ingestion pipeline, tax record fetch integration, ML AVM model with confidence interval output, GPT-4o LLM narrative generation via PrivateLink, Redis response cache, shadow_runs parallel legacy AVM recording, Next.js appraisal review UI with comp sales table, appraiser certification workflow, Auth0 MFA, audit_events table, GitLab CI/CD. Phase 2 (6 weeks): listing photo analysis via GPT-4o vision for property condition scoring, MLS direct comp integration replacing manual API calls, shadow-mode accuracy dashboard for review appraisers, Fair Housing bias monitoring monthly report. Phase 3 (4 weeks): legacy AVM decommission after 90-day shadow validation sign-off, automated USPAP compliance report export, self-service comp radius and model weight adjustment for review appraisers.
target_output.development_guidelines: LLM prompt templates managed as versioned DB records in llm_prompt_templates, not hardcoded strings; all LLM calls reference active template_version. Mock LLM responses using recorded fixtures in pytest - never call real OpenAI API in CI. Comp sales and tax record fields must pass input validation before prompt assembly - no raw external API responses in LLM context. 80% test coverage gate with mandatory tests for: pipeline stage failure handling, cache hit and miss paths, appraiser override audit event creation, USPAP certification gate (certify endpoint rejects uncertified report delivery). Financial amounts stored as NUMERIC(12,2) - no floating point for valuation figures.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GPT-4o narrative quality degrades for property types underrepresented in training data (e.g., rural land parcels). Mitigation: shadow-mode accuracy tracking by property_type; high delta_pct segments trigger manual prompt refinement. Risk: comp data provider API deprecates fields used in LLM prompt, causing incomplete narratives. Mitigation: provider API response schema validated at ingestion with Datadog alert on unexpected field changes. Tradeoff: 24-hour LLM cache TTL reduces cost but may return stale narratives when new comp sales occur on active properties; accepted for MVP with monitoring of appraiser override rate as signal. Tradeoff: async SQS pipeline over synchronous LLM call adds complexity but prevents appraiser UI timeouts on 2-8 second LLM response times.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Comp data provider confirmed as CoreLogic API pending contract sign-off; architecture is provider-agnostic with adapter pattern. Selected SQS over direct async await for LLM calls to allow background-worker scaling independent of API service. Confirmed USPAP requires numeric confidence interval in output - ML model must expose confidence_low/confidence_high, not just point estimate. LLM prompt stores hash only in audit_events (not full prompt text) to prevent sensitive financial data in audit log.

metadata.notes: LLM-augmented AVM migration with shadow-mode parallel operation; comp data provider is an open question reducing plan completeness to moderate.

project_goal.value: Decompose a monolithic property maintenance request system into independently deployable microservices: work order creation, vendor dispatch, status tracking, and invoice reconciliation, communicating via a message broker
project_goal.rationale: Monolithic system has 4-hour deployment windows affecting all maintenance workflows; decomposition enables independent deployability per service domain

target_users.value: Internal property operations staff (maintenance coordinators, facility managers) and vendors who receive dispatch notifications; no external tenant-facing interface in scope
target_users.rationale: Internal operations tool; tenant-facing request submission is handled by the existing tenant portal which will post to the new work order API

project_class.rationale: Backend automation system decomposing a workflow domain into coordinated services; no interactive frontend in scope

capabilities.rationale: Multi-service backend with message broker integration, invoice reconciliation batch job, and AKS devops infrastructure

complexity_level.rationale: Microservices decomposition with event-driven coordination, distributed data ownership per service, and saga pattern for cross-service workflows

risk_level.rationale: Internal operational system; data is business-internal with no direct financial transaction processing

data_sensitivity.rationale: Work orders, vendor data, and invoice amounts are internal business operations data

external_exposure.rationale: All services operate on internal Azure VNet; vendor notifications sent outbound but no external API exposure

access_model.value: Azure AD authentication via MSAL for internal staff; vendor notifications delivered via outbound email/SMS with no vendor login to the system
access_model.rationale: Internal staff access via corporate Azure AD SSO; vendors receive read-only dispatch notifications

feature_scope.value: Work order creation from tenant portal webhook, vendor assignment from approved vendor list by trade category, dispatch event publication to vendor notification service, real-time status tracking across work order lifecycle, digital invoice submission by vendor, invoice-to-work-order reconciliation with discrepancy flagging, manager approval workflow for flagged invoices, payment processing trigger to ERP
feature_scope.rationale: Full maintenance workflow from tenant request to vendor payment

mvp_scope.value: Work order creation service, vendor dispatch service with assignment logic, status tracking API, basic invoice submission; invoice reconciliation and ERP payment trigger in Phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: Core dispatch workflow delivers immediate value before reconciliation complexity

security_baseline.value: Azure AD MSAL for all internal API calls, TLS 1.3 on all inter-service communication via Service Mesh (Linkerd), secrets in Azure Key Vault, all inter-service events authenticated with signed Azure Service Bus messages
security_baseline.rationale: Internal microservices security baseline with service mesh for mTLS between pods

frontend_stack.value: none - backend automation services only; existing tenant portal and operations dashboard consume the new APIs
frontend_stack.rationale: Frontend work is out of scope; existing portals will integrate via API

backend_stack.value: Node.js 20 with Express; each microservice is a separate Node.js application
backend_stack.rationale: Existing team expertise in Node.js; Express for lightweight per-service HTTP servers

data_platform.value: MongoDB 7 with per-service database isolation (each microservice owns its own MongoDB database); Redis 7 for distributed cache and idempotency keys
data_platform.rationale: MongoDB document model suits work order and vendor data flexibility; per-service DB isolation enforces microservice data ownership boundaries

hosting_target.value: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) Autopilot
hosting_target.rationale: AKS Autopilot for managed node provisioning; Kubernetes native for microservices orchestration

privacy_retention_policy.value: Work orders and vendor records retained 5 years for compliance; invoices retained 7 years per financial record requirements; audit events retained 3 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Financial record retention requirements for invoice data

future_scope.value: Tenant-facing work order status portal, predictive maintenance scheduling based on historical work order patterns, vendor performance scoring
future_scope.rationale: Phase 3 roadmap items pending MVP stabilization

constraints.value: Must integrate with existing tenant portal via webhook (no tenant portal code changes); must publish payment trigger events compatible with existing SAP ERP integration; Azure-only cloud; migration from monolith must be zero-downtime
constraints.rationale: Operational continuity requirements for active maintenance workflows during migration

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor + Application Insights for distributed tracing across services, correlation IDs propagated via message headers, PagerDuty for work order processing failures
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate monitoring standard; distributed tracing essential for multi-service debugging

execution_preference.value: Strangler-fig migration pattern: new services deployed alongside monolith, traffic migrated per domain, monolith modules retired progressively
execution_preference.rationale: Strangler-fig minimizes migration risk by allowing rollback per service domain

project.goal: Decompose monolithic maintenance system into 4 independently deployable microservices on AKS
project.target_users: Internal maintenance coordinators, facility managers; vendors via outbound notifications
project.timeline: Phase 1 (work orders + dispatch) live in 10 weeks

backend.framework: Express with per-service router structure; Mongoose for MongoDB ODM
backend.api: Internal REST APIs per service; Azure Service Bus for cross-service events

security.auth: Azure AD MSAL JWT validation per service
security.transport: Linkerd service mesh for mTLS inter-service; TLS 1.3 for external
security.secrets: Azure Key Vault with pod-managed identity

data.db: MongoDB 7, separate database per service on shared Atlas cluster
data.cache: Redis 7 for idempotency keys and status cache

devops.ci: Azure DevOps pipelines with Jest tests, Docker build, Helm chart deploy to AKS

constraints.tenant_portal: Webhook integration only - no tenant portal code changes
constraints.erp: SAP ERP payment trigger event format must match existing integration

open_questions.saga_pattern: Distributed saga or choreography for cross-service work order lifecycle? Saga preferred for explicit failure compensation.

reasoner_reviews.product: The four service boundaries (work order creation, vendor dispatch, status tracking, invoice reconciliation) are well-aligned with the natural ownership boundaries in the maintenance workflow. The strangler-fig migration pattern is correct for zero-downtime migration. Invoice reconciliation is appropriately scoped to Phase 2 since it touches the SAP ERP integration which has the most external dependency risk.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: MongoDB per-service database isolation enforces the microservice data ownership pattern correctly. The choreography-based event model via Azure Service Bus is the right choice over orchestration for this workflow - each service reacts to events without a central coordinator becoming a bottleneck. However, the saga compensation logic for failed vendor dispatch (when no available vendor matches trade category) needs explicit design. Node.js 20 is appropriate for event-driven services; async/await with proper error handling for Service Bus message processing is critical.
reasoner_reviews.security: Azure AD MSAL JWT validation per service is correct for internal staff authentication. Linkerd service mesh provides mTLS for inter-pod communication without application-layer certificate management. Azure Key Vault with pod-managed identity prevents credential sprawl. Service Bus message signing should be validated at the consumer side to prevent message spoofing between services.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The SAP ERP payment trigger event format is a hard constraint that must be documented as an interface contract before invoice reconciliation service development begins. The strangler-fig migration requires a routing layer (API gateway or Azure Application Gateway) that can direct traffic to monolith vs new services per domain. Zero-downtime requirement means the monolith must continue processing work orders until the new work order service is validated in production.

backend.service_design: Four Node.js 20 Express microservices: WorkOrderService (creates work_orders on tenant portal webhook, validates urgency and property context, publishes WorkOrderCreated event), VendorDispatchService (consumes WorkOrderCreated, queries vendors by trade_category and availability, assigns vendor, publishes VendorDispatched event), StatusTrackingService (consumes all lifecycle events, maintains denormalized work_order_status document for fast read, exposes status query API to operations dashboard), InvoiceReconciliationService (consumes WorkOrderCompleted event, receives vendor invoice submission, matches against work order scope, flags discrepancies, triggers SAP ERP payment event on approval).
backend.api_patterns: Each service exposes an internal REST API within the AKS VNet. WorkOrderService: POST /work-orders (from tenant portal webhook), GET /work-orders/:id. VendorDispatchService: GET /vendors (filtered by trade_category, availability). StatusTrackingService: GET /status/:work_order_id (real-time status). InvoiceReconciliationService: POST /invoices/:work_order_id (vendor submits invoice), PATCH /invoices/:id/approve (manager approval). Azure Service Bus topics for cross-service events.
backend.business_modules: Vendor assignment logic in VendorDispatchService: query vendors collection filtered by trade_category matching work order type, sort by service_contract.priority_score descending, filter availability_status=available, assign first match. If no match: publish VendorAssignmentFailed event, trigger escalation notification. Saga compensation: StatusTrackingService detects VendorAssignmentFailed and updates work_order_status to escalated for manual coordinator intervention.
backend.failure_handling: Azure Service Bus message lock timeout with 3 retries before dead-letter. Each service implements idempotent message processing using Redis idempotency keys (message_id hash, 24h TTL). Unprocessable messages in dead-letter queue trigger Application Insights alert. Circuit breaker per external dependency (SAP ERP endpoint, vendor notification SMS gateway).

security.auth_design: Azure AD MSAL JWT validation middleware in each Express service. Pod-managed identity for AKS pods to access Key Vault secrets without stored credentials. Service Bus connections use managed identity connection strings from Key Vault.
security.authorization_model: RBAC enforced per service endpoint: maintenance_coordinator role can create work orders and view status; facility_manager role can approve invoices and access reconciliation reports; vendor_dispatch role (service account) for VendorDispatchService internal operations.
security.secrets_management: Azure Key Vault stores MongoDB connection strings, Service Bus connection strings, SAP ERP endpoint credentials, SMS gateway API key. Key Vault references injected into AKS pods via Azure Key Vault Provider for Secrets Store CSI Driver - no secrets in Helm chart values or environment variables.

data.entities: WorkOrderService DB: work_orders (id, tenant_id, property_id, unit_id, trade_category ENUM plumbing/electrical/hvac/general, urgency ENUM routine/urgent/emergency, description, status, created_at, source_webhook_id). VendorDispatchService DB: vendors (id, name, trade_categories Array, availability_status, service_contract OBJECT, notification_email, notification_phone), dispatch_events (id, work_order_id, vendor_id, dispatched_at, acknowledged_at). StatusTrackingService DB: work_order_statuses (work_order_id as _id, current_status, status_history Array, last_updated). InvoiceReconciliationService DB: invoices (id, work_order_id, vendor_id, line_items Array, total_amount, submitted_at, reconciliation_status ENUM pending/approved/flagged/rejected), service_contracts (id, vendor_id, work_type, agreed_rate_schedule OBJECT).
data.storage_design: MongoDB Atlas shared cluster with four separate databases (workorder-db, dispatch-db, status-db, invoice-db). Atlas Search indexes on work_orders for trade_category + status queries. Redis 7 Azure Cache for message idempotency keys and StatusTrackingService hot-path cache (work_order_status TTL 60s).
data.consistency_notes: Eventual consistency between services via Service Bus events. StatusTrackingService is the read model for work order lifecycle; it may lag by one event delivery cycle (typically < 1 second). Invoice reconciliation must re-fetch work order scope from WorkOrderService API at time of reconciliation to ensure most current approved scope.

devops.deployment_topology: AKS Autopilot cluster in Azure East US 2. Each microservice deployed as a separate Kubernetes Deployment with its own Helm chart. Linkerd service mesh installed at cluster level. Azure Application Gateway Ingress Controller routes internal API traffic. Azure Service Bus Standard tier with four topics (WorkOrderEvents, DispatchEvents, StatusEvents, InvoiceEvents) and per-service subscriptions.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps pipeline per service: Jest unit tests (85% coverage gate), integration tests against MongoDB memory server and Service Bus emulator, Docker build, ACR push, Helm chart upgrade to AKS. Canary deployment via Flagger for production rollout (10% traffic, 5-minute stability window before 100%). Monolith strangler-fig routing: Azure Application Gateway path-based routing moves /work-orders to new service when Phase 1 validated.
devops.rollback_strategy: Helm rollback to previous chart version per service. Azure Service Bus dead-letter queue monitored for processing failures during rollout. Canary rollout aborted by Flagger on error rate > 1% during 5-minute window.

target_output.thinking_summary: Decomposing a monolithic property maintenance system into four Node.js 20 Express microservices on AKS using choreography-based event coordination via Azure Service Bus. Key architectural decisions: MongoDB per-service database isolation enforces data ownership boundaries and prevents the cross-service join anti-pattern; choreography over orchestration avoids a central coordinator bottleneck for the work order lifecycle state machine; StatusTrackingService as a dedicated read model (CQRS pattern) enables fast status queries without coupling to the source-of-truth services; strangler-fig migration with Application Gateway path routing enables per-service rollout without tenant portal changes.
target_output.title: Property Maintenance Microservices Decomposition Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Node.js 20 Express microservices architecture on Azure AKS Autopilot decomposing a monolithic property maintenance system into four independently deployable services: WorkOrderService, VendorDispatchService, StatusTrackingService, and InvoiceReconciliationService. Services communicate via Azure Service Bus choreography events. Each service owns a dedicated MongoDB 7 database. Linkerd service mesh provides mTLS for inter-service communication. Strangler-fig migration pattern enables zero-downtime transition from the monolith with per-service rollout via Application Gateway path routing.
target_output.architecture_overview: Four Node.js 20 Express services deployed as independent Kubernetes Deployments on AKS Autopilot, communicating asynchronously via Azure Service Bus topics using choreography-based event flow. WorkOrderService receives tenant portal webhooks and publishes WorkOrderCreated events. VendorDispatchService consumes WorkOrderCreated events, applies vendor assignment logic against its vendors database, and publishes VendorDispatched events. StatusTrackingService consumes all lifecycle events and maintains a denormalized work_order_statuses read model (CQRS pattern) for fast status queries by the operations dashboard. InvoiceReconciliationService consumes WorkOrderCompleted events, handles vendor invoice submission via REST, performs line-item scope matching against service_contracts, flags discrepancies for manager approval, and publishes SAP ERP-compatible payment trigger events. Linkerd service mesh enforces mTLS between all pods. Azure Key Vault with pod-managed identity stores per-service credentials. Azure Application Gateway routes /work-orders path to new service during strangler-fig migration with monolith fallback.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Node.js 20, Express 4, Mongoose 8 ODM for MongoDB, @azure/service-bus SDK, @azure/identity for managed identity, ioredis for Redis. Infrastructure: AKS Autopilot (Azure East US 2), Azure Service Bus Standard (4 topics), MongoDB Atlas (4 databases on shared cluster), Azure Cache for Redis (Standard C1), Azure Application Gateway with Ingress Controller, Azure Container Registry, Azure Key Vault, Linkerd 2.14 service mesh. CI/CD: Azure DevOps pipelines, Helm 3 charts per service, Flagger for canary deployments, mongodb-memory-server for integration tests. Monitoring: Azure Monitor + Application Insights with distributed tracing, PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Work Order Creation: tenant portal POST webhook triggers WorkOrderService to validate trade_category, urgency, and property context; creates work_orders document; publishes WorkOrderCreated Service Bus event. Vendor Assignment: VendorDispatchService applies priority-scored vendor matching by trade_category; publishes VendorDispatched on success or VendorAssignmentFailed on no-match for escalation. Status Tracking: StatusTrackingService maintains work_order_statuses read model updated by all service events; GET /status/:work_order_id returns current status and full history. Invoice Submission and Reconciliation: vendor submits digital invoice to InvoiceReconciliationService; line items matched against service_contract agreed_rate_schedule; discrepancies > 10% flagged for manager approval; approved invoices trigger SAP ERP-compatible payment event. Strangler-Fig Migration: Application Gateway path routing gradually migrates /work-orders traffic from monolith to new services per domain.
target_output.system_components: Azure Application Gateway Ingress Controller (path-based routing for strangler-fig migration, TLS termination), AKS Autopilot cluster (WorkOrderService, VendorDispatchService, StatusTrackingService, InvoiceReconciliationService Deployments), Linkerd service mesh (mTLS between all pods, traffic metrics), Azure Service Bus Standard (WorkOrderEvents topic, DispatchEvents topic, StatusEvents topic, InvoiceEvents topic with per-service subscriptions), MongoDB Atlas cluster (workorder-db, dispatch-db, status-db, invoice-db databases), Azure Cache for Redis Standard (message idempotency keys TTL 24h, status hot-path cache TTL 60s), Azure Key Vault (MongoDB connection strings, Service Bus connection strings, SAP ERP credentials, SMS gateway API key), Azure Container Registry (service Docker images), Application Insights (distributed traces with correlation IDs propagated via Service Bus message properties).
target_output.workflows: Work Order Lifecycle Workflow: tenant portal POSTs webhook to WorkOrderService /work-orders with {tenant_id, unit_id, trade_category, urgency, description}; WorkOrderService validates trade_category enum, creates work_orders document with status=created, publishes WorkOrderCreated event to Service Bus with message correlation_id; VendorDispatchService consumes event, queries vendors by trade_category and availability_status=available sorted by service_contract.priority_score, assigns top match, creates dispatch_events document, updates vendor availability_status=dispatched, publishes VendorDispatched event; StatusTrackingService consumes VendorDispatched and upserts work_order_statuses with status=vendor_assigned and appended history entry; vendor receives outbound email/SMS via notification gateway. Invoice Reconciliation Workflow: on job completion, vendor POSTs to InvoiceReconciliationService /invoices/:work_order_id with line_items array; service fetches original scope from WorkOrderService GET /work-orders/:id, loads service_contract for vendor/work_type, compares line_item totals against agreed_rate_schedule; if all items within agreed rates: status=approved, publish PaymentAuthorized event in SAP ERP message format; if any item > 10% over agreed rate: status=flagged, notify facility_manager for PATCH /invoices/:id/approve; approved event triggers ERP payment. Vendor Assignment Failure Saga: VendorDispatchService finds no available vendor for trade_category; publishes VendorAssignmentFailed event; StatusTrackingService updates work_order_status to escalated; WorkOrderService notification module sends escalation alert to maintenance_coordinator for manual vendor selection.
target_output.data_model: WorkOrderService (workorder-db): work_orders collection {_id UUID, tenant_id, property_id, unit_id, trade_category ENUM plumbing/electrical/hvac/general/landscaping, urgency ENUM routine/urgent/emergency, description String, status ENUM created/dispatched/in_progress/completed/cancelled, source_webhook_id String UNIQUE, created_at, updated_at}. VendorDispatchService (dispatch-db): vendors collection {_id UUID, name, trade_categories Array<String>, availability_status ENUM available/dispatched/on_leave, service_contract {vendor_id, trade_categories Array, priority_score Int, rate_schedule Object}, notification_email, notification_phone, active Boolean}; dispatch_events collection {_id UUID, work_order_id, vendor_id, dispatched_at, acknowledged_at, completed_at}. StatusTrackingService (status-db): work_order_statuses collection {_id work_order_id, current_status, status_history Array<{status, timestamp, actor_service}>, last_updated}. InvoiceReconciliationService (invoice-db): invoices collection {_id UUID, work_order_id, vendor_id, line_items Array<{description, quantity, unit_price, total}>, submitted_total NUMERIC, approved_total NUMERIC, reconciliation_status ENUM pending/approved/flagged/rejected, discrepancy_reason String, submitted_at, resolved_at}; service_contracts collection {_id UUID, vendor_id, work_type, agreed_rate_schedule Object, effective_from DATE, effective_to DATE}.
target_output.api_design: WorkOrderService: POST /v1/work-orders (tenant portal webhook authentication via shared secret header), GET /v1/work-orders/:id (internal, Azure AD auth). VendorDispatchService: GET /v1/vendors?trade_category=plumbing&availability=available (internal query), POST /v1/dispatch/:work_order_id/assign (internal, triggered by Service Bus consumer - not HTTP). StatusTrackingService: GET /v1/status/:work_order_id returns {work_order_id, current_status, status_history, last_updated} (operations dashboard consumer, Azure AD auth). InvoiceReconciliationService: POST /v1/invoices/:work_order_id body {vendor_id, line_items} (vendor invoice submission via managed vendor portal integration), PATCH /v1/invoices/:id/approve body {approved_amount, notes} (facility_manager role, Azure AD auth), GET /v1/invoices?status=flagged (manager review queue). All services propagate X-Correlation-ID header for distributed tracing.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Azure AD MSAL JWT validation middleware in every Express service endpoint; pod-managed identity eliminates service account password management. Linkerd service mesh enforces mTLS for all pod-to-pod communication within AKS cluster without application-layer certificate management. Azure Key Vault secrets injected via Secrets Store CSI Driver; no secrets in Helm values or ConfigMaps. Service Bus message idempotency: each consumer checks Redis for message_id hash before processing; prevents duplicate work orders or double-dispatches on message retry. Vendor invoice submission authenticated via tenant portal service account MSAL token. Audit trail: every Service Bus event published to Application Insights as a custom event with correlation_id, service_name, event_type, and work_order_id for end-to-end tracing. InvoiceReconciliationService audit log records approval actor_id and timestamp for financial compliance. Data retention: MongoDB Atlas TTL index on work_orders older than 5 years (excluding invoices which use 7-year TTL per financial retention policy).
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Azure DevOps pipeline per microservice: Jest unit tests with mongodb-memory-server and Service Bus emulator for integration tests (85% coverage gate), Docker build tagged with commit SHA, ACR push, Helm chart upgrade to AKS dev namespace for smoke tests, promote to staging, canary production rollout via Flagger (10% traffic for 5-minute stability window, auto-promote on error rate < 1%, auto-rollback on breach). Independent per-service deployments are the key migration benefit: WorkOrderService deployable without touching InvoiceReconciliationService. Strangler-fig migration sequence: (1) deploy new services to AKS alongside monolith, (2) configure Application Gateway to route /work-orders to WorkOrderService with /admin as monolith fallback, (3) validate 1 week with synthetic production traffic, (4) migrate /dispatch routes, (5) validate, (6) retire monolith module per domain. MongoDB Atlas backup: continuous cloud backup with 7-day point-in-time recovery. Azure Monitor + Application Insights dashboards per service with correlation ID drilldown.
target_output.observability: Application Insights distributed tracing with correlation IDs propagated as Service Bus message user properties. Custom Application Insights events: work_order_created (with trade_category and urgency dimensions), vendor_dispatched (with vendor_id and assignment_duration_ms), vendor_assignment_failed (with trade_category and count of available vendors), invoice_flagged (with discrepancy_pct), payment_authorized (with approved_total). Azure Monitor alert rules: work_order_created with no VendorDispatched event within 10 minutes (P2 - vendor assignment stuck), vendor_assignment_failed count > 3 in 1 hour by trade_category (P2 - vendor capacity issue), Service Bus dead-letter queue depth > 5 per subscription (P2), InvoiceReconciliationService error rate > 2% (P3). PagerDuty integration on P2 alerts. Per-service Application Insights dashboard with request rate, error rate, and dependency call latency (MongoDB, Service Bus).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: AKS Autopilot: ~$200/month for 4 microservice deployments at 2 replicas each (node provisioning on demand). MongoDB Atlas M10 shared cluster: ~$180/month. Azure Service Bus Standard: ~$10/month for 4 topics at typical work order volume. Azure Cache for Redis C1: ~$55/month. Application Insights: ~$30/month at typical log volume. Azure Application Gateway: ~$125/month. Estimated total ~$600/month. Scaling: each service scales independently via Kubernetes HPA on CPU. VendorDispatchService scales on Service Bus queue depth during peak maintenance periods. MongoDB Atlas autoscaling enabled per database.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (10 weeks): WorkOrderService (tenant portal webhook integration, work_orders CRUD, WorkOrderCreated Service Bus publisher), VendorDispatchService (vendor assignment logic, VendorDispatched publisher, VendorAssignmentFailed saga), StatusTrackingService (event consumers, work_order_statuses read model, GET /status API), Linkerd service mesh installation, Azure Service Bus topic configuration, Application Insights distributed tracing, Azure DevOps CI/CD pipelines, strangler-fig routing configuration for /work-orders migration. Phase 2 (6 weeks): InvoiceReconciliationService (invoice submission, line-item reconciliation, discrepancy flagging, manager approval workflow, SAP ERP payment trigger event), service_contracts management API, invoice financial audit trail, MongoDB 7-year TTL index for invoice-db. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Retire monolith maintenance modules post validation, vendor performance scoring service, predictive maintenance batch job analyzing historical work_orders patterns, tenant-facing work order status read API.
target_output.development_guidelines: Each microservice owns its MongoDB database exclusively; cross-service queries forbidden - use Service Bus events for data propagation to consumer read models. Service Bus consumers must be idempotent: check Redis idempotency key (SHA-256 of message_id, 24h TTL) before processing any event. Express error middleware must return consistent RFC 7807 problem+json across all services for operations dashboard compatibility. X-Correlation-ID header propagated from inbound webhook through all Service Bus message user properties and outbound API calls - required for Application Insights trace stitching. 85% Jest coverage gate with mandatory tests for: vendor assignment priority sort logic, reconciliation discrepancy detection boundary conditions (exactly 10%, just over, just under), saga compensation flow on VendorAssignmentFailed, and Service Bus dead-letter trigger scenario.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Eventual consistency in StatusTrackingService means operations dashboard may show a 1-2 second lag behind actual work order state when Service Bus delivery is slow. Mitigation: cache TTL set to 60s for status documents; acceptable for maintenance coordinator use case. Risk: SAP ERP payment trigger event format changes require synchronized updates to InvoiceReconciliationService and ERP integration. Mitigation: document ERP event schema as a versioned contract in ADR; any format change requires joint change review. Tradeoff: MongoDB per-service database isolation over shared PostgreSQL prevents direct joins across service data; accepted as the correct microservices pattern - cross-service queries route through APIs. Tradeoff: Azure Service Bus Standard tier over Premium loses VNET integration; accepted for cost at current work order volume with upgrade path to Premium if compliance requires network isolation.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Choreography over orchestration (saga) selected to avoid central coordinator; explicit saga compensation handles VendorAssignmentFailed. StatusTrackingService as dedicated CQRS read model selected over querying WorkOrderService directly from operations dashboard. MongoDB per-service isolation confirmed as the correct pattern despite forgoing cross-service joins. Application Gateway path routing confirmed as the strangler-fig mechanism; no tenant portal changes required.

metadata.notes: Strangler-fig microservices decomposition of maintenance monolith with Azure Service Bus choreography, per-service MongoDB isolation, and CQRS status tracking.

project_goal.value: Build a scalable AI-driven tenant screening platform processing thousands of rental applications per day, analyzing credit reports, rental history, income verification documents, and eviction records to produce risk-scored recommendations for landlords
project_goal.rationale: Existing manual screening takes 3-5 days per application; AI screening reduces to under 2 hours while processing at portfolio scale

target_users.value: Landlords and property management companies submitting rental applications for AI screening; applicants whose data is processed but who are not direct platform users
target_users.rationale: Landlords are the paying customers; applicants are data subjects with FCRA rights

project_class.rationale: AI risk scoring and document analysis are the primary platform differentiators

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, ai_llm, auth, analytics, integrations, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: Full-stack platform with LLM document analysis, bureau integrations, batch processing, and analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Thousands of applications per day requires async pipeline at scale with multiple external bureau integrations

risk_level.rationale: FCRA-regulated consumer credit data; Fair Housing Act compliance; adverse action notice requirements

data_sensitivity.rationale: Credit reports, income data, and eviction records are consumer financial data regulated under FCRA

external_exposure.rationale: Only registered landlord accounts can submit and view screening results

access_model.value: Cognito with MFA; landlord and admin RBAC roles; landlords scoped to their own applications only; admins access aggregate analytics and compliance reports
access_model.rationale: FCRA requires permissible purpose verification per landlord account; access scoping prevents cross-landlord data leakage

feature_scope.value: Application submission with document upload, credit bureau integration (Experian), rental history verification, income document OCR extraction, eviction record lookup, composite risk scoring with component breakdowns, landlord-configurable weighting rules, plain-language score explanation, adverse action notice generation, batch processing for portfolio applications
feature_scope.rationale: Complete screening workflow from application to adverse action compliance

mvp_scope.value: Application submission, Experian credit pull, income OCR extraction via LLM vision, composite risk score with default weights, plain-language explanation, basic landlord dashboard
mvp_scope.rationale: Core scoring workflow validated before configurable weighting and adverse action automation

security_baseline.value: Cognito MFA, AES-256 at rest with KMS, TLS 1.3, FCRA permissible purpose verification per landlord account, credit report data purged 7 years after applicant decision per FCRA, audit log for all credit data access
security_baseline.rationale: FCRA mandates permissible purpose, data security, and retention limits for consumer credit data

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; React ecosystem for complex application form and dashboard components

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI
backend_stack.rationale: Python ML and NLP ecosystem; FastAPI for async screening pipeline orchestration

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for applications and screening decisions; Redis 7 for pipeline state and idempotency; S3 for income document storage with KMS encryption
data_platform.rationale: Relational integrity for FCRA audit trail; Redis for high-throughput pipeline coordination

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers; Fargate for variable screening pipeline load without cluster management

privacy_retention_policy.value: Credit reports and screening decisions retained 7 years per FCRA; income documents purged 90 days after application decision; LLM extraction results retained with application record; applicant right to dispute screening decision supported
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: FCRA Section 607 retention requirements; income document minimization after decision

future_scope.value: State-specific compliance rule engine (NY ban-the-box, CA income ratio caps), multi-bureau integration (TransUnion, Equifax), self-service landlord weighting configuration UI, automated adverse action notice mailing
future_scope.rationale: Compliance rule engine and multi-bureau deferred until core platform validated

constraints.value: Experian FCRA API requires permissible purpose on each pull; LLM provider must support data processing agreements for consumer financial data; risk score must include component breakdown for FCRA adverse action notice compliance; no score explanation can reference protected class characteristics
constraints.rationale: FCRA and Fair Housing legal requirements embedded in platform design constraints

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with per-application pipeline traces, LLM extraction cost tracking, screening throughput and queue depth metrics, FCRA audit log integrity alerts
observability_baseline.rationale: Pipeline throughput and compliance audit integrity are critical operational requirements

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints; FCRA compliance review milestone before any credit bureau integration goes live
execution_preference.rationale: Compliance review milestone prevents regulatory exposure

compliance_context.value: FCRA compliance: permissible purpose per landlord account, adverse action notice with component-level score explanation, 7-year data retention, applicant dispute rights; Fair Housing Act: no protected class characteristics in scoring logic or explanations; GDPR for EU applicants if feature enabled; state-specific screening laws (NY, CA) in Phase 2 rule engine
compliance_context.rationale: FCRA Section 604 permissible purpose, Section 615 adverse action, and Fair Housing Act are non-negotiable compliance requirements

llm_integration.value: GPT-4o Vision via AWS PrivateLink for income document OCR extraction (pay stubs, bank statements, tax forms) - structured JSON output with extracted_monthly_income, income_source, document_confidence_score; GPT-4o text completion for plain-language risk score explanation generation from component scores - explanation must not reference protected class characteristics (validated by content filter); LLM outputs cached in Redis by document fingerprint (SHA-256 of S3 object ETag) for 24 hours to reduce repeat costs on resubmission
llm_integration.rationale: GPT-4o Vision selected for multi-format income document support; PrivateLink required for financial data DPA compliance

project.goal: Process thousands of rental applications per day with AI credit and income analysis and FCRA-compliant risk scoring
project.target_users: Landlords and property managers submitting screening requests
project.timeline: Phase 1 credit + income screening live in Q3

frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for application wizard state

backend.framework: FastAPI with async SQS consumer workers for pipeline
backend.api: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.0; Cognito JWT auth on all endpoints

security.encryption: AES-256 KMS at rest for all applicant data

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 RDS for applications and decisions
data.cache: Redis 7 for pipeline idempotency and LLM result cache

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with pytest, coverage, Docker, ECS deploy

constraints.fcra: Permissible purpose required per credit pull; adverse action with component breakdown
constraints.fair_housing: No protected class in scoring or explanations

reasoner_reviews.product: The round 1 plan correctly identified the FCRA adverse action notice requirement but did not fully design the component-level breakdown format required by law. The adverse action notice must identify the top 4 factors that negatively impacted the score in plain language without referencing protected class characteristics - this is a specific FCRA Section 615 requirement that must be hardcoded into the explanation generation pipeline.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The async SQS pipeline design is correct for high-scale processing. The round 1 plan had an architectural gap: the landlord custom weighting rules were stored as application-level config but the scoring engine re-read them on every application, creating a race condition where mid-batch weighting changes could produce inconsistent scores across a batch. Weights must be snapshotted at batch submission time and stored with the screening_decisions record.
reasoner_reviews.security: Round 1 plan lacked specificity on Fair Housing Act content filtering for LLM-generated explanations. The explanation generation prompt must explicitly instruct the model to avoid any reference to neighborhood demographics, school districts, or other Fair Housing proxy characteristics. Output must be validated against a prohibited-term list before storage. FCRA audit log completeness must be tested - every credit report fetch must have a corresponding audit event with permissible_purpose_verified=true.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: FCRA DPA with OpenAI is required before any production credit data passes through the LLM pipeline. Experian FCRA API rate limits (typically 10 concurrent pulls per account) must be modeled in the pipeline worker concurrency design to prevent API suspension during peak volume.

backend.service_design: Three FastAPI service modules: ApplicationIngestionService (handles landlord application submission, document upload to S3, SQS enqueue), ScreeningPipelineWorker (SQS consumer: credit bureau pull, income OCR, eviction lookup, composite scoring, explanation generation), DecisionQueryService (landlord-facing report API, adverse action notice generation). Pipeline workers scale independently on SQS queue depth.
backend.llm_integration_design: GPT-4o Vision for income OCR: S3 pre-signed URL passed to vision endpoint with prompt requesting structured JSON {extracted_monthly_income, income_source, document_type, confidence_score}. Output validated for required fields before storage. Cache in Redis by SHA-256 of S3 object ETag (24h TTL). GPT-4o text for explanation: prompt includes component scores (credit_score, income_ratio, rental_history, eviction_flag) with explicit instruction to generate FCRA-compliant top-4 adverse factors in plain language; output passed through Fair Housing content filter checking prohibited terms before storage. DPA verification: OpenAI Business Associate Agreement obtained before production use.
backend.api_patterns: POST /v1/applications (landlord submission with document presigned upload), GET /v1/applications/:id/status (pipeline progress polling), GET /v1/applications/:id/report (full screening report with component scores and explanation), POST /v1/applications/:id/adverse-action (generate FCRA adverse action notice PDF). GET /v1/analytics/throughput (admin, daily application volume and pipeline metrics).
backend.failure_handling: SQS dead-letter queue after 3 processing attempts. Failed applications trigger landlord notification and manual review flag. Experian API circuit breaker with fallback to cached bureau response for repeat applicants (same SSN last 24h). Pipeline worker idempotency via Redis key on application_id before each stage.

frontend.app_structure: React 18 SPA with three main feature areas: ApplicationWizard (multi-step form for application submission and document upload), ScreeningDashboard (landlord view of all applications with status and risk score summary), ReportViewer (detailed screening report with score breakdown, explanation, adverse action generation).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Application Submission: landlord fills applicant info, uploads income documents to S3 via presigned URL, submits; polling loop shows pipeline stage (fetching_credit, extracting_income, scoring, complete). Report View: displays composite risk_score with color-coded tier, component breakdown bars (credit 35%, income_ratio 30%, rental_history 25%, eviction 10%), LLM plain-language explanation, adverse action notice download button if score below landlord threshold.
frontend.state_management: React Query for server state (application status polling, report data). Zustand slice for ApplicationWizard multi-step form state. Polling interval: 5 seconds while status != complete.
frontend.frontend_security_notes: Income document uploads go directly to S3 via presigned URL - never through API server. Cognito JWT refreshed silently; no financial data stored in browser localStorage or sessionStorage.

security.auth_design: Cognito user pool with MFA enforcement for all landlord accounts. Permissible purpose attestation: at account creation, landlord signs FCRA permissible purpose agreement stored in landlord_accounts.fcra_agreement_signed_at. Each credit bureau pull validates fcra_agreement_signed_at is not null before executing.
security.authorization_model: Landlord role scoped to applications where landlord_id = JWT sub claim. Admin role accesses all applications and aggregate analytics. No cross-landlord data access. Applicant data redacted from API responses after 7-year retention period.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: fcra_audit_events table: every credit report fetch (application_id, landlord_id, experian_request_id, permissible_purpose_verified, fetched_at). Every LLM call (application_id, model_version, document_type, cost_usd, output_hash). Every screening decision access (landlord_id, application_id, action, accessed_at). Audit events are append-only (INSERT only, no UPDATE/DELETE) enforced by Row Level Security policy.
security.security_risks: LLM-generated explanations inadvertently referencing Fair Housing proxy terms (school districts, neighborhood quality) is the highest compliance risk. Mitigated by explicit prompt instruction and post-generation content filter against CFPB-published prohibited-term list.

data.entities: applications (id UUID PK, landlord_id FK, applicant_first_name_encrypted, applicant_last_name_encrypted, applicant_ssn_hash CHAR(64), property_address, monthly_rent NUMERIC, status ENUM submitted/processing/complete/failed, submitted_at, batch_id). income_documents (id UUID PK, application_id FK, s3_key, document_type ENUM pay_stub/bank_statement/tax_return/offer_letter, upload_status, purge_after DATE). credit_reports (id UUID PK, application_id FK, experian_report_id, credit_score INT, report_json_encrypted JSONB, pulled_at, expires_at). screening_decisions (id UUID PK, application_id FK, composite_risk_score NUMERIC, risk_tier ENUM approved/conditional/declined, score_components JSONB, weight_snapshot JSONB, llm_explanation TEXT, adverse_factors JSONB, decided_at). landlord_accounts (id UUID PK, cognito_sub, company_name, fcra_agreement_signed_at, custom_weight_config JSONB, created_at). fcra_audit_events (id UUID PK, event_type, application_id, landlord_id, actor, detail_json JSONB, created_at) - append-only.
data.retention_and_deletion: credit_reports.expires_at set to submitted_at + 7 years; automated deletion job purges expired records weekly. income_documents.purge_after set to decision date + 90 days; S3 lifecycle policy deletes objects after purge_after date. fcra_audit_events retained 7 years.
data.analytics_events: Datadog metrics: application_submissions_total (counter by landlord_id), pipeline_stage_duration_seconds (histogram by stage), llm_extraction_cost_usd (counter), risk_tier_distribution (gauge by tier), adverse_action_notices_generated_total (counter), credit_bureau_error_rate (gauge).

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate: api service (FastAPI, 3-10 tasks autoscale CPU), pipeline-worker service (SQS consumer, 5-20 tasks autoscale on ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible), frontend service (React SPA served via CloudFront). RDS PostgreSQL 16 multi-AZ r6g.2xlarge. Redis ElastiCache r6g.large. SQS standard queue with 14-day retention and dead-letter queue.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest with mocked Experian API and LLM responses, 85% coverage gate, Fair Housing content filter unit tests, FCRA audit event insertion tests, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with health check gate. Staging environment runs full pipeline with synthetic applicant fixtures.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM with per-application trace spanning all pipeline stages. Custom metrics for throughput, queue depth, LLM costs, and risk tier distribution. FCRA audit log integrity check: daily batch verifies every credit_reports record has a corresponding fcra_audit_events entry.

ISS-001.title: Weight snapshot not stored with screening decisions - batch consistency race condition
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 scoring engine re-reads landlord custom_weight_config at application processing time. If a landlord updates weights mid-batch, applications in the same batch receive different weight configurations, producing inconsistent and legally indefensible score comparisons across the batch.

history.note: Identified by architect_reasoner in round 1 review; scoring engine reads live weight config at runtime

ISS-002.title: Fair Housing content filter for LLM explanations not implemented
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan relied on prompt instruction alone to prevent Fair Housing proxy terms in LLM-generated explanations. No post-generation content filter exists. A violation in a production explanation could constitute a Fair Housing Act violation.

history.note: Identified by security reviewer; prompt-only mitigation insufficient for compliance requirement

ISS-003.title: FCRA adverse action notice missing top-4 factor breakdown required by Section 615
ISS-003.detail: Round 1 adverse action notice design generated a generic decline message. FCRA Section 615 requires the notice to identify the principal reasons (up to 4) for the adverse action in a standardized format. Missing component-level breakdown makes notices non-compliant.

history.note: Identified by compliance reviewer; FCRA Section 615 requires specific factor disclosure format

focus_issues.title: Weight snapshot race condition in batch processing
focus_issues.detail: Landlord weight updates mid-batch produce inconsistent scores. Must snapshot weights at batch submission.

focus_issues.title: Fair Housing content filter absent from LLM explanation pipeline
focus_issues.detail: Prompt instruction alone insufficient; post-generation filter required before storage.

focus_issues.title: FCRA adverse action notice missing Section 615 top-4 factor format
focus_issues.detail: Adverse action notices must include top-4 principal adverse factors with FCRA-standardized codes.

latest_recommendations: Snapshot landlord weight_config at application submission and store in screening_decisions.weight_snapshot
Implement post-generation Fair Housing content filter against CFPB prohibited-term list before LLM explanation storage
Redesign adverse action notice to include FCRA Section 615 top-4 adverse factor codes and descriptions from score_components

convergence_state.reason: Three high-severity compliance issues blocking any production credit data processing

previous_audits.summary: Promising overall architecture for high-scale tenant screening but three high-severity compliance gaps in FCRA and Fair Housing controls block any production launch. LLM pipeline and async design are sound.
strengths: Async SQS pipeline design handles high-scale volume correctly
Income document OCR via GPT-4o Vision is well-designed with S3 presigned URL pattern
Cognito MFA and FCRA permissible purpose verification correctly implemented
concerns: Weight snapshot race condition means batch applications could receive inconsistent scores
Fair Housing content filter relies only on prompt instruction with no programmatic safeguard
FCRA adverse action notice format does not meet Section 615 top-4 factor requirement
blocking_issues: All three issues are legal compliance requirements; launching without fixes exposes platform to FCRA and Fair Housing Act liability
recommendations: Store weight_snapshot JSONB in screening_decisions at submission time, not read-time
Implement FairHousingContentFilter class checking LLM output against CFPB prohibited-term list before storing explanation
Redesign adverse action notice to output FCRA_ADVERSE_FACTOR codes (standardized 2-digit codes per Reg B) alongside plain-language descriptions

issue_updates.title: Weight snapshot race condition
issue_updates.detail: scoring engine reads live custom_weight_config at processing time, not submission-time snapshot

issue_updates.title: Fair Housing content filter absent
issue_updates.detail: No programmatic filter; prompt-only instruction insufficient for legal compliance

issue_updates.title: FCRA Section 615 adverse action format non-compliant
issue_updates.detail: Generic decline message without top-4 factor codes fails FCRA Section 615

previous_plan.title: AI Tenant Screening Platform v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: React 18 and Python 3.12 FastAPI platform on ECS Fargate processing rental applications via Experian credit pull, GPT-4o Vision income OCR, and composite risk scoring with landlord-configurable weights.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: ECS Fargate api, pipeline-worker, and frontend services. SQS async pipeline. PostgreSQL 16 for applications and decisions. Redis for LLM cache. S3 for income documents.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Cognito MFA, FCRA permissible purpose per credit pull, AES-256 KMS. No weight snapshot - scoring engine reads live config. No Fair Housing content filter. Adverse action notice generates generic decline without top-4 factor codes.
previous_plan.data_model: applications, income_documents, credit_reports, screening_decisions (no weight_snapshot column), landlord_accounts, fcra_audit_events tables.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 addresses three high-severity compliance issues from round 1 audit: (1) weight snapshot race condition fixed by capturing landlord weight_config at application submission and storing as weight_snapshot JSONB in screening_decisions - scoring engine reads snapshot only; (2) Fair Housing content filter added as a FairHousingContentFilter post-generation pass against CFPB prohibited-term list before any LLM explanation is stored; (3) FCRA Section 615 adverse action notice redesigned to output standardized 2-digit adverse factor codes (per Regulation B Appendix C) alongside plain-language descriptions. All three fixes are critical compliance requirements; none change the core async pipeline architecture.

fix_report.action_taken: ApplicationIngestionService now snapshots landlord_accounts.custom_weight_config at application submission time and stores in applications.weight_snapshot_json. ScreeningPipelineWorker reads weight_snapshot_json from applications record, not live from landlord_accounts. screening_decisions.weight_snapshot column added to persist the snapshot used for each decision for audit reproducibility.
fix_report.expected_outcome: All applications in a batch use identical weight configurations regardless of mid-batch landlord config changes; screening decisions are auditably reproducible with stored weight snapshot.

fix_report.action_taken: FairHousingContentFilter class added to explanation generation pipeline. After GPT-4o generates explanation text, filter checks for CFPB Fair Housing prohibited terms (school district names, neighborhood quality descriptors, racial or ethnic proxy language, familial status references). If any prohibited term detected: explanation generation retried with stricter prompt; if second attempt still triggers filter, explanation replaced with template-based fallback and alert emitted. Filter term list loaded from S3-backed config file updated by compliance team.
fix_report.expected_outcome: No LLM-generated explanation containing Fair Housing proxy language ever stored or delivered to landlords; filter provides programmatic compliance backstop beyond prompt instruction.

fix_report.action_taken: Adverse action notice redesigned to comply with FCRA Section 615 and Regulation B Appendix C. scoring_decisions.adverse_factors JSONB now stores top-4 adverse factors ordered by score impact, each with fcra_factor_code (2-digit standardized code per Reg B), factor_name, and impact_description. POST /v1/applications/:id/adverse-action generates PDF notice including: applicant name, landlord name, Experian credit bureau contact info, top-4 adverse factor codes and plain-language descriptions, applicant dispute rights statement, and FCRA summary of rights.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Adverse action notices meet FCRA Section 615 and Reg B Appendix C requirements; landlords can deliver compliant notices without manual drafting.

target_output.title: AI Tenant Screening Platform - FCRA-Compliant Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A React 18 and Python 3.12 FastAPI AI platform on AWS ECS Fargate processing thousands of rental applications per day via Experian FCRA credit pulls, GPT-4o Vision income document extraction, and composite risk scoring. Round 2 resolves three high-severity compliance gaps: landlord weight snapshots captured at submission to eliminate batch consistency race conditions, a FairHousingContentFilter applied post-LLM-generation before explanation storage, and FCRA Section 615-compliant adverse action notices with standardized 4-factor codes per Regulation B Appendix C.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three ECS Fargate service groups on AWS us-east-1: React 18 SPA via CloudFront, Python 3.12 FastAPI api service (3-10 tasks), and pipeline-worker service (5-20 tasks autoscaling on SQS ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible). Landlord submits application via React wizard; api service creates applications record with weight_snapshot_json captured from landlord_accounts.custom_weight_config at submission time, uploads income documents to S3 via presigned URL, and enqueues SQS message. Pipeline worker dequeues, pulls credit report from Experian FCRA API (validated against fcra_agreement_signed_at), calls GPT-4o Vision via PrivateLink for income OCR, runs composite risk scoring using weight_snapshot (not live config), generates LLM explanation passed through FairHousingContentFilter, writes screening_decisions with adverse_factors JSONB in FCRA Reg B format. All applicant financial data encrypted at rest with KMS. fcra_audit_events table is append-only for every credit pull and data access.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand, React Hook Form. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, asyncpg, boto3 for SQS/S3/KMS, openai SDK via PrivateLink, reportlab for PDF adverse action notice generation. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, RDS PostgreSQL 16 multi-AZ r6g.2xlarge, ElastiCache Redis r6g.large, SQS standard queue with DLQ, S3 with KMS SSE for income documents, CloudFront, Cognito, AWS PrivateLink for OpenAI, ECR, GitHub Actions. Monitoring: Datadog APM with pipeline-stage span tagging.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Application Submission: landlord fills applicant info in React wizard, uploads income documents to S3 presigned URL, submits; weight_snapshot_json captured from landlord_accounts at submission time. Screening Pipeline: Experian FCRA credit pull (permissible purpose verified), GPT-4o Vision income OCR (structured JSON extraction), eviction lookup, composite risk score using weight_snapshot, LLM plain-language explanation through FairHousingContentFilter, adverse_factors JSONB with top-4 Reg B codes. Landlord Dashboard: application list with status and risk_tier, report detail view with component score breakdown and explanation. Adverse Action: POST /v1/applications/:id/adverse-action generates FCRA Section 615-compliant PDF with top-4 factor codes and applicant dispute rights. FCRA Compliance: fcra_audit_events for every credit pull, 7-year retention, applicant dispute endpoint.
target_output.system_components: CloudFront (React 18 SPA CDN), ECS Fargate api service (FastAPI, Cognito JWT validation, application ingestion, weight snapshot capture, presigned S3 upload URL generation, report and adverse action API), ECS Fargate pipeline-worker service (SQS consumer, Experian FCRA credit pull, GPT-4o Vision income OCR via PrivateLink, composite scoring engine reading weight_snapshot, FairHousingContentFilter, explanation storage), RDS PostgreSQL 16 multi-AZ (applications, income_documents, credit_reports, screening_decisions with weight_snapshot and adverse_factors, landlord_accounts, fcra_audit_events append-only), ElastiCache Redis r6g.large (LLM document extraction cache by S3 ETag hash, pipeline idempotency keys, application status cache), SQS standard queue with 14-day retention (screening pipeline jobs, DLQ for failed applications), S3 with KMS SSE (income documents with lifecycle purge per purge_after date), AWS PrivateLink endpoint for OpenAI (no LLM traffic on public internet), Cognito (MFA, landlord/admin RBAC), Datadog APM sidecar on all Fargate tasks.
target_output.workflows: Application Submission Workflow: landlord POSTs /v1/applications with applicant info and document metadata; api service reads landlord_accounts.custom_weight_config and writes weight_snapshot_json to applications record (snapshot immutable after this point); generates S3 presigned URLs for income document upload; enqueues SQS message with application_id; returns application_id for polling. Screening Pipeline Workflow: worker dequeues SQS message, checks Redis idempotency key for application_id stage; pulls Experian credit report after verifying fcra_agreement_signed_at not null; writes fcra_audit_event (credit_pull, permissible_purpose_verified=true); calls GPT-4o Vision via PrivateLink with income document S3 presigned URL, extracts {extracted_monthly_income, document_type, confidence_score}; caches result in Redis by SHA-256(S3 ETag); reads weight_snapshot_json from applications record (never live config); computes composite_risk_score = sum(component_score * weight_snapshot[component]); orders components by negative score impact to identify top-4 adverse_factors with Reg B factor codes; calls GPT-4o text endpoint for plain-language explanation; passes output through FairHousingContentFilter - on prohibited term detected: retry with stricter prompt, on second failure: use template fallback and emit Datadog fair_housing_filter_triggered metric; writes screening_decisions record; updates application status=complete. Adverse Action Workflow: landlord calls POST /v1/applications/:id/adverse-action; api service loads screening_decisions.adverse_factors JSONB; generates PDF via reportlab including Experian contact info, top-4 Reg B factor codes and descriptions, FCRA summary of rights, applicant dispute rights statement; stores PDF in S3 and returns presigned download URL.
target_output.data_model: applications (id UUID PK, landlord_id UUID FK, applicant_first_name_encrypted BYTEA, applicant_last_name_encrypted BYTEA, applicant_ssn_hash CHAR(64), property_address TEXT, monthly_rent NUMERIC(10,2), weight_snapshot_json JSONB NOT NULL, status ENUM submitted/processing/complete/failed, batch_id UUID, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). income_documents (id UUID PK, application_id UUID FK, s3_key TEXT, document_type ENUM pay_stub/bank_statement/tax_return/offer_letter, upload_status ENUM pending/uploaded/extracted/purged, extracted_monthly_income NUMERIC, extraction_confidence NUMERIC, purge_after DATE). credit_reports (id UUID PK, application_id UUID FK, experian_report_id TEXT, credit_score INT, derogatory_count INT, revolving_utilization NUMERIC, report_json_encrypted BYTEA, pulled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ). screening_decisions (id UUID PK, application_id UUID FK UNIQUE, composite_risk_score NUMERIC(5,2), risk_tier ENUM approved/conditional/declined, score_components JSONB, weight_snapshot JSONB NOT NULL, llm_explanation TEXT, adverse_factors JSONB, decided_at TIMESTAMPTZ). landlord_accounts (id UUID PK, cognito_sub TEXT UNIQUE, company_name TEXT, fcra_agreement_signed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, custom_weight_config JSONB DEFAULT '{credit_score: 0.35, income_ratio: 0.30, rental_history: 0.25, eviction: 0.10}', created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). fcra_audit_events (id UUID PK, event_type TEXT, application_id UUID, landlord_id UUID, actor TEXT, permissible_purpose_verified BOOLEAN, detail_json JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ) - INSERT only via Row Level Security.
target_output.api_design: POST /v1/applications body {applicant_info, document_metadata_array} returns {application_id, s3_presigned_urls, weight_snapshot_version}. GET /v1/applications/:id/status returns {status, pipeline_stage, progress_pct}. GET /v1/applications/:id/report returns {composite_risk_score, risk_tier, score_components, weight_snapshot, llm_explanation, adverse_factors Array<{fcra_factor_code, factor_name, impact_description}>}. POST /v1/applications/:id/adverse-action returns {pdf_download_url, expires_at}. GET /v1/applications?status=complete&limit=50 (landlord dashboard, scoped to landlord_id). GET /v1/analytics/throughput?period=7d (admin role, daily application volume and risk tier distribution). POST /v1/applicants/:ssn_hash/dispute (FCRA applicant dispute flag). All endpoints require Cognito JWT; landlord role scoped to own applications via RLS.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Cognito MFA enforced for all landlord accounts. FCRA permissible purpose: every credit pull validates fcra_agreement_signed_at not null before Experian API call; fcra_audit_events record written with permissible_purpose_verified=true - missing audit event triggers daily integrity alert. Weight snapshot immutability: weight_snapshot_json written to applications at submission, screening_decisions.weight_snapshot copied from applications at decision time; no code path reads live landlord_accounts.custom_weight_config during pipeline execution. Fair Housing content filter: FairHousingContentFilter class loads prohibited-term list from S3 config bucket (compliance-team-managed); checks LLM explanation text; retry on trigger; template fallback on second trigger; every filter event emits Datadog fair_housing_filter_triggered counter for monitoring. FCRA Section 615 adverse action: adverse_factors JSONB stores top-4 factors ordered by composite_risk_score impact with Regulation B Appendix C 2-digit factor codes; PDF generation includes all required Section 615 disclosures. Applicant data encryption: all PII fields (name, SSN hash only - raw SSN never stored) encrypted with KMS key per-landlord. fcra_audit_events append-only enforced by PostgreSQL Row Level Security policy allowing INSERT only. Income documents S3 lifecycle deletes objects at purge_after date.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: pytest with mocked Experian API responses, mocked OpenAI responses using recorded fixtures, FairHousingContentFilter unit tests with prohibited-term corpus, FCRA adverse action notice PDF content tests, weight snapshot immutability tests, 85% coverage gate. Docker multi-stage build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with minimum healthy 100%. ECS autoscaling: api service on CPU 70%, pipeline-worker on SQS ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible (scale out at > 100 messages, scale in at < 10). Pipeline-worker Experian API concurrency limited to 8 simultaneous pulls via asyncio.Semaphore matching Experian account limit. Environments: dev (single tasks, RDS t3.large, synthetic applicant fixtures), staging (3 api + 5 worker tasks, RDS r6g.large, production-volume synthetic fixtures for throughput validation), production (autoscaling, RDS r6g.2xlarge multi-AZ). Secrets in Secrets Manager: Experian API key, OpenAI API key, KMS key ARNs. 90-day rotation with automated connectivity test. FCRA compliance milestone: legal sign-off required before production credit bureau calls enabled.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM tags every pipeline trace with landlord_id, application_id, pipeline_stage, and risk_tier. Custom metrics: application_submissions_total (counter), pipeline_stage_duration_seconds (histogram by stage - credit_pull, income_ocr, scoring, explanation_gen), llm_extraction_cost_usd (counter), llm_cache_hit_rate (gauge, target > 25%), risk_tier_distribution (gauge by tier - approved/conditional/declined), adverse_action_notices_generated_total (counter), fair_housing_filter_triggered (counter - P2 alert on any increment), credit_bureau_error_rate (gauge), sqs_pipeline_queue_depth (gauge), fcra_audit_integrity_failures (counter - P1 alert on any increment). Datadog dashboard: daily throughput, pipeline stage latency percentiles, LLM cost per day, risk tier distribution trend, Fair Housing filter event log. PagerDuty: SQS depth > 500 with no worker progress (P2), credit bureau error rate > 5% (P2), fair_housing_filter_triggered (P2 for immediate compliance review), fcra_audit_integrity_failures (P1).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: At 1000 applications/day: GPT-4o Vision ~$0.04/document (1 income doc avg) = ~$40/day; GPT-4o text explanation ~$0.01/application = ~$10/day; LLM total ~$50/day offset by ~$12/day at 25% cache hit rate.
