Experian credit pulls: negotiated bulk pricing estimate $0.50/pull = ~$500/day at 1000 volume. ECS Fargate: ~$400/month baseline scaling to ~$800/month at peak. RDS r6g.2xlarge multi-AZ: ~$700/month. Redis r6g.large: ~$120/month. S3 income documents: ~$30/month. Datadog APM: ~$200/month. Estimated infrastructure ~$1500/month plus bureau costs. Scaling: pipeline-worker autoscaling 5-20 tasks handles 5x volume surge without configuration change.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): Application submission with S3 document upload, Experian FCRA credit pull with permissible purpose validation, GPT-4o Vision income OCR with FairHousingContentFilter, composite risk scoring with weight_snapshot capture, screening_decisions with adverse_factors Reg B codes, FCRA adverse action PDF notice, React dashboard with report viewer, Cognito MFA, fcra_audit_events append-only, GitHub Actions CI/CD, Datadog APM, FCRA compliance legal milestone gate before production credit bureau calls. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Landlord custom weighting configuration UI, rental history verification integration, eviction record lookup API integration, batch submission for portfolio applications (SQS batch send), analytics dashboard for landlord screening statistics, applicant dispute endpoint. Phase 3 (6 weeks): Multi-bureau integration (TransUnion, Equifax) with bureau selection logic, state-specific compliance rule engine (NY, CA screening law restrictions), automated adverse action notice mailing via SES, Fair Housing bias monitoring monthly report by property geography.
target_output.development_guidelines: Weight snapshot immutability is a hard rule: the scoring engine function signature accepts weight_dict as a parameter and never accesses database or config directly - tested via unit test asserting no DB calls during scoring. FairHousingContentFilter prohibited-term list loaded from S3 on service startup and refreshed every 6 hours; never hardcoded in application code (compliance team must be able to update without deployment). FCRA audit event insertion tested in CI: every code path that calls Experian API must have a corresponding fcra_audit_events INSERT verified by integration test. Raw applicant SSN never stored - SHA-256 hash at ingestion, original discarded immediately. 85% test coverage gate. All LLM calls use recorded fixture responses in pytest - never call real OpenAI in CI. reportlab adverse action PDF must include all required FCRA Section 615 elements verified by PDF content assertion test.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Experian FCRA API deprecates a credit report field used in scoring component calculation, producing incorrect risk scores. Mitigation: Experian API response schema validated at ingestion with Datadog alert on unexpected field changes; scoring engine tested against fixed fixture responses. Risk: FairHousingContentFilter prohibited-term list becomes outdated as CFPB publishes new guidance. Mitigation: S3-backed list refreshed every 6 hours; compliance team owns update process with PagerDuty alert on update failure. Tradeoff: weight_snapshot immutability prevents landlords from retroactively adjusting weights on completed batches - accepted as correct FCRA behavior since decisions must be reproducible as-made. Tradeoff: 8-concurrent Experian API slot limit constrains peak throughput at 8 credit pulls per pipeline-worker task; scaling to 20 workers provides 160 concurrent pulls sufficient for thousands of daily applications.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Weight snapshot captured at application submission (not at pipeline processing time) to ensure immutability even if worker is delayed. Fair Housing content filter retry with stricter prompt before template fallback - preserves explanation quality while maintaining compliance backstop. FCRA adverse action notice generated on-demand via POST endpoint rather than auto-generated at decision time - landlord must explicitly request to ensure they review the screening result before delivering notice to applicant. Experian selected as sole bureau for MVP; multi-bureau addition deferred to Phase 3 to avoid compound compliance complexity at launch.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision resolving three FCRA and Fair Housing compliance gaps in AI tenant screening platform: weight snapshot immutability, Fair Housing content filter, and FCRA Section 615 adverse action format.

profile.domain: CI/CD Modernization for Property Management Tooling

project_goal.value: Replace manual shell-script deployments for internal property management tooling with containerized GitLab CI pipelines, environment-scoped secrets management via GCP Secret Manager, and automated rollback on smoke-test failure
project_goal.rationale: Current deploy process causes 2-3 hour outages per botched release; modernization is a Q2 OKR

target_users.value: Internal engineering and DevOps teams maintaining the property management tooling suite
target_users.rationale: No external consumers; purely internal CI/CD infrastructure

project_class.rationale: Delivers automated build, test, and deploy workflows — not a user-facing product

capabilities.rationale: Orchestrates containerized builds, integrates with GitLab and GCP, runs scheduled jobs for artifact cleanup

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-environment pipeline with rollback logic; complex but not high-traffic

risk_level.rationale: Controls production deployments; a bug here causes outages but does not expose customer data

data_sensitivity.rationale: Stores build metadata and deployment logs; no PII or financial data

external_exposure.rationale: CI/CD system not internet-facing; all access via VPN and GCP IAM

access_model.value: Google IAM service accounts per environment; engineers authenticate via Google Workspace SSO to GitLab
access_model.rationale: Least-privilege IAM prevents cross-environment secret access

feature_scope.value: Containerized Docker builds in GitLab CI, environment-specific secret injection from GCP Secret Manager, smoke-test gate post-deploy, automated rollback restoring previous image, pipeline audit log in PostgreSQL, Slack alerting for failures
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for modernization objective

mvp_scope.value: GitLab CI pipeline with Docker build and GKE Autopilot deploy for staging and production; rollback on smoke-test failure; Slack failure alerts
mvp_scope.rationale: Unblocks safe releases without full audit dashboard

security_baseline.value: GCP Secret Manager for all credentials; no secrets in CI YAML; short-lived OIDC tokens for GKE auth; image signing via Cosign
security_baseline.rationale: Eliminates plaintext secrets in shell scripts; satisfies SOC 2 change-management controls

frontend_stack.value: none — internal CLI and GitLab CI YAML; no browser UI
frontend_stack.rationale: Automation tool with no user-facing frontend

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with custom CLI; GitLab CI runners on GKE Autopilot node pools
backend_stack.rationale: Go for lightweight orchestration binaries; native GKE runner support

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL for pipeline audit logs and deployment state
data_platform.rationale: Structured audit data requires relational queries; Cloud SQL managed failover

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot for CI runners; Cloud SQL for state DB; GCR/Artifact Registry for images
hosting_target.rationale: Autopilot eliminates node management overhead; Artifact Registry enforces image signing

privacy_retention_policy.value: Pipeline logs retained 90 days; build artifacts retained 30 days then deleted; deployment audit records kept 1 year
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SOC 2 audit trail requirement; storage cost controls on artifacts

future_scope.value: Multi-repo pipeline orchestration via Atlantis; automated dependency scanning with Renovate; DORA metrics dashboard
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap post-stabilization

constraints.value: Must integrate with existing GitLab self-hosted instance; GCP only; under $400/month additional infrastructure cost
constraints.rationale: No cloud migration budget; GitLab contract locked for 18 months

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for runner metrics; GitLab pipeline analytics for build duration; Slack webhook alerts for failures and rollbacks
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing Grafana stack leveraged; no additional observability spend

execution_preference.value: Iterative delivery: staging pipeline in sprint 1, production rollback in sprint 2, audit dashboard in sprint 3
execution_preference.rationale: Derisks production rollout by validating on staging first

project.goal: Containerize CI/CD for property management tooling suite with automated rollback
project.target_users: Internal engineering and DevOps teams

backend.framework: Custom CLI binary for deploy orchestration
backend.api: Internal gRPC between orchestrator and runner hooks

security.auth: Google IAM service accounts with OIDC federation; Google Workspace SSO for GitLab
security.secrets: GCP Secret Manager with version pinning; no hardcoded credentials

data.db: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 for audit log and deployment_state tables
data.artifacts: GCR Artifact Registry with 30-day retention lifecycle

devops.ci: GitLab CI with GKE Autopilot runners
devops.environments: dev, staging, production with separate GCP service accounts

open_questions.rollback_depth: How many previous image versions to retain for rollback — confirm with ops

reasoner_reviews.product: The modernization scope is well-bounded. Replacing shell scripts with containerized pipelines directly addresses the 2-3 hour outage pattern. The phased sprint plan appropriately derisks production changes.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go CLI orchestrator is a good fit — lightweight binary with no runtime dependencies, easy to test and cross-compile for runner containers. The OIDC token approach for GKE auth avoids long-lived service account key files which are a common SOC 2 finding.
reasoner_reviews.security: GCP Secret Manager with version-pinned references and OIDC federation eliminates the three most common CI/CD credential leakage patterns seen in the current shell scripts. Cosign image signing closes the supply-chain gap between build and deploy.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: $400/month budget is achievable: GKE Autopilot scales to zero outside business hours, Cloud SQL micro instance covers audit log volume, Artifact Registry storage at ~50GB is under $5/month.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan lacked specificity on rollback trigger conditions and did not address the smoke-test timeout configuration. The audit log schema was underspecified — without defined retention queries, the 1-year retention SLA cannot be enforced.

backend.service_design: Go CLI binary 'deploy-ctl' with subcommands: 'build' (Docker image via kaniko in-cluster), 'push' (Artifact Registry tag + Cosign sign), 'deploy' (kubectl rollout with health wait), 'rollback' (kubectl rollout undo with image pin), 'smoke' (HTTP probe runner against health endpoints)
backend.api_patterns: Internal gRPC service exposing DeploymentService with RPCs: Promote(env, image_sha), Rollback(env, previous_sha), GetStatus(deploy_id) — consumed by GitLab CI jobs via service account token
backend.background_jobs: Nightly artifact pruner deletes images older than 30 days from Artifact Registry; weekly Cloud SQL vacuum on audit_log table; daily rollback_events summary emailed to on-call lead
backend.failure_handling: Smoke-test failure triggers automatic Rollback RPC; if rollback itself fails, PagerDuty alert is raised and deployment_state is set to 'degraded'; circuit breaker prevents repeated failed deploys within 10-minute window

security.auth_design: GitLab CI jobs authenticate to GCP via Workload Identity Federation OIDC; no service account JSON keys; each environment (staging, prod) maps to a separate GCP service account with minimal IAM roles (roles/container.developer, roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor)
security.secrets_management: All pipeline secrets stored in GCP Secret Manager under project-scoped paths; GitLab CI injects via gcloud secrets versions access at runtime; secret rotation triggers pipeline re-validation
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every deploy, rollback, and smoke-test result written to audit_log table with deployer identity, image SHA, environment, outcome, and duration; Cloud Audit Logs capture Secret Manager access for SOC 2 evidence

data.entities: pipelines (id, gitlab_pipeline_id, service_name, triggered_by, created_at), build_artifacts (id, pipeline_id, image_sha, registry_path, signed BOOL, created_at, expires_at), deployment_environments (id, name ENUM dev/staging/production, gke_namespace, current_image_sha, status ENUM healthy/degraded/rolling), rollback_events (id, pipeline_id, environment_id, previous_sha, trigger ENUM smoke_failure/manual, resolved_at), service_registrations (id, service_name, health_endpoint_path, smoke_timeout_seconds INT DEFAULT 120, gke_deployment_name)
data.schema_notes: deployment_environments.status drives the circuit breaker in deploy-ctl; rollback_events links to build_artifacts via previous_sha for full audit trail
data.retention_and_deletion: build_artifacts rows with expires_at < NOW() deleted by nightly pruner; pipeline rows retained 90 days; rollback_events and audit_log retained 1 year per SOC 2 requirement

devops.deployment_topology: GitLab CI runner pods on GKE Autopilot (us-central1); staging and production as separate Kubernetes namespaces in same cluster; Cloud SQL instance in same region; Artifact Registry in us-central1
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI stages: lint (golangci-lint on deploy-ctl), test (go test with coverage gate 75%), build (kaniko image build), sign (cosign with KMS key), deploy-staging, smoke-staging (per-service timeout), promote-prod (manual gate), deploy-prod, smoke-prod, notify
devops.rollback_strategy: Automatic rollback on smoke-test exit code non-zero; deploy-ctl rollback pins previous image SHA from build_artifacts table; Slack message posts diff of rolled-back image tags
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scrapes deploy-ctl /metrics endpoint (deploy_duration_seconds, smoke_test_result, rollback_total); Grafana dashboard with pipeline success rate, mean deploy time, rollback frequency; GitLab built-in DORA metrics for lead time and change failure rate

previous_plan.title: Property Management CI/CD Modernization — Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Containerized GitLab CI pipeline on GKE Autopilot replacing shell-script deployments, with GCP Secret Manager injection, Cosign image signing, and automatic rollback on smoke-test failure. Round 1 established the pipeline topology but did not resolve per-service timeout configuration or full audit identity capture.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: GitLab CI triggers kaniko builds on GKE Autopilot runners; images pushed to Artifact Registry and signed via Cosign; deploy-ctl CLI orchestrates rolling deploys to staging and production namespaces; smoke tests gate promotion; rollback restores previous image SHA.
previous_plan.data_model: pipelines, build_artifacts, deployment_environments, rollback_events — audit_log lacked triggered_by_user column; smoke timeout was hardcoded in deploy-ctl binary.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: Three GitLab CI stages: build/sign, deploy-staging with 60s smoke, deploy-prod with manual gate and 60s smoke. Rollback automated on non-zero smoke exit.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must close ISS-001 (per-service smoke timeout) and ISS-002 (audit log human identity). Adding smoke_timeout_seconds to service_registrations and triggered_by_user to audit_log are surgical schema changes that fix both issues without restructuring the pipeline. Quality target is weak per manifest — sections are functional but non-critical areas like cost and future scope are kept lean.

fix_report.action_taken: Added smoke_timeout_seconds column to service_registrations table (default 120, overridable per service); deploy-ctl smoke subcommand reads this value from DB before starting probe loop; property-data-sync registered with 180s timeout
fix_report.expected_outcome: Smoke-test false rollbacks eliminated for slow-start services; timeout is auditable per service registration

fix_report.action_taken: Added triggered_by_user (GitLab username string) and triggered_by_user_id (integer) to audit_log table; deploy-ctl populates from CI_GITLAB_USER_LOGIN and CI_GITLAB_USER_ID environment variables injected by GitLab CI; manual triggers capture the approver identity
fix_report.expected_outcome: SOC 2 CC6.1 named-user attribution satisfied; security reviewers can trace every production change to a specific engineer

target_output.title: Property Management CI/CD Modernization — Round 2
target_output.executive_summary: Containerized GitLab CI pipeline on GKE Autopilot with Go-based deploy-ctl orchestrator, GCP Secret Manager injection, Cosign image signing, per-service smoke-test timeouts, and human-attributed audit logging. Resolves the two blocking issues from Round 1 — configurable smoke timeouts and SOC 2-compliant audit identity capture — while staying within the $400/month infrastructure budget.
target_output.architecture_overview: GitLab CI runners execute as ephemeral pods on GKE Autopilot in us-central1. The deploy-ctl Go binary drives four pipeline stages: kaniko in-cluster image build, Cosign signing against Artifact Registry, rolling deploy to the target GKE namespace, and HTTP probe smoke-test with per-service timeout read from Cloud SQL service_registrations. On smoke-test failure, deploy-ctl issues a kubectl rollout undo pinned to the previous image SHA. All pipeline events — build, deploy, smoke result, rollback — are written to audit_log with GitLab user identity captured from CI environment variables. GCP Secret Manager provides runtime secret injection with no credentials in GitLab YAML.
target_output.technology_stack: Orchestrator: Go 1.22 deploy-ctl CLI with cobra for subcommand routing. CI: GitLab CI self-hosted with GKE Autopilot runner pool. Container build: kaniko v1.21 (no Docker daemon required). Image registry: GCR Artifact Registry with Cosign KMS signing. Runtime: GKE Autopilot (us-central1, staging and prod namespaces). State DB: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (db-f1-micro). Secrets: GCP Secret Manager with Workload Identity OIDC. Monitoring: Prometheus + Grafana. Alerting: Slack webhooks.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Containerized Build: kaniko builds Docker images inside GKE without privileged access; image tagged with git SHA and branch. Image Signing: Cosign attaches attestation to Artifact Registry; deploy-ctl verify rejects unsigned images. Environment Promotion: staging auto-triggered on main merge; production requires manual GitLab approval gate. Per-Service Smoke Test: deploy-ctl reads smoke_timeout_seconds from service_registrations and polls the service health endpoint. Automated Rollback: non-zero smoke exit triggers Rollback RPC, restoring previous_sha; Slack alert includes image diff. Audit Trail: every pipeline action logged to audit_log with triggered_by_user and image SHA.
target_output.system_components: GitLab CI self-hosted: pipeline trigger and manual approval gate. GKE Autopilot runner pool: ephemeral kaniko and deploy-ctl job pods. Artifact Registry (us-central1): signed Docker images with 30-day lifecycle. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16: pipelines, build_artifacts, deployment_environments, rollback_events, service_registrations, audit_log tables. GCP Secret Manager: per-environment credentials with OIDC-scoped access. deploy-ctl binary: build/push/deploy/smoke/rollback subcommands. Prometheus + Grafana: pipeline metrics dashboard.
target_output.workflows: Merge-to-Main Deploy: engineer merges PR to main; GitLab CI triggers pipeline; kaniko builds image tagged with commit SHA; Cosign signs and pushes to Artifact Registry; deploy-ctl deploy staging executes kubectl rolling update; deploy-ctl smoke staging reads service_registrations.smoke_timeout_seconds (default 120s, up to 180s for slow services) and polls /health; on 200 within timeout, pipeline proceeds to manual production gate. Production Rollback: smoke-prod returns non-zero exit after timeout; deploy-ctl invokes Rollback RPC fetching previous_sha from build_artifacts; kubectl rollout undo is issued with image pin; deploy-ctl logs rollback_event with trigger=smoke_failure and posts Slack alert with before/after image SHAs; if rollback pod also fails health check, PagerDuty alert fires and deployment_environments.status is set to degraded.
target_output.data_model: pipelines (id UUID PK, gitlab_pipeline_id INT, service_name VARCHAR, triggered_by_user VARCHAR, triggered_by_user_id INT, branch, image_sha, status ENUM building/deploying/smoke_testing/healthy/rolled_back/failed, created_at). build_artifacts (id UUID PK, pipeline_id FK, image_sha, registry_path, signed BOOL, cosign_attestation_digest, created_at, expires_at). deployment_environments (id UUID PK, name ENUM dev/staging/production, gke_namespace, current_image_sha, previous_image_sha, status ENUM healthy/degraded/rolling, last_deploy_at). rollback_events (id UUID PK, pipeline_id FK, environment_id FK, previous_sha, trigger ENUM smoke_failure/manual, triggered_by_user, resolved_at). service_registrations (id UUID PK, service_name UNIQUE, health_endpoint_path, smoke_timeout_seconds INT DEFAULT 120, gke_deployment_name, updated_at). audit_log (id UUID PK, pipeline_id FK, action VARCHAR, environment, triggered_by_user, triggered_by_user_id INT, image_sha, outcome, duration_ms, logged_at).
target_output.api_design: Internal gRPC DeploymentService: Promote(env, image_sha) returns DeployResult — triggers rolling update and smoke test. Rollback(env, previous_sha) returns RollbackResult — issues kubectl rollout undo and logs rollback_event. GetStatus(deploy_id UUID) returns DeploymentStatus — current environment state and last audit entry. GetServiceConfig(service_name) returns ServiceRegistration — used by deploy-ctl smoke subcommand to fetch per-service timeout. All RPCs consumed by GitLab CI jobs via Workload Identity OIDC token; no external exposure.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Workload Identity Federation: GitLab CI jobs authenticate via OIDC; no service account JSON keys on disk. Least-privilege IAM: staging SA holds roles/container.developer on staging namespace only; prod SA is separate. GCP Secret Manager: version-pinned secret references; access logged to Cloud Audit Logs for SOC 2 CC6.1. Image signing: Cosign with Cloud KMS key; deploy-ctl rejects unsigned images at deploy stage. Audit identity: triggered_by_user and triggered_by_user_id in every audit_log row satisfies SOC 2 named-user change management evidence. Pipeline secrets never printed to logs; deploy-ctl redacts env vars matching _SECRET or _KEY patterns.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot us-central1: staging namespace (1-3 runner pods, scale-to-zero evenings) and production namespace (2 runner pods baseline). GitLab CI pipeline stages: lint (golangci-lint) then test (go test, 75% coverage gate) then build (kaniko) then sign (cosign) then deploy-staging then smoke-staging (per-service timeout from DB) then manual production gate then deploy-prod then smoke-prod then Slack notify. Cloud SQL db-f1-micro covers audit log volume (~50K rows/month). Secrets rotation: GCP Secret Manager 90-day rotation policy; deploy-ctl validates secret version on startup. Artifact Registry 30-day image lifecycle enforced by nightly Cloud Scheduler + Cloud Run pruner job.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics from deploy-ctl /metrics endpoint: deploy_duration_seconds histogram by service and environment, smoke_test_result counter (success/timeout/error) by service, rollback_total counter by trigger type. Grafana dashboard panels: pipeline success rate (7-day), mean deploy time per service, rollback frequency trend, smoke-test p95 duration versus configured timeout per service. GitLab built-in DORA panel tracks deployment frequency and change failure rate. Slack alert includes service name, environment, image SHA, and smoke-test exit log snippet.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot scale-to-zero outside business hours keeps runner cost under $80/month. Cloud SQL db-f1-micro ~$15/month. Artifact Registry at 50GB ~$5/month. GCP Secret Manager ~$2/month. Total ~$102/month, within $400 budget. Autopilot auto-provisions runner pods on demand; Cloud SQL upgrades to db-g1-small if audit queries slow beyond 10ms.
target_output.phased_implementation: Sprint 1 (2 weeks): deploy-ctl build/push/deploy subcommands; GitLab CI pipeline for staging only; Cloud SQL schema with service_registrations including smoke_timeout_seconds column and audit_log with triggered_by_user columns; GCP Secret Manager OIDC setup. Sprint 2 (2 weeks): smoke subcommand reading per-service timeout from service_registrations; automated rollback RPC; production pipeline with manual gate; Slack alerting for failures and rollbacks. Sprint 3 (2 weeks): Prometheus metrics instrumentation; Grafana dashboard; nightly artifact pruner via Cloud Scheduler; SOC 2 audit log export query validated with security team.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go modules with go.sum committed; golangci-lint enforced in CI. deploy-ctl subcommands unit-tested with mock gRPC and mock DB via testcontainers. No secrets in git; pre-commit hook blocks files matching secret, key, or pem patterns. PR requires one senior approval plus passing coverage gate at 75%.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GKE Autopilot cold-start delays for kaniko pods (30-60s) extend pipeline time; mitigation is 1 warm runner pod during business hours. Risk: Cloud SQL single instance is SPOF for deploy-ctl DB reads during deploy; deploy-ctl falls back to cached service_registrations values if DB unreachable within 2s. Tradeoff: kaniko over Docker-in-Docker avoids privileged containers but requires --cache-repo flag in Artifact Registry. Tradeoff: per-service smoke timeout in DB over config file allows runtime changes without redeployment but adds DB dependency to the deploy hot path.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Smoke timeout configured per-service in service_registrations table resolving ISS-001. Audit log captures triggered_by_user from CI_GITLAB_USER_LOGIN resolving ISS-002. Rollback depth set to 1 previous image per ops confirmation. GKE Autopilot confirmed over standard GKE for zero node-management overhead within $400 budget.

metadata.notes: Round 2 CI/CD modernization for property management tooling; fixes per-service smoke timeout and SOC 2 audit identity; weak quality per manifest with functional but thinner non-critical sections.

profile.domain: Compliance Information Website for Commercial Property Management

project_goal.value: Publish Fair Housing Act policies, ADA accessibility statements, privacy notices, and regulatory contact information for a commercial property management firm's tenants and prospects as a statically hosted, WCAG 2.1 AA compliant website
project_goal.rationale: Replaces a manually maintained PDF archive that tenants cannot reliably find or verify as current

target_users.value: Current and prospective tenants, HOA members, and regulatory contacts of the commercial property management firm
target_users.rationale: Public-facing compliance resource with no authentication requirement

project_class.rationale: No server-side runtime or database required; all content is static regulatory text and policy documents

capabilities.rationale: Static content rendering and lightweight analytics to track policy page views; no backend compute or auth

complexity_level.rationale: Static site with CMS-driven content updates and automated accessibility checks in CI

risk_level.rationale: No user data collected or stored; read-only public information site

data_sensitivity.rationale: Publishes only regulatory policy text and contact information; no PII stored

external_exposure.rationale: Must be publicly accessible to tenants, prospects, and regulatory bodies without authentication

access_model.value: Fully public read access; no authentication; content editing restricted to legal team via CMS login
access_model.rationale: Compliance documents must be accessible without barriers

feature_scope.value: Fair Housing Act policy pages, ADA accessibility statement, privacy notice with GDPR cookie banner for EU visitors, regulatory contact directory, policy version history changelog, automated WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility validation on deploy
feature_scope.rationale: Complete compliance content required for regulatory defensibility

mvp_scope.value: Fair Housing policy, ADA statement, privacy notice, contact directory — deployed and accessibility-gated CI — within 3 weeks
mvp_scope.rationale: These four pages cover the highest-risk compliance exposure

security_baseline.value: HTTPS enforced via CloudFront; security headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options) set at CloudFront response headers policy; no form submissions or server-side code
security_baseline.rationale: No attack surface beyond static file delivery; headers prevent clickjacking and content injection

frontend_stack.value: Astro 4 with TypeScript; content in MDX files managed via Decap CMS; Tailwind CSS for styling
frontend_stack.rationale: Astro compiles to zero-JS static HTML by default; ideal for compliance content SEO and accessibility

backend_stack.value: none — fully static; no server-side runtime
backend_stack.rationale: Static hosting on S3 + CloudFront requires no backend compute

data_platform.value: none — content stored as MDX files in git; no database
data_platform.rationale: Policy documents are versioned in git; no structured data storage needed

hosting_target.value: AWS S3 + CloudFront with custom domain and ACM certificate
hosting_target.rationale: Cost-effective global CDN for static assets; existing AWS account

privacy_retention_policy.value: No personal data collected or retained; CloudFront access logs retained 30 days for security review then purged
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Site is read-only; no user data lifecycle management needed

future_scope.value: Spanish-language policy translations; automated policy diff notifications to subscribed tenants via email; integration with document management system for audit trail
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 items pending legal approval of translated content

constraints.value: All content changes must be approvable by legal team without engineering involvement; no cookies except GDPR consent banner; must score 100 on Lighthouse accessibility audit
constraints.rationale: Legal team owns policy content; accessibility score is a firm legal requirement

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch for CloudFront metrics (requests, error rate, cache hit ratio); Plausible Analytics for privacy-compliant page view tracking with no PII
observability_baseline.rationale: Plausible used instead of GA to avoid GDPR cookie complications on a compliance-focused site

execution_preference.value: Legal-driven content sprints: legal drafts policy in CMS, engineer reviews CI output, deploy to production after accessibility gate passes
execution_preference.rationale: Legal owns the publishing cadence; engineering role is CI gating and infrastructure

project.goal: Statically hosted compliance information site for commercial property management firm
project.target_users: Tenants, prospects, regulatory contacts
project.timeline: MVP in 3 weeks; ongoing legal content updates

frontend.content: MDX files with Decap CMS for legal team editing
frontend.accessibility: WCAG 2.1 AA enforced via axe-core in CI

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: build, axe-core scan, Lighthouse accessibility audit, S3 sync, CloudFront invalidation

constraints.cookies: No tracking cookies; GDPR consent banner only
constraints.accessibility: Lighthouse accessibility score must be 100 to pass CI gate
constraints.content_ownership: Legal team edits via Decap CMS without engineering

open_questions.translation_timing: Spanish translation timeline depends on legal review; deferred to Year 2

reasoner_reviews.product: The compliance site scope is unusually well-constrained. Four core policy pages cover the highest regulatory exposure. The legal team CMS workflow is essential — requiring engineering for every policy update would create unacceptable delays during regulatory changes. Round 2 gaps around policy version history display and cookie banner implementation needed closing.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro is the right choice here: it outputs zero-JavaScript HTML by default, which maximizes accessibility score and minimizes attack surface. Decap CMS on git means legal edits trigger GitHub Actions without any CMS server to maintain. The axe-core gate in CI is the most important pipeline step — it prevents an accessibility regression from reaching production.
reasoner_reviews.security: No server-side code means no injection surface. The main security concern is the CloudFront response headers policy — CSP must be strict enough to prevent third-party script injection but permissive enough to allow Plausible analytics. Round 2 CSP was too permissive with a broad script-src directive.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Round 2 policy version history was displayed as raw git commit messages, which legal team found unreadable. Round 3 must implement a human-readable changelog derived from MDX frontmatter version fields rather than git log.

frontend.app_structure: Astro 4 project with src/pages/ for policy routes (fair-housing, ada-statement, privacy-notice, contacts), src/content/ for MDX policy files, src/components/ for PolicyPage layout and VersionHistory components. Decap CMS config at public/admin/config.yml pointing to git backend.
frontend.pages_and_flows: / (index with links to all policy pages), /fair-housing (Fair Housing Act policy with last-updated date and version badge), /ada-statement (ADA accessibility statement with VPAT link), /privacy-notice (privacy policy with GDPR cookie consent toggle for EU visitors), /contacts (regulatory contact directory with jurisdiction labels), /changelog (human-readable policy update history derived from MDX frontmatter version fields)
frontend.accessibility_notes: Every page targets Lighthouse accessibility score 100. All images have descriptive alt text. Heading hierarchy enforced (single H1 per page). Color contrast ratios validated via Tailwind palette audit. Skip-navigation link on all pages. Decap CMS previews include axe-core overlay so legal editors see accessibility warnings before publishing.
frontend.performance_notes: Astro static output has no client-side JS bundle except Plausible analytics snippet (5KB). CloudFront cache-control headers set to max-age=86400 for policy pages, max-age=3600 for contact directory (changes more frequently). CloudFront compression enabled for HTML and CSS.

security.auth_design: No user authentication on public site. Decap CMS uses GitHub OAuth for editorial login — legal editors authenticate with GitHub accounts in the org.
security.abuse_prevention: CloudFront WAF rule set blocks SQL injection and common bot patterns on the /admin path. Rate limiting at CloudFront (100 req/min per IP) prevents scraping of contact directory.
security.privacy_controls: Plausible analytics is cookieless and GDPR-compliant by design. GDPR cookie banner displayed to EU visitors (geo-detected via CloudFront viewer country header) for any future cookies. CloudFront access logs stripped of full IP addresses before retention.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions workflow: checkout, bun install, bun run build (Astro static build), axe-core scan against built HTML files (exit 1 on any violation), Lighthouse CI audit (fail if accessibility score < 100), sync to S3 staging bucket, CloudFront staging invalidation, [manual gate for production], sync to S3 production bucket, CloudFront production invalidation.
devops.deployment_topology: S3 bucket (staging): pm-compliance-staging.s3-website-us-east-1.amazonaws.com. S3 bucket (production): pm-compliance.s3-website-us-east-1.amazonaws.com behind CloudFront distribution with custom domain compliance.propertyco.com and ACM certificate. CloudFront response headers policy enforces Strict-Transport-Security, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options: DENY, Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' plausible.io.
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch metrics: CloudFrontRequests, CloudFrontErrorRate, CacheHitRate — alerted if error rate exceeds 1%. Plausible dashboard for page views, bounce rate per policy page, country breakdown for GDPR banner targeting.

data.entities: policy_pages (MDX files with frontmatter: title, policy_type ENUM fair_housing/ada/privacy/contact, version string, effective_date, last_reviewed_by, summary_of_changes), regulatory_notices (MDX files for jurisdiction-specific notices), contact_entries (MDX: name, title, jurisdiction, email, phone), accessibility_statements (MDX: vpat_link, last_audit_date, conformance_level)
data.schema_notes: All content stored as MDX in git under src/content/policies/. Astro content collections provide type-safe frontmatter parsing. Version history page aggregates all policy frontmatter version and summary_of_changes fields into a chronological changelog — no database query needed.
data.retention_and_deletion: Git history is the authoritative record of all policy versions; no deletion policy needed. CloudFront access logs auto-deleted after 30 days via S3 lifecycle rule.

target_output.title: Commercial Property Management Compliance Website
target_output.executive_summary: A fully static Astro 4 website deployed to AWS S3 and CloudFront, publishing Fair Housing Act policies, ADA accessibility statements, privacy notices, and regulatory contacts for a commercial property management firm. Legal team edits content via Decap CMS without engineering involvement. GitHub Actions CI gates every deploy on WCAG 2.1 AA compliance via axe-core and a Lighthouse accessibility score of 100. Plausible Analytics provides cookieless page view tracking. Round 3 closes the two outstanding issues: CSP now restricts script-src to an explicit allowlist, and policy version history is sourced from MDX frontmatter rather than raw git log.
target_output.architecture_overview: Zero-server static architecture: Astro 4 compiles MDX content files to HTML at build time with no client-side JavaScript except the Plausible analytics snippet. Legal team authors content in Decap CMS which commits MDX edits directly to the git repo, triggering a GitHub Actions workflow. CI runs an axe-core accessibility scan and Lighthouse CI audit against the built HTML — any WCAG 2.1 AA violation or accessibility score below 100 blocks the deploy. Passing builds sync to S3 (staging then production with a manual gate) followed by CloudFront cache invalidation. CloudFront response headers policy enforces a strict CSP (script-src 'self' plausible.io), HSTS, X-Frame-Options DENY, and X-Content-Type-Options nosniff. No database, no server-side runtime, no cookies except the GDPR consent banner for EU visitors detected via CloudFront viewer-country header.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Astro 4 with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, MDX for policy content, Decap CMS for editorial interface. Build: Bun as package manager and script runner for Astro build. CI: GitHub Actions with @axe-core/cli, Lighthouse CI, AWS CLI for S3 sync. Hosting: AWS S3 static website hosting behind CloudFront distribution with ACM TLS certificate. Analytics: Plausible Analytics (self-hosted instance, cookieless). CDN security: CloudFront managed response headers policy with strict CSP.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Fair Housing Policy Page: /fair-housing renders the firm's Fair Housing Act statement with version badge (from MDX frontmatter), effective date, and downloadable PDF link stored in S3. ADA Accessibility Statement: /ada-statement renders ADA conformance statement with VPAT document link, last audit date, and conformance level badge. Privacy Notice: /privacy-notice with GDPR consent banner displayed to EU visitors (CloudFront CF-IPCountry header detection); no cookies set without consent. Regulatory Contact Directory: /contacts renders jurisdiction-labeled contact entries sourced from MDX contact_entries collection. Policy Changelog: /changelog aggregates all policy MDX frontmatter fields (version, effective_date, summary_of_changes, last_reviewed_by) into a chronological timeline — no git log dependency. Accessibility Gate: axe-core CI step scans every built HTML file; Lighthouse CI enforces score 100 before any S3 sync.
target_output.system_components: GitHub repository: MDX content files, Astro source, Decap CMS config at public/admin/config.yml, GitHub Actions workflow. S3 staging bucket: pm-compliance-staging, versioning enabled. S3 production bucket: pm-compliance-prod, versioning enabled, public read policy. CloudFront distribution: compliance.propertyco.com, ACM wildcard certificate, origin access control for S3, response headers policy with strict CSP and HSTS. Plausible Analytics: self-hosted or Plausible Cloud, receiving page view events from the Plausible script allowed in CSP. CloudWatch: CloudFront metrics for error rate and cache hit ratio.
target_output.workflows: Content Publish Workflow: legal editor logs into Decap CMS via GitHub OAuth, drafts or edits a policy MDX file including updating version, effective_date, and summary_of_changes frontmatter fields, commits via CMS UI; GitHub Actions triggers on push to main — Astro build, axe-core scan against dist/ HTML, Lighthouse CI (fails if accessibility < 100), AWS CLI s3 sync to staging bucket, CloudFront staging invalidation, Slack notification to legal channel with staging preview URL; legal reviewer approves the GitHub Actions manual gate; CI syncs to production bucket and invalidates CloudFront production cache. Emergency Policy Retraction: legal team triggers a manual GitHub Actions workflow dispatch to restore the previous S3 object version using the bucket versioning history, followed by CloudFront invalidation — no engineering code change required.
target_output.data_model: policy_pages (MDX frontmatter: title, policy_type ENUM fair_housing/ada_statement/privacy_notice, version string e.g. 2.3, effective_date ISO date, last_reviewed_by string, summary_of_changes string, pdf_s3_key optional). regulatory_notices (MDX: jurisdiction, notice_type, effective_date, body). contact_entries (MDX: name, title, jurisdiction, email, phone, department). accessibility_statements (MDX: conformance_level ENUM A/AA/AAA, vpat_s3_key, last_audit_date, auditor_name). All content typed via Astro content collection schemas in src/content/config.ts for build-time validation.
target_output.api_design: No server-side API. Decap CMS communicates directly with GitHub API for content commits. Plausible Analytics receives page view events via client-side script POST to plausible.io/api/event (external, listed in CSP). CloudFront serves all HTML/CSS/JS from S3 origin. No REST or GraphQL endpoints.
target_output.security_and_compliance: CSP (Round 3 fix): Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' plausible.io; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src 'self' data:; frame-src 'none'; object-src 'none' — enforced at CloudFront response headers policy level. HSTS: Strict-Transport-Security max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload. X-Frame-Options: DENY prevents clickjacking. X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff. S3 bucket policy blocks all direct public access; CloudFront origin access control is the only authorized reader. Decap CMS admin path (/admin) has CloudFront rate limiting (100 req/min per IP) and WAF SQL injection rule set. GDPR cookie banner displayed to EU visitors via CloudFront viewer-country CF-IPCountry header; no cookies set without banner interaction. CloudFront access logs stripped of query strings before 30-day S3 retention.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Environments: staging (S3 bucket pm-compliance-staging, CloudFront distribution d1staging.cloudfront.net) and production (S3 bucket pm-compliance-prod, CloudFront distribution compliance.propertyco.com). GitHub Actions workflow stages: (1) bun install and bun run build (Astro static build to dist/), (2) npx @axe-core/cli dist/ -- exit-on-error (fails on any WCAG violation), (3) lhci autorun --config lighthouserc.json (fails if accessibility score < 100), (4) aws s3 sync dist/ s3://pm-compliance-staging --delete, (5) aws cloudfront create-invalidation --distribution-id STAGING_ID --paths '/*', (6) Slack notify with staging preview URL, (7) [manual approval gate via GitHub Environments], (8) aws s3 sync dist/ s3://pm-compliance-prod --delete, (9) aws cloudfront create-invalidation --distribution-id PROD_ID --paths '/*'. Rollback: S3 bucket versioning allows restoring prior object versions via workflow dispatch without code revert. S3 lifecycle rule: CloudFront access logs deleted after 30 days.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch dashboard: CloudFrontRequests (5-minute granularity), CloudFrontErrorRate (alert if > 1% for 10 minutes), CloudFrontCacheHitRate (target > 90%), CloudFront4xxErrorRate. Plausible Analytics dashboard: unique page views per policy page (tracks which policies tenants read most), country breakdown (monitors EU visitor share for GDPR banner relevance), bounce rate per page. GitHub Actions run history serves as deployment audit log with timestamps and approver identity for each production deploy.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: S3 storage for static assets: < $1/month. CloudFront: estimated $2-5/month for compliance-focused traffic (low volume, high cache hit ratio). ACM certificate: free. Total estimated infrastructure: $5-10/month. Scaling is automatic via CloudFront CDN; no capacity planning needed for static content.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (3 weeks): Astro project scaffolding with Tailwind, MDX content collection schema, Fair Housing and ADA pages, privacy notice with GDPR banner, contacts directory, Decap CMS config, GitHub Actions CI with axe-core and Lighthouse gates, S3 + CloudFront setup with strict CSP (Round 3 fix applied from day one), production deploy. Phase 2 (2 weeks): Changelog page sourced from MDX frontmatter version fields (Round 3 fix applied), policy PDF upload to S3 and download links, Plausible self-hosted instance setup, CloudWatch alarms. Phase 3 (ongoing): Spanish-language MDX translations pending legal review; VPAT document hosting; S3 versioned rollback workflow dispatch.
target_output.development_guidelines: All content changes via Decap CMS only — direct MDX edits to src/content/ via git are permitted for engineers but must include updated frontmatter version and summary_of_changes. Astro content collection schemas in src/content/config.ts enforce required frontmatter fields at build time; missing fields fail the build. Lighthouse CI config in lighthouserc.json sets assertions.categories.accessibility to error at score below 1.0. No inline styles except Tailwind utilities. PR requires one engineering review for infrastructure changes; content-only PRs from legal team auto-merge after CI passes.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Decap CMS GitHub OAuth requires legal editors to have GitHub org membership — onboarding friction for non-technical staff. Mitigation: provide detailed CMS guide and use GitHub teams to restrict access to content directory only. Risk: CloudFront cache invalidation propagation takes up to 60 seconds, creating a brief window where old policy content is served after a legal update. Mitigation: S3 versioning allows rollback; CloudFront invalidation is logged in GitHub Actions for audit trail. Tradeoff: Astro zero-JS output means no client-side interactivity, but this is a feature for a compliance site — reduced attack surface and maximum accessibility score.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: CSP tightened to 'self' plus plausible.io resolving ISS-001. Changelog sourced from MDX frontmatter resolving ISS-002. Spanish translations deferred to Year 2 pending legal approval. CloudFront viewer-country header confirmed as GDPR EU detection mechanism. Decap CMS chosen over Netlify CMS rename for stability.

metadata.notes: Round 3 compliance static site; closes CSP and changelog issues; strong quality with rich CI/CD, accessibility tooling, and CloudFront security detail.

project_goal.value: Real-time web auction platform for distressed asset sales enabling licensed buyers to bid on bank-owned and foreclosure properties with live countdowns, instant outbid WebSocket notifications, and live bid history streams
project_goal.rationale: Distressed asset sales currently managed via phone and email; digital platform reduces transaction time and expands buyer pool

target_users.value: Licensed real estate buyers and investor firms participating in foreclosure and REO property auctions
target_users.rationale: Platform restricted to credentialed buyers with verified auction license and earnest money deposit on file

project_class.rationale: Browser-based auction interface with real-time bid streaming; not a mobile-first product

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, realtime, payments, analytics
capabilities.rationale: Requires live bidding UI, real-time WebSocket broadcast, payment deposit processing, buyer analytics dashboard

complexity_level.rationale: Distributed bid validation with Redis transactions, WebSocket fan-out, Stripe payment integration, and auction finalization state machine

risk_level.rationale: Handles earnest money deposits and binding purchase commitments; fraud or race conditions have direct financial consequences

data_sensitivity.rationale: Stores bid amounts, payment deposit records, buyer financial credentials, and purchase agreements

external_exposure.rationale: All auction participation requires verified buyer account with Auth0 authentication; no anonymous access to live auctions

access_model.value: RBAC with buyer, seller_admin, auction_manager, and platform_admin roles; buyers restricted to auctions where they have an approved deposit on file
access_model.rationale: Only pre-qualified buyers with deposit can bid; auction managers control auction state transitions

feature_scope.value: Live auction room with countdown timer, real-time bid history stream, outbid push notifications, reserve price validation, auction finalization with winning bid charge, purchase agreement generation, buyer dashboard with auction history and documents
feature_scope.rationale: Core auction participation and post-auction document workflow

mvp_scope.value: Single-property auction with live bidding, outbid WebSocket notifications, earnest money deposit charge on win, and basic buyer dashboard
mvp_scope.rationale: Single-auction MVP validates platform before multi-auction simultaneous support

security_baseline.value: Auth0 RBAC, TLS 1.3, PCI-DSS SAQ-A for Stripe tokenization, Redis WATCH/MULTI for bid atomicity, rate limiting on bid endpoint, audit log for all bids and payment events
security_baseline.rationale: Financial transaction integrity and PCI compliance required for earnest money processing

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript, Socket.IO client for WebSocket bid stream
frontend_stack.rationale: Next.js SSR for auction property pages; Socket.IO for real-time bid updates

backend_stack.value: Node.js 20 with Fastify, Socket.IO server for WebSocket, Stripe SDK for payments
backend_stack.rationale: Fastify high-throughput for bid endpoint; Socket.IO for managed WebSocket connections

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for auction state and bid records; Redis 7 for bid validation transactions and WebSocket pub/sub
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for durable financial records; Redis WATCH/MULTI for atomic bid acceptance

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1 for API and WebSocket servers; RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ; ElastiCache Redis cluster
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers for API; multi-AZ PostgreSQL for auction state durability

privacy_retention_policy.value: Bid records and payment events retained 7 years for financial audit; buyer PII retained per state real estate law (minimum 3 years post-transaction)
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Real estate transaction records subject to state retention requirements

future_scope.value: Simultaneous multi-auction support, mobile bidding app, automated auction scheduling from MLS feed, seller portal for REO disposition management
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 items after single-auction MVP validation

constraints.value: Must support 200 concurrent bidders per auction; sub-500ms bid confirmation latency; deploy to us-east-1 only for data residency
constraints.rationale: Performance SLA ensures auction integrity; US data residency for state real estate law compliance

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM on ECS tasks; custom metrics for bid rate, WebSocket connection count, auction state transitions; PagerDuty for bid endpoint errors > 0.1%
observability_baseline.rationale: Bid processing errors in live auction require immediate response; custom metrics needed beyond generic APM

execution_preference.value: Two-week sprints with weekly auction simulation load tests before MVP launch
execution_preference.rationale: Live auction reliability requires load testing before real buyers participate

compliance_context.value: PCI-DSS SAQ-A for Stripe card tokenization; state real estate auction licensing requirements (varies by state); RESPA disclosure requirements for REO transactions
compliance_context.rationale: Earnest money deposit is a financial transaction requiring PCI compliance; auction platform must comply with state licensing laws

project.goal: Real-time property auction platform with live bidding and payment finalization
project.target_users: Licensed real estate buyers and investor firms

frontend.realtime: Socket.IO client for bid stream and countdown
frontend.state: Zustand for auction room state, React Query for REST data

backend.payments: Stripe SDK with PaymentIntent and SetupIntent for deposit

security.pci: Stripe Elements for card tokenization; no raw card data on server
security.bid_integrity: Redis WATCH/MULTI transaction for atomic bid acceptance

data.cache: ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster for bid transactions and Socket.IO pub/sub adapter

devops.hosting: ECS Fargate (API + WebSocket), RDS Multi-AZ, ElastiCache

open_questions.multi_auction: Simultaneous auction support deferred to Year 2

reasoner_reviews.product: The auction room UI is the core value proposition. Sub-500ms bid confirmation is non-negotiable for buyer trust — any visible lag will erode confidence in bid fairness. The outbid notification must be instantaneous or buyers will perceive the platform as unreliable.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Redis WATCH/MULTI for bid atomicity is the right approach but requires careful implementation: the transaction must validate that the incoming bid exceeds current_high_bid AND that auction status is active before accepting. Race conditions between two simultaneous bids are the highest engineering risk.
reasoner_reviews.security: PCI-DSS SAQ-A scope is achievable via Stripe Elements — no raw card data touches the server. However, the bid endpoint must have strict rate limiting per buyer (max 5 bids/second) to prevent automated sniping bots. The Round 1 plan omitted rate limiting on the bid route.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 200 concurrent WebSocket connections per auction is feasible on a single Fargate task. The Socket.IO Redis adapter is required for horizontal scaling — if a second Fargate task is added, bids processed by task A must be broadcast to Socket.IO clients connected to task B.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan had two serious flaws: bid race condition mitigation relied on PostgreSQL row-level locking rather than Redis atomic transactions, which introduces database contention under high concurrency. Additionally, the auction finalization workflow did not handle the partial failure case where Stripe charge succeeds but PostgreSQL write fails — leaving the auction in an inconsistent state.

backend.service_design: Fastify API with domain modules: auctions (CRUD, state machine), bids (validation and persistence), payments (Stripe integration), notifications (Socket.IO emit). Separate Socket.IO server process sharing Redis adapter for WebSocket fan-out scalability.
backend.api_patterns: REST for auction and buyer data; WebSocket (Socket.IO) for real-time bid stream and countdown updates. Bid placement via POST /api/v1/auctions/:id/bids — validated atomically in Redis before PostgreSQL write.
backend.failure_handling: Stripe charge failure on auction finalization triggers auction manager alert and places auction in 'payment_failed' state; winning bidder has 30 minutes to retry before second-highest bidder is offered the property. Redis outage falls back to PostgreSQL row lock with circuit breaker alert.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js app router with auction room at /auctions/[id]/live — SSR for initial auction state, then Socket.IO client takes over for live updates. Countdown timer rendered client-side with server-synchronized tick.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Login (Auth0 redirect), Buyer Dashboard (/dashboard — auction history, deposit status, won properties), Auction Catalog (/auctions — upcoming and live auctions), Auction Room (/auctions/[id]/live — live countdown, bid history, place bid form, outbid banner), Post-Auction (/auctions/[id]/result — purchase agreement download)
frontend.state_management: Zustand store for auction room state (currentHighBid, timeRemaining, myLastBid, outbid status). Socket.IO event handlers update Zustand store on bid_placed and auction_extended events. React Query for buyer profile and deposit status.

security.auth_design: Auth0 with RBAC; buyer role assigned after license verification and deposit confirmation. JWT validated at Fastify middleware; WebSocket connections authenticated via JWT in Socket.IO handshake auth header.
security.authorization_model: Buyers can only join auction rooms for auctions where their deposit is confirmed (checked at Socket.IO connection time). Bid endpoint checks buyer.deposit_confirmed and auction.status === 'active' before Redis transaction.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every bid attempt (accepted and rejected), auction state transition, and payment event written to bid_audit_log table with timestamp, buyer_id, amount, outcome, and Redis transaction ID for forensic tracing.

data.entities: auctions (id, property_id FK, status ENUM scheduled/active/extended/finalized/cancelled, reserve_price, current_high_bid, current_high_bidder_id, start_time, end_time, extended_until), bids (id, auction_id FK, buyer_id FK, amount NUMERIC, status ENUM accepted/rejected, redis_txn_id, placed_at), buyer_accounts (id, auth0_sub, license_number, license_state, deposit_confirmed BOOL, deposit_payment_intent_id), payment_deposits (id, buyer_id FK, stripe_payment_intent_id, amount, status, auction_id FK), auction_results (id, auction_id FK, winning_bid_id FK, final_amount, purchase_agreement_s3_key, finalized_at), outbid_notifications (id, buyer_id FK, auction_id FK, outbid_by_amount, notified_at)
data.consistency_notes: Bid acceptance uses Redis WATCH on auction:current_high_bid key; if WATCH fails (concurrent modification), bid is rejected with 'outbid' response. After Redis transaction succeeds, bid is written to PostgreSQL and Socket.IO event broadcast.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate: API service (2-4 tasks auto-scaling on CPU 70%) and WebSocket service (2-4 tasks auto-scaling on Socket.IO connection count). Both services behind ALB with sticky sessions for WebSocket (ALB target group stickiness). RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ in us-east-1. ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster (3 nodes) for bid transactions and Socket.IO adapter pub/sub.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint, test (Jest unit + integration, 80% coverage gate), build Docker, push ECR, deploy ECS rolling update. Pre-deploy auction simulation load test in staging (k6 script simulating 200 concurrent bidders).
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM on each Fargate task. Custom Datadog metrics: auction.bid_rate (bids/sec), websocket.connection_count, auction.state_transitions, payment.charge_success_rate. PagerDuty on bid endpoint error rate > 0.1% and WebSocket disconnect rate > 5%.

ISS-001.title: Bid race condition relies on PostgreSQL row lock instead of Redis WATCH transaction
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 bid validation uses SELECT FOR UPDATE on bids table which serializes bid writes through PostgreSQL; under 200 concurrent bidders this creates lock contention and p99 latency exceeds 500ms SLA

history.note: Identified in load test simulation

ISS-002.title: Auction finalization has no saga pattern for Stripe charge + PostgreSQL write partial failure
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 finalization flow: Stripe charge then PostgreSQL write — if Stripe succeeds but PostgreSQL write fails, winning buyer is charged but auction_results record is never created; auction stays in 'active' state indefinitely

history.note: Data consistency gap identified by architect reviewer

focus_issues.title: Bid race condition relies on PostgreSQL row lock instead of Redis WATCH transaction
focus_issues.detail: PostgreSQL SELECT FOR UPDATE causes lock contention at 200 concurrent bidders; must switch to Redis WATCH/MULTI atomic transaction

focus_issues.title: Auction finalization has no saga pattern for partial failure
focus_issues.detail: Stripe charge success + PostgreSQL write failure leaves auction in inconsistent state

latest_recommendations: Replace PostgreSQL SELECT FOR UPDATE bid validation with Redis WATCH/MULTI transaction
Implement outbox or saga pattern for auction finalization to handle Stripe + PostgreSQL partial failure

convergence_state.reason: Critical and high severity issues unresolved; score 5.9 below passing threshold

previous_audits.summary: Solid realtime architecture foundation but critical bid atomicity and finalization consistency gaps make the plan not production-ready
strengths: Socket.IO Redis adapter design correctly enables horizontal ECS scaling
Auth0 RBAC with deposit pre-qualification is the right buyer access model
Stripe Elements PCI-DSS SAQ-A scoping is correct
concerns: PostgreSQL row locking for bid acceptance will fail the 500ms SLA under 200 concurrent bidders
Auction finalization has no compensating transaction for Stripe charge + PostgreSQL write partial failure
blocking_issues: Bid acceptance must use Redis WATCH/MULTI atomic transaction, not PostgreSQL SELECT FOR UPDATE
Finalization workflow must handle partial failure with idempotency key and retry logic
recommendations: Bid service: WATCH auction:{id}:high_bid in Redis, validate amount, MULTI SET new high bid EXEC; only write to PostgreSQL after Redis transaction succeeds
Finalization: store Stripe idempotency key in PostgreSQL before charging; on PostgreSQL failure after charge, use idempotency key to confirm charge status and retry write

issue_updates.title: Bid race condition via PostgreSQL row lock
issue_updates.detail: SELECT FOR UPDATE contention at 200 concurrent bids

issue_updates.title: Auction finalization partial failure gap
issue_updates.detail: No compensating transaction for Stripe + PostgreSQL partial failure

previous_plan.title: Real-Time Property Auction Platform — Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Next.js 14 web app with Fastify WebSocket backend on ECS Fargate for live property auctions. Round 1 used PostgreSQL row locking for bid acceptance and did not handle Stripe + PostgreSQL finalization partial failure.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Fastify API + Socket.IO on ECS Fargate; RDS PostgreSQL for auction state; ElastiCache Redis for WebSocket pub/sub only (not bid validation). Round 1 bid validation used SELECT FOR UPDATE on bids table.
previous_plan.data_model: auctions, bids, buyer_accounts, payment_deposits, auction_results — bid acceptance in Round 1 used PostgreSQL row lock causing p99 latency breach under load.
previous_plan.workflows: Bid placement: buyer POSTs bid, Fastify acquires SELECT FOR UPDATE lock on auction row, validates bid amount, inserts bid record, releases lock — contended under 200 concurrent bidders.

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a flawed Round 2 plan per the manifest. The fix_report addresses ISS-001 and ISS-002 but the implementation has intentional flaws: the data_model section describes Redis transaction logic but omits the idempotency key mechanism for finalization, and the security section fails to mention rate limiting on the bid endpoint despite the security reviewer flagging this in Round 1. The plan is internally inconsistent — the api_design section references a /bids endpoint with no rate limit headers while the security section claims PCI controls are sufficient.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced PostgreSQL SELECT FOR UPDATE bid validation with Redis WATCH/MULTI transaction: WATCH auction:{id}:high_bid key, validate incoming bid amount exceeds watched value, MULTI SET auction:{id}:high_bid to new amount EXEC; on transaction success write bid to PostgreSQL and broadcast Socket.IO bid_placed event
fix_report.expected_outcome: Bid validation no longer contends on PostgreSQL row lock; Redis transaction completes in < 1ms; p99 bid confirmation stays under 500ms at 200 concurrent bidders

fix_report.action_taken: Wrapped auction finalization in a two-step sequence: first write auction_results record with stripe_charge_status='pending' and idempotency_key to PostgreSQL, then call Stripe charge with that idempotency key; on Stripe success update auction_results.stripe_charge_status to 'completed'; on PostgreSQL failure use Stripe idempotency key to verify charge and retry write
fix_report.expected_outcome: Partial failure cases are recoverable; idempotency key prevents double charges; auction reaches finalized state regardless of which step fails first

target_output.title: Real-Time Property Auction Platform — Round 2
target_output.executive_summary: Next.js 14 and Fastify web auction platform on ECS Fargate enabling licensed buyers to bid on distressed and REO properties with sub-500ms bid confirmation via Redis atomic transactions, live WebSocket bid streaming, and Stripe earnest money deposit processing. Addresses Round 1 bid atomicity and finalization consistency gaps. Note: bid endpoint rate limiting omitted from security section — this is a known gap carried forward.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier architecture: Next.js 14 SSR frontend on CloudFront for auction property pages, Fastify API on ECS Fargate for REST and bid processing, separate Socket.IO server process on ECS for WebSocket fan-out. Bid acceptance now uses Redis WATCH/MULTI transaction on the auction:{id}:high_bid key for atomic validation — the PostgreSQL SELECT FOR UPDATE from Round 1 is removed. Socket.IO Redis adapter on ElastiCache enables bid broadcasts across multiple Fargate tasks. Auction finalization uses a two-phase write: PostgreSQL record with idempotency key before Stripe charge, preventing inconsistent state on partial failure. Auth0 JWT validated on all API routes and Socket.IO handshake. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ in us-east-1 stores durable bid records and auction results.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Zustand for auction room state, Socket.IO client 4.x. Backend: Node.js 20, Fastify 4, @fastify/websocket, Socket.IO server 4.x with Redis adapter, Stripe Node SDK. Database: RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (us-east-1a/1c). Cache: ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster (3 nodes) for bid WATCH/MULTI transactions and Socket.IO pub/sub. Auth: Auth0 with RBAC. CDN: CloudFront for Next.js static assets. Payments: Stripe PaymentIntent for deposits. Monitoring: Datadog APM, PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Live Auction Room: real-time bid history updated via Socket.IO bid_placed events; countdown timer synchronized with server time; outbid notification banner on Socket.IO outbid event. Bid Placement: POST /api/v1/auctions/:id/bids validates buyer deposit status, executes Redis WATCH/MULTI transaction, writes accepted bid to PostgreSQL, emits bid_placed to all auction room subscribers. Earnest Money Deposit: Stripe SetupIntent flow for buyers to register payment method; PaymentIntent charged on auction win. Auction Finalization State Machine: scheduled/active/extended/finalized states; finalization writes auction_results with idempotency key before Stripe charge. Buyer Dashboard: won auctions, active deposit status, purchase agreement PDF download.
target_output.system_components: CloudFront CDN: Next.js static assets. ALB with sticky sessions: routes WebSocket connections to same Fargate task. ECS Fargate API service: Fastify + bid processing (2-4 tasks). ECS Fargate WebSocket service: Socket.IO server (2-4 tasks). ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster: bid WATCH/MULTI transactions (db0) and Socket.IO Redis adapter pub/sub (db1). RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ: auctions, bids, buyer_accounts, payment_deposits, auction_results, bid_audit_log. Stripe: PaymentIntent and SetupIntent for earnest money. Auth0: buyer RBAC and JWT issuance. Datadog: APM and custom auction metrics.
target_output.workflows: Bid Placement (Round 2 — Redis atomicity): buyer clicks Place Bid in auction room UI; Next.js frontend calls POST /api/v1/auctions/:id/bids with amount and Auth0 JWT; Fastify validates JWT and checks buyer_accounts.deposit_confirmed; Fastify executes Redis WATCH auction:{id}:high_bid, reads current value, if incoming amount > current value executes MULTI SET auction:{id}:high_bid {amount} SET auction:{id}:high_bidder {buyer_id} EXEC; if EXEC returns nil (concurrent modification), responds 409 Conflict with 'outbid_during_validation'; on success inserts bid record to PostgreSQL and emits Socket.IO bid_placed event to auction:{id} room with new high bid. Auction Finalization (Round 2 — idempotency): auction timer reaches zero; finalization worker writes auction_results row with stripe_charge_status=pending and stripe_idempotency_key=auction_{id}_final; worker calls Stripe PaymentIntent confirm with idempotency key; on Stripe success updates auction_results.stripe_charge_status=completed and auction.status=finalized; on PostgreSQL write failure uses Stripe idempotency key to retrieve charge status and retries PostgreSQL write.
target_output.data_model: auctions (id UUID PK, property_id FK, status ENUM scheduled/active/extended/finalized/cancelled/payment_failed, reserve_price NUMERIC, current_high_bid NUMERIC, current_high_bidder_id FK, start_time, end_time, extended_until). bids (id UUID PK, auction_id FK, buyer_id FK, amount NUMERIC, status ENUM accepted/rejected/outbid_during_validation, redis_txn_id VARCHAR, placed_at). buyer_accounts (id UUID PK, auth0_sub, license_number, license_state, deposit_confirmed BOOL, deposit_payment_intent_id). payment_deposits (id UUID PK, buyer_id FK, stripe_payment_intent_id, amount NUMERIC, status ENUM pending/confirmed/refunded, auction_id FK). auction_results (id UUID PK, auction_id FK, winning_bid_id FK, final_amount NUMERIC, stripe_idempotency_key VARCHAR, stripe_charge_status ENUM pending/completed/failed, purchase_agreement_s3_key, finalized_at). bid_audit_log (id UUID PK, auction_id FK, buyer_id FK, bid_amount, outcome, redis_txn_id, logged_at).
target_output.api_design: POST /api/v1/auctions/:id/bids — place bid (Auth0 JWT required, buyer must have deposit_confirmed); no rate limiting headers configured on this endpoint (gap: rate limiting omitted in Round 2 implementation). GET /api/v1/auctions/:id — auction state with current_high_bid and time_remaining. GET /api/v1/auctions/:id/bids — paginated bid history. POST /api/v1/buyers/deposits — initiate Stripe SetupIntent for deposit. GET /api/v1/buyers/me/auctions — buyer auction history. WebSocket events: bid_placed (new high bid broadcast), outbid (personalized event to displaced high bidder), auction_extended (timer updated), auction_finalized (winner announcement).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth0 RBAC: buyer role requires license verification and deposit confirmation before auction room access. JWT validated at Fastify global hook; Socket.IO handshake validates JWT in auth.token header. PCI-DSS SAQ-A: Stripe Elements handles card tokenization; no raw card data on Fastify server. Bid audit log captures all bid attempts for dispute resolution. RESPA disclosures embedded in purchase agreement template. Note: bid endpoint rate limiting not implemented in Round 2 — automated sniping bot protection is an open gap. Stripe idempotency key mechanism documented in finalization workflow for partial failure recovery.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate us-east-1: API service (Fastify, target 2 tasks, max 4, CPU 70% scale-out) and WebSocket service (Socket.IO, target 2 tasks, max 4, scale on custom metric websocket.connection_count > 150/task). ALB: API target group (path /api/*) and WebSocket target group (path /socket.io/*) with connection draining 30s and sticky sessions for WebSocket. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ us-east-1a/1c, automated backups 7 days. ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster 3 nodes, Multi-AZ. GitHub Actions CI: lint, Jest tests (80% coverage), Docker build/push to ECR, ECS rolling update. Pre-production: k6 load test simulating 200 concurrent Socket.IO bidders and 50 bid/second burst.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM traces on all Fastify routes and Socket.IO event handlers. Custom metrics: auction.bid_rate (bids/second per auction), auction.redis_txn_conflict_rate (WATCH EXEC nil responses / total bids — key health indicator for concurrency), websocket.connection_count per ECS task, auction.finalization_duration_ms. PagerDuty alerts: bid endpoint error rate > 0.1% for 2 minutes, Redis cluster node failure, RDS failover event, Stripe charge failure rate > 1%.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate API 2 tasks ~$60/month; WebSocket service 2 tasks ~$60/month; RDS Multi-AZ db.t3.medium ~$120/month; ElastiCache 3-node cluster ~$80/month; ALB ~$20/month; CloudFront ~$10/month. Total ~$350/month for MVP single-auction load. Multi-auction concurrent support will require Redis cluster sharding by auction_id and WebSocket service horizontal scaling — planned for Year 2.
target_output.phased_implementation: Sprint 1 (2 weeks): Fastify API with bid endpoint using Redis WATCH/MULTI (ISS-001 fix), auction state machine, Auth0 integration, Socket.IO server with Redis adapter. Sprint 2 (2 weeks): Next.js auction room UI with Socket.IO client, countdown timer, bid history stream, outbid banner. Sprint 3 (2 weeks): Stripe deposit flow, auction finalization with idempotency key pattern (ISS-002 fix), purchase agreement generation. Sprint 4 (2 weeks): k6 load test 200 concurrent bidders, Datadog custom metrics, PagerDuty alerts, staging auction simulation.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode for both Next.js and Fastify. Jest unit tests for Redis WATCH/MULTI bid logic with mock Redis client. Integration tests for finalization saga against test Stripe account. Socket.IO load tests with socket.io-client in k6 script. No raw SQL — use pg-typed for type-safe queries. Bid and payment code reviewed by two engineers before merge.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Redis WATCH transaction conflict rate spikes during auction end-of-period bid flurry (sniping). Mitigation: auction auto-extension when bid placed in final 30 seconds reduces peak conflict rate. Risk: Stripe rate limits during simultaneous auction finalization events in Year 2 multi-auction scenario. Mitigation: Stripe idempotency keys allow safe retries; stagger finalization times by 30 seconds. Tradeoff: Socket.IO Redis adapter adds 5-10ms latency to bid broadcasts versus direct Fastify broadcast — acceptable for sub-500ms SLA. Known gap: bid endpoint rate limiting not implemented in Round 2; automated sniping bot risk remains unmitigated until Sprint 5.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Redis WATCH/MULTI chosen over Lua scripting for bid atomicity — simpler to test and debug. Stripe idempotency key stored in auction_results before charge to handle partial failure. Multi-auction support deferred to Year 2 with Redis cluster sharding plan noted. ALB sticky sessions confirmed for WebSocket connection affinity.

metadata.notes: Intentionally flawed Round 2 plan; fix_report addresses ISS-001 and ISS-002 but bid endpoint rate limiting is omitted from security despite being flagged; api_design section notes gap explicitly creating internal inconsistency.

profile.domain: Security-Hardened Tenant Portal for Multifamily Residential

project_goal.value: Security-hardened tenant portal for multifamily residential operator enabling tenants to pay rent, submit maintenance requests, view lease documents, and communicate with building management — with emphasis on protecting sensitive personal and financial payment data
project_goal.rationale: Current tenant interactions via phone and paper; digital portal reduces staff workload and tenant payment friction

target_users.value: Residential tenants across the multifamily portfolio; building management staff
target_users.rationale: Tenants are the primary self-service users; staff use management view for request handling

project_class.rationale: Requires both a tenant-facing React UI and a Django backend with payment and CMMS integrations

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, payments, integrations, analytics
capabilities.rationale: Full tenant lifecycle: authentication, rent payment, maintenance, document access, CMMS integration, and staff analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Standard fullstack with payment and CMMS webhook integrations; not a high-concurrency real-time system

risk_level.rationale: Handles rent payment processing and sensitive lease document access; PCI and potential GDPR exposure

data_sensitivity.rationale: Stores rent payment records, bank account details for ACH, and lease terms with financial obligations

external_exposure.rationale: Portal requires Azure AD B2C tenant account; no anonymous access

access_model.value: RBAC with tenant and staff roles; tenants access only their own lease and payment data; staff can view all units in assigned buildings
access_model.rationale: Tenant data isolation is a legal requirement; staff building-scoped access limits blast radius of compromised credentials

feature_scope.value: Rent payment via ACH or card (Stripe), maintenance request submission with photo upload, lease document viewer, building announcement feed, payment history ledger, staff management dashboard for request routing
feature_scope.rationale: Core tenant self-service features

mvp_scope.value: Rent payment flow, maintenance request submission, lease document viewer for MVP; staff dashboard and announcements in Phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: Payment and maintenance are highest-value features for tenant adoption

security_baseline.value: Azure AD B2C MFA, TLS 1.3, PCI-DSS SAQ-D for Stripe ACH and card tokenization, field-level encryption for payment_methods table, CSRF protection on all forms, Azure Key Vault for secrets
