target_output.workflows: RUBS Allocation Run: node-cron triggers at 02:00 UTC; job queries utility_bills where status=pending and billing_period matches the closing month; fetches allocation_rules config and units.sqft for the property; computes allocated_amount = (unit.sqft / sum(property_units.sqft)) * bill.total_amount for each unit; inserts resident_charges records with status=pending in a single PostgreSQL transaction; on success updates bill.status=allocated and fires SendGrid notification to property manager listing count of charges generated. Manager opens Allocation Run Preview, reviews per-unit breakdown, approves or adjusts individual charges, then posts — setting status=sent and dispatching resident statements via SendGrid. Dispute Resolution: Resident clicks 'Dispute this charge' on their portal; POST /disputes creates a disputes row with status=open and sends manager a push-style Sentry-tagged log and email. Manager opens Dispute Queue, reviews the charge RUBS calculation detail, sets status=under_review while investigating, then resolves by adding resolution_note — SendGrid sends resident an email with the decision; all state transitions insert rows to dispute_audit_log with actor user_id and timestamp.
target_output.data_model: utility_bills (id UUID PK, property_id FK, billing_period DATE, utility_type ENUM electric/gas/water/trash, total_amount NUMERIC(10,2), source ENUM csv/email, status ENUM pending/allocated/posted, imported_at TIMESTAMP). allocation_rules (id UUID PK, property_id FK, method ENUM rubs_sqft/rubs_occupants/submetered, config_json JSONB, effective_date DATE). units (id UUID PK, property_id FK, unit_number VARCHAR, sqft NUMERIC(8,2), occupant_count INT). residents (id UUID PK, unit_id FK, name VARCHAR, email VARCHAR, stripe_customer_id VARCHAR, move_in DATE, move_out DATE). resident_charges (id UUID PK, bill_id FK, unit_id FK, resident_id FK, allocated_amount NUMERIC(10,2), status ENUM pending/sent/paid/disputed, created_at TIMESTAMP). payment_records (id UUID PK, charge_id FK, stripe_payment_intent_id VARCHAR, amount NUMERIC(10,2), status ENUM initiated/succeeded/failed, processed_at TIMESTAMP). disputes (id UUID PK, charge_id FK, resident_id FK, reason TEXT, status ENUM open/under_review/resolved, resolution_note TEXT, resolved_by UUID FK users, created_at TIMESTAMP, resolved_at TIMESTAMP). charge_audit_log (id BIGSERIAL PK, charge_id FK, user_id FK, action VARCHAR, old_amount NUMERIC(10,2), new_amount NUMERIC(10,2), ts TIMESTAMP) -- INSERT-ONLY enforced via BEFORE UPDATE/DELETE trigger raising exception and no UPDATE/DELETE grants on app DB role.
target_output.api_design: REST at /api/v1. POST /billing-runs (trigger RUBS allocation for property_id + billing_period), GET /billing-runs/:id (run status and charge preview). PATCH /charges/:id/approve (manager approves charge amount). GET /residents/me/charges?period= (resident own charge list), GET /residents/me/payments (payment history). POST /payments/intent (create Stripe ACH payment intent for charge_id), POST /webhooks/stripe (Stripe payment confirmation webhook). POST /disputes (resident submits dispute with charge_id and reason), GET /disputes?status=open (manager dispute queue), PATCH /disputes/:id/resolve (manager sets resolution_note and status). POST /webhooks/pms/yardi and POST /webhooks/pms/appfolio (inbound ledger sync confirmations). Auth: JWT Bearer on all endpoints; resident endpoints enforce unit_id claim scoping.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Authentication: Custom JWT — 15-min access token, 7-day refresh token in httpOnly SameSite=Strict cookie. RBAC middleware: property_manager gates billing run triggers and charge approval; accounting_admin gates allocation_rules edits; resident role scoped to own unit_id claim for charges and disputes. Transport: TLS 1.3 enforced at DigitalOcean load balancer. Encryption at rest: DigitalOcean Managed PostgreSQL AES-256. Audit log tamper protection: charge_audit_log has a BEFORE UPDATE/DELETE trigger raising 'charge_audit_log is append-only'; application DB role has no UPDATE or DELETE privileges on this table (NACHA compliance). ACH via Stripe payment intents — no bank account numbers in application database (PCI-DSS SAQ A scope). RUBS compliance: allocation_rules config_json records method and parameters per billing period for state regulator audit trail. SendGrid webhook signature validation on inbound email parse events.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: DigitalOcean App Platform components: web service (2x Basic $12/mo instances, CPU-based autoscale to 4), worker service (1x Basic instance for cron jobs), managed PostgreSQL 16 ($50/mo, 1 vCPU 2 GB, 7-day automated backups), managed Redis 7 ($15/mo, 1 GB). GitHub Actions CI: ESLint + TypeScript strict check, Jest unit tests with 90% branch coverage gate on allocation formulas, integration tests against ephemeral PostgreSQL spun up in CI. Merge to main deploys to staging automatically; production deploy triggered on semantic version git tag. Database migrations via node-pg-migrate run as App Platform release command before app instance start — ensures schema is in sync before traffic. Rollback: App Platform one-click rollback to previous build; migration down scripts tested in CI.
target_output.observability: Sentry captures unhandled exceptions with billing run context (property_id, billing_period, bill_id in breadcrumbs). Prometheus metrics at /metrics: allocation_run_duration_seconds histogram per property, resident_charges_generated_total counter, pms_sync_retry_total counter by provider, stripe_payment_failure_total counter. Grafana dashboards: Billing Operations (run success rate, charges generated per period, open dispute count), Payment Health (ACH success rate, failed payment count by failure reason). Alerts: PagerDuty if nightly allocation_run fails; Slack if pms_sync_retry_total exceeds 10 for a single property in one hour; Sentry alert on dispute submission errors.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: DigitalOcean App Platform web + worker services: ~$36/month. Managed PostgreSQL: ~$50/month. Managed Redis: ~$15/month. SendGrid transactional: ~$15/month. Total infrastructure: ~$120/month, well under $400 budget. Stripe ACH: $0.80 per debit (operator-passed cost). Scaling path: upgrade web service to Professional instances ($75/month each) if allocation load exceeds 10,000 units per nightly cycle; add read replica at ~$40/month for reporting queries if needed.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): CSV bill upload with column mapping wizard, RUBS square-footage allocation engine, resident charge generation and approval, Stripe ACH payment integration, dispute workflow (open/under_review/resolved with SendGrid notifications), charge_audit_log with PostgreSQL tamper-protection trigger, Yardi and AppFolio PMS ledger sync with Redis retry queue, manager and resident dashboards. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Email-based bill ingestion via SendGrid inbound parse, multi-property portfolio billing dashboard with cross-property cost comparisons, RUBS occupant-count formula variant, bulk dispute resolution tooling. Phase 3 (8 weeks): Sub-meter IoT API integration for consumption-based billing, utility spend analytics with cost-per-unit trending charts, resident mobile PWA enhancements with push notifications.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode throughout frontend and backend. Zod schemas validate all API request bodies before controller logic. Allocation engine unit-tested at 90% branch coverage — include edge cases for zero-occupant units, mid-month move-out pro-ration (daily rate = monthly_charge / days_in_month * days_occupied), and partial billing periods. charge_audit_log is INSERT-ONLY invariant: no application code may call UPDATE or DELETE on this table; enforced by PostgreSQL trigger and DB role grants — any attempt to work around this is a security violation requiring team review. All money values stored as NUMERIC(10,2); never use JavaScript floats for billing calculations — use a decimal library. PMS webhook payloads validated via HMAC-SHA256 signature header before processing.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: RUBS state regulation variation — some states cap RUBS admin fees or require specific disclosure language in resident statements. Mitigation: allocation_rules config_json includes state-specific disclosure_text field; legal checklist required before enabling per-state deployment. Risk: Stripe ACH return rate (NSF, unauthorized debits) causing reconciliation complexity. Mitigation: Stripe charge.failed webhook sets payment_records.status=failed and notifies property manager; resident prompted to update payment method. Risk: Yardi/AppFolio API rate limits causing PMS sync delays at month-end billing peaks when hundreds of properties sync simultaneously. Mitigation: Redis retry queue with per-provider rate-limit awareness and staggered retry windows. Tradeoff: DigitalOcean App Platform over AWS reduces cost by ~60% but limits to DigitalOcean managed services; acceptable for current SMB scale under 5,000 units.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Dispute SLA: 7 business days for manager response, matching standard state tenant-landlord communication requirements. Pro-ration method for mid-month move-out: daily rate calculation (monthly_charge / days_in_month * days_occupied) implemented in allocation engine and documented in development_guidelines. Email bill ingestion deferred to Phase 2 — CSV upload is sufficient for MVP rollout.

metadata.notes: Round 4 revision closing two high-severity blocking issues: dispute workflow design and charge audit log tamper protection for NACHA compliance.

project_goal.value: TypeScript/JavaScript SDK abstracting RESO Web API 2.0 OAuth flows, pagination, field mapping, and WebHook subscriptions so PropTech developers can consume MLS listing data without parsing raw RESO standards
project_goal.rationale: Eliminates per-developer MLS integration boilerplate; core value proposition of the SDK

target_users.value: PropTech developers building property search, analytics, and CRM applications on top of MLS data
target_users.rationale: Primary SDK consumers identified in developer survey

project_class.rationale: Distributed as an npm package with no hosted runtime; consumers bring their own execution environment

capabilities.rationale: SDK handles API client logic and data mapping; no frontend or hosted service

complexity_level.rationale: OAuth token lifecycle management and RESO field mapping add non-trivial complexity beyond basic HTTP client

risk_level.rationale: No hosted infrastructure; security surface is OAuth token handling and HMAC validation in consumer applications

data_sensitivity.rationale: SDK itself stores no data; consumers are responsible for their data handling

external_exposure.rationale: Published on npm; documentation and changelog public on GitHub

access_model.value: Consumer-supplied MLS credentials (client_id, client_secret) injected at SDK initialization; no shared credentials
access_model.rationale: Each consumer authenticates with their own MLS OAuth application

feature_scope.value: OAuth 2.0 client credentials and authorization code flows, automatic token refresh, typed listing query builder, RESO field name normalization, cursor-based pagination, WebHook subscription management, HMAC signature validation for inbound events
feature_scope.rationale: Complete RESO API integration surface for most PropTech use cases

mvp_scope.value: OAuth client credentials flow, token refresh, typed listing queries with field projection and filter builder, cursor pagination, basic field mapping for the 50 most common RESO Standard Names
mvp_scope.rationale: Covers the primary MLS search use case for initial release

security_baseline.value: OAuth tokens stored in consumer-managed storage; SDK never persists credentials to disk; HMAC-SHA256 signature validation for webhook payloads; no eval() or dynamic code execution in SDK
security_baseline.rationale: SDK ships in consumer environments; must not introduce credential leakage vectors

frontend_stack.value: N/A — library package only
frontend_stack.rationale: No UI component; SDK targets Node.js and browser environments via dual CJS/ESM build

backend_stack.value: TypeScript with Bun runtime for development and testing; compiled to CJS + ESM for distribution
backend_stack.rationale: Bun for fast iteration; dual build ensures compatibility across Node.js, Bun, and bundler environments

data_platform.value: No persistent storage in SDK; consumers use their own databases
data_platform.rationale: SDK is a stateless HTTP client library

hosting_target.value: npm registry (public package); GitHub for source and releases
hosting_target.rationale: Standard distribution channel for TypeScript SDK

privacy_retention_policy.value: SDK retains no user data; OAuth tokens are held in memory during a session only, never written to disk by SDK code
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No retention obligations for a stateless library

future_scope.value: Authorization code flow support for user-delegated access, batch listing fetch with rate-limit handling, GraphQL transport option, React hooks wrapper package
future_scope.rationale: Roadmap to support richer MLS integration patterns

constraints.value: Must support Node.js 18+, Bun 1.x, and modern bundler environments (Vite, webpack); zero mandatory runtime dependencies (only peer-optional for fetch polyfill on Node 16)
constraints.rationale: Broad compatibility required; heavy dependency chains are a blocker for adoption

observability_baseline.value: Consumer-integrated Sentry via optional error handler callback; SDK emits structured log events via optional logger interface injection
observability_baseline.rationale: SDK must not impose a logging library on consumers

execution_preference.value: Feature branch workflow with semver releases; changelog maintained via conventional commits
execution_preference.rationale: Clear versioning is critical for SDK consumers managing upgrades

project.goal: TypeScript SDK for RESO-compliant MLS API consumption
project.target_users: PropTech developers

backend.runtime: TypeScript compiled via bun build to CJS + ESM
backend.framework: No framework — pure TypeScript class-based SDK
backend.api: RESO Web API 2.0 over OData HTTP

security.auth: OAuth 2.0 client credentials; HMAC-SHA256 webhook validation
security.transport: Consumers responsible for HTTPS; SDK validates HTTPS-only endpoints

data.db: No database; in-memory token cache only
data.schema: RESO Standard Name field mapping table as JSON asset

open_questions.webhook_delivery_retry: Should SDK include a webhook delivery retry helper or leave that to consumers?

reasoner_reviews.product: The SDK addresses a real developer pain point — every PropTech team currently writes their own RESO OAuth and pagination handling. Focusing the MVP on client credentials flow and the 50 most common Standard Names is the right scope cut; authorization code flow can wait for v1.1.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Dual CJS/ESM build via bun build is the correct choice for broad compatibility. Keeping zero mandatory runtime dependencies is essential for an SDK — fetch is available natively in Node 18+ and Bun, so no polyfill needed for the target environments. Token storage must be fully delegated to consumers via a TokenStorage interface to avoid imposing any particular caching strategy.
reasoner_reviews.security: OAuth tokens must never be persisted by SDK internals. The TokenStorage interface approach is correct — consumers inject their own storage (e.g. Redis, in-memory). HMAC-SHA256 validation for webhook payloads must use a constant-time comparison function to prevent timing attacks. No dynamic require() or eval() in the bundle.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Zero-dependency constraint is achievable since the RESO field mapping table is a static JSON asset bundled with the SDK. The dual CJS/ESM build adds CI complexity but is standard practice for modern npm packages.

backend.service_design: SDK organized as a single npm package with exported classes: ResoClient (entry point), ListingQueryBuilder (fluent query API), WebhookHandler (HMAC validation and event deserialization), TokenManager (OAuth lifecycle), FieldMapper (RESO Standard Name translation)
backend.api_patterns: Fluent builder pattern for listing queries: client.listings().where('ListPrice', 'gt', 500000).select(['ListingKey','ListPrice','BedroomsTotal']).paginate(100). Returns async iterators for pagination.
backend.failure_handling: ResoApiError class with status_code, reso_error_code, and request_id fields. Token refresh on 401 response with single retry. Configurable timeout with AbortController.

security.auth_design: TokenManager accepts a TokenStorage interface (get/set/clear methods); consumers inject their own storage. Client credentials flow: POST to token endpoint with client_id/client_secret; stores token with expiry minus 30-second buffer. Token refresh triggered automatically on 401 or pre-emptively 30s before expiry.
security.abuse_prevention: HMAC-SHA256 webhook signature validation uses crypto.timingSafeEqual to prevent timing attacks. SDK validates that MLS endpoint URLs use HTTPS before making any requests.
security.secrets_management: SDK never logs or serializes client_secret or access_token values. Logger interface receives sanitized request metadata only.

data.entities: In-memory: mls_connections (config), oauth_tokens (in TokenStorage), field_mappings (static JSON), webhook_subscriptions (consumer-managed)
data.schema_notes: RESO Standard Name field mapping table: JSON asset mapping 50 common MLS-specific field names to RESO Standard Names (e.g. 'LP' -> 'ListPrice', 'BD' -> 'BedroomsTotal'). Bundled as a static import.
data.migration_notes: No database migrations. Schema version tracked via SDK package version and CHANGELOG.

devops.deployment_topology: GitHub repository with npm package. No hosted infrastructure.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: type-check (tsc --noEmit), bun test suite, bun build dual CJS/ESM, package size audit (< 50KB), npm publish on semver tag via OIDC provenance
devops.rollback_strategy: npm deprecate for security issues; semver patch releases for bug fixes; consumers pin to major version

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a library SDK — architecture centers on package design, public API surface, and distribution, not hosted services. The critical decisions are: zero-dependency constraint drives using native fetch and crypto; dual CJS/ESM build for broad compatibility; TokenStorage interface to avoid imposing consumer storage choices; fluent builder pattern for type-safe listing queries; HMAC constant-time validation for webhook security. No FrontendAgent subplan since this is a backend-only package.
target_output.title: RESO MLS SDK for TypeScript
target_output.executive_summary: A TypeScript/JavaScript npm package providing a type-safe client for RESO Web API 2.0 compliant MLS systems. The SDK abstracts OAuth 2.0 client credentials token lifecycle, automatic token refresh, a fluent listing query builder with RESO Standard Name field mapping, cursor-based pagination via async iterators, and WebHook subscription management with HMAC-SHA256 signature validation. Distributed as a dual CJS/ESM package with zero mandatory runtime dependencies, targeting Node.js 18+, Bun 1.x, and modern bundler environments.
target_output.architecture_overview: Pure TypeScript library with no hosted runtime. The package exposes five primary classes: ResoClient as the consumer entry point, ListingQueryBuilder for constructing typed OData filter and select expressions, TokenManager for OAuth token lifecycle with a consumer-injected TokenStorage interface, FieldMapper for translating MLS-specific field names to RESO Standard Names using a bundled JSON mapping table, and WebhookHandler for validating HMAC-SHA256 signatures and deserializing typed listing change events. Built with Bun for development and testing; distributed as dual CJS and ESM modules compiled by bun build with TypeScript declaration files. No mandatory third-party runtime dependencies — uses native fetch (Node 18+/Bun) and the Node/Bun crypto module for HMAC validation.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: TypeScript 5.x in strict mode. Runtime (dev/test): Bun 1.x. Build: bun build targeting CJS and ESM with declaration files via tsc. Test: bun test with mock fetch interceptors. CI: GitHub Actions with type-check, test, build, and npm publish steps. Distribution: npm registry with OIDC provenance. Zero runtime dependencies. Peer-optional: node-fetch for Node.js 16 consumers (not in support matrix). Dev dependencies: TypeScript, @types/node.
target_output.functional_feature_map: OAuth Token Management: client credentials flow with automatic token refresh 30 seconds before expiry; consumer-injected TokenStorage interface (get/set/clear) for Redis, memory, or file-based caching. Listing Query Builder: fluent API with .where(field, operator, value), .select([fields]), .orderBy(field), .top(n), .paginate(pageSize) returning an async iterator that fetches subsequent pages automatically. RESO Field Mapping: FieldMapper.toStandard('LP') returns 'ListPrice'; mapping table covers 50 most common MLS-specific aliases. WebHook Subscription Management: subscribe/unsubscribe to RESO replication events; WebhookHandler.validate(payload, signature, secret) returns boolean using crypto.timingSafeEqual. Error Handling: ResoApiError with status_code, reso_error_code, request_id, and retry_after properties.
target_output.system_components: ResoClient class: accepts MLSConfig (baseUrl, clientId, clientSecret, tokenStorage, logger, timeout). TokenManager: OAuth token lifecycle with pre-emptive refresh and 401-triggered retry. ListingQueryBuilder: OData expression builder producing URL query strings; validates field names against RESO Standard Name set. FieldMapper: static JSON asset with mls-to-standard and standard-to-mls maps; loaded once at module initialization. WebhookHandler: HMAC-SHA256 validation using crypto.timingSafeEqual; typed event deserializer for ListingAdded, ListingUpdated, ListingRemoved event types. ResoApiError: extends Error with structured MLS error context.
target_output.workflows: Listing Search: Developer initializes ResoClient with MLS credentials and TokenStorage. Calls client.listings().where('ListPrice', 'gt', 500000).where('BedroomsTotal', 'ge', 3).select(['ListingKey','ListPrice','BedroomsTotal','Latitude','Longitude']).paginate(200). SDK checks TokenStorage for a valid token; if absent or expiring, fetches a new token via POST to the MLS token endpoint with client_id and client_secret. Executes OData GET request with Authorization: Bearer header. Returns async iterator; consumer iterates with for await — each iteration fetches the next page cursor automatically until no next-link remains. WebHook Event Processing: Developer registers a WebhookHandler with their MLS secret. On incoming POST from MLS system, passes raw body and X-RESO-Signature header to WebhookHandler.validate(body, signature, secret). If valid, calls WebhookHandler.parse(body) which returns a typed ResoListingEvent with eventType and a fully mapped ListingResource object using RESO Standard Names.
target_output.data_model: In-memory only. mls_connections: MLSConfig object held in ResoClient instance (baseUrl, clientId, timeout). oauth_tokens: delegated to consumer TokenStorage — SDK calls storage.get(mls_key) and storage.set(mls_key, {access_token, expires_at}). field_mappings: static JSON bundled at build time — array of {mls_field: string, standard_name: string, data_type: string} covering 50 entries. webhook_subscriptions: consumer-managed; SDK provides subscribe(resourceName, callbackUrl) helper that calls the RESO replication endpoint but does not maintain subscription state internally.
target_output.api_design: Public SDK API (not HTTP endpoints — this is a library). new ResoClient(config: MLSConfig). client.listings(): ListingQueryBuilder. builder.where(field: string, op: FilterOperator, value: string | number | boolean): ListingQueryBuilder. builder.select(fields: ResoStandardName[]): ListingQueryBuilder. builder.paginate(pageSize: number): AsyncIterable<ListingResource[]>. client.webhooks.validate(body: string, signature: string, secret: string): boolean. client.webhooks.parse(body: string): ResoListingEvent. client.subscriptions.subscribe(resource: ResoResourceName, callbackUrl: string): Promise<SubscriptionResult>. FieldMapper.toStandard(mlsFieldName: string): ResoStandardName | null. All public types exported from package root index.ts.
target_output.security_and_compliance: OAuth client credentials: TokenManager calls POST {baseUrl}/token with client_id and client_secret via URL-encoded body; stores token in consumer TokenStorage with expires_at computed from expires_in minus 30-second buffer. SDK never logs client_secret or access_token values — logger interface receives only sanitized metadata (endpoint, status_code, latency_ms). HMAC-SHA256 webhook validation: WebhookHandler.validate uses crypto.createHmac('sha256', secret).update(body).digest() compared via crypto.timingSafeEqual to prevent timing attacks. SDK enforces HTTPS-only endpoints — throws ResoConfigError if baseUrl does not start with 'https://'. No eval(), no dynamic require(), no code generation in bundle. RESO Web API 2.0 standards compliance maintained via versioned field mapping table updated with each RESO standard release.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: No hosted infrastructure — package distributed via npm. GitHub Actions CI pipeline on every pull request: tsc --noEmit type check, bun test suite with mock fetch interceptors for OAuth and listing query flows, bun build dual CJS/ESM with declaration files, package size audit failing if bundled size exceeds 50 KB (current target: ~30 KB), and dependency audit via npm audit --audit-level=high. Release workflow triggered on semver git tag (e.g. v1.2.0): runs full CI, publishes to npm with OIDC provenance attestation, creates GitHub release with auto-generated changelog from conventional commits. Deprecation policy: security issues trigger npm deprecate on affected versions within 24 hours; consumers notified via GitHub Advisory.
target_output.observability: SDK emits structured log events via an optional Logger interface injected at initialization: logger.debug({event: 'token_refresh', mls: 'mls_key', latency_ms: 120}). Consumers wire this to their own logging framework (Winston, Pino, console). ResoApiError includes request_id from the MLS response header for correlation with MLS server logs. Optional onError callback on ResoClient for consumer-integrated Sentry or similar; receives ResoApiError with full context. No telemetry or analytics collected by the SDK itself.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No infrastructure cost — package is stateless and runs in consumer environments. GitHub Actions free tier covers CI for open-source or small team usage. npm registry free tier for public packages. Scaling of MLS API calls is bounded by the MLS provider rate limits, not the SDK — TokenManager's pre-emptive refresh eliminates token expiry as a source of failed requests under load.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 v1.0 MVP (6 weeks): OAuth client credentials flow with TokenStorage interface, ListingQueryBuilder with where/select/paginate, cursor-based async iterator pagination, RESO Standard Name field mapping for 50 common fields, WebhookHandler HMAC validation and event deserialization, dual CJS/ESM build, 90% test coverage, npm publish with provenance. Phase 2 v1.1 (4 weeks): Authorization code flow support for user-delegated MLS access, extended field mapping to 150 Standard Names, batch listing fetch helper with configurable concurrency and rate-limit backoff. Phase 3 v2.0 (8 weeks): GraphQL transport option for RESO servers supporting it, React hooks wrapper package (useMlsListings, useMlsListing), OpenTelemetry tracing instrumentation via optional OTEL provider injection.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode with noUncheckedIndexedAccess and exactOptionalPropertyTypes enabled. All public API types must be exported from the root index.ts. No default exports — named exports only for tree-shaking compatibility. bun test for all test runs; mock fetch with interceptors, never make real MLS API calls in CI. Package size budget: 50 KB bundled, enforced in CI. Conventional commits for changelog generation (feat:, fix:, chore:). Semver: breaking API changes require major version bump with migration guide in CHANGELOG. RESO field mapping JSON file versioned alongside the RESO standard version it covers.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: MLS provider OData dialect variations — not all RESO-compliant MLSs implement the same OData filter operators. Mitigation: ListingQueryBuilder validates operators against a supported-operators config injected at initialization; consumers can extend for their specific MLS. Risk: OAuth token storage interface adds boilerplate for simple use cases. Mitigation: provide an InMemoryTokenStorage default export for consumers who do not need persistence. Tradeoff: zero-dependency constraint means the SDK cannot use axios or got for HTTP — native fetch lacks retry and interceptor ergonomics. Mitigation: implement minimal retry logic internally with AbortController for timeouts. Risk: npm supply chain attack on the package itself. Mitigation: OIDC provenance attestation on every release; lockfile committed to repo; no postinstall scripts.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Webhook delivery retry helper: SDK will not include retry logic — that is consumer infrastructure responsibility. SDK provides WebhookHandler.validate and parse only. Node.js 16 support dropped — fetch native in Node 18+ makes zero-dependency constraint achievable without polyfill complexity.

metadata.notes: Library SDK for RESO MLS API — no hosted infra, pure package architecture with fluent builder, OAuth lifecycle, and HMAC webhook validation.

profile.domain: Real Estate REIT - Multi-Source Property Data Migration

project_goal.value: Consolidate property, unit, tenant, and financial ledger records from three legacy on-premises property management systems (Yardi Voyager, MRI Software, AppFolio) into a Snowflake cloud data warehouse, enabling unified cross-portfolio reporting and eliminating manual Excel reconciliations across the REIT's 12,000-unit portfolio
project_goal.rationale: Disparate source systems produce conflicting rent roll figures; board-level reporting requires a single source of truth before the next SEC filing cycle

target_users.value: REIT finance team, asset managers, and BI analysts who currently spend 40 hours per week reconciling reports across three systems
target_users.rationale: Direct beneficiaries of consolidated reporting; also data engineers who will maintain the pipeline

project_class.rationale: Pure ETL/ELT workload with no user-facing UI; Airflow DAGs orchestrate extraction, transformation, and loading into Snowflake

capabilities.rationale: Core ETL with batch scheduling, three external system integrations, and CI/CD for DAG deployment

complexity_level.rationale: Three heterogeneous source schemas, incremental delta extraction, financial reconciliation logic, and data quality gating add significant complexity

risk_level.rationale: Errors in rent roll or financial ledger data could result in incorrect SEC filings; any data loss requires manual recovery from source systems

data_sensitivity.rationale: Includes tenant financial ledgers, rent rolls, and REIT-level financial data subject to SOX controls

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline runs within AWS VPC; Snowflake access restricted to corporate IP ranges and MWAA execution role

access_model.value: AWS IAM roles for MWAA and Lambda execution; Snowflake RBAC with data_engineer, analyst_read, and pipeline_service roles; human access via Snowflake SSO federation with corporate IdP
access_model.rationale: Service-to-service authentication via IAM; human access federated through corporate identity provider

feature_scope.value: Delta extraction DAGs for Yardi, MRI, and AppFolio; canonical property data model in Snowflake with raw, staging, integration, and presentation layers; dbt transformation models; financial reconciliation job; Great Expectations data quality gates; migration checkpoint tracking; BI-ready views for rent roll, vacancy rate, and NOI by property
feature_scope.rationale: Full pipeline scope from raw extraction through BI-ready output

mvp_scope.value: AppFolio full historical load and incremental DAG; canonical property and tenant models in Snowflake; dbt models for rent roll and vacancy; reconciliation job for AppFolio data; Datadog pipeline monitoring with Slack alerting
mvp_scope.rationale: AppFolio is the smallest source and lowest-risk starting point for validating the canonical schema before tackling Yardi and MRI

security_baseline.value: All data in transit over TLS 1.3; Snowflake data encrypted at rest with Snowflake-managed keys; SFTP credentials and API keys in AWS Secrets Manager; VPC-only connectivity; column-level masking on tenant PII fields for analyst role
security_baseline.rationale: SOX controls require access restriction and audit trails for financial data

frontend_stack.value: None; data pipeline only
frontend_stack.rationale: No UI component in this project; BI access via Snowflake-connected tools Tableau and Metabase

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Apache Airflow on AWS MWAA; dbt Core 1.8 for transformations; Great Expectations 0.18 for data quality checks
backend_stack.rationale: MWAA provides managed Airflow with IAM integration; dbt is the standard transformation layer with lineage documentation satisfying SOX audit requirements

data_platform.value: Snowflake Enterprise on AWS us-east-1 with separate raw, staging, integration, and presentation databases; S3 landing zone for SFTP extracts with 90-day lifecycle
data_platform.rationale: Snowflake separation of compute and storage allows BI queries without impacting pipeline loads; S3 provides durable intermediate storage

hosting_target.value: AWS MWAA for Airflow orchestration; AWS Lambda for lightweight extraction tasks; S3 for extract landing zone; Snowflake SaaS for the data warehouse
hosting_target.rationale: MWAA eliminates Airflow infrastructure management; Lambda handles on-demand API pulls from vendor endpoints

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw extracts in S3 retained 90 days then deleted; Snowflake staging tables truncated post-load; integration and presentation layers retained 7 years per SOX; tenant PII masked at column level for analyst access
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SOX requires 7-year retention for financial records; tenant data minimized in analytical layers

future_scope.value: Real-time streaming from AppFolio webhook events; predictive NOI forecasting models in Snowflake ML; automated SEC EDGAR report generation from Snowflake presentation views
future_scope.rationale: Post-migration roadmap once canonical model is validated by finance team

constraints.value: Must not impact source system performance (read-only SFTP extracts or vendor-sanctioned APIs only); pipeline must complete full nightly cycle within 4-hour window midnight to 4AM ET; budget under $8,000/month for AWS and Snowflake combined
constraints.rationale: Source system SLAs prohibit heavy API polling; BI team needs fresh data by 6AM ET for morning reporting

observability_baseline.value: Datadog integration for MWAA task duration, DAG failure alerts, and S3 extract file size anomaly detection; Snowflake query history logging; dbt test result reporting via Slack webhook on failure
observability_baseline.rationale: Finance team needs immediate notification of pipeline failures before 6AM reporting window

execution_preference.value: Iterative delivery by source system: AppFolio first, then MRI, then Yardi; each source validated with reconciliation sign-off from finance before next source onboarded
execution_preference.rationale: Reduces risk of schema conflicts and allows finance team to validate data quality incrementally

compliance_context.value: SOX compliance for financial data integrity controls: pipeline changes require peer review and change management approval; SOX audit trail requires immutable logging of all data transformation steps; REIT SEC reporting requirements mandate accuracy of rent roll and NOI figures
compliance_context.rationale: risk_level=high and data_sensitivity=financial trigger SOX and SEC compliance requirements

project.goal: Consolidate Yardi, MRI, and AppFolio records into Snowflake with financial reconciliation
project.target_users: REIT finance team, asset managers, BI analysts
project.timeline: AppFolio MVP in 8 weeks; all three sources in 20 weeks

security.auth: AWS IAM roles; Snowflake RBAC with SSO federation
security.encryption: TLS 1.3 in transit; Snowflake at-rest encryption
security.secrets: AWS Secrets Manager for SFTP credentials and API keys with 90-day rotation

data.warehouse: Snowflake Enterprise (raw, staging, integration, presentation databases)
data.source_systems: Yardi Voyager SFTP, MRI Software OAuth2 REST API, AppFolio REST API

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for dbt model tests and DAG linting

reasoner_reviews.product: The iterative source-by-source delivery approach is well-matched to the risk profile. Starting with AppFolio as the smallest source lets the team validate the canonical schema design before tackling Yardi's more complex 12-CSV export format. Finance sign-off after each source reduces the risk of propagating a schema error across all three sources simultaneously.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The four-layer Snowflake architecture (raw, staging, integration, presentation) is the right pattern: raw preserves source fidelity for replay, staging applies initial type casts and null handling, integration applies canonical mapping and cross-source deduplication, and presentation provides BI-ready views. The S3 landing zone as the durable handoff between SFTP extraction and Snowflake load is correct for Yardi's file-based exports. One concern: the 4-hour nightly window may be tight for Yardi's estimated 2M+ records — incremental extraction by modified_date must be designed from day one, not retrofitted.
reasoner_reviews.security: Column-level masking in Snowflake for tenant SSN and email is the right approach for the analyst_read role, ensuring sensitive fields are never visible in BI queries. SFTP credentials in AWS Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation should be enforced via Lambda rotation function. All dbt model changes touching financial tables must go through the change management process to satisfy SOX change control requirements. The Snowflake private link endpoint is non-negotiable for data residency within the AWS VPC.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The $8,000/month budget is achievable: MWAA mw1.small at approximately $1,400/month, Snowflake compute estimated at $3,000/month for initial load phases dropping to $1,500/month steady-state, S3 and supporting services at approximately $800/month. The 4-hour nightly window is feasible for AppFolio and MRI but Yardi will require incremental-only extraction in steady state to fit within the constraint.

backend.service_design: Three source-specific extraction DAGs (yardi_extract, mri_extract, appfolio_extract) sharing a common BaseExtractOperator for S3 staging; one master_pipeline DAG sequencing extraction, dbt runs, and reconciliation; standalone reconciliation DAG triggered after each source load completes
backend.api_patterns: Yardi: scheduled SFTP pull of nightly CSV exports (Property, Unit, Tenant, Lease, GL files) from vendor SFTP endpoint using paramiko; MRI: OAuth2 client credentials token refresh, paginated GET /properties and GET /leases with since parameter; AppFolio: API key auth GET /listings and GET /tenants with updated_after filter and 200-record page size
backend.background_jobs: nightly_reconciliation DAG comparing Snowflake row counts and sum(rent_amount) per property against source system manifest totals; weekly pii_audit Lambda scanning staging tables for unmasked SSN patterns; monthly retention_cleanup Lambda deleting S3 extracts older than 90 days
backend.failure_handling: MWAA task retry with exponential backoff (3 retries at 5/15/30-minute intervals); on DAG failure after all retries Slack alert to data-eng channel with task ID and error excerpt; reconciliation variance above 0.1% on rent totals blocks dbt presentation layer promotion and pages on-call finance analyst via PagerDuty

security.auth_design: MWAA execution role with least-privilege IAM policy: s3:GetObject/PutObject on landing-zone bucket, secretsmanager:GetSecretValue for source credentials, Snowflake connection via private link endpoint; no public internet egress for pipeline tasks
security.secrets_management: AWS Secrets Manager: one secret per source system containing SFTP host/user/private_key or API key/OAuth2 credentials; secrets referenced in DAGs via boto3 get_secret_value call; 90-day rotation policy with Lambda rotation function per secret
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Snowflake ACCESS_HISTORY view captures every query against financial tables; MWAA task logs shipped to CloudWatch Logs with 90-day retention; all dbt run results stored in Snowflake dbt_audit schema with model_name, run_id, status, rows_affected; all DAG code changes require PR approval from data lead

data.entities: properties (property_id UUID PK, canonical_address, property_type ENUM office/retail/multifamily/industrial, acquisition_date, total_units, source_system_ids VARIANT storing yardi_id/mri_id/appfolio_id), units (unit_id UUID PK, property_id FK, unit_number, sqft, bedrooms, bathrooms, current_status ENUM vacant/occupied/notice), tenants (tenant_id UUID PK, unit_id FK, full_name_masked, email_masked, ssn_token via Snowflake Protect tokenization, move_in_date, move_out_date), leases (lease_id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, unit_id FK, monthly_rent DECIMAL(12,2), lease_start DATE, lease_end DATE, escalation_pct, status ENUM active/expired/notice), rent_rolls (property_id FK, period_month DATE, gross_potential_rent, vacancy_loss, concessions, net_collected, occupancy_rate), financial_ledgers (property_id FK, period_month DATE, gl_code, category ENUM revenue/expense/capex, amount, source_system), migration_checkpoints (source_system, entity_type, last_extracted_at, rows_extracted, s3_path, status ENUM pending/success/failed/replaying)
data.storage_design: Snowflake reit_raw database: one schema per source (yardi_raw, mri_raw, appfolio_raw) preserving source schemas exactly; reit_staging: typed and null-handled versions; reit_integration: canonical unified model with cross-source deduplication; reit_presentation: rent_roll_mv materialized view refreshed nightly, vacancy_rate_by_property and noi_by_property views for BI tools
data.retention_and_deletion: S3 landing zone: 90-day lifecycle rule transitioning to Glacier then deleting; Snowflake staging schemas: TRUNCATE after successful integration load cycle; integration and presentation databases: 7-year retention per SOX; migration_checkpoints table: permanent retention as immutable audit trail

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: on PR runs dbt compile, dbt test --select staging against staging Snowflake environment, Great Expectations dry-run, flake8 lint, and DAG import validation; on merge to main deploys DAG files to MWAA S3 bucket and Lambda function packages to AWS
devops.environments: dev: MWAA mw1.micro with personal Snowflake sandbox databases for DAG development; staging: MWAA mw1.small with Snowflake staging databases running full DAG cycle against production-size data sample; production: MWAA mw1.small with 2 workers and production Snowflake Enterprise databases
devops.rollback_strategy: MWAA DAG rollback via S3 versioned bucket restore of previous DAG file; Snowflake schema rollback via dbt revert command or manual DDL reversal requiring DBA approval through change management process; failed loads trigger migration_checkpoint status='failed' enabling full replay from S3 without re-hitting source systems
devops.devops_risks: MWAA cold start adds 3-5 minutes to first DAG run after environment restart; mitigation: keep MWAA environment always-on rather than scheduled start/stop. Snowflake Marketplace data sharing not applicable to internal pipeline but Snowflake compute credit spikes during initial full historical loads may temporarily exceed budget; mitigation: use virtual warehouse auto-suspend and separate historical-load warehouse

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a SOX-compliant multi-source property data migration pipeline for a REIT consolidating Yardi Voyager, MRI Software, and AppFolio into Snowflake. Key architectural decisions: four-layer Snowflake architecture (raw/staging/integration/presentation) to preserve source fidelity and enable canonical deduplication; S3 landing zone as durable handoff for SFTP-based Yardi source; dbt for transformation lineage required by SOX audit; Great Expectations gates block presentation layer promotion on financial reconciliation failures. The 4-hour nightly window constraint drives incremental extraction design from day one.
target_output.title: REIT Multi-Source Property Data Consolidation Pipeline
target_output.executive_summary: A SOX-compliant Apache Airflow pipeline on AWS MWAA consolidating property, lease, tenant, and financial ledger records from Yardi Voyager, MRI Software, and AppFolio into a four-layer Snowflake data warehouse. Nightly delta extraction DAGs load raw data through S3 into Snowflake raw schemas; dbt transformation models apply canonical mapping and cross-source deduplication; a financial reconciliation job gates presentation-layer promotion on rent roll and NOI accuracy. Delivered iteratively source-by-source with finance sign-off at each milestone across a 20-week program.
target_output.architecture_overview: ELT architecture with S3 as the durable extract landing zone, AWS MWAA orchestrating nightly DAGs, and Snowflake as the transformation and analytical layer. Each source has a dedicated extraction DAG: Yardi extracts via nightly SFTP CSV download from vendor-hosted endpoint, MRI via OAuth2 REST API with modified-date pagination, and AppFolio via REST API with updated_after filter. Raw extracts land in S3, are COPY INTO Snowflake raw schema, then dbt models cascade through staging (type casting, null handling), integration (canonical property model with cross-source property deduplication via address normalization), and presentation (rent_roll, vacancy_rate, NOI materialized views refreshed nightly). A reconciliation DAG runs after each source load, comparing Snowflake financial totals against source-system checksums; variance above 0.1% blocks presentation layer promotion and triggers Slack and PagerDuty alerts. All pipeline code changes require PR review and change management ticket to satisfy SOX change control.
target_output.technology_stack: Orchestration: Apache Airflow 2.9 on AWS MWAA (mw1.small, 2 workers). Extraction: Python 3.12 with boto3, requests, paramiko for SFTP. Transformation: dbt Core 1.8 with Snowflake adapter and 47 models. Data quality: Great Expectations 0.18 with Snowflake backend, 120 expectations. Warehouse: Snowflake Enterprise on AWS us-east-1 with four databases. Storage: S3 landing zone with 90-day lifecycle. Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with dbt test and DAG lint gates. Monitoring: Datadog MWAA integration, CloudWatch Logs, Slack webhooks for reconciliation failures.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Yardi Extraction DAG: SFTP download of nightly Property, Unit, Tenant, Lease, and GeneralLedger CSV exports; row count integrity check against vendor manifest; COPY INTO yardi_raw schema. MRI Extraction DAG: OAuth2 token refresh; paginated REST API pull of properties, leases, and transactions with since filter; COPY INTO mri_raw schema. AppFolio Extraction DAG: API key authentication; GET listings, tenants, and ledger_entries with updated_after filter; COPY INTO appfolio_raw schema. dbt Transformation: 47 models including canonical property deduplication resolving cross-source conflicts via address normalization and source_system_ids VARIANT field. Financial Reconciliation Job: compares sum(monthly_rent) per property per period across source and warehouse; generates discrepancy_report; blocks presentation promotion on threshold breach. Migration Checkpoint Tracker: records last_extracted_at and rows_extracted per entity per source enabling full replay from S3.
target_output.system_components: AWS MWAA (mw1.small, 2 workers): hosts all Airflow DAGs and task execution within VPC. S3 landing-zone bucket: partitioned by source/entity/date with 90-day Glacier lifecycle. AWS Secrets Manager: one secret per source system with 90-day rotation via Lambda. AWS Lambda (pii-audit, retention-cleanup): weekly PII scan and monthly S3 cleanup. Snowflake Enterprise: four databases (reit_raw, reit_staging, reit_integration, reit_presentation); X-Small warehouse for dbt runs auto-suspended after 5 minutes idle, Medium warehouse for BI queries. dbt project: 47 models with documented lineage and schema.yml data tests. Great Expectations: 120-expectation suite including financial totals reconciliation checks. Snowflake private link: all MWAA-to-Snowflake traffic stays within AWS VPC.
target_output.workflows: Nightly ETL Cycle (midnight ET start): master_pipeline DAG triggers source DAGs in parallel (yardi_extract, mri_extract, appfolio_extract), waits for S3 file arrival with 90-minute sensor timeout, runs COPY INTO Snowflake raw schemas, executes dbt run --select staging integration, runs Great Expectations checkpoint (blocks on critical expectation failure), runs reconciliation DAG comparing source vs warehouse totals, on success executes dbt run --select presentation and refreshes materialized views; full cycle must complete by 4AM ET. Financial Reconciliation Workflow: reconciliation DAG queries each source for expected row counts and sum(monthly_rent) per property, compares against Snowflake integration.rent_rolls aggregates, writes results to reit_staging.reconciliation_results; if max_variance_pct exceeds 0.1% sets reconciliation_status='BLOCKED' and sends Slack alert with property_id, source_system, expected_total, actual_total, and variance_pct; BI dashboards display data-refresh-pending banner when reconciliation is blocked.
target_output.data_model: properties (property_id UUID PK, canonical_address TEXT, property_type ENUM office/retail/multifamily/industrial, acquisition_date DATE, total_units INT, source_system_ids VARIANT storing yardi_id/mri_id/appfolio_id cross-references, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). units (unit_id UUID PK, property_id FK, unit_number VARCHAR, sqft INT, bedrooms SMALLINT, bathrooms DECIMAL(3,1), current_status ENUM vacant/occupied/notice/down). tenants (tenant_id UUID PK, unit_id FK, full_name_masked VARCHAR masked for analyst role, email_masked VARCHAR masked, ssn_token VARCHAR Snowflake-Protect tokenized, move_in_date DATE, move_out_date DATE, source_system VARCHAR). leases (lease_id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, unit_id FK, monthly_rent DECIMAL(12,2), lease_start DATE, lease_end DATE, escalation_pct DECIMAL(5,4), option_to_renew BOOLEAN, status ENUM active/expired/notice). rent_rolls (rent_roll_id UUID PK, property_id FK, period_month DATE, gross_potential_rent DECIMAL(14,2), vacancy_loss DECIMAL(14,2), concessions DECIMAL(14,2), net_collected DECIMAL(14,2), occupancy_rate DECIMAL(5,4)). financial_ledgers (ledger_id UUID PK, property_id FK, period_month DATE, gl_code VARCHAR, category ENUM revenue/expense/capex, amount DECIMAL(14,2), source_system VARCHAR). migration_checkpoints (id UUID PK, source_system VARCHAR, entity_type VARCHAR, last_extracted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, rows_extracted INT, s3_path VARCHAR, status ENUM pending/success/failed/replaying).
target_output.api_design: No public API surface. Internal integration interfaces: Yardi SFTP pull via paramiko SSH key auth to sftp.yardi-client.com; files Property_YYYYMMDD.csv, Unit_YYYYMMDD.csv, Tenant_YYYYMMDD.csv, Lease_YYYYMMDD.csv, GL_YYYYMMDD.csv. MRI REST API: POST /oauth/token with client_credentials grant, GET /api/v1/properties?since={iso_date}&page={n}&page_size=500, GET /api/v1/leases?since={iso_date}, GET /api/v1/transactions?since={iso_date}. AppFolio REST API: GET /api/v1/listings?updated_after={iso_date}&limit=200, GET /api/v1/tenants?updated_after={iso_date}, GET /api/v1/accounting/ledger_entries?updated_after={iso_date}. Snowflake private link endpoint for all MWAA and Lambda warehouse connections.
target_output.security_and_compliance: SOX controls: all dbt model changes require PR review from senior data engineer plus data lead approval; schema changes affecting financial models require change management ticket with rollback plan; dbt run results logged to reit_staging.dbt_audit with model_name, run_id, status, and rows_affected for SOX audit trail. Snowflake RBAC: pipeline_service role (COPY INTO raw schemas, execute dbt transformations), analyst_read role (SELECT on presentation database only, column masking returns SHA256 hash for full_name and email, ssn_token is Snowflake-Protect tokenized and never reversible), data_engineer role (full development database access, read-only on production raw schemas). AWS IAM: MWAA execution role scoped to landing-zone S3 bucket and Secrets Manager GetSecretValue only. TLS 1.3 for all SFTP and REST API connections. Snowflake private link ensures all MWAA-to-Snowflake traffic stays within AWS VPC with no public internet routing.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub repo structure: /dags (Airflow DAGs with BaseExtractOperator shared class), /dbt (dbt project with 47 models and schema.yml tests), /lambdas (pii-audit and retention-cleanup), /expectations (Great Expectations suites per entity). GitHub Actions CI: on PR runs dbt compile, dbt test --select staging against staging Snowflake, Great Expectations dry-run, flake8 lint, and DAG import validation (no syntax errors); all gates must pass before merge. On merge to main: sync /dags to s3://mwaa-prod-dags bucket (MWAA picks up new files within 5 minutes), deploy Lambda packages to AWS. Environment promotion: dev MWAA (mw1.micro) for DAG development with personal Snowflake sandboxes, staging MWAA (mw1.small) with staging Snowflake databases and production-size data sample, production MWAA (mw1.small, 2 workers) with Snowflake Enterprise. Rollback: MWAA DAG rollback via S3 versioned bucket restore; Snowflake schema rollback via dbt revert or manual DDL with DBA approval through change management process.
target_output.observability: Datadog MWAA integration: collects task_duration_seconds, dag_success_rate, task_failure_count per DAG; PagerDuty page to on-call data engineer when any DAG task fails after all retries. S3 anomaly: CloudWatch Alarm fires when extract file size deviates more than 30% from 7-day rolling average, indicating source system export issues. Snowflake query history: ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY queried nightly to identify dbt models running over 30 minutes and alert on credit spikes. Great Expectations: validation results stored in Snowflake; failed suites trigger Slack webhook to #data-quality with suite_name, failed_expectation_count, and affected_table. Reconciliation dashboard in Metabase: reconciliation_status per source per night, variance trend over 30 days, and pipeline completion time tracking. Key SLOs: pipeline_completion_time target under 4 hours, reconciliation_variance_pct target under 0.01%, expectation_pass_rate target above 99.5%.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Steady-state estimate approximately $6,200/month: MWAA mw1.small 2-worker environment $1,400/month, Snowflake X-Small dbt warehouse $800/month plus Medium BI warehouse $1,500/month auto-suspended after 5 minutes idle, S3 storage and transfer $600/month, Lambda executions $50/month, Secrets Manager $30/month, Datadog $800/month, AWS data transfer $400/month. Well within $8,000 budget leaving $1,800 headroom for historical load bursts. Scaling path: at 30,000-unit portfolio upgrade MWAA to mw1.medium and Snowflake to Large warehouse; Snowflake credit usage scales with query complexity and data volume rather than raw cluster size.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - AppFolio MVP (Weeks 1-8): implement appfolio_extract DAG with full historical load and incremental delta logic; build raw to staging to integration dbt models for AppFolio properties, units, tenants, and leases; build rent_roll and vacancy_rate presentation views; implement reconciliation DAG for AppFolio financial totals; finance team sign-off comparing Snowflake output against AppFolio UI reports; deploy Datadog monitoring and Slack alerting. Phase 2 - MRI Integration (Weeks 9-14): implement mri_extract DAG with OAuth2 token refresh and paginated REST extraction; extend canonical property model to deduplicate MRI vs AppFolio properties by address normalization; extend reconciliation DAG to cover MRI financial ledger entries; finance sign-off on MRI-to-AppFolio cross-source reconciliation accuracy. Phase 3 - Yardi Integration (Weeks 15-20): implement yardi_extract SFTP DAG with file arrival sensor; build Yardi-specific staging models handling Yardi GL encoding and unit_type taxonomy; extend canonical model for three-source property deduplication with manual resolution queue for ambiguous address matches; final cross-source reconciliation sign-off from CFO; sunset manual Excel reconciliation process.
target_output.development_guidelines: All dbt models must have description, owner, and data tests (not_null on primary keys, accepted_values on ENUM columns, relationships for foreign key columns) defined in schema.yml. DAG code must pass flake8, include module docstrings with data source, schedule, and point-of-contact, and use the BaseExtractOperator shared class for all source extractions. Great Expectations suites must include a financial_totals expectation for any model touching rent_amount or ledger amount columns. PR template checklist: schema change requires DBA review, financial model change requires data lead review, new secret requires Secrets Manager entry and rotation function. Airflow connections stored in MWAA Secrets Manager backend rather than Airflow metadata DB. No hardcoded credentials, connection strings, or S3 bucket names anywhere in codebase.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Yardi nightly SFTP export may arrive late or be malformed due to source system maintenance; mitigation: file-arrival sensor task with 90-minute timeout alerts ops team rather than silently skipping the load, and migration_checkpoint enables replay from last successful S3 extract. Risk: MRI OAuth2 token expiry mid-extraction during large paginated pulls; mitigation: implement token refresh logic with 5-minute expiry buffer before each paginated request. Risk: Cross-source property deduplication via address normalization produces false positives for properties with inconsistent address formatting across source systems; mitigation: build property_match_review staging table for data team to manually resolve ambiguous matches before integration layer promotion. Tradeoff: Snowflake-managed encryption keys chosen over customer-managed Tri-Secret Secure for initial launch simplicity; document upgrade path if compliance requirements escalate to require key custody.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Snowflake selected over Redshift for superior performance on wide-column analytical models and native dbt adapter maturity. MWAA selected over self-managed Airflow on EKS to eliminate infrastructure management overhead for a data engineering team without dedicated platform engineers. AppFolio selected as first source due to smallest data volume, cleanest REST API, and no SFTP complexity. Column-level masking chosen over row-level security for tenant PII to allow aggregate financial analysis while protecting individual tenant records for analyst role.

metadata.notes: SOX-compliant REIT data consolidation pipeline; Yardi/MRI/AppFolio to Snowflake via AWS MWAA with dbt Core and Great Expectations financial reconciliation gates.

profile.domain: Property Document Storage and Retrieval API

project_goal.value: Cost-optimized property document storage and retrieval API consolidating lease agreements, inspection reports, and title documents from multiple property management systems into a unified, deduplicated object store with full-text search.
project_goal.rationale: Eliminates duplicate storage costs and fragmented retrieval across 5 legacy property management systems.

target_users.value: Internal property managers, legal teams, and title officers querying documents by property, tenant, or document type. External systems via API key for PMS integration.
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers identified in requirements discovery.

project_class.rationale: Headless document API with no frontend; consumed by internal tools and external PMS integrations.

capabilities.rationale: Covers API endpoints, document storage, JWT auth, and nightly migration batch jobs.

complexity_level.rationale: Document deduplication, OCR extraction, and Elasticsearch indexing add complexity beyond simple CRUD.

risk_level.rationale: Confidential legal documents require access controls but no PHI or payment card data.

data_sensitivity.rationale: Lease agreements, title documents, and inspection reports are confidential business records.

external_exposure.rationale: API key authentication required for all endpoints; no public access.

access_model.value: API key authentication per integrated system; Cognito JWT for human user access; RBAC with property_manager/legal/title_officer/admin roles.
access_model.rationale: Service-to-service keys for PMS integrations; human JWT for direct queries.

feature_scope.value: Document upload with deduplication by content hash, OCR metadata extraction, Elasticsearch full-text search, S3 object storage with Glacier lifecycle, bulk migration batch job, cost savings dashboard, access grants per property, versioning.
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for consolidated document platform.

mvp_scope.value: Upload, dedup, OCR metadata extraction, basic search by property and document type, S3 storage, API key auth, nightly migration job from 2 legacy systems.
mvp_scope.rationale: Core deduplication and consolidation value for first 2 legacy system migrations.

security_baseline.value: API key auth via Cognito, TLS 1.3 in transit, S3 SSE-KMS encryption at rest, IAM least-privilege for Lambda execution roles, CloudTrail for all S3 and API operations.
security_baseline.rationale: SOC 2 controls baseline for confidential document handling.

frontend_stack.value: None — API service only.
frontend_stack.rationale: No UI component; consumed via API by internal tools and PMS integrations.

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI on AWS Lambda via API Gateway.
backend_stack.rationale: Lambda scales to zero for cost optimization; FastAPI type safety and async support.

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 (RDS) for document metadata, S3 for object storage, OpenSearch Service 2.11 for full-text search.
data_platform.rationale: Relational metadata plus object store plus search index is the correct three-tier storage architecture for document APIs.

hosting_target.value: AWS Lambda + API Gateway (serverless); RDS PostgreSQL in private VPC subnet; OpenSearch Service domain.
hosting_target.rationale: Serverless for cost-per-request billing; RDS for transactional metadata; managed OpenSearch for search.

privacy_retention_policy.value: Lease and title documents retained 7 years per state real estate law. Inspection reports retained 5 years. Glacier lifecycle transition after 1 year of no access. Deletion on legal hold release.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: State real estate records retention requirements.

future_scope.value: Document e-signature workflow integration, tenant self-service document access portal, automated lease abstraction via LLM.
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 and 3 roadmap features.

constraints.value: Must demonstrate 30% storage cost reduction vs legacy multi-system approach within 6 months. Lambda cold start must not exceed 1 second for document metadata queries. OpenSearch cluster must fit within $400/month.
constraints.rationale: Cost justification requirement for executive approval.

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch for Lambda metrics and logs, CloudTrail for S3 and API audit, custom CloudWatch metrics for deduplication rate, OCR success rate, and storage cost per document type.
observability_baseline.rationale: SOC 2 audit trail and cost tracking requirements.

execution_preference.value: Serverless-first; Lambda for API and batch jobs; no persistent compute unless justified by latency SLA.
execution_preference.rationale: Cost optimization mandate requires pay-per-request compute model.

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II: change management controls, access logging, encryption at rest and in transit. State real estate records retention laws (7-year minimum for lease and title documents). Access grant audit trail for all document reads.
compliance_context.rationale: Data sensitivity is confidential and document types have statutory retention requirements.

project.goal: Unified deduplicated document store replacing 5 fragmented legacy PMS file stores
project.target_users: Property managers, legal teams, title officers, and PMS integration API clients
project.timeline: Q3 for MVP with 2 legacy migrations

backend.framework: FastAPI on AWS Lambda via Mangum adapter

security.auth: Cognito API keys for service-to-service; JWT for human users
security.encryption: S3 SSE-KMS at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit
security.audit: CloudTrail for all S3 and API Gateway operations

data.storage: S3 with Glacier lifecycle after 1 year

reasoner_reviews.product: The deduplication-first approach is the core value driver — migrating 5 legacy systems to a single deduplicated store targets estimated 40-60% duplicate document rate based on discovery. The cost dashboard is essential for executive buy-in on the ROI case.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Lambda with FastAPI via Mangum is the right serverless pattern for this query-oriented document API. The three-tier storage model (PostgreSQL metadata, S3 objects, OpenSearch index) is appropriate. Key concern: Lambda cold start for PostgreSQL connection pooling — recommend RDS Proxy to reuse connections across Lambda invocations and avoid connection exhaustion.
reasoner_reviews.security: SOC 2 Type II requires immutable audit trail for all document access. CloudTrail covers S3 object reads/writes and API Gateway calls. The access_grants table provides row-level authorization per property, ensuring cross-property data leakage is prevented at the application layer.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Lambda cold start under 1 second is achievable with FastAPI on Python 3.12 using Lambda SnapStart or provisioned concurrency for hot paths. OpenSearch dev.t3.medium instance fits within $400/month budget for current document volume.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The batch migration job architecture needs more specificity — how does the nightly job handle duplicate detection across legacy systems with differing file naming conventions? Content hash is the right dedup key, but the plan should document the hash algorithm and any normalization applied before hashing.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with domain modules: documents (upload, retrieve, search), storage (S3 operations, hash computation), migration (legacy system connectors), access_control (RBAC enforcement)
backend.api_patterns: REST with versioned endpoints at /v1/; OpenAPI 3.0 schema generated from FastAPI type annotations; idempotent uploads keyed by content hash
backend.background_jobs: Nightly Lambda batch job triggered by EventBridge: scans legacy PMS file shares via API connectors, computes SHA-256 hashes, skips duplicates, uploads new documents, backfills OCR metadata
backend.failure_handling: S3 upload with server-side retry; OCR failures logged and requeued for next batch cycle; dead-letter queue for failed Lambda invocations via SQS DLQ

security.auth_design: API Gateway custom authorizer validating Cognito JWT for human users; API key via Cognito usage plan for PMS service integrations
security.authorization_model: RBAC with roles property_manager (own properties), legal (all properties read-only), title_officer (assigned properties), admin (all). Row-level access enforced via access_grants table join on document queries
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudTrail S3 data events for all GetObject operations; custom access_audit_log table recording user_id, document_id, action, timestamp, source_ip for SOC 2 audit trail
security.secrets_management: Lambda environment variables sourced from AWS Secrets Manager; KMS key for S3 SSE-KMS; rotation every 90 days

data.entities: documents (id, content_hash SHA256, filename, document_type ENUM lease/inspection/title/amendment, property_id, tenant_id, s3_key, file_size_bytes, ocr_extracted_at, created_at), document_versions (id, document_id FK, version_number, s3_key, uploaded_by, created_at), access_grants (id, document_id FK, grantee_id, role, expires_at, granted_by), access_audit_log (id BIGSERIAL, document_id, user_id, action, source_ip, accessed_at append-only)
data.storage_design: S3 primary bucket with SSE-KMS; S3 Intelligent-Tiering for objects 6-12 months; Glacier Instant Retrieval lifecycle transition after 365 days no access. OpenSearch index per document_type for targeted search. RDS PostgreSQL in private subnet with RDS Proxy for connection pooling
data.retention_and_deletion: S3 lifecycle: Intelligent-Tiering at 6 months, Glacier after 1 year no access. Database retention: 7 years for lease/title, 5 years for inspection. Legal hold flag on document record prevents deletion.

devops.deployment_topology: Lambda functions in VPC private subnets with NAT Gateway for OpenSearch and Secrets Manager access; API Gateway regional endpoint; RDS in isolated subnet; OpenSearch in VPC
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pip install, pytest (90% coverage gate), SAM build, SAM deploy to staging, integration test suite, SAM deploy to production on approval. Lambda layers for FastAPI dependencies to reduce cold start package size
devops.cost_controls: OpenSearch dev.t3.medium ($180/month); RDS db.t3.medium Multi-AZ ($240/month); Lambda pay-per-request; S3 Intelligent-Tiering reduces cost as documents age. Cost dashboard Lambda queries CloudWatch metrics for storage bytes per document_type weekly

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a cost-optimized document consolidation API for a real estate operator migrating from 5 fragmented PMS file stores. Key architectural decisions: Lambda + FastAPI via Mangum for pay-per-request cost model, RDS Proxy to prevent Lambda connection exhaustion on PostgreSQL, content SHA-256 hash for deduplication across heterogeneous legacy sources, three-tier storage (PostgreSQL metadata + S3 objects + OpenSearch index). SOC 2 requires immutable audit trail — CloudTrail plus custom access_audit_log table. Must hit 30% storage cost reduction within 6 months.
target_output.title: Property Document Consolidation API
target_output.executive_summary: A serverless REST API built with Python FastAPI on AWS Lambda that consolidates lease agreements, inspection reports, and title documents from five legacy property management systems into a unified, deduplicated S3 object store with PostgreSQL metadata index and OpenSearch full-text search. Content SHA-256 hashing drives deduplication across heterogeneous source systems. A nightly EventBridge-triggered Lambda batch job incrementally migrates legacy documents, targeting 40-60% duplicate elimination. CloudTrail and a custom access audit log satisfy SOC 2 Type II controls.
target_output.architecture_overview: Serverless three-tier architecture: API Gateway (regional endpoint) routes authenticated requests to FastAPI Lambda functions packaged with Mangum adapter. Human users authenticate via Cognito JWT; PMS integrations authenticate via Cognito API key usage plans. Application Lambda functions run in VPC private subnets with RDS Proxy pooling connections to RDS PostgreSQL 16 for document metadata and access grants. S3 stores document objects with SSE-KMS encryption and lifecycle policies (Intelligent-Tiering at 6 months, Glacier Instant Retrieval after 1 year of no access). OpenSearch Service domain indexes full-text content extracted by Textract OCR at upload time. A separate batch Lambda triggered nightly by EventBridge scans legacy PMS file share APIs, computes SHA-256 hashes to detect duplicates, uploads novel documents, and backfills OCR metadata. CloudTrail captures all S3 data events and API Gateway calls for SOC 2 audit trail. Total estimated cost at steady state: $820/month vs $1,400/month for fragmented legacy storage — 41% reduction.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI 0.110, Mangum 0.17 (Lambda adapter), SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, asyncpg, boto3. Compute: AWS Lambda (Python 3.12 runtime, Lambda layers for heavy dependencies). API: API Gateway v2 HTTP API regional endpoint. Database: RDS PostgreSQL 16 via RDS Proxy (db.t3.medium Multi-AZ). Search: OpenSearch Service 2.11 (dev.t3.medium). OCR: AWS Textract for document text extraction at upload. Storage: S3 with SSE-KMS, Intelligent-Tiering, Glacier Instant Retrieval lifecycle. Auth: Cognito user pools (JWT) + Cognito usage plans (API keys). CI: GitHub Actions + AWS SAM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Document Upload: POST /v1/documents accepts multipart file + metadata; computes SHA-256 hash; checks documents table for existing hash; returns 200 with existing doc_id if duplicate, 201 with new doc_id if novel; uploads to S3; enqueues Textract OCR job. Document Retrieval: GET /v1/documents/{id} returns presigned S3 URL (15-minute TTL); enforces access_grants RBAC; logs to access_audit_log. Full-Text Search: GET /v1/documents/search?q=month-to-month+lease&property_id=&doc_type=lease queries OpenSearch index with property_id filter. Access Grants: POST /v1/documents/{id}/grants creates time-scoped access for external parties. Bulk Migration: nightly EventBridge-triggered batch Lambda. Cost Dashboard: GET /v1/admin/costs/savings returns weekly storage bytes and deduplication savings estimate.
target_output.system_components: API Gateway HTTP API (regional, custom domain docs-api.propco.internal), FastAPI Lambda (document module, storage module, access control module, search module — packaged with Lambda layers), Batch Migration Lambda (EventBridge cron 02:00 UTC; Yardi and AppFolio connectors; SHA-256 dedup; Textract queue), Textract OCR Pipeline (S3 trigger on object create; SNS result notification; Lambda writes extracted text to OpenSearch), RDS Proxy (connection pool for Lambda-to-PostgreSQL; prevents connection exhaustion at burst scale), RDS PostgreSQL 16 (documents, document_versions, access_grants, access_audit_log tables; private VPC subnet), OpenSearch Service 2.11 (document_index with property_id, document_type, full_text fields), S3 Document Bucket (SSE-KMS, versioning, intelligent-tiering, Glacier lifecycle), Secrets Manager (DB credentials, legacy PMS API keys — 90-day rotation), CloudTrail (S3 data events, API Gateway access logs).
target_output.workflows: Document Upload and Deduplication: Client POSTs multipart to POST /v1/documents with file and metadata (property_id, document_type, tenant_id). Lambda computes SHA-256 of file bytes. Query: SELECT id FROM documents WHERE content_hash = $1. If found, return 200 with existing document record — no storage written. If novel, upload to S3 at s3://propco-docs/{property_id}/{doc_type}/{hash[:8]}/{filename}, insert documents row, enqueue Textract job via SNS, return 201. Nightly Batch Migration: EventBridge cron triggers batch Lambda at 02:00 UTC. Lambda iterates legacy_pms_sources from config: for each source, fetches file listing from Yardi or AppFolio connector API, downloads file bytes, computes SHA-256, checks PostgreSQL for existing hash, uploads novel files to S3, inserts documents row, queues Textract. Skips duplicates with log entry. On completion, pushes CloudWatch metric: migration_documents_processed, migration_duplicates_skipped, migration_cost_savings_estimated_usd.
target_output.data_model: documents (id UUID PK, content_hash CHAR(64) UNIQUE NOT NULL, filename VARCHAR, document_type ENUM lease/inspection/title/amendment/other, property_id UUID FK, tenant_id UUID FK NULLABLE, s3_key VARCHAR NOT NULL, s3_bucket VARCHAR, file_size_bytes INT, ocr_status ENUM pending/complete/failed, ocr_extracted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, legal_hold BOOLEAN DEFAULT false, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). document_versions (id, document_id FK, version_number INT, s3_key, uploaded_by UUID, created_at). access_grants (id, document_id FK, grantee_id UUID, grantee_type ENUM user/service, role ENUM read/download, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ, granted_by UUID, created_at). access_audit_log (id BIGSERIAL, document_id UUID, user_id UUID, action ENUM view/download/grant/revoke, source_ip INET, accessed_at TIMESTAMPTZ) — append-only, no UPDATE/DELETE enforced by RDS row security policy. OpenSearch index: document_id, property_id, document_type, full_text, indexed_at.
target_output.api_design: REST API at /v1/ with OpenAPI 3.0. POST /v1/documents (multipart: file, property_id, document_type, tenant_id optional; returns 201 DocumentCreated or 200 DocumentDuplicate). GET /v1/documents/{id} (metadata + presigned_url 15-min TTL; logs access_audit_log). GET /v1/documents?property_id=&doc_type=&page=&limit= (paginated metadata list). GET /v1/documents/search?q=&property_id=&doc_type= (OpenSearch ranked results with excerpt). POST /v1/documents/{id}/grants (body: grantee_id, role, expires_at). GET /v1/admin/costs/savings (CloudWatch metrics aggregation; admin only). Auth: Cognito JWT in Authorization header; API key in x-api-key header for PMS service accounts. Rate limit: 1000 req/min per API key, 100 req/min per JWT user.
target_output.security_and_compliance: SOC 2 Type II controls: CloudTrail S3 data events enabled for all GetObject and PutObject operations; custom access_audit_log table records every document view and download with user_id, source_ip, and timestamp — append-only enforced by RDS row security policy preventing DELETE. S3 SSE-KMS with dedicated CMK; key rotation enabled. TLS 1.3 enforced on API Gateway and RDS connections. Cognito JWT expiry 1 hour; refresh tokens 30 days. IAM Lambda execution role: s3:PutObject/GetObject on specific bucket prefix only; no admin permissions. RBAC: property_manager role restricted to documents where property_id in their assigned properties via access_grants join. Legal hold flag prevents S3 lifecycle transition and deletion.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: pip install, pytest with 90% coverage gate, SAM build (packages Lambda layers), SAM deploy --config-env staging, integration test suite against staging (upload/dedup/search/access-grant flows), manual approval step, SAM deploy --config-env production.
