Lambda function concurrency: reserved 10 for document upload, 5 for search, 3 for batch migration. Batch Lambda timeout: 15 minutes. API Lambda timeout: 30 seconds. Environments: dev (single-AZ RDS, mock search), staging (full stack minus Multi-AZ RDS), production (Multi-AZ RDS, OpenSearch, reserved concurrency). Rollback: SAM rollback to prior version via CloudFormation ChangeSet. Secrets rotation: Secrets Manager with Lambda rotation function, 90-day schedule.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch Lambda metrics: invocation count, error rate, p99 duration per function. CloudWatch custom metrics (pushed by batch Lambda): dedup_rate_pct (duplicates/total processed), ocr_success_rate_pct, storage_cost_savings_usd_weekly, migration_documents_backlogged. CloudTrail: S3 data events and API Gateway access logs forwarded to CloudWatch Logs with 90-day retention. Alarms: Lambda error rate > 1% for 5 minutes (SNS alert to ops), batch job failure (EventBridge rule on Lambda error), OpenSearch cluster red status. Cost dashboard queries CloudWatch Metrics for S3 storage bytes by document_type, computes cost at $0.023/GB.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $820/month steady state: RDS db.t3.medium Multi-AZ $240, OpenSearch dev.t3.medium $180, S3 Intelligent-Tiering ~$200/month for 10TB (declining as Glacier lifecycle activates), Lambda invocations ~$40, Textract OCR ~$80, API Gateway ~$30, Secrets Manager $10, CloudWatch $40. Legacy baseline: $1,400/month across 5 fragmented systems with estimated 40-60% duplicate storage. Lambda auto-scales with API Gateway concurrency. OpenSearch scales vertically to r6g.large if query latency exceeds 500ms. RDS read replica can be added for metadata queries if write contention emerges.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — MVP (8 weeks): Document upload API with SHA-256 dedup, S3 SSE-KMS storage, PostgreSQL metadata, Cognito JWT and API key auth, access_grants RBAC, access_audit_log, GET and search endpoints, nightly batch migration for Yardi and AppFolio connectors, GitHub Actions CI/CD, cost dashboard CloudWatch metrics. Phase 2 — Full Migration (4 weeks): Remaining 3 legacy system connectors (MRI, Buildium, custom FTP), Textract OCR pipeline for full-text indexing, OpenSearch search endpoint, document versioning, legal hold flag. Phase 3 — Enhancement (post-migration): Presigned URL download with watermarking, e-signature workflow integration hook, automated lease abstraction POC via Bedrock Claude.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with strict type hints throughout; mypy in CI. FastAPI dependency injection for database sessions and auth context — no global state. All database queries via SQLAlchemy async ORM; no raw SQL except for migration scripts. 90% pytest coverage gate — document module requires 95%. Integration tests in /tests/integration/ run against staging Lambda via SAM local or deployed staging stack. No legacy PMS credentials in code — all via Secrets Manager. SHA-256 computed on raw file bytes before any normalization — filenames and metadata do not affect the dedup key.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Lambda cold start latency for PostgreSQL connections exceeds 1-second SLA. Mitigation: RDS Proxy reuses pooled connections eliminating TCP handshake overhead; Lambda SnapStart for Python 3.12 reduces initialization to under 200ms. Risk: OpenSearch index drift from PostgreSQL if Textract OCR fails for a document. Mitigation: ocr_status enum tracks pending/complete/failed; nightly reconciliation job re-queues failed OCR documents. Risk: Legacy PMS API rate limits throttling batch migration job. Mitigation: connector modules implement exponential backoff and per-source rate limit budgets. Tradeoff: OpenSearch over RDS full-text search — adds operational cost but required for ranked multi-field search across 10TB+ document corpus.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Lambda + RDS Proxy over ECS for compute to honor cost-optimization mandate. Chose OpenSearch over Elasticsearch self-managed for operational simplicity. Confirmed SHA-256 of raw file bytes as dedup key — filename normalization not applied to avoid false negatives on renamed legitimate duplicates. Selected Textract over open-source OCR for AWS-native integration and accuracy on legal documents.

project.goal: Developer-facing landing page for PropTech data aggregation API with waitlist and live status widget
project.target_users: PropTech developers

frontend.state: Astro islands for form and status widget

reasoner_reviews.product: The landing page correctly targets developers with sample JSON responses and persona-based use case examples — these are the elements that convert developer evaluators. The waitlist form with Turnstile bot protection is the right MVP conversion mechanism before the API is live.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro islands architecture is ideal here: the hero, feature cards, and JSON snippets render as static HTML with no client-side JS, keeping Time-to-Interactive minimal. Only the waitlist form island and the status widget island ship JavaScript to the browser. Cloudflare Pages Functions handle the form POST at the edge with no cold starts.
reasoner_reviews.security: CSP headers must explicitly allowlist the Turnstile script origin (challenges.cloudflare.com) and the status page API origin. The Pages Function must validate the Turnstile token server-side before calling the Mailchimp API — never trust the client-side validation alone.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Lighthouse >= 90 on mobile is achievable with Astro zero-JS default and Tailwind's JIT purging. The status widget must use a lightweight fetch call from an Astro island — no heavy charting library for a simple up/degraded/down indicator.

backend.service_design: Single Cloudflare Pages Function at /api/waitlist: validates Turnstile token via POST to https://challenges.cloudflare.com/turnstile/v0/siteverify, then calls Mailchimp API to add subscriber to the waitlist list
backend.api_patterns: POST /api/waitlist with {email, use_case, turnstile_token}; returns {success: true} or {error: 'invalid_token'|'duplicate_email'|'server_error'}
backend.failure_handling: Turnstile validation failure returns 400; Mailchimp duplicate email returns 200 with message 'already on waitlist'; Mailchimp API error returns 500 with generic message

frontend.app_structure: Astro site with page-based routing: index.astro (single page), components/ (HeroSection, FeatureCards, JsonSnippetDisplay, WaitlistForm, ApiStatusWidget, UseCaseExamples, Footer)
frontend.pages_and_flows: Single-page layout: Hero -> Feature Highlights with JSON snippets -> API status indicator -> Use Case Examples (3 developer personas) -> Waitlist CTA -> Footer with legal links
frontend.performance_notes: Astro zero-JS by default for all non-interactive components; WaitlistForm and ApiStatusWidget use client:visible directive for lazy hydration; images optimized via Astro Image with AVIF/WebP; Tailwind JIT purges unused styles

security.auth_design: No authentication on the public site
security.abuse_prevention: Cloudflare Turnstile on waitlist form with server-side token validation in Pages Function; Cloudflare WAF rules block high-frequency form POST attempts
security.privacy_controls: Cloudflare Web Analytics (no cookies, no fingerprinting); no third-party tracking pixels; privacy notice link in footer for data collected via waitlist form

data.entities: feature_highlights (static Astro content), api_status_widget (client-side fetch from status page API), waitlist_form (form island), use_case_examples (static content)
data.storage_design: No database; email forwarded to Mailchimp list via Pages Function
data.analytics_events: Cloudflare Web Analytics tracks: page view, waitlist_form_submit, status_widget_loaded, json_snippet_copy_click

devops.deployment_topology: Cloudflare Pages: static assets on global CDN edge, Pages Functions at edge for /api/waitlist
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: npm ci, astro check (type + template validation), astro build, Cloudflare Pages deployment via wrangler. Preview deploys on every PR; production deploy on merge to main
devops.observability_stack: Cloudflare Analytics dashboard; Cloudflare Pages deployment logs; Pages Function invocation logs for waitlist submission errors

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a static landing page — architecture is about content delivery performance and the two interactive islands: waitlist form and API status widget. Key decisions: Astro islands pattern keeps JS off static sections; Cloudflare Pages edge functions handle form POST with Turnstile validation before touching Mailchimp API; Cloudflare Web Analytics satisfies tracking without cookie consent overhead. No database, no backend service, no authentication — these are architectural non-starters for a landing page.
target_output.title: PropTech Data API Developer Landing Page
target_output.executive_summary: A developer-targeted static landing page for a real estate data aggregation platform, built with Astro on Cloudflare Pages. Showcases API capabilities for property ownership records, zoning data, and permit history through feature cards with live sample JSON responses. Captures developer sign-ups via a waitlist form protected by Cloudflare Turnstile, with form data forwarded to Mailchimp via a Pages edge function. Includes a live API status widget fetching from the platform status page API, giving developers confidence in production readiness.
target_output.architecture_overview: Static Astro site compiled to HTML/CSS/JS and served from Cloudflare's global edge CDN. Astro islands architecture limits JavaScript delivery to two interactive components: WaitlistForm (Astro island with client:visible hydration, handles form state and submission to /api/waitlist) and ApiStatusWidget (Astro island fetching current service status from a public status page API on mount). A Cloudflare Pages Function handles POST /api/waitlist at the edge — validates the Turnstile bot-detection token server-side, then calls the Mailchimp API to add the subscriber. All other page sections — hero, feature cards, JSON snippet displays, use case examples — render as pure static HTML with zero client JavaScript. CSP, HSTS, and X-Frame-Options headers configured via Cloudflare Pages _headers file.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Astro 4.x with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS 3.x with JIT, Astro Image for AVIF/WebP optimization. Serverless: Cloudflare Pages Functions (TypeScript). Analytics: Cloudflare Web Analytics (cookie-free). Mailing list: Mailchimp Marketing API. Bot protection: Cloudflare Turnstile. Hosting: Cloudflare Pages. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with wrangler CLI for deployment. Build time: astro build.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Hero Section: headline emphasizing property ownership, zoning, and permit data breadth with CTA button scrolling to waitlist form. Feature Cards: three cards for Ownership Records API (parcel owner, deed history, tax assessed value), Zoning Data API (zoning classification, permitted uses, overlay districts), and Permit History API (permit type, filing date, contractor, status) — each with a collapsible sample JSON response block. API Status Widget: fetches current operational status (operational/degraded/outage) from public status endpoint and renders a colored indicator badge; refreshes on page focus. Use Case Examples: three developer persona sections — property search app, title research tool, and municipal analytics dashboard — each with a code snippet showing the relevant API call. Waitlist Form: email input, optional use_case dropdown (property search / title research / analytics / other), Turnstile challenge, submit button with loading state.
target_output.system_components: Cloudflare Pages CDN serving static assets globally. Cloudflare Pages Function (/api/waitlist edge function). Cloudflare Turnstile (bot detection CAPTCHA-alternative). Mailchimp Marketing API (waitlist subscriber list). External status page API (platform uptime status, read-only public endpoint). Cloudflare Web Analytics (privacy-preserving analytics beacon). GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline.
target_output.workflows: Waitlist Sign-Up: Visitor fills email and optional use_case field; Cloudflare Turnstile renders a non-intrusive challenge token. On submit, WaitlistForm island POSTs {email, use_case, turnstile_token} to /api/waitlist Pages Function. Function calls Turnstile siteverify endpoint with the token and the TURNSTILE_SECRET_KEY environment variable — if invalid, returns 400. If valid, calls Mailchimp POST /lists/{list_id}/members with email_address and SUBSCRIBED status; if Mailchimp returns 400 Member Exists, responds with {already_on_waitlist: true}; otherwise returns {success: true}. Form island shows confirmation message or error inline. API Status Widget: On component mount (client:visible), ApiStatusWidget fetches GET {STATUS_PAGE_API_URL}/v2/status.json. Parses indicator field (none/minor/major/critical) and maps to badge color (green/yellow/orange/red) and text ('All systems operational' / 'Minor degradation' etc). Re-fetches on window focus event to keep status current during long browsing sessions.
target_output.data_model: No persistent data model on the landing page infrastructure. Static content entities in Astro: feature_highlights (array of {title, description, sample_json} defined in a TypeScript content collection), use_case_examples (array of {persona, headline, code_snippet}), pricing_tiers (placeholder for future launch). Runtime state (ephemeral, browser-only): WaitlistForm component state (email string, useCase string, submitting boolean, success boolean, error string | null). ApiStatusWidget state (status: 'loading' | 'operational' | 'degraded' | 'outage', fetched_at: Date).
target_output.api_design: Single Pages Function endpoint: POST /api/waitlist. Request body: {email: string, use_case: string, turnstile_token: string}. Response 200: {success: true} or {already_on_waitlist: true}. Response 400: {error: 'invalid_token'} if Turnstile validation fails or {error: 'invalid_email'} if email format invalid. Response 500: {error: 'server_error'} on Mailchimp API failure. External read: ApiStatusWidget fetches GET https://{statuspage_domain}/api/v2/status.json (public, no auth). No other server-side endpoints.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Cloudflare WAF active on all traffic — blocks known bot signatures and rate-limits POST /api/waitlist to 10 requests per IP per minute. Cloudflare Turnstile validates form submissions server-side in the Pages Function using TURNSTILE_SECRET_KEY stored as a Pages encrypted environment variable — client-side token alone is not trusted. Content Security Policy via _headers file: script-src 'self' challenges.cloudflare.com static.cloudflareinsights.com; img-src 'self' data:; frame-src challenges.cloudflare.com. HSTS max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains in _headers. X-Frame-Options: DENY. No cookies set by the site itself; Cloudflare Web Analytics uses no cookies and requires no consent banner. Mailchimp TURNSTILE_SECRET_KEY and MAILCHIMP_API_KEY stored as Cloudflare Pages encrypted secrets, never in source code.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cloudflare Pages project connected to GitHub repository. GitHub Actions pipeline on pull request: npm ci, astro check (TypeScript type checking and Astro template validation), astro build. On successful build, wrangler pages deploy dist/ creates a preview deployment with a unique URL for PR review. On merge to main, production deployment triggered automatically. Deploy time target: under 2 minutes from git push to live production URL. Rollback: Cloudflare Pages dashboard allows instant rollback to any previous deployment. Pages Function environment variables (TURNSTILE_SECRET_KEY, MAILCHIMP_API_KEY, MAILCHIMP_LIST_ID) set in Cloudflare Pages project settings as encrypted secrets.
target_output.observability: Cloudflare Analytics: page views, unique visitors, top countries, bandwidth. Cloudflare Web Analytics custom events tracked via the analytics beacon: waitlist_submit_success, waitlist_submit_error, status_widget_loaded, json_snippet_expand. Cloudflare Pages Function logs capture waitlist submission outcomes (Turnstile validation result, Mailchimp response code) for debugging failed sign-ups. Uptime monitored via Cloudflare Health Check on the production URL; alert to Slack if site returns non-200 for more than 2 minutes.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Cloudflare Pages free tier covers unlimited static requests and 500 Pages Function invocations per day — sufficient for a pre-launch waitlist page. At scale (>500 daily sign-ups), $5/month Cloudflare Pages paid plan provides 100,000 Function invocations. Mailchimp free tier supports up to 500 contacts; upgrade to Essentials at $13/month when waitlist exceeds 500. Total launch cost: $0-$18/month depending on waitlist size.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Launch (2 weeks): Hero, feature cards with sample JSON for ownership/zoning/permit endpoints, API status widget, waitlist form with Turnstile, Cloudflare Web Analytics, CSP headers, Cloudflare Pages deployment. Phase 2 Post-launch (4 weeks): Interactive API explorer sandbox allowing developers to run live queries with a demo API key, pricing table when product launches publicly, testimonial section with early beta user quotes. Phase 3 (ongoing): Developer documentation section with full endpoint reference, SDKs download section, changelog feed.
target_output.development_guidelines: Astro TypeScript strict mode. All static content (feature highlights, use case examples) defined in Astro content collections with typed schemas — no hardcoded strings in component templates. Pages Function input validated with Zod before calling external APIs. Sample JSON blocks rendered via a syntax-highlighted code component using Shiki (Astro's built-in, zero-JS at runtime). Lighthouse CI runs in GitHub Actions — PR blocked if mobile score drops below 90. No third-party JS added without explicit review — every script added to the page must justify its impact on Core Web Vitals.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Mailchimp API outage causing waitlist form errors at launch. Mitigation: form shows a friendly 'try again' message on 500; email is preserved in browser localStorage so the user can resubmit after recovery. Risk: Turnstile rendering delayed on slow connections causing form to appear broken. Mitigation: show form fields immediately; Turnstile challenge renders lazily; submit button disabled until token received. Tradeoff: Astro over Next.js for a landing page — Astro produces smaller bundles and better Lighthouse scores for primarily-static content with limited interactivity.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: API status widget data source: platform status page API v2 (status.io compatible) returning indicator field. Use case dropdown options confirmed: property_search, title_research, analytics, other. Waitlist confirmation: redirect to /thank-you static page rather than inline message for cleaner analytics attribution.

metadata.notes: Static developer landing page on Cloudflare Pages — Astro islands, Turnstile-protected waitlist form, live API status widget, no backend or database.

profile.domain: Real Estate Property Management - Cloud Infrastructure Modernization

project_goal.value: Migrate a regional property management company's bare-metal on-premises server estate (14 physical servers running VMware ESXi) to a cloud-native Kubernetes environment on AWS EKS, containerizing legacy property management applications with minimal code changes and establishing a repeatable GitOps deployment model
project_goal.rationale: On-premises hardware reaching end-of-life in Q4; datacenter lease expiring; cloud migration enables elastic scaling for seasonal portfolio growth

target_users.value: Operations team (5 engineers) who currently maintain bare-metal servers and VMware; application teams whose legacy apps will run on the new platform; end users of property management applications who must see zero downtime during migration
target_users.rationale: Platform consumers, not the target persona for direct UI; success is measured by ops burden reduction and application uptime

project_class.rationale: Pure platform and networking work; Terraform for cloud resources, Helm for application packaging, EKS for container orchestration

capabilities.rationale: Infrastructure provisioning and containerization; no frontend, no user-facing API, no persistent application data layer owned by this project

complexity_level.rationale: 14-server migration with stateful legacy applications, LDAP federation, persistent volume migration, and phased traffic cutover adds significant complexity

risk_level.rationale: Production property management applications serve 200 property managers daily; any downtime impacts rent collection, maintenance dispatch, and lease management workflows

data_sensitivity.rationale: Infrastructure secrets and configuration data are internal; tenant PII is owned by the applications running on the platform, not by the platform itself

external_exposure.rationale: EKS cluster is private; all ingress via internal ALB within VPC; no public internet access to cluster API server or worker nodes

access_model.value: AWS IAM roles for cluster access using EKS access entries; LDAP federation via AWS Directory Service for human operator authentication; Kubernetes RBAC with cluster-admin for ops team, namespace-scoped developer roles per application team
access_model.rationale: Existing corporate LDAP used for SSO; EKS access entries replace deprecated aws-auth ConfigMap for auditability

feature_scope.value: EKS cluster provisioning with Terraform; VPC networking with private subnets and NAT gateway; containerization of 6 legacy applications as Dockerfiles; Helm chart packaging for each application; GitLab CI GitOps pipeline for automated deployment; Kubernetes RBAC configuration; persistent volume migration using Velero for stateful applications; weighted ingress cutover for phased traffic migration; Grafana/Prometheus/Loki observability stack via Helm
feature_scope.rationale: Full scope from network to GitOps delivery pipeline

mvp_scope.value: EKS cluster in staging VPC with Terraform; two lowest-risk stateless applications containerized and deployed via Helm; GitLab CI pipeline for automated image build and Helm upgrade; Grafana/Prometheus observability baseline; ops team kubectl access via LDAP federation
mvp_scope.rationale: Prove the platform and GitOps pipeline with stateless apps before tackling stateful application migrations

security_baseline.value: EKS cluster API server endpoint private (no public access); worker nodes in private subnets with no public IPs; all inter-service traffic over TLS 1.3; Kubernetes secrets encrypted at rest via AWS KMS envelope encryption; network policies enforcing namespace isolation; image scanning via Trivy in CI pipeline; RBAC least-privilege; no privileged containers
security_baseline.rationale: Private cluster configuration and KMS encryption are the minimum controls for production workloads handling property management data

frontend_stack.value: None; infrastructure project with no user-facing frontend
frontend_stack.rationale: All user interaction is through the property management applications already running on the platform

backend_stack.value: Terraform 1.8 for AWS resource provisioning; Helm 3.15 for Kubernetes application packaging; GitLab CI for pipeline orchestration; Velero for stateful workload backup and migration; Cert-Manager and External-Secrets-Operator as cluster add-ons
backend_stack.rationale: Standard IaC stack for EKS; Velero is the de-facto standard for Kubernetes persistent volume migration

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Amazon RDS Multi-AZ (migrated from on-premises PostgreSQL instances via AWS DMS); Redis 7 on ElastiCache for application caching layers; EBS gp3 persistent volumes for remaining stateful workloads
data_platform.rationale: RDS Multi-AZ eliminates database HA management burden; ElastiCache replaces self-managed Redis VMs; EBS gp3 for any workloads requiring local persistent storage

hosting_target.value: AWS EKS 1.30 in us-east-1 with managed node groups (m6i.xlarge, min 3 max 12 nodes); private VPC with three availability zones; ALB Ingress Controller for internal application access
hosting_target.rationale: Managed node groups reduce worker node patching burden; three-AZ deployment ensures HA for production workloads

privacy_retention_policy.value: Infrastructure configuration and Terraform state encrypted at rest in S3 with SSE-KMS; GitLab CI pipeline logs retained 90 days; Velero backup snapshots retained 30 days; no tenant PII stored in infrastructure layer
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Platform layer does not own tenant data; retention applies to operational metadata only

future_scope.value: Service mesh (Istio or Linkerd) for mTLS and traffic observability; Karpenter for bin-packing node autoscaling; multi-cluster federation for disaster recovery across AWS regions; Argo Rollouts for progressive delivery with automated canary analysis
future_scope.rationale: Post-migration platform maturity roadmap once all six applications are running stably on EKS

constraints.value: Zero downtime for production property management applications during cutover; migration must complete before on-premises datacenter lease expiry in Q4; infrastructure budget not to exceed $12,000/month steady-state; ops team has no prior Kubernetes experience and needs documentation and runbooks for day-2 operations
constraints.rationale: Hard deadline from datacenter lease; ops team upskilling is a critical project dependency

observability_baseline.value: Grafana for dashboards, Prometheus for metrics scraping and alerting, Loki for log aggregation — all deployed as Helm charts in the monitoring namespace; PagerDuty integration for critical alerts; key metrics: pod restart count, node CPU/memory utilization, PVC fill rate, ingress error rate
observability_baseline.rationale: Operations team needs visibility into cluster and application health with automated alerting before legacy servers are decommissioned

execution_preference.value: GitOps with GitLab CI; all infrastructure changes via Terraform PR and MR pipelines; all application deployments via Helm upgrade triggered by CI; no manual kubectl apply in production; ADRs for all major platform decisions
execution_preference.rationale: GitOps model prevents configuration drift and provides an audit trail for all infrastructure changes

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II controls required for cloud infrastructure handling property management operational data; CIS Kubernetes Benchmark v1.9 hardening applied to EKS cluster configuration; AWS Shared Responsibility Model documented with security responsibilities allocated between DevOps and AWS; Terraform state files encrypted at rest with SSE-KMS and access restricted to CI service roles via IAM least-privilege policies; infrastructure change management requires peer-reviewed PRs and automated compliance scans in GitLab CI pipeline before production apply; annual third-party infrastructure penetration test required.
compliance_context.rationale: High risk level for cloud infrastructure managing property management operational systems mandates SOC 2 controls and CIS Kubernetes hardening to satisfy enterprise tenant security review requirements.

project.goal: Migrate 14 on-premises VMware servers to AWS EKS with GitOps pipeline
project.target_users: Ops team of 5 engineers, 6 application teams
project.timeline: MVP (2 stateless apps) in 10 weeks; full migration before Q4 datacenter lease expiry

backend.migration: Velero for stateful workloads, AWS DMS for databases

security.cluster_access: Private EKS API endpoint; IAM access entries with LDAP federation
security.secrets: AWS Secrets Manager via External-Secrets-Operator; KMS envelope encryption for etcd
security.image_security: Trivy container scanning in CI; no privileged containers; network policies for namespace isolation

data.databases: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ via AWS DMS migration
data.persistent_storage: EBS gp3 PVCs for stateful workloads

devops.ci: GitLab CI with Terraform plan/apply and Helm upgrade stages
devops.environments: staging EKS cluster, production EKS cluster

reasoner_reviews.product: The phased migration approach starting with stateless applications is the right risk management strategy. The ops team's lack of Kubernetes experience is the highest project risk — runbooks and day-2 documentation should be treated as first-class deliverables alongside the Terraform code, not an afterthought. The Q4 hard deadline creates pressure to validate the platform with real workloads early.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Private EKS cluster with managed node groups is the correct baseline. Using EKS access entries over the legacy aws-auth ConfigMap is the right call for auditability and eliminates the operational risk of accidentally locking out cluster access. Velero for stateful application PVC migration is standard, but the database migration path via AWS DMS should be planned separately per application given schema compatibility risks. The weighted ALB ingress cutover pattern (starting at 5% traffic, incrementing to 100% over days) is essential for zero-downtime production cutover and must be designed before the first stateful application migration begins.
reasoner_reviews.security: KMS envelope encryption for EKS etcd is non-negotiable given the workloads. External-Secrets-Operator syncing from AWS Secrets Manager is the correct pattern to avoid storing secrets in Kubernetes etcd or GitLab CI environment variables. Trivy image scanning in CI must fail the pipeline on CRITICAL severity CVEs with a documented exception process for base image vulnerabilities pending upstream fixes. Network policies should be deployed from day one in deny-all-by-default mode with explicit allow rules per namespace.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The $12,000/month steady-state budget is achievable for the target footprint: three m6i.xlarge nodes (minimum) cost approximately $520/month, scaling to $2,000/month at peak 12 nodes. RDS Multi-AZ for six databases estimated at $3,000/month. ElastiCache and supporting services add approximately $1,500/month. Observability stack adds approximately $800/month. Total approximately $7,800/month at minimum footprint, leaving headroom for autoscaling events and data transfer costs.

backend.service_design: Terraform modules organized by concern: networking (VPC, subnets, NAT gateways, security groups), eks (cluster, node groups, add-ons), rds (per-application database instances), elasticache (Redis clusters), iam (roles and policies); Helm charts per application with values files per environment; GitOps repo structure with /infrastructure (Terraform), /charts (Helm), /manifests (raw Kubernetes YAML for cluster add-ons)
backend.api_patterns: No external API; cluster add-ons deployed via Helm: aws-load-balancer-controller, external-secrets-operator, cert-manager, metrics-server, cluster-autoscaler, kube-state-metrics; all add-ons pinned to specific chart versions in a versions.yaml lockfile
backend.failure_handling: Cluster autoscaler configured with scale-down disabled during business hours (8AM-8PM ET) to prevent node churn during peak property management activity; Velero scheduled backups every 6 hours with 30-day retention; RDS automated backups with 7-day retention and point-in-time recovery; ALB target group health checks with 2 unhealthy thresholds before traffic removal

security.auth_design: EKS access entries mapping AWS IAM roles to Kubernetes RBAC groups: ops-admin group bound to cluster-admin ClusterRole, app-team-{name} groups bound to namespace-scoped developer Role; AWS Directory Service connected to corporate LDAP for federated login; kubectl access via aws eks update-kubeconfig with IAM role assumption
security.secrets_management: External-Secrets-Operator: ExternalSecret resources per application namespace referencing AWS Secrets Manager secrets; secrets projected into pods as Kubernetes Secret objects with automatic rotation sync every 1 hour; no secrets stored in Helm values files or GitLab CI variables
security.abuse_prevention: Kubernetes network policies: default-deny-all ingress/egress in every namespace, explicit allow rules for application-to-database and application-to-cache traffic; OPA Gatekeeper policies enforcing no privileged containers, required resource limits, and prohibited latest image tags in production

data.entities: clusters (cluster_name, kubernetes_version, node_group_config, vpc_id, created_at), namespaces (namespace_name, cluster_id, team_owner, resource_quota_cpu, resource_quota_memory), workloads (workload_id, namespace_id, app_name, image_tag, replicas, pvc_attached BOOLEAN), persistent_volumes (pv_id, workload_id, storage_class, size_gb, ebs_volume_id, backup_schedule), secrets (secret_name, namespace_id, secrets_manager_arn, last_synced_at), ingress_rules (rule_id, workload_id, host, path, weight_pct, ssl_cert_arn), migration_runbooks (runbook_id, app_name, migration_status ENUM planned/in_progress/validated/decommissioned, cutover_date, rollback_procedure TEXT)
data.storage_design: Terraform state in S3 with DynamoDB locking and SSE-KMS encryption; EKS etcd encrypted with customer-managed KMS key; EBS gp3 for application PVCs with StorageClass encryption enabled; RDS storage encrypted at rest with RDS-managed KMS keys
data.migration_notes: AWS DMS for PostgreSQL-to-RDS migrations: full-load plus CDC (change data capture) mode to minimize cutover window; Velero for EBS-backed PVC snapshots; migration_runbooks table tracks per-application migration state and rollback procedure

devops.deployment_topology: Two EKS clusters: staging (2 m6i.large nodes, single AZ for cost) and production (3-12 m6i.xlarge nodes, three-AZ managed node group with cluster autoscaler). Production VPC: three private subnets (10.0.1.0/24, 10.0.2.0/24, 10.0.3.0/24) and three public subnets for NAT gateways and ALB. Internal ALB in public subnets routing to pods in private subnets via ALB Ingress Controller.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI pipeline: Terraform stage (terraform fmt check, terraform validate, terraform plan on MR; terraform apply on main merge with manual approval gate); Helm stage (helm lint, helm template validation, helm upgrade --atomic with rollback on failure); image build stage (docker build, Trivy scan with CRITICAL failure gate, push to ECR); deploy stage (helm upgrade with --wait and --timeout 5m).
devops.rollback_strategy: Helm rollback: helm rollback {release} {revision} restores previous deployment; Terraform state rollback via git revert of IaC change followed by terraform apply; database rollback via RDS point-in-time restore to pre-migration snapshot; weighted ALB ingress enables traffic rollback to on-premises servers during cutover window by setting new-cluster weight back to 0

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a bare-metal to EKS cloud migration for a property management company's on-premises VMware estate. Key decisions: private EKS cluster with managed node groups for zero ops overhead on worker node patching; EKS access entries replacing aws-auth ConfigMap for auditable LDAP-federated access; External-Secrets-Operator to avoid secrets in etcd; weighted ALB ingress cutover for zero-downtime traffic migration; AWS DMS for database migration with CDC mode to minimize cutover window. The ops team's Kubernetes inexperience elevates runbook quality to a first-class deliverable.
target_output.title: On-Premises VMware to AWS EKS Migration Platform
target_output.executive_summary: An infrastructure modernization project migrating a regional property management company from 14 bare-metal VMware ESXi servers to a private AWS EKS 1.30 cluster provisioned via Terraform and managed via GitLab CI GitOps pipelines. Six legacy property management applications are containerized with Dockerfiles and packaged as Helm charts with per-environment values. Stateless applications migrate first; stateful applications follow using AWS DMS for PostgreSQL and Velero for PVC snapshots. Zero downtime is achieved via weighted ALB ingress cutover. Grafana/Prometheus/Loki provide platform observability replacing manual server monitoring.
target_output.architecture_overview: Private EKS cluster (Kubernetes API endpoint not exposed to internet) in a three-AZ VPC with worker nodes exclusively in private subnets. An internal ALB managed by the AWS Load Balancer Controller routes inbound traffic from the corporate network to pods via Kubernetes Ingress resources. Terraform manages all AWS resources in modular form: networking, EKS cluster and node groups, RDS instances per application, ElastiCache clusters, and IAM roles. Applications are packaged as Helm charts stored in the /charts directory of the GitOps repository; deployments are triggered by GitLab CI on merge to main with helm upgrade --atomic ensuring automatic rollback on failure. External-Secrets-Operator synchronizes secrets from AWS Secrets Manager into Kubernetes Secret objects per namespace. OPA Gatekeeper enforces cluster-wide policies including no-privileged-containers, required-resource-limits, and no-latest-image-tags. Weighted ALB Ingress rules enable gradual traffic migration from on-premises servers to EKS pods, allowing rollback by adjusting weights without application code changes.
target_output.technology_stack: Infrastructure: Terraform 1.8 with modular structure, S3 remote state with DynamoDB lock. Orchestration: AWS EKS 1.30 with managed node groups (m6i.xlarge). Packaging: Helm 3.15 with per-application charts and environment values files. GitOps: GitLab CI with Terraform and Helm pipeline stages. Secrets: External-Secrets-Operator syncing from AWS Secrets Manager. Policy: OPA Gatekeeper with ConstraintTemplates. Security: Trivy image scanning in CI, AWS KMS for etcd and EBS encryption. Databases: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ, Redis 7 on ElastiCache. Migration: AWS DMS for PostgreSQL CDC, Velero for PVC snapshots. Observability: Grafana, Prometheus, Loki all deployed via kube-prometheus-stack Helm chart.
target_output.functional_feature_map: VPC and Networking: three-AZ private/public subnet design, NAT gateways per AZ, security groups per tier, VPC endpoints for ECR and Secrets Manager to eliminate NAT costs for AWS API traffic. EKS Cluster: managed node group with cluster autoscaler (min 3, max 12 m6i.xlarge nodes), Karpenter as future migration path. Cluster Add-ons: aws-load-balancer-controller, external-secrets-operator, cert-manager for TLS certificates, metrics-server, cluster-autoscaler, kube-state-metrics, node-exporter. Application Containerization: Dockerfile for each of 6 legacy apps with multi-stage builds to minimize image size; base images pinned to specific digest hashes. GitOps Pipeline: GitLab CI stages for Terraform plan/apply, Helm lint/upgrade, Trivy scan, and deployment validation. Observability Stack: Grafana dashboards for cluster health, node resource utilization, PVC fill rates, ingress error rates, and per-application pod metrics.
target_output.system_components: AWS VPC (10.0.0.0/16): three private subnets for pods and databases, three public subnets for NAT gateways and ALB, VPC endpoints for ECR API, ECR Docker, Secrets Manager, and S3. EKS Cluster (reit-propmanage-prod): private API endpoint, EKS access entries for LDAP-federated IAM roles, KMS-encrypted etcd, managed node group in three AZs. Internal ALB: routes corporate network traffic to EKS Ingress; weighted target groups for cutover. Amazon ECR: private container registry for all six application images with Trivy scan results stored as image scan findings. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ: one instance per stateful application, automated backups 7 days, point-in-time recovery enabled. ElastiCache Redis 7: two-node replication group per caching application. S3 Terraform state bucket: SSE-KMS encryption, versioning enabled, DynamoDB lock table. AWS Secrets Manager: one secret per application containing all configuration values synced by External-Secrets-Operator.
target_output.workflows: Application Migration Workflow: ops engineer creates migration runbook entry for target application; application team builds Dockerfile using multi-stage pattern; CI pipeline builds image, runs Trivy scan, pushes to ECR; Helm chart created with Deployment, Service, Ingress, HorizontalPodAutoscaler, and PodDisruptionBudget; deployed to staging EKS for validation against staging database; load tests run comparing staging response times against on-premises baseline; on approval, weighted ALB Ingress created routing 5% of production traffic to EKS pods while 95% remains on on-premises; if error rate stays below 0.1% for 24 hours, weight increments to 25%, 50%, 75%, 100% at 24-hour intervals; on-premises servers decommissioned after 7-day full-traffic stabilization period. Database Migration Workflow: AWS DMS replication instance created; full-load task copies PostgreSQL database from on-premises VM to RDS Multi-AZ; CDC task enabled to capture ongoing changes with sub-second lag; application cutover coincides with ALB weight reaching 100%; DMS task stopped and replication instance terminated; on-premises PostgreSQL instance decommissioned after 30-day backup retention period.
target_output.data_model: clusters (cluster_name VARCHAR PK, kubernetes_version VARCHAR, api_endpoint_private BOOLEAN, node_instance_type VARCHAR, min_nodes INT, max_nodes INT, vpc_id VARCHAR, kms_key_arn VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). namespaces (namespace_name VARCHAR, cluster_name FK, team_owner VARCHAR, resource_quota_cpu VARCHAR, resource_quota_memory VARCHAR, network_policy_mode ENUM deny_all/allow_all/custom). workloads (workload_id UUID PK, namespace_name FK, app_name VARCHAR, image_uri VARCHAR, current_replicas INT, pvc_count INT, last_deployed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, health_status ENUM healthy/degraded/failed). persistent_volumes (pv_id UUID PK, workload_id FK, storage_class VARCHAR, size_gb INT, ebs_volume_id VARCHAR, backup_schedule VARCHAR, last_backup_at TIMESTAMPTZ). ingress_rules (rule_id UUID PK, workload_id FK, host VARCHAR, path VARCHAR, on_prem_weight_pct INT, eks_weight_pct INT, ssl_cert_arn VARCHAR). migration_runbooks (runbook_id UUID PK, app_name VARCHAR, migration_status ENUM planned/staging/cutover/validated/decommissioned, cutover_date DATE, rollback_procedure TEXT, dms_task_arn VARCHAR, ops_owner VARCHAR).
target_output.api_design: No public API surface. Internal management interfaces: kubectl access via aws eks update-kubeconfig (IAM role assumption required, LDAP-federated). GitLab CI Terraform API: terraform plan output stored as MR artifact; terraform apply triggered on main branch merge with manual approval gate. Helm release management: helm upgrade {app} ./charts/{app} -f values/{env}.yaml --atomic --timeout 5m --wait. Velero CLI for backup/restore: velero backup create {app}-pre-cutover --selector app={app}, velero restore create --from-backup {app}-pre-cutover. AWS DMS replication task management via AWS Console and Terraform aws_dms_replication_task resources.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Cluster security: EKS API server private endpoint only; worker nodes in private subnets with no public IPs; security groups restrict node-to-node traffic to required Kubernetes ports only. Secrets: External-Secrets-Operator syncs AWS Secrets Manager secrets to pod-local Kubernetes Secrets; no secrets in Helm values files, GitLab CI variables, or ConfigMaps; KMS envelope encryption for EKS etcd and all EBS volumes. Image security: Trivy scan in CI with CRITICAL severity gate blocking deployment; base images pinned to SHA256 digest; ECR image scanning enabled for all repositories. Policy enforcement: OPA Gatekeeper ConstraintTemplates enforcing no-privileged-containers, required-resource-limits (CPU and memory requests/limits on all pods), no-latest-image-tags in production namespaces, and allowed-image-registries (ECR only). Access control: RBAC ClusterRole for ops-admin group, namespace-scoped developer Role per application team, read-only Role for Grafana service account; no wildcard permissions in any role.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI pipeline stages: (1) validate - terraform fmt check, terraform validate, helm lint, Trivy scan; (2) plan - terraform plan output stored as MR artifact for review; (3) apply - terraform apply on main branch merge with manual approval gate in production; (4) deploy - helm upgrade --atomic --wait --timeout 5m for application deployments; (5) verify - post-deploy smoke test checking application health endpoints. Environment promotion: all changes validated in staging EKS (single-AZ cost-optimized cluster) before production promotion. Rollback: helm rollback {release} {revision} for application failures; Terraform state revert via git revert plus terraform apply for infrastructure changes; weighted ALB ingress allows traffic rollback to on-premises during cutover window by setting eks_weight_pct back to 0 via Terraform variable change. Day-2 runbooks: documented in /docs/runbooks covering node cordon/drain, PVC resize, secret rotation, cluster version upgrade, and emergency on-premises traffic failback.
target_output.observability: kube-prometheus-stack Helm chart deploys Prometheus (metrics scraping with 30-day retention), Alertmanager (PagerDuty integration for critical alerts), Grafana (dashboards for cluster and application health), and node-exporter on every worker node. Loki with promtail deployed for log aggregation; log streams labeled by pod, namespace, and application. Key cluster metrics tracked: node CPU utilization (alert above 80%), node memory utilization (alert above 85%), PVC fill rate (alert when under 10% free), pod restart count (alert on crash-loop), pending pods count (alert if pods unschedulable for 5 minutes). Application metrics tracked: ingress error rate per application (alert above 1%), request latency p99 per ingress (alert above 2 seconds). Migration-specific metric: ALB target group healthy host count per weight tier, tracking that on-premises and EKS targets remain healthy during cutover window.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Steady-state estimate approximately $9,200/month at minimum footprint: EKS cluster management fee $73/month, three m6i.xlarge nodes $522/month (scaling to $2,088/month at 12 nodes), RDS Multi-AZ instances for 6 applications approximately $3,200/month, ElastiCache two-node clusters approximately $900/month, NAT gateways $330/month, data transfer approximately $400/month, Secrets Manager and KMS approximately $150/month, Grafana/Prometheus EBS storage approximately $200/month. Under $12,000 budget at minimum footprint. Cluster autoscaler maintains cost efficiency during off-peak hours; scale-down disabled 8AM-8PM ET to prevent disruption during business hours.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Platform Foundation (Weeks 1-5): Terraform VPC and EKS cluster provisioning; IAM roles and LDAP federation; ECR repositories; External-Secrets-Operator and OPA Gatekeeper deployment; kube-prometheus-stack observability; ops team kubectl access validation; GitLab CI Terraform pipeline. Phase 2 - Stateless Application Migration (Weeks 6-10): Dockerize 2 stateless legacy apps (no PVCs); Helm chart creation; staging EKS validation; GitLab CI deploy pipeline; weighted ALB cutover at 5%-25%-50%-100% over 4 days; on-premises VM decommission after 7-day stability window. Phase 3 - Database Migration (Weeks 11-14): AWS DMS full-load and CDC tasks per application database; validate RDS read replica lag under 1 second; coordinate database cutover with application ALB weight cutover to 100%. Phase 4 - Stateful Application Migration (Weeks 15-20): Velero PVC snapshot migration for remaining 4 applications; Helm chart creation with PersistentVolumeClaim specs; staged cutover per application with 7-day validation windows; final on-premises server decommission before Q4 datacenter lease expiry.
target_output.development_guidelines: All Terraform resources must be in named modules under /infrastructure/modules with variables, outputs, and README. No direct aws_* resource declarations in root module. Helm chart templates must pass helm lint and helm template validation in CI. No secrets in any file tracked in Git: use ExternalSecret resources referencing AWS Secrets Manager ARNs. All Kubernetes workload specs must include resource requests and limits; CI fails if missing. ADR documents committed to /docs/adrs for: EKS access entries vs aws-auth, External-Secrets-Operator vs Sealed Secrets, Helm vs Kustomize, weighted ingress cutover strategy. Runbooks in /docs/runbooks are first-class deliverables reviewed by ops team before each application migration begins.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ops team Kubernetes inexperience may cause misconfiguration of RBAC or network policies leading to access outages; mitigation: runbook validation sessions with ops team before each phase, staging environment mirrors production for practice. Risk: AWS DMS CDC replication lag spikes during high-write periods on source PostgreSQL could cause stale data during cutover window; mitigation: schedule database cutovers during low-traffic Sunday maintenance windows; monitor DMS lag metric in CloudWatch with alert threshold of 30 seconds. Risk: Legacy applications may use OS-level features (cgroups, /proc mounts) incompatible with containerization; mitigation: containerization assessment completed in Phase 1 parallel track before Phase 2 begins. Tradeoff: managed node groups over self-managed worker nodes reduces control over node configuration but eliminates AMI patching overhead, which is the right tradeoff given the ops team's limited Kubernetes experience.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Private EKS API endpoint selected over public with CIDR restriction to avoid accidental exposure if CIDR allowlist is misconfigured. EKS access entries selected over aws-auth ConfigMap for auditability and to eliminate lockout risk from ConfigMap corruption. External-Secrets-Operator selected over Sealed Secrets because AWS Secrets Manager is already the corporate secrets management platform, avoiding a second secrets system. Weighted ALB Ingress selected over DNS-based cutover for faster rollback capability during production migration windows.

metadata.notes: VMware bare-metal to AWS EKS migration; Terraform GitOps with Helm, weighted ALB cutover for zero-downtime, AWS DMS for PostgreSQL and Velero for PVCs.

profile.domain: Commercial Real Estate Back-Office Deal Room Application

project_goal.value: Security-hardened back-office application for a commercial real estate firm's asset management team, enabling secure document exchange with law firms, title companies, and lenders during property acquisitions and dispositions.
project_goal.rationale: Eliminates insecure email attachment workflows for confidential CRE transaction documents.

target_users.value: Internal asset managers and analysts; external counterparties including external counsel, title agents, lenders, and brokers invited as guests to specific deal rooms.
target_users.rationale: Dual internal/external user base with strict access scoping per deal room.

project_class.rationale: Requires both a Next.js frontend for deal room UI and a Go backend API for access control and document operations.

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, analytics, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: Full capability set for secure multi-party document exchange platform.

complexity_level.rationale: mTLS for partner-facing API, watermarking pipeline, immutable audit trail, and Okta SSO/OTP add architectural complexity.

risk_level.rationale: Confidential M&A documents with external partner access require highest security controls.

data_sensitivity.rationale: Purchase agreements, NDAs, and title documents are highly confidential transaction records.

external_exposure.rationale: External counsel, lenders, and title agents are invited as partners to specific deal rooms.

access_model.value: Okta SSO for internal users; OTP email/SMS for external counterparty guests; RBAC scoped per deal room with owner/editor/viewer/guest roles; mTLS required for partner API integrations.
access_model.rationale: Partner-facing exposure with confidential data mandates strong per-deal-room access scoping and mTLS.

feature_scope.value: Deal room creation and management, counterparty invitation with scoped access, document upload and versioning, watermarked download, e-signature via DocuSign integration, immutable audit trail, activity analytics, admin panel for user and permission management.
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for secure CRE transaction collaboration.

mvp_scope.value: Deal room create/invite, document upload/view with watermarking, Okta SSO + OTP for guests, immutable audit trail, basic deal room activity dashboard.
mvp_scope.rationale: Core secure exchange workflow for first acquisition transaction.

security_baseline.value: mTLS for partner API connections, AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit, Okta MFA, watermarking on all document downloads, immutable audit trail, WAF on ALB.
security_baseline.rationale: Partner-facing confidential data exposure mandates defense-in-depth.

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query for server state.
frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise and SSR capability for deal room document previews.

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Gin for REST API; background workers for watermarking pipeline.
backend_stack.rationale: Go's performance and concurrency model suited for document processing and audit logging throughput.

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 (RDS) for deal room metadata and audit trail; S3 for document storage.
data_platform.rationale: Relational model for complex deal room RBAC; S3 for scalable document storage.

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1 with ALB and WAF.
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers with data residency in us-east-1 for legal jurisdiction compliance.

privacy_retention_policy.value: Deal room documents retained 10 years per SEC retention rules for REIT entities. Audit trail retained indefinitely. Guest access expires 90 days after deal room closes.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SEC document retention rules for REIT; E-SIGN Act requires audit trail preservation.

future_scope.value: AI-powered document abstraction for lease and purchase agreement key terms extraction; automated compliance checklist per deal type.
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap for accelerating deal team due diligence.

constraints.value: All data must remain in us-east-1. mTLS required for all partner API connections. Document downloads must be watermarked within 5 seconds of request. No public internet access to admin panel.
constraints.rationale: Data residency, partner security, and performance requirements.

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM for Go API and Next.js SSR, structured JSON logging with deal_room_id correlation, PagerDuty alerts for audit trail write failures and watermark job failures.
observability_baseline.rationale: Audit trail integrity is a compliance requirement; alerting on failures is mandatory.

execution_preference.value: Agile sprints with 2-week iterations; security review required before each production deployment.
execution_preference.rationale: High-risk data handling requires security sign-off at each milestone.

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II; E-SIGN Act for electronic signatures via DocuSign; SEC document retention rules for REIT entities (10-year retention); mTLS enforced for all partner-facing API connections per security policy.
compliance_context.rationale: Risk level high and data sensitivity confidential with partner-facing exposure trigger all compliance requirements.

project.goal: Secure multi-party deal room for CRE acquisition and disposition document exchange
project.target_users: Internal asset managers; external counsel, lenders, title agents as deal room guests
project.timeline: Q4 for MVP covering first live acquisition

security.auth: Okta SSO for internal; OTP for external guests
security.encryption: AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit

reasoner_reviews.product: Deal room model correctly scopes access to individual transactions. The watermarking-before-download pattern is the right trade-off for a 5-second SLA at expected document volumes (50-200 documents per deal room). DocuSign integration adds significant value for purchase agreement execution within the platform.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go 1.22 with Gin is well-suited for the concurrent watermarking pipeline. The immutable audit trail requires careful PostgreSQL schema design — recommend an audit_events table with NO UPDATE/DELETE permissions for the API service role. mTLS for partner API connections must be enforced at the ALB layer, not just the application layer.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS is required per contract but the round 1 plan only described TLS 1.3 without specifying mTLS certificate provisioning. This must be remediated. ACM Private CA should be used for partner client certificates. Watermarking must embed user_id and download_timestamp in the document pixel layer, not just a visible header.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 5-second watermarking SLA is achievable for PDFs under 50 pages using a Go PDF library (pdfcpu). For larger documents, implement async watermarking with a presigned URL polling pattern. Admin panel restricted to VPC internal access via ALB internal load balancer.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan described mTLS as a requirement but the architecture_overview and deployment_and_operations sections only mentioned TLS 1.3. The mTLS certificate lifecycle (ACM Private CA, certificate rotation, partner onboarding process) was not addressed. This is the primary blocking issue for round 2.

backend.service_design: Go Gin API with domain packages: dealrooms (CRUD, member management), documents (upload, version, download), watermark (PDF processing pipeline), audit (append-only event logging), admin (user and permission management)
backend.api_patterns: REST with versioned endpoints at /api/v1/; OpenAPI 3.0 spec; idempotent document upload by SHA-256; signed presigned download URLs from S3
backend.failure_handling: Watermark worker failures retry 3x with exponential backoff; failed jobs pushed to SQS DLQ with alert; audit log write failures trigger PagerDuty critical alert
backend.scaling_notes: ECS Fargate auto-scaling on CPU 60% target; watermark workers in separate ECS task definition for independent scaling; RDS read replica for analytics queries

frontend.app_structure: Next.js App Router with SSR for deal room document preview pages; client components for real-time activity feed via polling; admin panel behind internal ALB
frontend.pages_and_flows: Deal Room List, Deal Room (documents, members, activity), Document Viewer (watermarked preview), Invite Counterparty, Admin (users, permissions, deal room audit), Guest OTP Login
frontend.state_management: React Query for deal room data and document lists; local state for document viewer; no global store
frontend.frontend_security_notes: CSP blocking inline scripts; no document binary bytes sent to client — only presigned S3 URLs; watermarking happens server-side before presigned URL generation

security.auth_design: Okta OIDC for internal users with MFA enforced; OTP via SES email for external guest authentication; Okta JWT validated in Go middleware; guest tokens scoped to specific deal_room_id and valid 72 hours
security.authorization_model: RBAC per deal room: owner (internal, full control), editor (upload/delete docs), viewer (view/download), guest (view only, downloads watermarked). Enforced in Go middleware via deal_room_members table lookup on every request
security.audit_and_logging_controls: audit_events table: append-only enforced via PostgreSQL REVOKE DELETE ON audit_events FROM api_service_role. Events: room_created, member_invited, document_uploaded, document_viewed, document_downloaded (with watermark_id), signature_requested, signature_completed
security.incident_response_notes: On unauthorized access attempt, auto-suspend guest account and alert deal owner via SES; security team paged via PagerDuty for any audit trail write failure

data.entities: deal_rooms (id, title, deal_type ENUM acquisition/disposition/refinance, status, created_by, created_at), transactions (id, deal_room_id FK, property_address, asset_class, transaction_value_usd, closing_date), documents (id, deal_room_id FK, filename, s3_key, version_number, content_hash, uploaded_by, created_at), document_downloads (id, document_id FK, downloaded_by, watermark_id, watermark_s3_key, downloaded_at), deal_room_members (id, deal_room_id FK, user_id FK, role ENUM, invited_by, joined_at, expires_at), audit_events (id BIGSERIAL, deal_room_id, user_id, event_type, metadata_json, occurred_at) append-only
data.storage_design: S3 bucket propco-dealrooms with SSE-KMS; separate prefix per deal_room_id; signed URLs 15-min TTL for downloads; watermarked copies stored in s3://propco-dealrooms/watermarked/{watermark_id}.pdf
data.retention_and_deletion: S3 objects tagged with deal_room_id and deal_close_date; lifecycle rule retains for 10 years from close date; audit_events retained indefinitely; guest access records purged 90 days post-room-close

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster: api-service task (Go Gin, 2-6 tasks auto-scaling), watermark-worker task (Go PDF processor, 1-4 tasks), Next.js-ssr task (Next.js 14, 2-4 tasks). Internal ALB for admin panel; external ALB + WAF for API and frontend. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ in private subnet
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go test (90% coverage), next build, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy to staging, security review gate, ECS rolling deploy to production
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM sidecar on all ECS tasks; structured JSON logs with deal_room_id and user_id correlation; custom metrics: watermark_job_duration_p99, audit_write_success_rate, download_presign_latency
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS rolling deploy with health check; automatic rollback on task failure rate > 5%; manual rollback via ECS task definition revision

ISS-001.title: mTLS described in contract but only TLS 1.3 implemented in architecture — certificate lifecycle not addressed
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 architecture_overview and deployment_and_operations sections described TLS 1.3 but did not mention mTLS. The contract requires mTLS for all partner-facing API connections. ACM Private CA provisioning, partner client certificate onboarding, and certificate rotation process were not documented.

history.note: Identified by security reviewer and critic in round 1 audit.

focus_issues.title: mTLS described in contract but only TLS 1.3 implemented in architecture — certificate lifecycle not addressed
focus_issues.detail: Round 1 architecture_overview and deployment_and_operations sections described TLS 1.3 but did not mention mTLS. The contract requires mTLS for all partner-facing API connections.

latest_recommendations: Add mTLS to architecture_overview and deployment_and_operations with ACM Private CA provisioning steps, partner client certificate onboarding process, and 90-day rotation schedule.

convergence_state.reason: Critical ISS-001 unresolved; mTLS contract requirement not reflected in architecture.

previous_audits.summary: Strong domain-specific plan for a CRE deal room but a critical contract violation: mTLS required by contract is absent from architecture and deployment sections. Score heavily penalized by critical unresolved issue.
strengths: Deal room RBAC model per-room is well-designed
Watermarking pipeline with 5-second SLA is specified
Okta SSO + OTP guest auth is correct for partner-facing exposure
concerns: mTLS absent from architecture despite contract requirement
ACM Private CA and partner certificate lifecycle not addressed
blocking_issues: ISS-001: Contract requires mTLS for partner API connections; architecture only describes TLS 1.3
recommendations: Add ACM Private CA setup for partner client certificates to deployment_and_operations; describe partner onboarding process for certificate provisioning; document 90-day rotation schedule in security_and_compliance

issue_updates.title: mTLS absent from architecture
issue_updates.detail: architecture_overview and deployment_and_operations describe TLS 1.3 but contract field security_baseline explicitly requires mTLS for partner API connections.

previous_plan.title: CRE Deal Room — Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Secure deal room platform with Go Gin backend on ECS Fargate, Next.js 14 frontend, Okta SSO and guest OTP, watermarked document downloads, and immutable audit trail.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Three-tier architecture: Next.js 14 on ECS Fargate, Go Gin REST API on ECS Fargate behind ALB with WAF, RDS PostgreSQL for metadata and audit trail, S3 SSE-KMS for documents. TLS 1.3 in transit.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Okta OIDC for internal users; OTP for guests; RBAC per deal room; TLS 1.3; watermarking on downloads; audit_events append-only table.

This is a plan_quality flawed row — I need to generate a plan that still partially fixes the issue but leaves a detectable flaw: the mTLS description will be added to security_and_compliance but the deployment_and_operations section will still not describe ACM Private CA provisioning steps or the partner certificate onboarding process, leaving the certificate lifecycle unaddressed. This represents a weak attempt at remediation that an auditor would flag as still failing to fully resolve ISS-001.

target_output.title: CRE Asset Management Deal Room Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A security-hardened fullstack application for a commercial real estate firm's deal team, enabling secure multi-party document exchange during property acquisitions and dispositions. Internal asset managers collaborate with external counsel, lenders, and title agents inside scoped deal rooms with Okta SSO, guest OTP access, watermarked document downloads, and an immutable PostgreSQL audit trail. Built with Next.js 14 on ECS Fargate backed by a Go 1.22 Gin API, PostgreSQL 16, and S3 SSE-KMS storage.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier architecture with Next.js 14 SSR application and Go 1.22 REST API co-deployed on ECS Fargate behind separate ALBs — external ALB with WAF for the frontend and API, internal ALB for the admin panel. Deal room documents stored in S3 with SSE-KMS encryption; metadata, RBAC grants, and audit events in RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ. Watermarking pipeline runs as a separate ECS task processing PDF downloads asynchronously. TLS 1.3 enforced at all ALB listeners. Partner-facing API connections require mTLS.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query. Backend: Go 1.22, Gin, pgx/v5 for PostgreSQL, pdfcpu for PDF watermarking, AWS SDK Go v2. Database: RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ. Storage: S3 SSE-KMS with versioning. Auth: Okta OIDC (internal), SES OTP (external guests). E-signature: DocuSign eSignature API. CDN/WAF: ALB with AWS WAF managed rules. Monitoring: Datadog APM, PagerDuty. CI: GitHub Actions.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Deal Room Management: create room with deal_type (acquisition/disposition/refinance), add transaction metadata (property address, asset class, transaction value, closing date target). Member Invitation: invite external counterparties by email; system sends OTP link scoped to deal_room_id with 72-hour token expiry. Document Operations: upload with automatic versioning, SHA-256 content hash, S3 SSE-KMS storage; viewer role sees watermarked preview; download triggers watermark job embedding user_id and download timestamp in PDF. DocuSign Integration: request e-signature on uploaded document; signature events logged to audit_events. Activity Dashboard: real-time event feed per deal room (uploads, views, downloads, signatures). Admin Panel: manage all users, deal rooms, permission grants — accessible from internal VPC only.
target_output.system_components: External ALB with WAF (API and Next.js frontend traffic; TLS 1.3; mTLS for partner API endpoint /api/v1/partner/*), Internal ALB (admin panel; VPC-internal only), ECS Fargate api-service task (Go Gin; 2-6 tasks; auto-scale on CPU 60%), ECS Fargate watermark-worker task (pdfcpu PDF processor; 1-4 tasks; SQS-driven), ECS Fargate nextjs-ssr task (Next.js 14; 2-4 tasks), RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (deal_rooms, transactions, documents, document_downloads, deal_room_members, audit_events tables; private subnet), S3 propco-dealrooms bucket (SSE-KMS; per-deal-room prefix; watermarked copies in /watermarked/ prefix), Okta OIDC tenant (internal user authentication; MFA enforced), SES OTP service (external guest token delivery), DocuSign eSignature API integration, Datadog APM sidecar on all ECS tasks.
target_output.workflows: Guest Counterparty Access: Asset manager invites external counsel via POST /api/v1/deal-rooms/{id}/members with email and role=guest. System creates deal_room_members record and sends SES OTP email with scoped token. Guest clicks link, enters OTP, receives 72-hour JWT scoped to deal_room_id with role=guest. Guest browses document list, clicks view — backend validates JWT deal_room_id match, triggers watermark job for their user_id, returns presigned URL to watermarked PDF copy when ready (p95 under 5 seconds). Document Download Audit: Every download triggers: watermark_job (embed user_id + timestamp in PDF pixel layer), document_downloads row insert, audit_events row insert (event_type=document_downloaded, watermark_id, user_id, deal_room_id, occurred_at). Audit trail write failure triggers PagerDuty critical alert and aborts the download — document is not served if audit cannot be recorded.
target_output.data_model: deal_rooms (id UUID PK, title, deal_type ENUM acquisition/disposition/refinance, status ENUM active/closed/archived, created_by UUID FK, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). transactions (id UUID PK, deal_room_id UUID FK, property_address TEXT, asset_class ENUM office/retail/multifamily/industrial, transaction_value_usd NUMERIC, closing_date DATE). documents (id UUID PK, deal_room_id UUID FK, filename, s3_key, version_number INT, content_hash CHAR(64), uploaded_by UUID FK, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). document_downloads (id UUID PK, document_id UUID FK, downloaded_by UUID FK, watermark_id UUID, watermark_s3_key TEXT, downloaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ). deal_room_members (id UUID PK, deal_room_id UUID FK, user_id UUID FK, role ENUM owner/editor/viewer/guest, invited_by UUID, joined_at TIMESTAMPTZ, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_events (id BIGSERIAL, deal_room_id UUID, user_id UUID, event_type ENUM room_created/member_invited/document_uploaded/document_viewed/document_downloaded/signature_requested/signature_completed, metadata_json JSONB, occurred_at TIMESTAMPTZ) — append-only via REVOKE DELETE ON audit_events FROM api_service_role.
target_output.api_design: REST API at /api/v1/ with OpenAPI 3.0. POST /api/v1/deal-rooms (create room; internal only). GET /api/v1/deal-rooms/{id} (room metadata; RBAC enforced). POST /api/v1/deal-rooms/{id}/documents (upload; editor+ role). GET /api/v1/deal-rooms/{id}/documents/{doc_id}/download (triggers watermark job; returns presigned URL when ready; logs audit event). POST /api/v1/deal-rooms/{id}/members (invite counterparty; owner only). GET /api/v1/deal-rooms/{id}/audit (audit event stream; owner/admin only). POST /api/v1/signatures/{doc_id}/request (trigger DocuSign envelope; editor+ role). Auth: Okta JWT for internal users; scoped guest JWT for external. mTLS enforced on /api/v1/partner/* endpoints for system-to-system integrations.
target_output.security_and_compliance: mTLS required for all partner-facing API connections at /api/v1/partner/* — ALB mutual TLS authentication configured with client certificate validation. Partner integrations must present valid client certificates. TLS 1.3 for all other connections. Okta OIDC with MFA enforced for all internal users. Guest OTP tokens scoped to deal_room_id, 72-hour expiry, single-use via Redis token store. Watermarking embeds user_id and download_timestamp in PDF pixel layer (not just visible header) via pdfcpu library. Audit trail: REVOKE DELETE ON audit_events FROM api_service_role enforces append-only; audit write failure aborts download. WAF managed rules blocking OWASP Top 10 on external ALB. S3 SSE-KMS with dedicated CMK per environment. E-SIGN Act: DocuSign audit trail satisfies electronic signature record requirements. SOC 2 Type II: CloudTrail for all AWS resource changes; Datadog audit logs for API operations.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: go test (90% coverage), next build, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy to staging, security review gate, ECS rolling deploy to production. ECS Fargate cluster in us-east-1 across 1a/1b/1c. External ALB with WAF: HTTPS on 443 with TLS 1.3; mTLS configured on /partner/* listener rule. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ with automated backups (7-day retention). S3 versioning enabled with 10-year lifecycle retention. Secrets Manager for Okta client secret, DocuSign API key, DB password — 90-day rotation. Environments: dev (single-AZ, no WAF), staging (multi-AZ, WAF), production (multi-AZ, WAF, RDS Multi-AZ, reserved ECS capacity).
target_output.observability: Datadog APM sidecar on all ECS tasks with deal_room_id and user_id as trace tags. Structured JSON logs: all API requests include deal_room_id, user_id, event_type for audit correlation. Custom Datadog metrics: watermark_job_duration_p99 (SLA: under 5000ms), audit_write_success_rate (SLA: 100% — any failure pages PagerDuty), download_presign_latency_p95, active_deal_rooms_count. PagerDuty escalation: audit trail write failure (critical), ECS task failure rate > 5%, RDS failover event, watermark worker SQS queue depth > 100.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $2,100/month for production: ECS Fargate (api + watermark + nextjs tasks) ~$800, RDS Multi-AZ db.t3.large ~$600, S3 storage ~$200, ALB + WAF ~$150, DocuSign API $200, Datadog ~$150. ECS auto-scales api-service on CPU 60% target (2-6 tasks); watermark-worker scales on SQS queue depth (1-4 tasks). RDS read replica addable for analytics queries if needed at scale.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — MVP (10 weeks): deal room create/close, member invite with Okta SSO and OTP guest auth, document upload/view with watermarked download, immutable audit trail, basic activity dashboard, admin panel, GitHub Actions CI/CD, ECS Fargate production deployment. Phase 2 — Full Feature (4 weeks): DocuSign e-signature integration, advanced deal room analytics (time-to-close, counterparty engagement), bulk document operations, deal room templates for standard acquisition/disposition workflows. Phase 3 — AI Enhancement (post-launch): Bedrock Claude integration for lease and purchase agreement key terms extraction, automated compliance checklist per deal type.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go 1.22 strict error handling — no ignored errors in document or audit operations. All audit_events inserts in database transactions with the triggering operation — if audit insert fails, the entire transaction rolls back and the operation is aborted. React Query cache invalidated on all document mutations. TypeScript strict mode. 90% test coverage for Go API; audit package requires 100% coverage. Security review required before each production ECS deployment.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: mTLS certificate provisioning for external partners is operationally complex — each new law firm or lender integration requires certificate issuance and distribution. Mitigation: partner onboarding runbook with ACM Private CA CLI steps; automated rotation via Lambda scheduled job. Risk: Watermarking SLA of 5 seconds may be exceeded for large PDFs (100+ pages). Mitigation: async watermark with presigned URL polling; synchronous path only for documents under 20 pages. Tradeoff: Okta over in-house OIDC — vendor dependency but eliminates MFA infrastructure build. Tradeoff: pdfcpu over a commercial watermarking SDK — open-source but requires validation against edge-case PDF structures.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Go pdfcpu for watermarking over AWS Textract-based approach — pdfcpu modifies PDF structure directly, Textract is OCR-only. Confirmed DocuSign over Adobe Sign for e-signature due to existing enterprise agreement. ACM Private CA selected for partner mTLS certificate issuance — provisioning runbook to be documented in ops wiki.

metadata.notes: Flawed round 2: mTLS mentioned in security section but ACM Private CA provisioning, partner certificate onboarding, and rotation schedule absent from deployment_and_operations — ISS-001 not fully resolved.

backend.api: REST per service; aggregator calls all four in parallel
