open_questions.composite_scoring: How to weight the four risk dimensions in the composite score?

reasoner_reviews.product: Round 1 established the basic four-service structure but left the aggregator's composite scoring formula undefined. For the prototype to generate meaningful research findings, the scoring weights and normalization method need to be specified — even as a configurable default.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The aggregator calling all four services in parallel via Go goroutines with context deadline is the right pattern. Round 1 did not specify what happens when one risk service is unavailable — the aggregator needs a partial-result strategy (return available scores with a null for unavailable) rather than failing the entire composite score.
reasoner_reviews.security: Round 1 plan's inter-service JWT validation was described but not fully designed. In round 2, the aggregator must include the service-to-service JWT in the Authorization header, and each scoring service must validate it using a shared secret. Fair Housing Act considerations: the composite score should include source attribution so downstream consumers can audit which data sources contributed to a score.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Four Cloud Run services plus Cloud Memorystore Redis can fit within $200/month with scale-to-zero. The main cost risk is Cloud SQL — four separate PostgreSQL instances would exceed budget. Round 2 should reconsider whether all four services actually need separate PostgreSQL instances for a prototype, or whether separate schemas on a single Cloud SQL instance would satisfy the isolation goal.

backend.service_design: Five Go services: flood-risk-svc, crime-stats-svc, school-ratings-svc, walkability-svc (scoring services), neighborhood-aggregator-svc (orchestrator). Each scoring service: data ingestion job, score computation logic, REST API exposing GET /score?lat=&lon=
backend.api_patterns: Each scoring service: GET /score?lat={lat}&lon={lon} returns {score: float, label: string, source: string, data_as_of: date}. Aggregator: GET /composite?lat=&lon= calls all four in parallel with 2-second timeout per service
backend.failure_handling: Aggregator uses Go context with 2s deadline per service call; on timeout returns partial CompositeScore with nil for timed-out service and partial_result: true flag

security.auth_design: Aggregator generates a service JWT signed with INTER_SERVICE_SECRET env var; each scoring service validates JWT in Authorization header; no external-facing auth
security.abuse_prevention: Cloud Run services not publicly accessible — internal VPC ingress only; IAM-based Cloud Run invocation for service-to-service calls as backup
security.audit_and_logging_controls: GCP Cloud Logging captures each composite score request with lat/lon (rounded to 3 decimal places for privacy), service response times, and any partial_result flags

data.entities: neighborhoods, flood_risk_scores, crime_statistics, school_ratings, walkability_scores, composite_risk_profiles
data.storage_design: Single Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 instance with four separate schemas (flood, crime, school, walkability) to satisfy isolation while fitting budget; Redis 7 on Cloud Memorystore for composite score caching (TTL 24h)
data.schema_notes: flood schema: flood_zones (geoid, zone_class ENUM A/AE/X/X500, base_flood_elevation, last_updated). crime schema: crime_statistics (geoid, violent_rate, property_rate, year). school schema: school_ratings (geoid, avg_rating, school_count, data_year). walkability schema: walkability_scores (geoid, walk_score, transit_score, bike_score)

devops.deployment_topology: GCP Cloud Run: 5 services, each independently deployed. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL shared instance with 4 schemas. Cloud Memorystore Redis. Cloud Run services use internal ingress only.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go test ./... for each service, docker build, gcloud run deploy for each service independently — validates that each service can be deployed without touching others
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus metrics sidecar on each Cloud Run service; Grafana on Cloud Run reading metrics; GCP Cloud Logging for structured logs

ISS-001.title: Composite scoring formula undefined
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan describes the aggregator calling four services but does not specify how scores are combined into a composite — no weighting scheme, no normalization method.

history.note: Raised by product reviewer

ISS-002.title: Aggregator partial-result handling not designed
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 aggregator fails entirely if any scoring service times out. Prototype needs a graceful partial-result pattern to evaluate service independence under realistic failure conditions.

history.note: Raised by architect_reasoner

ISS-003.title: Four separate Cloud SQL instances exceed budget
ISS-003.detail: Round 1 specified separate PostgreSQL instances per service — four Cloud SQL instances exceed the $200/month prototype budget. Round 2 to use single instance with separate schemas.

history.note: Budget analysis raised by constraints reviewer
Specialist subplan updated to single Cloud SQL instance with four schemas

focus_issues.title: Composite scoring formula undefined
focus_issues.detail: Aggregator has no weighting scheme or normalization method for combining four risk scores.

focus_issues.title: Aggregator partial-result handling not designed
focus_issues.detail: Aggregator fails entirely on any service timeout rather than returning available scores.

latest_recommendations: Define the composite scoring formula: specify default weights (flood 30%, crime 30%, school 25%, walkability 15%) and 0-100 normalization per dimension
Design aggregator partial-result pattern: on service timeout, return available scores with null for unavailable dimensions and a partial_result boolean

convergence_state.reason: Two medium-severity design gaps prevent prototype from producing meaningful research output

previous_audits.summary: Five-service decomposition structure is sound for a research prototype but two design gaps prevent the prototype from generating useful research data: undefined composite formula and brittle aggregator.
strengths: Clean per-service schema isolation within single Cloud SQL instance satisfies independence goal within budget
Go goroutine parallel calls in aggregator is the correct performance pattern for concurrent service calls
Prometheus metrics per service will produce the latency comparison data the research needs
concerns: Composite score formula is undefined — prototype cannot produce comparable results without a consistent weighting scheme
Aggregator fails entirely on single service timeout — undermines the purpose of researching service independence under partial failure
blocking_issues: ISS-001: Composite scoring formula must be specified to generate research-grade output
ISS-002: Aggregator must handle partial results to test service independence under failure conditions
recommendations: Add CompositeScoreConfig struct with per-dimension weights (flood, crime, school, walkability) defaulting to 30/30/25/15 and normalization to 0-100 scale
Implement aggregator PartialCompositeScore type: each dimension field is *float64 (nullable); set partial_result=true when any service timed out; still return available scores

previous_plan.title: Neighborhood Risk Microservices Prototype v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Five Go Gin services on GCP Cloud Run decomposing neighborhood risk scoring into flood, crime, school, and walkability dimensions. Aggregator calls four services in parallel and returns a composite score. Single Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instance with separate schemas per service.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Five Cloud Run services communicating over internal VPC. Aggregator uses goroutines for parallel service calls. Redis on Cloud Memorystore caches composite scores with 24h TTL.
previous_plan.workflows: Aggregator receives GET /composite?lat=&lon=; spawns four goroutines calling each scoring service; waits for all responses; combines scores. No handling for partial failures — returns error if any service times out.
previous_plan.data_model: Four separate schemas in shared Cloud SQL instance: flood (flood_zones), crime (crime_statistics), school (school_ratings), walkability (walkability_scores). Composite score formula unspecified.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must close the two medium-severity research blockers: define the composite scoring formula with configurable weights and normalization, and redesign the aggregator to return partial results when individual services time out. The prototype's research value depends on being able to run it under partial-failure conditions to evaluate service independence. The overall architecture from round 1 is sound — single Cloud SQL with four schemas, five Cloud Run services, Redis caching — so round 2 is a targeted fix, not a structural redesign.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined CompositeScoreConfig with default weights (flood: 0.30, crime: 0.30, school: 0.25, walkability: 0.15) and per-dimension normalization to 0-100 scale. Each scoring service now returns a raw_score and normalized_score. Aggregator applies weights to normalized scores and rounds to two decimal places.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Prototype produces consistent, comparable composite scores enabling research analysis of scoring accuracy across neighborhoods; ISS-001 resolved

fix_report.action_taken: Redesigned aggregator to use *float64 nullable fields in CompositeScore struct; on service timeout (2s context deadline), the corresponding dimension field is set to nil and partial_result flag set to true. Aggregator computes composite score from available dimensions using normalized weights. Research team can analyze how partial availability affects composite scores.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Aggregator returns useful partial scores under service failure conditions; prototype can evaluate service independence behavior; ISS-002 resolved

target_output.title: Neighborhood Risk Microservices Prototype
target_output.executive_summary: A Go-based research prototype validating microservices decomposition for neighborhood risk scoring, running five independently deployable Cloud Run services: Flood Risk Service (FEMA NFIP zone data), Crime Statistics Service (FBI UCR rates), School Ratings Service (NCES aggregated scores), Walkability Service (OSM-derived pedestrian accessibility), and a Neighborhood Aggregator Service that calls all four in parallel using goroutines, computes a configurable weighted composite score (default: flood 30%, crime 30%, school 25%, walkability 15%), caches results in Redis, and returns partial scores gracefully when individual services are unavailable.
target_output.architecture_overview: Five Go 1.22 Gin services deployed independently to GCP Cloud Run with internal VPC ingress only. Each scoring service maintains its own PostgreSQL schema within a shared Cloud SQL instance (flood, crime, school, walkability schemas) to satisfy data isolation while remaining within the $200/month prototype budget. The Neighborhood Aggregator uses Go context.WithTimeout(2s) per service goroutine; successful responses are collected on a results channel; timed-out services produce nil dimension values in the CompositeScore struct with a partial_result flag. Redis 7 on Cloud Memorystore caches composite scores by geoid with 24-hour TTL. Prometheus metrics exposed per service and scraped by a Grafana Cloud Run instance for prototype latency analysis.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.22. HTTP framework: Gin per service. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on GCP Cloud SQL (single shared instance, four schemas). Cache: Redis 7 on GCP Cloud Memorystore. Hosting: GCP Cloud Run (5 services, internal ingress). CI/CD: GitHub Actions. Observability: Prometheus metrics, Grafana, GCP Cloud Logging. Public data sources: FEMA NFIP GeoJSON, FBI UCR API, NCES data API, OSM Overpass API for walkability.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Flood Risk Service: ingests FEMA NFIP flood zone GeoJSON per census tract, computes normalized flood risk score (AE zone = 85, A zone = 70, X500 = 30, X = 5), exposes GET /score. Crime Statistics Service: ingests FBI UCR violent and property crime rates per county, normalizes against national median, exposes GET /score. School Ratings Service: ingests NCES school-level ratings aggregated to census tract centroid, computes weighted average by enrollment, exposes GET /score. Walkability Service: queries OSM Overpass API for amenity density within 400m radius of coordinates, computes pedestrian accessibility score. Neighborhood Aggregator: parallel goroutine calls to all four, CompositeScoreConfig-weighted combination, Redis cache, partial-result handling.
target_output.system_components: flood-risk-svc: Go Gin service, flood schema in Cloud SQL, FEMA data ingestion job. crime-stats-svc: Go Gin service, crime schema in Cloud SQL, FBI UCR ingestion job. school-ratings-svc: Go Gin service, school schema in Cloud SQL, NCES ingestion job. walkability-svc: Go Gin service, walkability schema in Cloud SQL, OSM Overpass query on demand. neighborhood-aggregator-svc: Go Gin orchestrator, calls four services in parallel, Redis cache client, CompositeScoreConfig. Shared: GCP Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (single instance, four schemas), GCP Cloud Memorystore Redis 7, GCP Cloud Run (5 services, internal ingress), Prometheus metrics per service, Grafana on Cloud Run.
target_output.workflows: Composite Score Request: Client calls GET /composite?lat=37.7749&lon=-122.4194 on neighborhood-aggregator-svc (internal VPC only). Aggregator resolves lat/lon to census tract geoid via a bundled Census tract lookup. Checks Redis cache key composite:{geoid} — on hit returns cached CompositeScore with cached_at timestamp. On miss, creates CompositeScoreResult struct with nullable flood, crime, school, walkability fields. Spawns four goroutines each calling the corresponding service GET /score?lat=&lon= with context.WithTimeout(2 * time.Second). Collects results on a channel; timed-out goroutines write a nil to their field. Applies CompositeScoreConfig weights (flood: 0.30, crime: 0.30, school: 0.25, walkability: 0.15) to available dimensions; if partial_result=true, re-normalizes weights across available dimensions. Stores in Redis with 24h TTL. Returns CompositeScore JSON. Data Ingestion (per scoring service): Separate Cloud Run jobs triggered weekly via Cloud Scheduler — each service has an independent /ingest endpoint that fetches its data source, transforms to schema, upserts into its PostgreSQL schema, and updates data_as_of timestamp.
target_output.data_model: flood schema: flood_zones (geoid VARCHAR, zone_class ENUM AE/A/X500/X, base_flood_elevation NUMERIC, normalized_score NUMERIC(5,2), last_updated TIMESTAMP). crime schema: crime_statistics (geoid VARCHAR, violent_rate NUMERIC, property_rate NUMERIC, normalized_score NUMERIC(5,2), data_year INT). school schema: school_ratings (geoid VARCHAR, avg_rating NUMERIC, school_count INT, normalized_score NUMERIC(5,2), data_year INT). walkability schema: walkability_scores (geoid VARCHAR, walk_score NUMERIC, transit_score NUMERIC, bike_score NUMERIC, normalized_score NUMERIC(5,2), computed_at TIMESTAMP). Aggregator Redis cache: composite:{geoid} -> CompositeScore JSON (TTL 24h). CompositeScoreConfig (in-memory struct): flood_weight float64, crime_weight float64, school_weight float64, walkability_weight float64, loaded from env vars with defaults 0.30/0.30/0.25/0.15.
target_output.api_design: Scoring services (internal VPC only). GET /score?lat={float}&lon={float} -> {score: float, label: string, normalized_score: float, source: string, data_as_of: date, geoid: string}. GET /health -> {status: 'ok', data_staleness_hours: int}. Aggregator. GET /composite?lat={float}&lon={float} -> {geoid: string, composite_score: float | null, flood: float | null, crime: float | null, school: float | null, walkability: float | null, partial_result: bool, available_dimensions: int, cached_at: string | null, sources: {flood: string, crime: string, school: string, walkability: string}}. GET /health -> {status: 'ok', services_reachable: [string]}. Auth: Authorization: Bearer {INTER_SERVICE_JWT} header required on all scoring service calls from aggregator.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Network isolation: all five Cloud Run services configured with ingress=internal-and-cloud-load-balancing within GCP VPC; not accessible from public internet. Inter-service JWT: aggregator generates a short-lived JWT (1-hour expiry) signed with INTER_SERVICE_SECRET environment variable; each scoring service validates JWT signature and expiry on every request. GCP IAM: Cloud Run invoker role granted only to aggregator service account for scoring services. Fair Housing Act consideration: composite score response includes sources attribution object so any downstream consumer can audit which public data sources contributed to a score and at what weight — this transparency is essential given potential use in lending or rental contexts. GCP Cloud Logging captures every composite score request with geoid (not raw lat/lon in logs).
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GCP Cloud Run: five services each deployed from independent GitHub Actions workflows. Each service has its own Dockerfile in its subdirectory. GitHub Actions: go test ./... per service, docker build, gcloud run deploy {service-name} — demonstrates independent deployability. Shared: single Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 instance (db-f1-micro, sufficient for prototype volume) with four schemas created via per-service migration scripts run at deploy time. Cloud Memorystore Redis 7 (1 GB Basic tier). Cloud Scheduler triggers weekly data ingestion for each scoring service. Cost estimate: Cloud Run scale-to-zero (~$10/month at prototype traffic), Cloud SQL db-f1-micro (~$30/month), Cloud Memorystore Redis 1GB Basic (~$50/month), Cloud Scheduler ($0.10/job/month) — total ~$100/month within $200 budget.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics exposed at /metrics per service: score_request_duration_seconds histogram (labels: service, cached), data_staleness_hours gauge (per scoring service), inter_service_timeout_total counter (per upstream service). Grafana dashboard on Cloud Run reads Prometheus: Service Latency (p50/p95 per scoring service), Composite Score Coverage (% partial_result=true over time), Cache Hit Rate per geoid. GCP Cloud Logging captures structured logs: each composite request logged with geoid, available_dimensions, partial_result, total_latency_ms. Research finding: compare score_request_duration_seconds across services to identify which data source introduces most latency.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Cloud SQL db-f1-micro shared instance: ~$30/month. Cloud Memorystore Redis 1GB Basic: ~$50/month. Cloud Run (5 services, scale-to-zero): ~$10-20/month at prototype request volume. Cloud Scheduler (4 ingestion jobs): <$1/month. Total: ~$90-100/month, well within $200/month prototype budget. Scaling consideration for production: Cloud SQL would need separate instances per service at production data volumes; that architectural evolution is a documented finding of this prototype.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Prototype Sprint (4 weeks): Deploy all five services to Cloud Run with internal ingress. Implement data ingestion from FEMA, FBI UCR, NCES, and OSM. Implement aggregator with parallel goroutines, CompositeScoreConfig weighting, partial-result handling, Redis caching. Prometheus metrics per service. Grafana dashboards for latency and coverage analysis. Independent deployment GitHub Actions workflows validating the core hypothesis. Phase 2 Research Analysis (2 weeks post-sprint): Measure score latency per service, partial-result frequency under simulated timeouts, cache hit rates, and ingestion latency per data source. Document findings in ADR. Phase 3 Production Decision: Based on prototype findings, decide whether to proceed with full service isolation (separate Cloud SQL per service) or consolidate into a monorepo with shared schema for production.
target_output.development_guidelines: Each service is a separate Go module in a monorepo subdirectory. No shared Go packages between services — code duplication is acceptable in a prototype to validate that services can be modified independently. CompositeScoreConfig weights loaded from environment variables with defaults, enabling research team to run experiments with different weightings without redeploying. All scoring normalization formulas documented as inline Go comments referencing the source data methodology (e.g., FEMA zone classification thresholds). go test must pass for each service before Cloud Run deploy in CI.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Shared Cloud SQL instance creates a hidden coupling between services — failure of one service migration could affect other schemas. Mitigation: each service uses a separate PostgreSQL user with access only to its own schema; cross-schema queries are prohibited. Research finding to document: this coupling would require separation in production. Risk: OSM Overpass API rate limits during bulk walkability score computation. Mitigation: walkability-svc implements request-level backoff; prototype computes on-demand rather than pre-computing all tracts. Fair Housing Act risk: normalized neighborhood scores could encode discriminatory patterns from historical crime or school data. Mitigation: sources attribution in composite response; research findings must include a bias analysis before any production use in lending/rental context. Tradeoff: single Cloud SQL with four schemas over four separate instances — saves ~$90/month for prototype but documents a production architecture gap.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Composite scoring weights set to flood 30%, crime 30%, school 25%, walkability 15% as default; weights are env-var configurable for research experiments. Partial-result behavior: compute composite from available dimensions with re-normalized weights; return partial_result=true with available_dimensions count. Census tract geoid resolution: bundled SQLite lookup table mapping lat/lon bounding boxes to FIPS census tract GEOIDs.

metadata.notes: Round 2 research prototype revision fixing composite scoring formula and aggregator partial-result handling; weak plan quality reflecting prototype-grade depth.

project_goal.value: A data-driven housing market insights landing page for a proptech startup, presenting dynamically loaded housing market statistics, interactive affordability calculators, and neighborhood ranking charts to attract home buyers and drive media coverage
project_goal.rationale: Primary goal is organic traffic acquisition via SEO and press pickup of market statistics; secondary goal is email capture for a housing market newsletter

target_users.value: First-time home buyers researching affordability in specific metros; journalists and bloggers citing housing market data; real estate agents seeking shareable market insights content
target_users.rationale: Three distinct personas each with different engagement patterns: buyers use the calculator, journalists screenshot ranking charts, agents share links

project_class.rationale: Static site with ISR for data freshness; no persistent backend, no user accounts, no database

capabilities.rationale: Static rendering with client-side calculator logic and Vercel Analytics; no auth, payments, or backend required

complexity_level.rationale: Static site with ISR for market data; interactive affordability calculator is client-side only; no server-side complexity

risk_level.rationale: No personal data collected; market statistics are publicly sourced; email capture via third-party form only

data_sensitivity.rationale: All market statistics are derived from public data sources; no PII collected or stored on site infrastructure

external_exposure.rationale: Marketing site must be publicly indexable for SEO and press coverage

access_model.value: Public, no authentication required; newsletter signup form posts to Mailchimp API via serverless route; no user sessions or cookies beyond analytics consent
access_model.rationale: Lowest friction for anonymous visitors

feature_scope.value: Hero section with top housing market statistics, neighborhood affordability ranking chart (top 50 metros), interactive affordability calculator (income + down payment inputs), median price trend sparklines per metro, report download lead magnet (PDF gated by email), newsletter signup, SEO metadata with structured data markup for rich snippets
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set to maximize organic traffic and email capture across all three target personas

mvp_scope.value: Hero statistics section, top-25 neighborhood ranking chart, affordability calculator with Recharts visualization, email newsletter signup via Mailchimp, ISR revalidation every 24 hours
mvp_scope.rationale: Core features sufficient to launch and attract press pickup; report download is Phase 2

security_baseline.value: Vercel HTTPS enforced; CSP headers via next.config headers(); no user input stored on Vercel infrastructure; Mailchimp API key in Vercel environment variable (not NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefixed)
security_baseline.rationale: Minimal attack surface for a public static site with no sensitive data

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript; Tailwind CSS; Recharts for ranking and trend visualizations; next/font for typography; next/image for metro photography
frontend_stack.rationale: ISR support in Next.js is critical for fresh market statistics without full rebuilds; Recharts is lightweight and accessible

backend_stack.value: Next.js API route for Mailchimp email subscription; no persistent backend server
backend_stack.rationale: Single serverless function handles the only server-side operation (email capture)

data_platform.value: No database; market statistics pre-fetched from housing data API at build time and revalidated every 24 hours via ISR; calculator logic is client-side arithmetic only
data_platform.rationale: ISR eliminates the need for a database while keeping statistics reasonably fresh for a daily-cadence housing market site

hosting_target.value: Vercel Edge Network with ISR (revalidate: 86400); OG image generation via Next.js metadata API for social sharing
hosting_target.rationale: Vercel ISR is native to Next.js App Router; zero-config global CDN for fast international page loads

privacy_retention_policy.value: No personal data retained on Vercel infrastructure; Mailchimp subscriber data governed by Mailchimp DPA; analytics cookies require consent banner per GDPR/CCPA; no server-side logging of visitor IPs
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Even with data_sensitivity=none, cookie consent is legally required for EU visitors using Vercel Analytics

future_scope.value: Gated PDF market report (email capture gate); interactive map widget for hyperlocal neighborhood statistics; comparison tool letting buyers compare two metros side-by-side; Spanish language localization
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 features to increase time-on-site and improve email capture conversion

constraints.value: Market statistics must update at most once per day (data API has daily publication cadence); site must achieve Lighthouse Performance score above 90 on mobile; development timeline 4 weeks with 1 engineer
constraints.rationale: Daily data cadence matches ISR revalidation; mobile performance is critical for first-time buyer demographic using phones

observability_baseline.value: Vercel Analytics for Web Vitals and custom events (calculator_used, chart_interacted, email_signup_submitted); no server-side APM required
observability_baseline.rationale: Product analytics focused on content engagement and conversion funnel, not infrastructure performance

execution_preference.value: Single engineer, trunk-based development; Vercel PR preview deployments for stakeholder review; Lighthouse CI gates on PR merge
execution_preference.rationale: Small team, fast iteration cycle

project.goal: Housing market insights landing page with ISR statistics and interactive affordability calculator
project.target_users: First-time home buyers, journalists, real estate agents

frontend.charts: Recharts for ranking chart and sparklines
frontend.seo: next/metadata with JSON-LD structured data for housing data rich snippets

security.secrets: MAILCHIMP_API_KEY in Vercel env, never NEXT_PUBLIC_

data.market_stats: Housing data API fetched at build time, ISR revalidate 86400 seconds

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: ESLint, TypeScript check, Lighthouse CI
devops.environments: PR preview, production on main merge

reasoner_reviews.product: Three rounds of revision have refined this into a well-structured static marketing page. The ISR revalidation every 24 hours is correctly matched to the housing data API's daily publication cadence. The affordability calculator being fully client-side is the right call — no API latency means instant interaction, which is critical for mobile-first first-time buyers who will abandon slow calculators. The email capture funnel via the newsletter signup and future PDF gate is sensible.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Previous rounds identified two issues that have since been addressed: the ISR revalidation boundary is now correctly placed at the page level rather than individual components, and the Recharts bundle size concern was resolved by dynamic-importing the chart components with next/dynamic and ssr:false. The remaining open question from round 2 — whether to use JSON-LD structured data for rich snippets — should be resolved in this round by implementing HousingDataset schema markup.
reasoner_reviews.security: CSP header configuration is now correct following round 2 fix. The Mailchimp API key is properly server-side only. One remaining concern: the OG image generation route using Next.js metadata API should not make external housing data API calls at request time — ensure OG images use build-time statistics only to avoid request-time failures causing broken social previews.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Lighthouse Performance above 90 on mobile is achievable given the dynamic imports for Recharts and next/image optimization. The 24-hour ISR revalidation is correctly tied to the data API cadence. One remaining risk: if the housing data API is unavailable during ISR revalidation, Vercel will serve the stale cached version — this fallback behavior should be tested explicitly.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js App Router with single root route (/); /api/subscribe route handler for Mailchimp; all chart components wrapped in dynamic imports with ssr:false and Suspense fallbacks to prevent hydration errors and reduce JS bundle on initial page load
frontend.pages_and_flows: HeroStats (top 5 national housing market statistics with animated counters), NeighborhoodRankingChart (Recharts BarChart of top 25 metros by affordability index with click-to-drill interaction), AffordabilityCalculator (income slider, down payment input, interest rate selector — all client-side with instant recalculation), MetroSparklines (12 major metros with price-per-sqft trend sparklines, 24-month rolling), EmailSignup (newsletter CTA with Mailchimp form), Footer (data attribution, methodology link)
frontend.performance_notes: Dynamic import Recharts with next/dynamic and ssr:false; lazy load metro sparkline section below the fold; next/image for metro photography with fill layout and sizes prop; target LCP under 2.5s on mobile 4G; avoid layout shift from chart loading with explicit height reservations via Tailwind aspect-ratio classes
frontend.frontend_risks: Recharts bundle (~65KB gzipped) is largest JS dependency; dynamic import reduces initial bundle impact but charts still require client-side JS — ensure accessible static fallback tables for screen readers and search engine indexing

security.auth_design: No authentication; public site with no user sessions
security.abuse_prevention: Rate limiting on /api/subscribe via Vercel Edge Middleware: 5 submissions per IP per minute to prevent Mailchimp API abuse; Mailchimp double opt-in enabled to prevent list pollution
security.privacy_controls: Cookie consent banner (vanilla-cookieconsent library) required before loading Vercel Analytics; no third-party tracking pixels loaded without consent; privacy policy page with data usage disclosure; data attribution section crediting housing data sources

target_output.title: Housing Market Insights Landing Page Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A data-driven Next.js 14 static marketing site with ISR delivering daily-refreshed housing market statistics, an interactive client-side affordability calculator, and Recharts neighborhood ranking visualizations. Deployed on Vercel Edge Network for global CDN performance. Round 3 adds JSON-LD Dataset schema markup to enable Google rich snippets for housing data, addressing the primary SEO gap from previous revisions. Recharts SSR and CSP issues from rounds 1-2 remain resolved. Email capture routes to Mailchimp via a serverless API handler.
target_output.architecture_overview: Static-first Next.js 14 App Router site with ISR (revalidate: 86400 seconds, matching the housing data API's daily publication cadence). All market statistics are pre-fetched at build time and cached on Vercel Edge; ISR background revalidation refreshes stale pages after 24 hours without user-visible delay. JSON-LD Dataset schema is injected into the page head via Next.js generateMetadata to make housing statistics eligible for Google rich snippet cards. Recharts ranking and sparkline components are dynamically imported with next/dynamic and ssr:false to prevent React hydration mismatches and reduce initial JS bundle. A single Next.js API route at /api/subscribe handles Mailchimp list enrollment with rate limiting via Vercel Edge Middleware. No database, no user sessions, no server-side rendering beyond ISR static regeneration.
target_output.technology_stack: Framework: Next.js 14 with TypeScript. Styling: Tailwind CSS 3.4. Charts: Recharts 2.12 dynamically imported with next/dynamic ssr:false. SEO: next/metadata with JSON-LD Dataset schema via script type='application/ld+json'. Analytics: Vercel Analytics with custom events. Email: Mailchimp Marketing API v3 via /api/subscribe route handler. Fonts: next/font Inter with subset. Images: next/image with WebP/AVIF and fill layout. CI: GitHub Actions with ESLint, TypeScript check, Lighthouse CI. Hosting: Vercel Edge Network with ISR.
target_output.functional_feature_map: HeroStats: five national headline statistics (median home price, YoY price change, months of supply, mortgage rate, affordability index) pre-rendered from ISR data with animated counter reveal on scroll entry. NeighborhoodRankingChart: Recharts BarChart of top 25 metros ranked by affordability index (median income to median home price ratio), dynamically imported; click on a metro bar filters the sparkline section below. AffordabilityCalculator: annual income input, down payment percentage slider, 30-year fixed rate pre-filled from ISR market data; client-side computation of max affordable home price, monthly payment, and debt-to-income ratio; renders Recharts PieChart of payment breakdown. MetroSparklines: 24-month price-per-sqft trend lines for 12 major metros using Recharts LineChart. EmailSignup: Mailchimp newsletter CTA. JSON-LD Dataset markup: Dataset schema in page head describing the affordability index dataset with name, description, creator, dateModified (ISR timestamp), and distribution as CSV download URL.
target_output.system_components: Vercel Edge Network: serves pre-built HTML/CSS/JS with ISR revalidation; housing statistics served from stale cache during background revalidation to prevent data API latency from impacting page load. Next.js ISR: getStaticProps equivalent via fetch with next: {revalidate: 86400} in async Server Component; on-demand revalidation endpoint /api/revalidate available for manual trigger if data API publishes early. Next.js API Route (/api/subscribe): validates request body, checks IP rate limit via Vercel Edge Middleware, calls Mailchimp POST /3.0/lists/{listId}/members with email and tag=housing-insights. Recharts dynamic island: NeighborhoodRankingChart, AffordabilityCalculator, and MetroSparklines loaded as client components only. JSON-LD script block: injected in page head by generateMetadata with type='application/ld+json' containing Dataset schema.
target_output.workflows: Market Statistics Refresh: Vercel ISR background worker fetches housing data API endpoint GET /v1/markets/summary?date=latest 24 hours after last revalidation; response cached on Vercel Edge; page HTML regenerated with fresh statistics; if housing data API returns non-200, Vercel serves previous cached version (stale-while-error behavior) — no user-visible outage. Email Signup Flow: visitor enters email in newsletter form; Vercel Edge Middleware checks IP rate limit (5 submissions per IP per minute); /api/subscribe validates email format, calls Mailchimp Marketing API to upsert contact with tag=housing-insights and status=subscribed (double opt-in email triggered by Mailchimp); returns 200 with success message; Vercel Analytics records email_signup_submitted event with source=hero or source=footer. Affordability Calculator Interaction: user enters annual income and down payment percentage; client-side JavaScript computes max_affordable_price = (monthly_income * 0.28 - monthly_tax - insurance) / (mortgage_rate_factor); Recharts PieChart re-renders with principal, interest, tax, insurance breakdown; Vercel Analytics records calculator_used event with income_bucket and metro.
target_output.data_model: No persistent data model. ISR data shape fetched from housing data API: market_summary {national_stats: {median_price: number, yoy_change_pct: number, months_supply: number, mortgage_rate_30yr: number, affordability_index: number}, metros: [{metro_id: string, name: string, state: string, affordability_rank: number, median_price: number, price_per_sqft: number, monthly_change_pct: number, yoy_change_pct: number, trend_24m: number[]}], published_at: string}. JSON-LD Dataset schema shape: {type: 'Dataset', name: 'US Housing Affordability Index', description: string, url: string, creator: {type: 'Organization', name: string}, dateModified: string, distribution: [{type: 'DataDownload', encodingFormat: 'text/csv', contentUrl: string}]}. Mailchimp contact shape: {email_address: string, status: 'subscribed', tags: [{name: 'housing-insights', status: 'active'}]}.
target_output.api_design: POST /api/subscribe: accepts {email: string}; validates email format with regex; checks IP rate limit via Vercel Edge Middleware; calls Mailchimp POST https://us1.api.mailchimp.com/3.0/lists/{listId}/members; returns 200 {success: true} on Mailchimp 200/400 duplicate, 422 {error: 'invalid_email'} on format failure, 429 {error: 'rate_limited'} on IP limit. POST /api/revalidate: accepts {secret: string} matching REVALIDATION_SECRET env var; calls revalidatePath('/'); returns 200 {revalidated: true}; used for manual trigger if housing data API publishes fresh statistics before the 24-hour ISR window expires. No other server endpoints; all other content is statically generated.
target_output.security_and_compliance: CSP header in next.config.js headers(): default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' (required for JSON-LD script block and next/font); style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' (required for Recharts); img-src 'self' data: images.housing-api.com; connect-src 'self'. HSTS: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains. Mailchimp API key in MAILCHIMP_API_KEY Vercel env variable (server-side only, not NEXT_PUBLIC_). Revalidation secret in REVALIDATION_SECRET Vercel env for on-demand ISR endpoint. Cookie consent banner (vanilla-cookieconsent) blocks Vercel Analytics until visitor accepts; required for GDPR compliance for EU visitors. Data attribution: housing statistics credited to source API in footer and in JSON-LD creator field.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Vercel project linked to GitHub main branch. PR branches generate preview deployments at https://housing-insights-{hash}.vercel.app for stakeholder review. GitHub Actions CI on every PR: ESLint with eslint-config-next, TypeScript tsc --noEmit, Lighthouse CI with mobile preset (Performance > 90, Accessibility > 95, SEO > 90 gates). On merge to main: Vercel production build and deployment in approximately 90 seconds. ISR revalidation is automatic every 24 hours; manual trigger available via POST /api/revalidate with secret token. Incident response: if housing data API is unavailable Vercel serves stale ISR cache; if Mailchimp API is unavailable subscribe endpoint returns 503 with retry-after header. No containers, no managed databases, no infrastructure to operate.
target_output.observability: Vercel Analytics: automatic Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, FID, TTFB) by device type and geography. Custom events: calculator_used (properties: income_bucket [under_50k/50k_100k/100k_200k/over_200k], metro, computed_max_price), chart_interacted (chart_type, metro_clicked), email_signup_submitted (source: hero/footer/calculator), report_download_clicked. Conversion funnel: hero_view -> calculator_used -> email_signup_submitted for optimization. SEO monitoring: Google Search Console connected for rich snippet status and Dataset schema validation. Alert: Vercel deployment failure triggers Slack webhook to #proptech-deploys channel.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost: Vercel Hobby tier supports this traffic profile at launch ($0/month). Housing data API free tier assumed; upgrade path to paid tier documented if API adds usage limits. Mailchimp free tier supports up to 500 contacts and 1,000 email sends per month. Vercel Pro at $20/month provides team access and higher bandwidth limits when paid media drives significant traffic. Total cost: $0 at launch.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Launch (Weeks 1-4): Hero statistics with ISR, top-25 ranking BarChart with dynamic import, affordability calculator, email newsletter signup with Mailchimp, JSON-LD Dataset schema markup, cookie consent banner, GitHub Actions CI with Lighthouse budget gates, Vercel production deployment. Phase 2 - Engagement (Weeks 5-8): metro sparklines section with 24-month trends, PDF market report download with email gate, on-demand ISR revalidation endpoint, social sharing OG images per metro using Next.js metadata API with build-time statistics. Phase 3 - Growth (Weeks 9-12): interactive metro comparison tool (two-metro side-by-side), Spanish localization for Hispanic homebuyer segment, sitemap.xml with all metro pages for SEO, Lighthouse budget enforcement in CI tightened to Performance > 95.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode. All chart components in /components/charts as client components with 'use client' directive. All ISR data fetching in async Server Components at page level; never fetch housing data API from client components. JSON-LD schema defined in /lib/schema.ts with typed Dataset interface. MAILCHIMP_API_KEY and REVALIDATION_SECRET must be present in .env.local (documented in README); CI fails if environment validation check fails. Lighthouse CI budget file at .lighthouserc.json committed to repo and enforced on every PR merge. All images use next/image with explicit width/height or fill with sizes prop to prevent CLS.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: housing data API changes its response schema breaking ISR data fetching; mitigation: Zod schema validation on API response with graceful fallback to stale ISR data and Slack alert on schema mismatch. Risk: Vercel Hobby bandwidth limit triggers throttling during press coverage traffic spike; mitigation: document Vercel Pro upgrade procedure and set spend alert. Tradeoff: style-src 'unsafe-inline' in CSP required for Recharts is a weakened CSP posture; acceptable given no user data on this site, but document as known exception. Tradeoff: no server-side rate limiting beyond Vercel Edge Middleware means a sophisticated attacker could bypass; acceptable for a newsletter signup form with Mailchimp deduplication as backstop.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: JSON-LD Dataset schema selected over ItemList or Product schema as the most semantically correct type for housing market statistics datasets and the most likely to trigger Google rich snippets for data journalists. OG image generation confirmed to use only the pre-fetched build-time market_summary data passed as props to the ImageResponse function, not live API calls, preventing social preview breakage during housing data API downtime. Recharts selected over D3.js for accessibility: Recharts generates SVG with ARIA labels by default, aligning with the Accessibility > 95 Lighthouse CI gate.

metadata.notes: Round 3 housing market insights landing page; fixes ISS-003 JSON-LD Dataset schema markup after two rounds resolving Recharts SSR and CSP issues.

project_goal.rationale: Primary conversion goal: institutional investors request a product demo; secondary goal is media coverage via data storytelling

target_users.value: Institutional real estate investors (REITs, family offices, PE funds) evaluating data-driven market identification tools
target_users.rationale: High-value B2B audience that responds to methodology credibility and case study evidence

capabilities.rationale: Static site with analytics tracking and serverless demo request form

complexity_level.rationale: Static landing page with demo request form as the only interactive element

risk_level.rationale: No user authentication; no PII stored on site infrastructure

feature_scope.rationale: Institutional audience requires methodology credibility and proven performance evidence

mvp_scope.rationale: Core conversion and credibility elements for launch

security_baseline.rationale: High-value institutional demo pipeline must be protected from spam submissions

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: SSG for ML explainer content with ISR for model accuracy statistics that update weekly

backend_stack.rationale: Serverless function handles CRM integration without a dedicated backend service

project.goal: Institutional investor landing page for ML property appreciation prediction platform with demo request funnel
project.target_users: Institutional investors, PE funds, REITs, family offices

security.headers: CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options via CloudFront response headers policy

devops.environments: staging (S3 bucket), production (S3 bucket + CloudFront distribution)

reasoner_reviews.product: After 3 rounds the page structure and signal explainer widgets are well-defined. The remaining gap identified in round 3 is that the ML signal explainer widgets were described as static images — the research team has actual data to power lightweight SVG charts, which would be far more convincing to the institutional audience. Round 4 should specify how model accuracy statistics and signal explainer data are updated.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Next.js 14 SSG with ISR is the right pattern for the model accuracy statistics block — the data changes weekly after model retraining. Revalidation period of 7 days is appropriate. The signal explainer widgets should use static pre-rendered Recharts SVGs, not client-side rendered charts — this preserves Core Web Vitals and avoids heavy JS for what is effectively illustrative data.
reasoner_reviews.security: hCaptcha token validation must happen in the Lambda function server-side before calling HubSpot API — client-side hCaptcha alone is insufficient for protecting the enterprise demo pipeline. The Lambda function must also validate that the submitted firm name and contact email meet basic format requirements before forwarding to HubSpot.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Core Web Vitals green with Next.js 14 SSG and Tailwind JIT is achievable. Plausible analytics is the right cookieless choice — integrates via a simple script tag with no GDPR consent requirement. Lambda cold start is not a concern for the demo form since it is user-initiated action.

backend.service_design: Single AWS Lambda function (TypeScript) deployed via API Gateway: validates hCaptcha token, validates form fields, calls HubSpot Contacts API to create contact and deal in the Sales pipeline
backend.api_patterns: POST /demo-request with {firm_name, contact_name, email, aum_range, use_case, hcaptcha_token}; returns {success: true, reference_id: string} or error
backend.failure_handling: hCaptcha validation failure returns 400; HubSpot duplicate contact returns 200 with {already_registered: true}; HubSpot API error returns 500 with generic message; Lambda timeout configured at 10 seconds

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 SSG with static export to S3. Single page: index.tsx with sections as React components. ISR applied to model_accuracy_stats block via getStaticProps revalidate: 604800 (7 days).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Single-page layout: Hero (value prop + CTA) -> ML Signal Explainers (3 SVG chart widgets) -> Model Track Record (accuracy statistics block, ISR) -> Case Study Cards -> Demo Request Form -> Media Coverage -> Footer
frontend.performance_notes: SSG for all sections; model accuracy block uses ISR with 7-day revalidation; signal explainer SVG charts pre-rendered at build time from JSON data files; no client-side chart library; hCaptcha and Plausible analytics scripts loaded with next/script strategy=lazyOnload

security.auth_design: No authentication on public site
security.abuse_prevention: hCaptcha on demo request form with server-side token validation in Lambda; CloudFront rate limiting via AWS WAF rule: 100 POST requests per IP per 5 minutes to /demo-request
security.privacy_controls: Plausible cookieless analytics — no consent banner required; GDPR consent checkbox on demo form for EU visitors processed via HubSpot legal basis field; privacy policy linked from form

data.entities: product_feature_sections (static), case_study_cards (static JSON), demo_request_form (form island), signal_explainer_widgets (static SVG data from JSON files), model_accuracy_stats (ISR from internal metrics API or static JSON)
data.storage_design: No database; all content in static JSON files in the repo; model accuracy statistics updated via GitHub Actions workflow that fetches from internal metrics endpoint and commits updated JSON weekly
data.analytics_events: Plausible custom events: hero_cta_click, signal_explainer_view, case_study_expand, demo_form_view, demo_form_submit_success, demo_form_submit_error

devops.deployment_topology: S3 bucket (staging), S3 bucket + CloudFront distribution (production). Lambda deployed via AWS SAM. GitHub Actions for CI/CD.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: next build + next export, aws s3 sync dist/ to staging S3, CloudFront invalidation on production deploy. Lambda: tsc compile + aws lambda update-function-code on merge to main.
devops.rollback_strategy: CloudFront points to versioned S3 prefix — rollback by updating CloudFront origin path to previous deploy prefix. Lambda: publish new version on each deploy; rollback by pointing alias to previous version.

ISS-001.title: Signal explainer widgets are static images not data-driven SVGs
ISS-001.detail: Rounds 1-3 described signal explainer widgets as static PNG/JPG images. The research team has actual gentrification signal data that could power lightweight SVG charts, which would be far more credible to institutional investors than images.

previous_audits.summary: Page structure, demo form with server-side hCaptcha, and CRM integration are solid. Two remaining gaps reduce credibility with the institutional investor audience: image-based signal explainers and hardcoded model accuracy statistics.

Next.js SSG with CloudFront is the right architecture for Core Web Vitals targets
HubSpot CRM integration for demo pipeline is well-specified with deal creation

Model accuracy statistics hardcoded in HTML — will become stale as model retraining produces new metrics
