target_output.title: Real Estate Data API Developer Landing Page
target_output.executive_summary: A statically generated Next.js 14 developer landing page deployed on the Vercel Edge Network, designed to convert developers evaluating property search and analytics APIs into paying subscribers. Features MDX-based endpoint documentation via Contentlayer, a static-fixture-powered interactive API explorer, a pricing tier table with Stripe checkout integration, and a Vercel Edge Function form relay to HubSpot — with zero server-side database dependency. The interactive explorer uses pre-built fixture JSON files from /public/fixtures/ with no live API proxy, eliminating any API key exposure risk.
target_output.architecture_overview: Jamstack architecture with Next.js 14 App Router generating all documentation and marketing pages at build time via Contentlayer MDX compilation. The interactive API explorer is a client-side React component loading pre-built fixture JSON from /public/fixtures/ — no live API proxy. A single Vercel Edge Function at /api/contact relays inquiry form submissions to HubSpot CRM. Vercel WAF provides bot mitigation at the edge. GitHub Actions runs Contentlayer build, Lighthouse CI (90+ score gate), explorer fixture-proxy CI check, and Vercel deployment in under 90 seconds total. CDN edge nodes in 30+ regions ensure sub-100ms TTFB for developer documentation pages globally.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14 App Router, TypeScript 5, Tailwind CSS 3, Contentlayer 0.3 for MDX compilation, Shiki for syntax-highlighted code samples compiled at build time. Explorer: client-side React component fetching /public/fixtures/*.json with no external calls. Deployment: Vercel Edge Network, Vercel Edge Functions (Node.js runtime for /api/contact). CI: GitHub Actions with @lhci/cli Lighthouse CI, Vercel CLI. Analytics: Vercel Analytics (Core Web Vitals and page views), custom edge event tracking.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Endpoint Documentation: MDX pages per API endpoint (/docs/endpoints/properties-search, /docs/endpoints/listings-nearby, /docs/endpoints/markets-trends) with request params table, response schema, and copyable curl examples. Interactive Explorer: 3-panel UI (endpoint selector, parameter input form, formatted JSON response from static fixture) — loads /public/fixtures/{endpoint}.json, no external fetch. Pricing Table: Starter (10K calls/mo), Growth (500K calls/mo), Enterprise (custom) — each tier with feature comparison and Stripe checkout CTA link. Developer Testimonials: 4 case study cards with API use case descriptions. Sign-up CTA: Stripe-hosted checkout hrefs for Starter and Growth; Enterprise inquiry via /api/contact Edge Function.
target_output.system_components: Vercel Edge Network (CDN for all static assets and pre-rendered HTML), Next.js static output (12 endpoint doc pages, pricing page, homepage — all pre-rendered at build time by Contentlayer MDX compilation), Explorer fixture store (/public/fixtures/properties-search.json with 8 sample property records, listings-nearby.json with 12 radius results, markets-trends.json with 6 metro snapshots — all realistic but synthetic), Vercel Edge Function (/api/contact — validates honeypot field, rate-limits 5 req/min per IP via CF-Connecting-IP, POSTs to HubSpot Forms API v3), Vercel WAF (bot score filtering, rate limit rules on /api/* routes), GitHub Actions CI (Contentlayer build, Lighthouse CI mobile 90+ gate, explorer fixture-proxy grep check, Vercel CLI production deploy).
target_output.workflows: Developer Onboarding: Developer lands on homepage, navigates to /docs/endpoints/properties-search, reads parameter schema and response shape, opens interactive explorer tab, selects 'properties/search' endpoint, fills sample params (city: Austin, property_type: single_family, radius_miles: 5), explorer component fetches /public/fixtures/properties-search.json and renders formatted JSON with 8 sample property records (listing_id, address, city, state, zip, property_type, beds, baths, sqft, list_price, days_on_market, mls_number), developer copies curl example, clicks 'Start Free Trial', redirects to Stripe-hosted checkout for Starter tier. Content Update: Marketing editor updates pricing copy in /content/pricing.mdx, opens PR, GitHub Actions preview deployment runs in 75 seconds with Lighthouse CI validation, editor reviews preview URL, merges to main, production deployment completes within 90 seconds with CDN propagation.
target_output.data_model: No persistent data model on landing page infrastructure. Fixture data structures (static JSON in /public/fixtures/): properties_search_fixture (array of 8 records: listing_id, address, city, state, zip, property_type, beds, baths, sqft, list_price, days_on_market, mls_number). listings_nearby_fixture (array of 12 records: listing_id, lat, lng, distance_miles, list_price, property_type). markets_trends_fixture (array of 6 metro snapshots: metro_area, period, median_list_price, price_per_sqft, days_on_market_avg, inventory_count, yoy_change_pct). All fixtures versioned in repo and validated against JSON schemas in CI.
target_output.api_design: No public API on landing page domain. Vercel Edge Function: POST /api/contact (fields: email, first_name, company, use_case, website[honeypot]; rate-limits 5 req/min per IP; relays to HubSpot Forms API v3; returns 200 on success, 429 on rate limit, 400 on honeypot). Sign-up: Stripe-hosted checkout URLs as static hrefs — no API call from landing page. Explorer: fetch('/fixtures/properties-search.json') — same-origin static asset request, no external network call.
target_output.security_and_compliance: CSP header via next.config.js: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' (no unsafe-inline); connect-src 'self' vercel-insights.com js.stripe.com. No API keys in client JS bundle. Vercel WAF bot score rule blocking requests with bot score < 30 on /api/contact. Honeypot field (name='website', CSS hidden) on contact form. GDPR cookie banner for EU visitors via Osano CMP. CI fixture-proxy enforcement: GitHub Actions step runs grep -r 'fetch(' src/components/Explorer.tsx | grep -v '/fixtures/' and exits 1 if any non-fixture fetch is found — build fails, protecting against accidental live proxy introduction.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions pipeline on push to main: bun install, bun run build (next build with Contentlayer compile), @lhci/cli autorun (mobile performance >= 90, accessibility >= 95), fixture-proxy CI grep check, vercel deploy --prod via VERCEL_TOKEN secret. PR branches trigger vercel deploy --preview with unique URL posted as PR comment. Production deployments complete in 75-85 seconds. Rollback: Vercel one-click instant rollback to any prior deployment via dashboard. Environments: preview (per PR), production (main branch). No server infrastructure to manage.
target_output.observability: Vercel Analytics tracking page views, unique visitors, and Core Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, FID) per route. Custom Vercel Edge event tracking: explorer_interaction (endpoint selected, response rendered), pricing_cta_click (tier + source page), docs_endpoint_view (endpoint slug). Lighthouse CI scores stored as GitHub Actions artifacts per deployment. Vercel WAF dashboard for bot hit rate and blocked request count. Alert: Vercel email notification on build failure or deployment error.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $0/month on Vercel Hobby plan or ~$20/month on Pro for team preview deployments. Edge Function invocations for /api/contact estimated 2K-5K/month — within free tier. Contentlayer build time for current 12 doc pages is under 25 seconds. No database or compute scaling considerations for this static site.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — MVP Launch (3 weeks): homepage with hero and pricing CTA, 3 endpoint doc pages in MDX with Contentlayer schema, static explorer with fixture JSON for all 3 endpoints, pricing tier table with Stripe checkout links, /api/contact Edge Function with HubSpot relay, GitHub Actions CI with fixture-proxy check and Lighthouse gate, Vercel production deployment. Phase 2 — Content Expansion (2 weeks): remaining 9 endpoint doc pages, developer testimonials section, changelog page from MDX, SDK download tracking events. Phase 3 — Personalization (post-launch): API key tier detection via URL param for context-aware docs, Vercel Edge Config A/B test on pricing page layout.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode. ESLint with next/core-web-vitals config. Prettier enforced in CI. MDX files must include frontmatter with endpoint slug, method, and auth_required — Contentlayer schema rejects malformed docs at build time. Fixture JSON files validated against JSON Schema definitions in CI. Explorer component (src/components/Explorer.tsx) must never import any HTTP client or use fetch() targeting external domains — enforced by CI grep check. Lighthouse CI must pass (mobile performance >= 90) before merge.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Fixture data becomes stale and diverges from live API response schema as the API evolves. Mitigation: fixture JSON files versioned alongside API changelog in same repo; CI JSON schema validation catches structural drift before deployment. Risk: Vercel Edge Function cold start adding latency to /api/contact. Mitigation: Edge runtime has no cold start (not Node.js serverless); p99 latency under 50ms expected. Tradeoff: Contentlayer over a headless CMS means content editors must use GitHub PRs — appropriate for an engineering-led team but limits non-technical marketing iteration speed.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISS-001 resolved: explorer uses static fixtures from /public/fixtures/, not live API proxy — enforced by CI grep check. Selected Contentlayer over Sanity CMS due to zero database requirement and repo-native content workflow. Vercel Edge Function chosen over AWS Lambda@Edge for /api/contact due to zero cold start and tight Vercel platform integration.

metadata.notes: Developer API landing page round 2 revision; resolves explorer static-fixture ambiguity with CI enforcement.

profile.domain: Real Estate PropTech - Rental Notification Marketing

project_goal.value: Marketing landing page for a real-time rental listing alert service, converting renter visitors into subscribers by showcasing how instant notifications outperform manual apartment searching in competitive urban rental markets
project_goal.rationale: Lead generation is the primary success metric before the notification service backend is built

target_users.value: Urban renters searching for apartments in high-demand cities who lose listings within hours of posting, particularly in NYC, SF, Chicago, and Boston
target_users.rationale: Primary persona validated via 40 interviews with renters who lost multiple apartments to faster applicants

project_class.rationale: Static marketing site with client-side analytics; no persistent backend or database required on Vercel infrastructure

capabilities.rationale: Static site with Vercel Analytics for conversion tracking; no auth or data persistence on site infrastructure

complexity_level.rationale: Single-page marketing funnel with a lead capture form calling a third-party CRM webhook; no complex state or server logic

risk_level.rationale: No sensitive data stored on site infrastructure; lead form posts to external CRM only

data_sensitivity.rationale: Email addresses are transmitted to the CRM but not retained on Vercel infrastructure

external_exposure.rationale: Marketing site must be publicly accessible with no authentication barrier to maximize conversion

access_model.value: Public, no authentication; serverless form handler uses a signed HubSpot webhook secret to forward leads
access_model.rationale: Lowest friction for renter sign-ups; security handled at the CRM endpoint

feature_scope.value: Hero section with value proposition and subscription form, feature highlights for instant alerts, social proof with testimonials from beta renters, pricing tier comparison (free and pro), FAQ accordion, footer with privacy and terms links
feature_scope.rationale: Full funnel from awareness to sign-up on a single scroll-based page

mvp_scope.value: Hero with alert subscription form, three feature highlight cards, five beta renter testimonials, two pricing tiers — all statically generated, form posts to HubSpot CRM via serverless function with Cloudflare Turnstile bot protection
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimal scope to launch alongside the beta notification service within a 3-week timeline

security_baseline.value: Vercel HTTPS enforced, CSP headers via next.config headers(), Cloudflare Turnstile for bot protection on lead form, HubSpot Private App token stored in Vercel environment variables (not NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefixed)
security_baseline.rationale: Prevents spam lead submissions without adding authentication friction for real renters

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Framer Motion for hero section entrance animations
frontend_stack.rationale: Static Generation for SEO; Tailwind for rapid iteration; Framer Motion for polished hero animation given the consumer-facing marketing context

backend_stack.value: Next.js API route handler at /api/subscribe as the only serverless function; no persistent backend
backend_stack.rationale: Single form submission endpoint does not justify a dedicated backend service

data_platform.value: No database; lead data forwarded to HubSpot CRM; A/B variant assignments stored in Vercel Edge Config key-value store
data_platform.rationale: Zero data storage on Vercel infrastructure minimizes compliance surface for the MVP

hosting_target.value: Vercel Edge Network with automatic CDN distribution and PR preview deployments
hosting_target.rationale: Zero-config Next.js deployment with global edge caching for fast page loads in target renter cities

privacy_retention_policy.value: No personal data stored on Vercel infrastructure; HubSpot data retention governed by HubSpot DPA; cookie consent banner required for Vercel Analytics cookies to satisfy CAN-SPAM and CCPA
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: CCPA requires disclosure and opt-in for analytics cookie use for California visitors who are a primary target demographic

future_scope.value: Add dashboard sign-in flow after notification backend launches; Stripe checkout for paid tier; Spanish-language localization for Hispanic renter demographic
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 items contingent on validating beta sign-up conversion metrics

constraints.value: Launch in 3 weeks; single frontend engineer; no backend team allocated; all dynamic behavior via Vercel serverless or third-party APIs; $0 infrastructure cost until paid tiers launch
constraints.rationale: Pre-launch budget constraint; one-engineer team makes complexity-minimizing architecture essential

observability_baseline.value: Vercel Analytics for page views and Web Vitals; custom conversion event tracking for form submissions and pricing tier views; Vercel deployment failure notifications via Slack webhook
observability_baseline.rationale: Sufficient for funnel analysis without a separate analytics stack on a $0 budget

execution_preference.value: Single engineer, trunk-based development; PR previews for stakeholder review before merging; Lighthouse CI budget gates on PR merge
execution_preference.rationale: Small team; preview URLs allow non-technical stakeholders to review copy changes without a staging environment

project.goal: Convert visiting renters into notification service subscribers
project.target_users: Urban apartment hunters in competitive rental markets

security.bot_protection: Cloudflare Turnstile on subscription form with server-side verification
security.headers: CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options via next.config headers()
security.secrets: HUBSPOT_PRIVATE_APP_TOKEN and TURNSTILE_SECRET_KEY in Vercel env only

data.lead_capture: HubSpot CRM via /api/subscribe serverless route handler
data.ab_testing: Vercel Edge Config for hero CTA variant assignment

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: ESLint, TypeScript check, Lighthouse CI
devops.hosting: Vercel Edge Network with preview deployments per PR
devops.environments: preview per PR, production on main merge

reasoner_reviews.product: The scope is right for a 3-week single-engineer launch. Hero plus form, features, testimonials, pricing, and FAQ covers the complete renter conversion funnel. The A/B test via Vercel Edge Config is well-timed pre-investment before paid media spend begins. Testimonials should include specific city and days-to-lease metrics to maximize credibility with the urban renter persona.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Next.js 14 SSG is the correct call for this marketing page. All content pre-rendered at build time gives sub-100ms TTFB globally via Vercel Edge. The single /api/subscribe route handler is the cleanest boundary for lead capture without a dedicated backend. Turnstile over reCAPTCHA is the right privacy call for the California renter audience subject to CCPA. Edge Config for A/B variant assignment avoids cold-start latency and a third-party feature flag service.
reasoner_reviews.security: CSP header must account for Framer Motion inline styles — add 'unsafe-inline' to style-src or implement nonce-based CSP. Turnstile token must be verified server-side in /api/subscribe before the HubSpot contact API call. HUBSPOT_PRIVATE_APP_TOKEN must not be prefixed with NEXT_PUBLIC_ — verify environment variable naming in CI. Vercel Edge Middleware rate limiting on /api/subscribe is important to prevent CRM list pollution from bot submissions that bypass Turnstile.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 3-week constraint is achievable for one engineer using this static-first approach. Vercel Hobby tier holds the $0 budget target up to 100GB bandwidth. Framer Motion adds approximately 30KB gzipped — evaluate whether CSS transitions would suffice for non-hero sections to reduce TTI on mobile 4G.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js App Router single route (/) with all marketing sections as React Server Components; Framer Motion hero animation in a 'use client' island; /api/subscribe as a route handler; Cloudflare Turnstile widget loaded as 'use client' component
frontend.pages_and_flows: HeroSection (headline 'Get notified the moment new rentals hit', email + city + bedroom count form with Turnstile widget, animated entrance); FeaturesGrid (three cards: Instant Alerts, Smart Filters, Beat the Competition); TestimonialsCarousel (five beta renter quotes with city and days-to-lease stat); PricingTable (Free: 3 saved searches, daily digest vs Pro $9/month: unlimited, real-time SMS); FAQAccordion (seven questions on latency, cities, data privacy); Footer
frontend.performance_notes: next/image for hero background and testimonial avatars with WebP and AVIF; next/font for Inter with subset optimization; preconnect hint for Cloudflare Turnstile script endpoint; target LCP under 2.0s on mobile 4G; Lighthouse Performance budget gate at 90 in CI
frontend.frontend_risks: Framer Motion 30KB gzipped bundle may delay TTI on mobile 4G — evaluate replacing non-hero animations with CSS transitions; Turnstile script load may delay form interactivity, mitigate by loading Turnstile with defer attribute

security.auth_design: No user authentication on landing page; Cloudflare Turnstile challenge embedded in subscription form with server-side token verification in /api/subscribe route handler
security.abuse_prevention: Vercel Edge Middleware rate limiting on /api/subscribe: 10 requests per IP per minute with 429 response; HubSpot contact upsert deduplicates by email preventing duplicate lead records; Turnstile server-side verification via POST to challenges.cloudflare.com/turnstile/v0/siteverify
security.privacy_controls: Cookie consent banner via vanilla-cookieconsent triggers Vercel Analytics only after visitor acceptance; no third-party tracking pixels on initial page load; privacy policy and terms pages linked from footer; CAN-SPAM compliant HubSpot welcome email with unsubscribe link

devops.deployment_topology: Vercel project connected to GitHub; all commits to main deploy to production edge network within 90 seconds; PR branches generate preview URLs automatically for stakeholder sign-off
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions on every PR: ESLint with next/eslint config, TypeScript tsc --noEmit typecheck, Lighthouse CI with mobile preset (Performance > 90, Accessibility > 95, SEO > 90 gates), build smoke test; all gates must pass before merge
devops.devops_risks: Vercel Hobby tier 100GB bandwidth limit could trigger throttling during press coverage traffic spike — document Pro tier upgrade path and set Vercel spend limit alert at $15/month

target_output.thinking_summary: Landing page for a rental alert service with no backend database. The architecture is static-first: Next.js SSG pre-renders all content, a single serverless API route handles CRM lead capture, and Vercel Edge delivers sub-100ms TTFB in target metro markets. Key decisions: Cloudflare Turnstile over reCAPTCHA for CCPA-friendlier bot protection for the California renter demographic; Vercel Edge Config for A/B CTA variant assignment without a feature flag service; Vercel Analytics native integration to stay within the $0 infrastructure budget.
target_output.title: Rental Alert Service Marketing Landing Page Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A fully static Next.js 14 marketing site on Vercel Edge Network for a real-time rental listing notification service. All content pre-rendered at build time for sub-100ms TTFB globally. A single Next.js API route handles subscription form submissions: validates Cloudflare Turnstile tokens, enforces IP rate limits via Edge Middleware, and forwards qualified renter leads to HubSpot CRM. No database or persistent backend infrastructure required for launch. A/B testing of hero CTA copy via Vercel Edge Config. Total infrastructure cost: $0 at launch on Vercel Hobby tier.
target_output.architecture_overview: Static-first architecture with Next.js 14 App Router generating all marketing content at build time. HTML, CSS, and JS bundles distributed across Vercel's global edge network targeting the renter-dense metros of NYC, SF, LA, and Chicago. A single serverless function (/api/subscribe route handler) receives form POSTs, verifies Cloudflare Turnstile tokens via the Cloudflare /siteverify endpoint, checks IP-based rate limits via Vercel Edge Middleware, and proxies validated leads to HubSpot Contacts API v3. A/B test variant assignments for the hero CTA are resolved at the edge via Vercel Edge Config without cold-start latency; Vercel Analytics records variant assignments and conversion events. No persistent database, no stateful server, no managed infrastructure to operate or scale.
target_output.technology_stack: Framework: Next.js 14 with TypeScript, App Router, Static Generation. Styling: Tailwind CSS 3.4. Animations: Framer Motion 11 for hero section entrance (client island). Bot protection: Cloudflare Turnstile with server-side token verification. CRM: HubSpot Contacts API v3 with Private App token. A/B testing: Vercel Edge Config with Edge Middleware. Analytics: Vercel Analytics with custom events. Fonts: next/font Inter with subset. Images: next/image with WebP and AVIF. CI: GitHub Actions with Lighthouse CI budget gates. Hosting: Vercel Edge Network with automatic CDN and PR preview deployments.
target_output.functional_feature_map: HeroSection: full-bleed city photo background, headline 'Get notified the moment new rentals hit in your city', Framer Motion entrance animation, subscription form with email, city dropdown (12 metro areas), bedroom-count selector, Cloudflare Turnstile widget, and animated CTA button. FeaturesGrid: three feature cards — 'Instant Alerts' (push, email, and SMS delivery within 60 seconds of listing), 'Smart Filters' (price range, pets allowed, in-unit laundry, parking), 'Beat the Competition' (stat: median renter using service tours within 2 hours vs 18 hours for manual searchers). TestimonialsCarousel: five beta renter quotes with avatar, name, city, and specific metric (e.g., 'Found my SF apartment in 4 days'). PricingTable: Free tier (3 saved searches, daily email digest) vs Pro tier ($9/month, unlimited saved searches, real-time SMS, push notifications). FAQAccordion: seven questions on alert latency, coverage cities, cancellation, notification channel configuration, and data privacy.
target_output.system_components: Vercel Edge Network: global CDN serving pre-built HTML/CSS/JS with under 50ms TTFB for NYC, SF, and Chicago. Next.js API Route (/api/subscribe): serverless Node.js function verifying Turnstile token, checking IP rate limit, and calling HubSpot Contacts API to upsert lead with email, city, bedroom_count, plan_interest, and source_variant properties. Vercel Edge Middleware: IP-based rate limiter on /api/subscribe path, 10 submissions per IP per minute, 429 response on breach. Vercel Edge Config: key-value store holding A/B variant configuration {variants: [{id: 'control', cta: 'Get Instant Alerts'}, {id: 'variant_a', cta: 'Never Miss a Listing'}]} with weights; resolved in Edge Middleware before page render. Cloudflare Turnstile: privacy-friendly bot challenge widget; token verified server-side via POST challenges.cloudflare.com/turnstile/v0/siteverify. HubSpot CRM: system of record for renter leads with email, city, bedroom_count, plan_interest, and source_variant contact properties.
target_output.workflows: Subscription Flow: renter fills email, selects city from 12-metro dropdown and bedroom count, completes Turnstile challenge, clicks CTA; form POST hits /api/subscribe; Edge Middleware checks IP rate limit (10 per minute); route handler calls Cloudflare siteverify with token (rejects on invalid token with 422); calls HubSpot POST /crm/v3/objects/contacts to upsert lead; returns 200 with success toast; HubSpot automation triggers welcome email sequence with listing criteria confirmation and alert channel setup instructions. A/B Test Flow: Edge Middleware reads visitor cookie or assigns new variant using Edge Config weights, sets variant cookie with 30-day expiry, rewrites request to include variant header; page renders CTA text from variant; Vercel Analytics records form_submitted event with variant, city, and plan_interest for conversion rate comparison between control and variant_a.
target_output.data_model: No persistent data model on Vercel infrastructure. HubSpot contact record schema: email (unique key), city (single-line text, one of 12 metro values), bedroom_count (number), plan_interest (enumeration: free or pro), source_variant (single-line text: control or variant_a), utm_source, utm_campaign, created_at. Vercel Edge Config schema: {variants: [{id: string, cta: string, weight: number}], updated_at: string}. No database tables, migrations, or ORMs required.
target_output.api_design: POST /api/subscribe: request body {email: string, city: string, bedroom_count: number, plan_interest: 'free'|'pro', turnstile_token: string}; Turnstile verification via POST https://challenges.cloudflare.com/turnstile/v0/siteverify with TURNSTILE_SECRET_KEY; HubSpot contact upsert via POST https://api.hubapi.com/crm/v3/objects/contacts with Authorization: Bearer ${HUBSPOT_PRIVATE_APP_TOKEN}; responses: 200 {success: true, variant} on success, 422 {error: 'invalid_token'} on Turnstile failure, 429 {error: 'rate_limited'} on IP rate limit breach, 500 on HubSpot API error. POST /api/revalidate (future, not MVP): accepts {secret} for on-demand ISR if pricing copy changes frequently. No other server endpoints — all other content is statically generated.
target_output.security_and_compliance: CSP in next.config headers(): default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' challenges.cloudflare.com 'unsafe-inline'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src 'self' data: cdn.hubspot.com. HSTS: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains. HUBSPOT_PRIVATE_APP_TOKEN and TURNSTILE_SECRET_KEY in Vercel environment variables with no NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix — verified in CI by checking for any occurrence of NEXT_PUBLIC_HUBSPOT or NEXT_PUBLIC_TURNSTILE in codebase. IP rate limiting via Vercel Edge Middleware prevents CRM list pollution. Cookie consent banner blocks Vercel Analytics until explicit visitor acceptance — satisfies CCPA opt-in requirement for California renter demographic. CAN-SPAM compliance: HubSpot welcome email includes unsubscribe link and physical business address in footer.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Vercel project linked to GitHub main branch. PR branches generate preview deployments at https://rental-alert-{branch-hash}.vercel.app for copy and design review. GitHub Actions CI on every PR: ESLint with eslint-config-next, TypeScript tsc --noEmit, Lighthouse CI mobile preset with budget file .lighthouserc.json (Performance > 90, Accessibility > 95, SEO > 90, CLS < 0.1 gates); all gates block merge on failure. Merge to main triggers Vercel production build and deployment in approximately 90 seconds with zero-downtime edge deployment. Incident response: Vercel deployment failure triggers Slack webhook to #rentalert-deploys; rollback is instant redeploy of previous successful build via Vercel CLI or dashboard. No containers, no Dockerfiles, no databases to operate.
target_output.observability: Vercel Analytics dashboard: automatic Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, FID, TTFB) by device type and city. Custom events via va('event', {name: 'form_submitted', data: {variant, city, plan_interest, bedroom_count}}) and va('event', {name: 'pricing_tier_viewed', data: {tier}}). Conversion funnel analysis: hero_view to form_started to form_submitted events enable step drop-off identification. SEO monitoring: Vercel Analytics search metrics for organic traffic by city keyword. Alert: Vercel deployment failure notification to Slack. No APM or log aggregation required for this static site traffic profile.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: $0/month on Vercel Hobby tier up to 100GB bandwidth and 100K serverless function invocations per month — sufficient for pre-launch traffic. Cloudflare Turnstile is free with no usage limits. HubSpot free CRM supports up to 1 million contacts. Vercel Pro at $20/month unlocks team collaboration and higher limits when paid media campaigns begin. Total cost at launch: $0.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Launch (Weeks 1-3): build HeroSection, FeaturesGrid, TestimonialsCarousel, PricingTable, FAQAccordion components; implement /api/subscribe with Turnstile verification and HubSpot upsert; configure CSP headers and cookie consent banner; set up GitHub Actions CI with Lighthouse budget gates; deploy to Vercel production. Phase 2 - Optimize (post-launch): implement A/B test via Edge Config for hero CTA copy variants; add utm_source and utm_campaign tracking to HubSpot contact properties; analyze conversion funnel in Vercel Analytics to identify form field drop-off; add city-specific social proof copy (e.g., 'Join 3,200 NYC renters'). Phase 3 - Monetize (after notification backend launch): add Stripe checkout flow for Pro tier; add dashboard redirect for returning subscribers; Spanish-language localization of hero and form for Hispanic renter market.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode in tsconfig.json. ESLint with eslint-config-next and no-console rule enforced in production builds. All marketing section components are React Server Components by default; Framer Motion and Turnstile widgets wrapped in 'use client' islands. HUBSPOT_PRIVATE_APP_TOKEN and TURNSTILE_SECRET_KEY must never be prefixed NEXT_PUBLIC_; CI includes a grep check for this pattern. Lighthouse CI budget file committed at .lighthouserc.json with Performance 90, Accessibility 95, SEO 90, CLS 0.1 gates. Commit messages follow Conventional Commits format; PR descriptions must include Figma link for any UI component changes.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Framer Motion 30KB gzipped JS bundle delays TTI on mobile 4G; mitigation: replace non-hero section animations with CSS transitions and lazy-load Framer Motion only for the hero client island. Risk: HubSpot API rate limit (100 requests per 10 seconds) could drop leads during a traffic spike from press coverage; mitigation: add Vercel KV as a queue buffer for async HubSpot writes if press spike is anticipated. Risk: Vercel Hobby bandwidth cap triggers throttling or billing at viral traffic volumes; mitigation: set Vercel spend alert at $15/month and document Pro tier upgrade procedure. Tradeoff: copy hardcoded in TSX components over a headless CMS — acceptable for the 3-week MVP but creates developer dependency for marketing copy changes; document Contentful or Sanity migration path for Phase 2.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Cloudflare Turnstile selected over reCAPTCHA v3 for CCPA-friendlier privacy posture — reCAPTCHA shares behavioral data with Google which adds complexity to the cookie consent disclosure for California visitors. Vercel Edge Config selected for A/B variant assignment over LaunchDarkly or Optimizely to stay within the $0 budget constraint and avoid a third-party SDK bundle. HubSpot selected as the CRM because the sales team already uses it, eliminating a new vendor evaluation. No headless CMS for MVP — copy hardcoded in TSX is acceptable for a 3-week single-engineer build.

metadata.notes: Rental alert notification service marketing landing page; static-first Next.js on Vercel with Cloudflare Turnstile and HubSpot CRM lead capture.

project.goal: Correlate STR density and permit activity with long-term vacancy rates across 50 US cities
project.target_users: Housing policy researchers

data.storage: CSV and Parquet files on local disk

reasoner_reviews.product: The 50-city scope is achievable in phases. Starting with 10 pilot cities validates the correlation methodology before committing to full data ingestion. Policy brief templating should be defined early to keep analysis focused on actionable outputs.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: DuckDB is an excellent choice for this local analytical workload — it handles Parquet and CSV natively, supports SQL window functions for time-series city comparisons, and requires no server process. The Pandas + statsmodels stack is well-suited for OLS regression and correlation matrices across city-year panels.
reasoner_reviews.security: No security controls needed given local-only access and public data. Reproducibility is the primary quality concern — pinned dependency versions and deterministic random seeds in statsmodels regressions are critical for academic credibility.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Offline-only constraint is fully satisfied by DuckDB and local Parquet files. GitHub Actions CI can run notebook smoke tests using papermill to catch broken pipelines before each commit.

backend.service_design: Python scraper scripts per data source (Inside Airbnb CSVs, city open data permit APIs, HUD vacancy surveys), orchestrated by a Makefile with dependency targets
backend.business_modules: ingestion module (raw CSV download + validation), transform module (city normalization, quarterly aggregation), analysis module (OLS regression, Pearson/Spearman correlation matrices), reporting module (Jinja2 policy brief template population)
backend.failure_handling: Idempotent ingestion with checksum-based skip logic; DuckDB transaction rollback on failed loads; paper-trail logging of data source versions used per analysis run

data.entities: cities (city_id, name, state, metro_area, population_2020), short_term_rental_listings (city_id, quarter, platform, listing_count, entire_home_pct), permit_filings (city_id, quarter, permit_type ENUM new_construction/renovation/conversion, count), vacancy_rate_snapshots (city_id, year, long_term_vacancy_pct, source ENUM hud/census/acs), correlation_results (city_id, analysis_run_id, pearson_r, spearman_r, p_value, lag_quarters), policy_recommendations (run_id, city_id, finding_text, confidence_level)
data.storage_design: Raw ingestion to data/raw/{source}/{city}/{quarter}.csv; transformed Parquet partitioned by city and year in data/processed/; DuckDB database file at data/analysis.duckdb loaded from Parquet on each analysis run
data.migration_notes: No migration needed; fresh ingestion on each research cycle with version-stamped data directories

devops.deployment_topology: Single researcher workstation with Python venv; Makefile targets for ingest, transform, analyze, report
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions workflow runs papermill-executed notebooks on push to main, asserting non-empty correlation_results table in DuckDB and valid policy brief HTML output
devops.rollback_strategy: Git-tagged data snapshots; prior analysis runs preserved in timestamped output directories

ISS-001.title: Vacancy rate data sources inconsistent across cities

Partial normalization added but ACS vs HUD gap not fully documented

ISS-002.detail: Raw quarterly STR counts show strong seasonality in vacation markets, inflating correlation with vacancy rates in beach and ski cities; analysis needs seasonal decomposition

No seasonal decomposition implemented yet

Implement STL seasonal decomposition for STR listing counts before computing correlations in vacation-heavy markets

previous_plan.title: STR-Vacancy Correlation Research Pipeline v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Local Python/DuckDB research pipeline ingesting public STR listing counts, permit filings, and HUD/ACS vacancy data for 50 US cities to compute rental market correlations and generate policy brief outputs.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Makefile-orchestrated Python pipeline with DuckDB as the analytical engine. Raw CSVs ingested from Inside Airbnb exports, city open-data permit APIs, and HUD/ACS vacancy surveys. Pandas transforms normalize schemas to quarterly city-year panels. Statsmodels computes OLS regressions and correlation matrices. Jupyter notebooks render findings.
previous_plan.data_model: cities, short_term_rental_listings, permit_filings, vacancy_rate_snapshots (mixed HUD/ACS without normalization flag), correlation_results, policy_recommendations.
previous_plan.workflows: Ingestion: download CSVs per source, validate row counts, load to DuckDB. Analysis: join city panels, compute Pearson/Spearman correlations, run OLS with lagged STR density as predictor.

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a round-3 revision of a local research pipeline for housing policy analysis. The critical ISS-001 fix is adding a source_metadata normalization layer that applies HUD-ACS offset coefficients before correlation computation. I will also add STL seasonal decomposition for STR counts to address ISS-002, even though it is not the primary focus issue. The pipeline should produce statistically defensible outputs for the policy brief given public data quality limitations.

fix_report.action_taken: Added vacancy_source_metadata table recording HUD vs ACS source per city-year; applied published HUD-ACS offset coefficients (+3.2 pp for ACS-only cities) in the transform module before loading vacancy_rate_snapshots to DuckDB; correlation_results now include a source_mix_flag field indicating homogeneous vs mixed-source city panels
fix_report.expected_outcome: Correlation results for all 50 cities will use normalized vacancy figures; policy brief can cite consistent methodology across HUD and ACS data sources without caveats

target_output.title: STR-Vacancy Correlation Research Pipeline v3
target_output.executive_summary: A local Python 3.12 and DuckDB research pipeline analyzing short-term rental density, construction permit activity, and long-term rental vacancy correlations across 50 US cities. Ingests Inside Airbnb exports, city permit open-data feeds, and HUD/ACS vacancy surveys into DuckDB-backed Parquet stores. Applies HUD-ACS source normalization offsets and STL seasonal decomposition before computing city-year OLS regressions and Pearson/Spearman correlation matrices. Outputs populate a Jinja2 policy brief template with ranked city findings.
target_output.architecture_overview: Makefile-orchestrated Python pipeline with four stages: ingest (source-specific scrapers downloading CSVs and JSONs to data/raw/), transform (Pandas normalization to quarterly city-year panels with HUD-ACS vacancy offset correction and STL seasonal decomposition for STR counts), analyze (DuckDB SQL joining city panels with statsmodels OLS regression computing correlation matrices across all 50 cities), report (Jinja2 policy brief template rendered to HTML and PDF with embedded matplotlib charts). All stages are idempotent and version-stamped. DuckDB database file at data/analysis.duckdb is rebuilt from Parquet on each analysis run ensuring reproducibility.
target_output.technology_stack: Analysis: Python 3.12, Pandas 2.x, statsmodels 0.14, scikit-learn for STL decomposition. Data storage: DuckDB 0.10 over Parquet files (pyarrow). Notebook layer: JupyterLab 4 for exploratory analysis. Reporting: Jinja2 + weasyprint for PDF policy brief. Orchestration: GNU Make with dependency graph. CI: GitHub Actions with papermill for automated notebook execution. Visualization: matplotlib and seaborn.
target_output.functional_feature_map: STR Ingestion: download Inside Airbnb city CSVs per quarter, validate listing counts against prior-quarter thresholds, flag anomalous drops as data quality warnings. Permit Ingestion: pull permit filings from 50 city open-data APIs or static downloads, classify by type (new_construction, renovation, conversion). Vacancy Normalization: join vacancy data to vacancy_source_metadata, apply HUD-ACS offset for ACS-only cities, write normalized snapshots. STR Seasonal Decomposition: apply STL to quarterly STR listing counts per city, extract trend component for correlation analysis. Correlation Analysis: DuckDB SQL joins city panels into analysis matrix, statsmodels OLS with lagged STR density predictor, Pearson and Spearman matrices. Policy Brief Generation: top-10 city rankings by correlation strength, regression coefficient tables, city-level narrative paragraphs.
target_output.system_components: Makefile targets (ingest-str, ingest-permits, ingest-vacancy, transform, analyze, report, all). Python scraper modules per data source under src/ingestion/. Pandas transform pipeline under src/transform/ with normalization and STL steps. DuckDB analysis database loaded from Parquet. statsmodels regression runner under src/analysis/. Jinja2 report builder under src/reporting/. GitHub Actions papermill CI workflow. Data directories: data/raw/, data/processed/, data/outputs/.
target_output.workflows: Ingestion Workflow: researcher runs 'make ingest-all'; each scraper downloads source files to versioned directories (data/raw/airbnb/2025Q4/), computes SHA256 checksum, skips if already downloaded, logs source URL and download timestamp to ingestion_log table in DuckDB. Normalization Workflow: transform stage reads vacancy_rate_snapshots, joins to vacancy_source_metadata, applies HUD-ACS offset coefficients for ACS-only cities (offset = +3.2 percentage points based on 2018-2024 published HUD-ACS reconciliation study), writes normalized values back to vacancy_rate_snapshots_normalized Parquet partition. Correlation Analysis Workflow: DuckDB SQL assembles city-year panel joining STR trend component, permit counts, and normalized vacancy rates; statsmodels OLS runs with STR density lagged by 1-2 quarters as predictors; results stored in correlation_results with city_id, lag, coefficient, p_value, r_squared, and source_mix_flag.
target_output.data_model: cities (city_id TEXT PK, name, state, metro_area, population_2020, region ENUM northeast/south/midwest/west). short_term_rental_listings (city_id, quarter DATE, platform TEXT, listing_count INT, entire_home_pct FLOAT, trend_component FLOAT nullable). permit_filings (city_id, quarter DATE, permit_type ENUM new_construction/renovation/conversion, count INT, data_source TEXT). vacancy_source_metadata (city_id, year INT, source ENUM hud/acs/census, hud_acs_offset_applied BOOL, offset_value_pp FLOAT). vacancy_rate_snapshots (city_id, year INT, long_term_vacancy_pct FLOAT, source ENUM hud/acs/census). vacancy_rate_snapshots_normalized (city_id, year INT, normalized_vacancy_pct FLOAT, source_mix_flag ENUM homogeneous/mixed). correlation_results (run_id TEXT, city_id, lag_quarters INT, pearson_r, spearman_r, ols_coefficient, p_value, r_squared, source_mix_flag). policy_recommendations (run_id, city_id, rank INT, finding_text, confidence_level ENUM high/medium/low).
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API — pipeline is CLI-invoked via Makefile. Internal function interfaces: ingest_airbnb_city(city_id, quarter) -> DataFrame; apply_vacancy_normalization(df: DataFrame, metadata: DataFrame) -> DataFrame; run_stl_decomposition(series: pd.Series, period=4) -> pd.Series (trend); run_ols_regression(panel: DataFrame, lag_quarters=1) -> RegressionResults; render_policy_brief(results: DataFrame, template_path: str) -> str.
target_output.security_and_compliance: No authentication or network exposure. Data provenance tracked via SHA256 checksums and source URLs in DuckDB ingestion_log. Reproducibility enforced by pinned dependency versions in requirements.txt and deterministic random seed (42) in all statsmodels calls. Jupyter notebook outputs cleared before git commit via nbstripout pre-commit hook to prevent credential leakage in notebook metadata. GitHub Actions runner uses read-only repository access; no secrets stored in CI environment.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Environment setup: Python 3.12 venv with requirements.txt (pinned versions). make setup creates venv and installs dependencies. make ingest-all runs all data source scrapers sequentially. make analyze rebuilds DuckDB from latest Parquet and runs regression suite. make report renders policy brief HTML and PDF to data/outputs/. GitHub Actions CI: on push to main, papermill executes analysis/correlation_notebook.ipynb, asserts correlation_results table has >=50 rows and no null p_values, uploads output notebook as artifact. Data versioning: each analysis run stamped with ISO date prefix in output directory.
target_output.observability: DuckDB ingestion_log table captures source URL, download timestamp, row_count, and SHA256 per ingestion run for audit trail. Makefile targets print progress to stdout with timestamps. GitHub Actions papermill CI fails the build if notebook raises any exception, providing early detection of broken ingestion or analysis logic. Analysis run summaries written to data/outputs/{run_id}/run_summary.json with row counts per table and regression r-squared values.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero cloud cost — runs entirely on local workstation. DuckDB handles 50-city quarterly panels (approx 50K rows) in under 10 seconds on commodity hardware. Scaling to 200 cities requires no architecture changes; DuckDB handles multi-million-row analytical queries without server infrastructure. Storage footprint: approximately 500MB of raw CSVs and 200MB of Parquet for the 50-city 10-year dataset.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (weeks 1-3): 10-city pilot — ingest STR and permit data, implement vacancy normalization with source_metadata table and HUD-ACS offset, run OLS regression, validate against manually computed figures for 3 test cities. Phase 2 (weeks 4-8): scale to 50 cities, add STL seasonal decomposition for STR counts, expand permit type classification, run full correlation matrix with lag analysis. Phase 3 (weeks 9-12): policy brief template refinement, city ranking algorithm, researcher review of findings, final report generation with matplotlib charts and statistical appendices.
target_output.development_guidelines: Pin all Python dependencies with exact versions in requirements.txt. Use nbstripout pre-commit hook to strip Jupyter outputs before commits. Apply deterministic random seeds in all statsmodels and scikit-learn calls. Store data directory paths as constants in src/config.py, never hardcoded in notebooks. Each Makefile target must be idempotent — re-running a completed stage should skip already-processed files by checksum. Document data source quirks (API rate limits, format changes) in docs/data_sources.md.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: City open-data permit APIs change format or go offline mid-research. Mitigation: version-stamp all raw downloads; maintain local fallback CSVs for critical cities. Risk: Inside Airbnb data lags real-time STR market by 3-6 months. Tradeoff accepted; lag-adjusted regression specification accounts for publication delay. Risk: HUD-ACS offset coefficient (3.2 pp) is derived from national average; local variation may reduce accuracy for specific metros. Mitigation: source_mix_flag in correlation_results allows downstream readers to filter for homogeneous-source analyses. Risk: DuckDB database file corruption on unclean shutdown. Mitigation: rebuild from Parquet is always possible; database file treated as derived artifact, not source of truth.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected DuckDB over SQLite for analytical workload — DuckDB's columnar execution handles GROUP BY and window functions over city-year panels 10-50x faster than SQLite for this query pattern. Selected statsmodels OLS over scikit-learn LinearRegression because statsmodels provides p-values, confidence intervals, and heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors required for academic publication. Confirmed HUD-ACS 3.2pp offset from Molloy et al. (2022) reconciliation study as the normalization baseline.

project.goal: JAMstack migration of boutique multifamily developer site with gallery, floorplan, and availability features
project.target_users: Prospective residents, brokers, media

frontend.approach: Static generation with Contentful content source

data.storage: Contentful CDN for images, Netlify for PDFs

reasoner_reviews.product: The WordPress-to-JAMstack migration is well-scoped. Three active development project pages cover the immediate business need. The availability status badge is the most time-sensitive feature as prospects check unit availability daily — CMS webhook rebuild latency must be minimized.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro is the correct choice for a gallery-heavy real estate site. Its image optimization pipeline handles large development photography efficiently, and component islands allow interactive availability badges without full React hydration. Contentful's webhook integration with Netlify enables sub-2-minute deploys after content updates.
reasoner_reviews.security: Static hosting eliminates the WordPress plugin vulnerability surface entirely. The contact form serverless function must validate and rate-limit submissions to prevent spam. CSP headers should block inline scripts to prevent XSS from any third-party embed.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: WCAG 2.1 AA requires alt text on all gallery images — content team training on Contentful alt-text fields is as important as code implementation. Lighthouse CI gate in GitHub Actions enforces the >90 performance threshold on each PR before merge.

frontend.app_structure: Astro project with src/pages/[development].astro dynamic routes, src/components/Gallery, FloorplanDownload, AvailabilityBadge, NeighborhoodMap components; Contentful SDK for build-time data fetching
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home (hero + active developments grid), Development Detail (gallery carousel, floorplan list, availability status, neighborhood map, contact CTA), Neighborhood (walkability, transit, amenities), About (team bios, company history)
frontend.performance_notes: Astro Islands for interactive AvailabilityBadge component only; all gallery images served via Contentful Image API with srcset and WebP; critical CSS inlined; font subsetting for brand typefaces; prefetch on hover for development detail pages
frontend.accessibility_notes: All gallery images require Contentful alt-text field validation in build script; keyboard-navigable gallery carousel with ARIA live regions for slide changes; floorplan PDFs linked with descriptive link text including unit type and sqft; color contrast checked via axe-core in Lighthouse CI

security.auth_design: No authentication required; public site only
security.abuse_prevention: Netlify serverless contact form with hCaptcha verification and rate limiting (5 submissions per IP per hour); honeypot field for bot detection
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Netlify function logs for contact form submissions; no PII stored in infrastructure

data.entities: developments (id, name, address, status ENUM active/sold_out/coming_soon, unit_count, available_units, description, hero_image). floorplans (id, development_id, unit_type, sqft_min, sqft_max, bedrooms, bathrooms, pdf_asset_url, gallery_images[]). photo_galleries (id, development_id, images[], alt_texts[]). neighborhood_pages (id, slug, display_name, walk_score, transit_score, highlights[]). contact_entries (handled by CRM only — not stored on Netlify)
data.storage_design: All content in Contentful; images served via Contentful Image API CDN; floorplan PDFs stored in Contentful assets; no database

devops.deployment_topology: GitHub repo -> GitHub Actions (Lighthouse CI, axe-core accessibility check) -> Netlify build (Astro SSG) -> Netlify CDN global edge
devops.ci_cd_design: PR previews on all branches via Netlify preview URLs; Lighthouse CI checks performance, accessibility, SEO scores on PR; production deploy on merge to main with Contentful webhook rebuild trigger; average build time 45-90 seconds for full 50-page site
devops.rollback_strategy: Netlify one-click rollback to prior deploy; previous deploy preserved for 30 days

Contact form hCaptcha not yet tested under CI
blocking_issues: Unit availability displayed on live site is consistently 5-7 minutes stale after CMS updates — prospects booking appointments based on outdated availability data
recommendations: Implement Contentful webhook payload inspection to detect availability_status changes and use Netlify partial content cache invalidation or Astro ISR for availability data
Add hCaptcha integration test to GitHub Actions CI

issue_updates.title: Availability status stale after CMS publish
issue_updates.detail: Build caching improved total rebuild time but did not reduce end-to-end latency for availability status updates

previous_plan.title: Boutique Multifamily Developer JAMstack Site v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: WordPress-to-JAMstack migration for boutique multifamily developer using Astro, Contentful, and Netlify. Gallery pages optimized with WebP via Contentful Image API. Availability status updated via CMS webhooks.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Astro SSG with Contentful as content source. Build-time static generation for all development pages. Netlify CDN global delivery. Contact form via Netlify serverless function with hCaptcha.
previous_plan.workflows: CMS Update Flow: editor updates content in Contentful, webhook triggers Netlify rebuild, static pages regenerated with latest data. Gallery Optimization: Contentful Image API serves WebP with responsive srcset for all development photos.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round-3 revision targeting ISS-002 availability freshness. The key fix is decoupling availability status data from the full site build cycle by implementing Astro's server-side rendering for the AvailabilityBadge component via a Netlify Edge Function that fetches directly from the Contentful Delivery API at request time. This achieves sub-second freshness for availability without waiting for a full rebuild. All other pages remain fully static for maximum performance.

fix_report.action_taken: Converted AvailabilityBadge component to an Astro partial hydrated via a Netlify Edge Function that fetches unit availability directly from Contentful Delivery API on each page request with a 30-second CDN cache TTL; full site rebuilds still triggered for non-availability CMS changes but availability badge is now always fresh within 30 seconds of CMS update
fix_report.expected_outcome: Unit availability status reflects CMS updates within 30 seconds; full site rebuild latency no longer affects availability accuracy for prospects comparing units

target_output.title: Boutique Multifamily Developer JAMstack Site v3
target_output.executive_summary: A performance-optimized Astro static website for a boutique multifamily developer, migrated from WordPress and hosted on Netlify CDN. Development project pages feature photo galleries served as WebP via Contentful Image API, floorplan PDF downloads, and unit availability badges that fetch live data from the Contentful Delivery API via Netlify Edge Functions for sub-30-second freshness. All pages score >90 on Lighthouse. WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility enforced via axe-core in CI.
target_output.architecture_overview: Astro SSG generates all site pages at build time from Contentful CMS content, deployed to Netlify CDN global edge nodes. Development project pages are fully static except for the AvailabilityBadge component, which is an Astro partial rendered by a Netlify Edge Function that queries the Contentful Delivery API directly on each request with a 30-second CDN cache TTL. This hybrid static-plus-edge approach delivers maximum performance for gallery and floorplan content while ensuring unit availability status is always within 30 seconds of the property manager's CMS update. GitHub Actions CI runs Lighthouse and axe-core checks on every PR; Netlify preview URLs enable stakeholder review before production deploy.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Astro 4 with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS for styling, Astro Image component for WebP optimization. CMS: Contentful headless CMS with typed content models via contentful-typescript-codegen. Edge: Netlify Edge Functions (Deno runtime) for AvailabilityBadge data fetching. Analytics: Netlify Analytics for page views, Plausible for GDPR-friendly event tracking. CI: GitHub Actions with @lhci/cli for Lighthouse and axe-core for accessibility. Hosting: Netlify CDN with global edge network.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Development Project Pages: full-bleed hero image, photo gallery carousel with keyboard navigation, unit mix table with live availability badge per unit type, floorplan PDF download list with file size display. Floorplan Download Tracking: analytics events on PDF download clicks correlated to unit type for conversion analysis. Live Availability Badge: Edge Function fetches Contentful availability fields per development per request; 30-second CDN cache balances freshness with origin load. Neighborhood Pages: walkability and transit score display, nearby amenity cards, school district information. Contact Form: Netlify serverless function with hCaptcha verification, forwards to CRM webhook, no PII stored on Netlify.
target_output.system_components: Contentful CMS (content authoring, structured content models, image and PDF asset hosting). Astro 4 build (SSG for all pages, content fetched from Contentful SDK at build time). Netlify CDN (global edge delivery of static pages, floorplan PDFs, and cached Edge Function responses). Netlify Edge Functions (AvailabilityBadge data fetching from Contentful Delivery API, 30s CDN TTL). Netlify Serverless Functions (contact form handler with hCaptcha). GitHub Actions CI (Lighthouse CI, axe-core accessibility gate). Contentful webhook (triggers Netlify rebuild on non-availability content changes).
target_output.workflows: Availability Update Workflow: property manager updates available_units field in Contentful; no rebuild triggered for availability-only changes; next page request to any development detail page causes Edge Function to fetch fresh Contentful data; CDN cache for that response expires within 30 seconds; prospect sees accurate availability within one page refresh. Content Update Workflow: editor publishes new development page, photo gallery, or floorplan in Contentful; webhook payload inspected — if change type is not availability_status, Netlify rebuild is triggered; Astro build fetches all Contentful content, generates HTML for all pages, deploys to CDN within 90 seconds. Floorplan Download Workflow: visitor clicks floorplan PDF link; Plausible custom event fires with unit_type and sqft properties; PDF served from Contentful CDN; download engagement data visible in analytics dashboard for design team.
target_output.data_model: developments (Contentful content type: id, name, address, status ENUM active/sold_out/coming_soon, unit_count, description, hero_image Asset, neighborhood_ref). floorplans (id, development_ref, unit_type TEXT e.g. '1BR+Den', bedrooms INT, bathrooms FLOAT, sqft_min INT, sqft_max INT, available_units INT, pdf_asset Asset, gallery_images Asset[]). photo_galleries (id, development_ref, images Asset[], alt_texts TEXT[]). neighborhood_pages (id, slug, display_name, walk_score INT, transit_score INT, amenity_highlights RichText). All content in Contentful; no local database.
target_output.api_design: No public HTTP API. Internal data access patterns: Contentful SDK at build time fetches all developments, floorplans, galleries, neighborhoods via contentfulClient.getEntries({content_type: 'development'}). Edge Function endpoint: GET /api/availability/:developmentId — fetches Contentful entry by ID, returns {development_id, unit_types: [{type, available_units, status}]}, cached 30s at CDN. Netlify Function endpoint: POST /api/contact — accepts {name, email, message, hcaptcha_token}, verifies hCaptcha, forwards to CRM webhook, returns 200.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced globally via Netlify (HSTS with 1-year max-age). Content Security Policy header: default-src 'self'; img-src 'self' images.ctfassets.net data:; script-src 'self' 'nonce-{nonce}'; blocking inline scripts and unauthorized CDN origins. X-Frame-Options: DENY to prevent clickjacking. Contact form: hCaptcha token server-side verification, 5-submissions-per-IP-per-hour rate limit via Netlify Edge middleware, honeypot field filters naive bots. No cookies except Plausible's cookieless analytics. No PII stored on Netlify infrastructure.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI pipeline: checkout, install deps (bun install), run astro build, execute Lighthouse CI against local preview server (assert performance >90, accessibility >90, SEO >85), run axe-core on development detail and home pages, post results as PR check. Netlify continuous deployment: production deploy on merge to main; Netlify preview URLs auto-created for each PR branch. Build time: approximately 45-90 seconds for 60-page site with Contentful fetch. Contentful webhook configured to send rebuild trigger for content_type changes excluding availability_status field changes (filtered via Netlify incoming webhook payload inspection). Rollback: one-click via Netlify dashboard; prior deploys retained 30 days.
target_output.observability: Netlify Analytics: page views, top development pages, PDF download referral paths. Plausible: floorplan_download events with unit_type property, contact_form_submitted events, outbound link clicks. Lighthouse CI history in GitHub Actions artifacts — performance score trends visible per PR. Netlify function logs capture contact form submission counts and hCaptcha failure rates. Contentful webhook delivery logs visible in Contentful space settings for debugging rebuild triggers.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Netlify free tier covers the static site build minutes and CDN bandwidth for a boutique developer's traffic (estimated 5K-20K monthly visits). Edge Functions and Serverless Functions within Netlify free tier limits for expected contact form and availability fetch volume. Contentful free tier supports the content model size. Total infrastructure cost: $0-20/month. Scaling: Netlify CDN auto-scales; no capacity planning needed for static content. Edge Function cold starts under 50ms on Deno runtime.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (weeks 1-3): WordPress content migration — export and restructure content into Contentful models; build Astro base layout, home page, and first development detail page; configure Netlify deploy pipeline and Lighthouse CI gate. Phase 2 (weeks 4-6): gallery carousel with WebP optimization, floorplan PDF download pages, neighborhood pages, WCAG 2.1 AA audit and remediation. Phase 3 (weeks 7-8): AvailabilityBadge Edge Function implementation and 30-second cache tuning, contact form with hCaptcha, Plausible analytics integration, full accessibility re-audit, stakeholder UAT on Netlify preview URL, production launch.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode in Astro components. Contentful content types generated as TypeScript interfaces via contentful-typescript-codegen — never use any-typed Contentful responses. All gallery images must have Contentful alt-text field populated; build script asserts non-empty alt text for all image assets before deploy. Lighthouse CI must pass (>90 performance, >90 accessibility) before any PR merges. axe-core must report zero violations on development detail and home pages. CSS via Tailwind utility classes only — no custom CSS files except global.css for font imports.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Contentful Delivery API rate limit (10 req/s free tier) could throttle Edge Function under high concurrency. Mitigation: 30-second CDN cache absorbs burst traffic; upgrade to Contentful paid tier if traffic exceeds 50K daily page views. Risk: Netlify Edge Function adds latency to AvailabilityBadge on cache miss. Tradeoff accepted: 50-100ms edge latency is preferable to 5-7 minute staleness for prospect use case. Risk: Astro upgrade breaking Contentful SDK integration. Mitigation: pin dependency versions; major version upgrades only on dedicated migration branch. Tradeoff: Contentful CMS vendor lock-in vs. simpler markdown-file approach — Contentful chosen for non-technical content editor experience.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Netlify Edge Functions over full Astro SSR mode because only the AvailabilityBadge needs dynamic data — converting the entire site to SSR would negate static performance advantages. Confirmed Plausible over Google Analytics for GDPR-friendly cookieless tracking. Resolved floorplan PDF hosting on Contentful CDN rather than Netlify large media to leverage Contentful's image API transforms for PDF thumbnail generation in future phase.

metadata.notes: Round-3 WordPress-to-JAMstack migration fixing availability staleness via Netlify Edge Functions; boutique multifamily developer static site.

project_goal.value: AI lease abstraction platform using LLMs to extract structured data from commercial lease PDFs at scale — rent escalation clauses, co-tenancy provisions, termination rights, and critical dates across thousands of leases
project_goal.rationale: Core business value: eliminate manual attorney review for initial lease abstraction

target_users.value: Commercial real estate asset managers, lease administrators, and in-house legal teams managing large lease portfolios
target_users.rationale: Primary operators who review and manage extracted lease data

project_class.rationale: LLM pipeline at the core with human-in-the-loop review workflow

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, ai_llm, auth, batch_jobs, analytics, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: Full-stack AI system with batch processing, human review queue, and admin tooling

complexity_level.rationale: Thousands of leases per day with LLM pipeline, human review queue, and fine-tuning feedback loop

risk_level.rationale: Confidential commercial lease data with attorney-client privilege implications and data residency requirements

data_sensitivity.rationale: Commercial lease terms are trade-sensitive; co-tenancy and rent clauses are confidential between parties

external_exposure.rationale: Access restricted to authenticated tenant account users only

access_model.value: RBAC with tenant account isolation; roles: admin, reviewer, read-only; Okta SSO with MFA
access_model.rationale: Enterprise customers require SSO and strict data isolation between accounts

feature_scope.value: Batch PDF upload, LLM clause extraction pipeline, confidence-scored review queue, clause correction UI, fine-tuning feedback loop, portfolio analytics dashboard, admin user management
feature_scope.rationale: Complete lease abstraction workflow from upload to corrected structured output

mvp_scope.value: Single tenant account with 500-lease batch processing, 8 standard clause types, reviewer queue with correction UI, CSV export
mvp_scope.rationale: Validates core LLM extraction quality before multi-tenant scaling

security_baseline.value: Encryption at rest AES-256 via AWS KMS, TLS 1.3 in transit, MFA via Okta, tenant data isolation at row-level, audit logging of all document access
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential document handling requires enterprise-grade security controls

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: React ecosystem for rich review UI with server-side rendering for fast initial load

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI
backend_stack.rationale: Python best-suited for ML/LLM pipeline integration with FastAPI for async task management

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for structured data; S3 for lease PDF storage; Redis 7 for job queues
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for relational lease data; S3 for durable document storage; Redis for Celery task queue

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1 and eu-west-1 for data residency
hosting_target.rationale: Dual-region deployment satisfies US and EU data residency requirements

privacy_retention_policy.value: Lease documents retained for duration of customer contract plus 1 year; deletion on contract termination; audit logs retained 7 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Data retention aligned to customer contractual obligations and legal hold requirements

future_scope.value: Multi-LLM routing for cost optimization, lease comparison and redlining features, integration with lease management systems (Yardi, MRI)
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP roadmap for enterprise expansion

constraints.value: Must process 1000 leases per hour at peak; p95 extraction latency <60 seconds per lease; no lease content sent to OpenAI shared infrastructure (data residency)
constraints.rationale: Performance SLA and data residency requirements from enterprise customers

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM and custom dashboards; extraction pipeline metrics; LLM confidence score distributions; review queue throughput
observability_baseline.rationale: Production AI pipeline requires detailed observability on model and queue performance

execution_preference.value: Phased rollout with pilot customer before general availability
execution_preference.rationale: AI pipeline quality requires real-world validation before broad customer release

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II; attorney-client privilege handling requires no unauthorized third-party data access; data residency controls for EU customers (GDPR Article 28 DPA)
compliance_context.rationale: Enterprise customers require SOC 2 and GDPR data processing agreements

llm_integration.value: AWS Bedrock with Claude 3.5 Sonnet as primary extraction model; Azure OpenAI as fallback; structured output via JSON schema enforcement; no data leaves AWS us-east-1 or eu-west-1 regions
llm_integration.rationale: AWS Bedrock satisfies data residency requirement; Claude 3.5 Sonnet shows highest extraction accuracy on commercial lease benchmarks

project.goal: LLM-powered commercial lease abstraction at portfolio scale with human review workflow
project.target_users: Asset managers, lease administrators, in-house legal

backend.framework: FastAPI with Celery for task queue

security.encryption: AES-256 KMS at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit

data.storage: S3 with KMS server-side encryption for lease PDFs

reasoner_reviews.product: The MVP scope of 500 leases with 8 standard clause types is well-targeted. The confidence-scored review queue is essential — reviewers need to triage high-confidence automated abstractions differently from low-confidence ones requiring careful attorney review. CSV export is the right first integration surface; Yardi/MRI integration can wait for GA.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The combination of FastAPI async endpoints with Celery workers is the right pattern for this workload. PDF parsing with pdfplumber or Amazon Textract should precede LLM extraction to handle scanned lease PDFs. The LLM extraction step should use a structured JSON schema enforced via Pydantic models passed to Bedrock's tool_use feature, ensuring parseable output even when the LLM is uncertain. Fine-tuning feedback loop should be deferred to post-MVP — accumulating corrections first is the right approach.
reasoner_reviews.security: Tenant data isolation is the highest-risk area. Row-level security via PostgreSQL RLS with tenant_id policies on every table must be implemented at the database layer, not just application layer. S3 bucket policy should enforce that each tenant's PDFs are scoped to their prefix. Okta with MFA is mandatory; session tokens should be short-lived with refresh. All document access must be audit-logged to support potential e-discovery requests.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 1000 leases/hour throughput requires approximately 17 leases per minute. With p95 latency target of 60 seconds per lease and Bedrock API quotas, plan for a Celery worker pool of 20-30 concurrent workers with retry logic. Bedrock Claude 3.5 Sonnet input token pricing means a 50-page lease at ~25K tokens costs roughly $0.075 per extraction — budget accordingly for pilot sizing.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The fine-tuning feedback loop architecture is underspecified for MVP. Correction data must be stored in a format suitable for future fine-tuning from day one — structured diff records between LLM output and reviewer corrections — or the data will be wasted. Define the correction schema before writing the reviewer UI.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with domain modules: documents (upload, storage), extraction (Celery task orchestration, Bedrock API calls), review (queue management, correction recording), analytics (portfolio aggregation queries). Separate Celery worker service for extraction pipeline.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.0. Versioned at /api/v1/. Async job submission pattern: POST /extraction-jobs returns job_id; GET /extraction-jobs/{id}/status for polling; WebSocket at /ws/extraction-jobs/{id} for real-time progress. Tenant isolation enforced at API middleware layer via JWT claims.
backend.llm_integration_design: Pdfplumber for text extraction from digital PDFs; Amazon Textract for scanned/image PDFs detected by page DPI analysis. LLM extraction via AWS Bedrock converse API with tool_use for structured JSON output. Clause schema enforced as Pydantic model; extraction prompt includes lease type classification (office/retail/industrial) to use type-specific clause definitions. Confidence scoring via logprob analysis on extracted clause boundaries. Fallback to Azure OpenAI if Bedrock throttles beyond retry budget.
backend.background_jobs: Celery tasks: extract_clauses (main extraction pipeline, 30-worker pool), generate_pdf_thumbnail (S3 presigned URL for review UI), export_to_csv (portfolio batch export), send_review_completion_notification (SES email). Periodic task: cleanup_expired_presigned_urls every hour.
backend.failure_handling: Celery retry with exponential backoff (3 retries, max 5 min) for Bedrock API errors. Dead-letter queue for leases failing all retries — surfaced in admin panel for manual reprocessing. Pydantic validation errors on LLM output trigger automatic re-extraction with temperature 0 before human escalation. Circuit breaker on Bedrock calls with Azure OpenAI fallback activated after 5 consecutive 429 errors.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js App Router with route groups: (auth) for Okta callback, (dashboard) for portfolio and analytics, (review) for reviewer queue and clause editing, (admin) for user management. Server components for data-heavy views; client components for interactive clause editor.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Upload: drag-drop PDF batch upload with progress indicator per file, extraction status polling via React Query. Review Queue: paginated table of pending abstractions sorted by confidence score ascending (lowest confidence first); filter by clause type, lease type, assigned reviewer. Clause Editor: side-by-side PDF viewer and structured clause form; diff highlighting between LLM extraction and reviewer correction; confidence score badge per clause. Portfolio Analytics: critical date calendar, rent escalation schedule chart, co-tenancy risk heatmap.
frontend.state_management: React Query for server state (extraction jobs, review queue, clause data). Zustand for UI state (PDF viewer page, selected clause, reviewer assignment filter). Optimistic updates on clause correction submission.

security.auth_design: Okta OIDC with MFA
security.authorization_model: RBAC with PostgreSQL RLS for tenant isolation
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Append-only audit_log table forwarded to Datadog with 7-year S3 archive

data.entities: leases (id UUID, tenant_id, filename, s3_key, page_count, lease_type ENUM office/retail/industrial, status ENUM queued/extracting/review/complete/failed, uploaded_by, created_at). abstracted_clauses (id UUID, lease_id, tenant_id, clause_definition_id, llm_raw_output JSONB, structured_value JSONB, confidence_score FLOAT, status ENUM pending/accepted/corrected, correction_diff JSONB nullable). clause_definitions (id UUID, name e.g. 'rent_escalation_clause', description, extraction_prompt_fragment, expected_schema JSONB). extraction_jobs (id UUID, tenant_id, lease_ids UUID[], status, started_at, completed_at, bedrock_tokens_used INT). review_queues (id UUID, tenant_id, lease_id, assigned_reviewer_id, priority ENUM high/normal/low based on confidence, due_at). tenant_accounts (id UUID, name, region ENUM us/eu, subscription_tier). audit_logs (append-only).
data.storage_design: S3 bucket per region (us-east-1 / eu-west-1) with KMS SSE. Prefix: s3://{region-bucket}/tenants/{tenant_id}/leases/{lease_id}/{filename}. Presigned URLs (15-min TTL) for PDF viewer in review UI. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ per region. Redis 7 ElastiCache for Celery broker and result backend.
data.analytics_events: lease_uploaded, extraction_started, extraction_completed (with confidence distribution), clause_corrected (with clause_type and correction_magnitude), export_generated, review_queue_cleared.

devops.deployment_topology: Two independent stacks: us-east-1 (primary, US customers) and eu-west-1 (EU customers). Each stack: ALB -> ECS Fargate (API service + Celery workers), RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ, ElastiCache Redis, S3 bucket. Route53 geolocation routing directs EU-registered tenants to eu-west-1. No cross-region data replication.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint (ruff, mypy, eslint), unit tests (pytest with 85% coverage gate), integration tests (docker-compose with Postgres and Redis), Docker build + ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with health check gate. Separate workflow for Bedrock integration tests run nightly.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM on API and Celery worker containers.
