Custom metrics: extraction_job_duration_p95, bedrock_tokens_per_lease, confidence_score_distribution, review_queue_depth, correction_rate_by_clause_type. PagerDuty on: extraction queue depth >500, p95 latency >120s, Bedrock error rate >5%.
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS service rolling update with automatic rollback on failed health checks. Blue-green deploy for major releases. Database migrations via Alembic with rollback SQL files.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a high-scale AI lease abstraction platform with LLM extraction via AWS Bedrock and human-in-the-loop review. Key decisions: Bedrock Claude 3.5 Sonnet with tool_use for structured clause extraction enforced by Pydantic schemas; Celery worker pool scaled to 30 workers for 1000-lease/hour throughput; PostgreSQL RLS for tenant isolation; dual-region deployment (us-east-1/eu-west-1) for data residency. The correction_diff JSONB schema for reviewer feedback is designed for future fine-tuning dataset construction from day one.
target_output.title: AI Lease Abstraction Platform Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A high-scale AI system for commercial lease abstraction using AWS Bedrock Claude 3.5 Sonnet to extract structured clause data from thousands of PDFs, delivered as a Next.js 14 + FastAPI platform hosted on ECS Fargate in dual AWS regions. Handles rent escalation clauses, co-tenancy provisions, termination rights, and critical dates with confidence-scored outputs routed to a human reviewer queue. Tenant data is isolated via PostgreSQL RLS, encrypted with KMS, and never leaves the customer's assigned AWS region.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier architecture with Next.js 14 frontend communicating over HTTPS to a FastAPI REST API on ECS Fargate behind an ALB. Extraction pipeline runs as a separate Celery worker fleet drawing tasks from Redis, calling AWS Bedrock in the same region as the API service. Lease PDFs are stored in KMS-encrypted S3 with tenant-scoped prefixes. PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ stores all structured data with row-level security policies enforcing tenant isolation on every table. Two fully independent stacks in us-east-1 (US customers) and eu-west-1 (EU customers) with Route53 geolocation routing — no cross-region data movement. Okta SSO with MFA enforced for all users. Correction data from reviewer workflow stored in structured JSONB diffs for future fine-tuning dataset construction.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand, NextAuth.js (Okta OIDC). Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, Celery 5, pdfplumber, Amazon Textract SDK, AWS Bedrock SDK, Pydantic v2. LLM: AWS Bedrock Claude 3.5 Sonnet (primary), Azure OpenAI gpt-4o (fallback). Data: PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ with RLS, Redis 7 ElastiCache (Celery broker), S3 with KMS SSE. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB with WAF, Route53, ECR, Secrets Manager, CloudTrail. Observability: Datadog APM with custom extraction metrics, PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Batch Upload: drag-drop upload of 1-500 PDFs per batch, file validation (PDF format, <100MB per file), per-file extraction status polling. Extraction Pipeline: pdfplumber text extraction for digital PDFs; Textract OCR for scanned documents detected by page pixel density; Bedrock tool_use call with lease-type-specific clause schema; confidence scoring from logprob analysis. Review Queue: sorted by confidence score ascending; assignable to named reviewers; due-date tracking for SLA compliance. Clause Editor: side-by-side PDF page viewer (presigned URL) with structured clause form; diff view of LLM output vs reviewer edits; correction stored as JSONB diff. Portfolio Analytics: critical date calendar with 180/90/30-day alert windows; rent escalation schedule aggregation across portfolio; co-tenancy clause risk exposure summary. Admin Panel: user provisioning, role assignment, extraction job history, Bedrock token consumption reporting.
target_output.system_components: ALB with WAF rules (SQL injection, rate limiting per IP). ECS Fargate API Service (FastAPI, 2-8 tasks, CPU autoscaling). ECS Fargate Celery Workers (30-task pool for extraction, 5-task pool for exports and notifications). PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ RDS (primary + replica for read queries). ElastiCache Redis 7 (Celery broker and result backend). S3 buckets (us-east-1: lease-abstractions-us, eu-west-1: lease-abstractions-eu) with KMS SSE and bucket policies restricting access to same-region ECS task roles. AWS Bedrock (Claude 3.5 Sonnet, same-region API calls only). AWS Secrets Manager (database, Bedrock, Okta, Azure OpenAI credentials). Datadog Agent sidecar on all ECS tasks.
target_output.workflows: Lease Extraction Workflow: user uploads batch of PDFs via Next.js frontend; POST /api/v1/extraction-jobs creates job record in PostgreSQL; per-file Celery tasks enqueued to Redis; worker pulls task, downloads PDF from S3, runs pdfplumber (or Textract if scanned), constructs extraction prompt with clause_definitions for detected lease_type, calls Bedrock tool_use with Pydantic clause schema, receives structured JSON, computes confidence score, writes abstracted_clauses rows, updates extraction_jobs status; frontend polls GET /extraction-jobs/{id}/status or receives WebSocket push on completion. Reviewer Correction Workflow: reviewer opens queue sorted by confidence ascending; selects lease, review_queue record status set to in_progress; PDF viewer displays presigned URL; reviewer edits clause fields in form; diff computed between llm_raw_output and reviewer_input; correction_diff JSONB stored in abstracted_clauses; clause status updated to corrected; review_queue marked complete; portfolio analytics updated. Fine-Tuning Data Collection: monthly Celery task aggregates all corrected abstractions with high reviewer confidence, formats as instruction-tuning pairs (prompt, llm_output, correction), writes to S3 fine-tuning dataset prefix for future Bedrock custom model training.
target_output.data_model: leases (id UUID PK, tenant_id UUID FK, filename TEXT, s3_key TEXT, page_count INT, lease_type ENUM office/retail/industrial, status ENUM queued/extracting/review/complete/failed, uploaded_by UUID, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). abstracted_clauses (id UUID PK, lease_id UUID FK, tenant_id UUID FK, clause_definition_id UUID FK, llm_raw_output JSONB, structured_value JSONB, confidence_score FLOAT, status ENUM pending/accepted/corrected, correction_diff JSONB nullable, corrected_by UUID nullable, corrected_at TIMESTAMPTZ nullable). clause_definitions (id UUID PK, name TEXT e.g. 'co_tenancy_provision', lease_type ENUM, extraction_prompt_fragment TEXT, expected_schema JSONB, active BOOL). extraction_jobs (id UUID PK, tenant_id UUID FK, batch_name TEXT, lease_ids UUID[], status ENUM queued/running/complete/partial_failure, started_at, completed_at, bedrock_tokens_used INT). review_queues (id UUID PK, tenant_id UUID FK, lease_id UUID FK, assigned_reviewer_id UUID nullable, confidence_tier ENUM high/medium/low, status ENUM pending/in_progress/complete, due_at TIMESTAMPTZ). tenant_accounts (id UUID PK, name, region ENUM us/eu, subscription_tier, created_at). audit_logs (id UUID PK, tenant_id, user_id, action ENUM view/download/correct/export/admin_change, resource_type, resource_id, ip_address, user_agent, timestamp). All tables have tenant_id with RLS policy: USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.current_tenant_id')::UUID).
target_output.api_design: RESTful API at /api/v1/ with Okta JWT authentication. POST /extraction-jobs (submit batch, returns job_id). GET /extraction-jobs/{id}/status (poll progress, returns per-lease status). GET /review-queue?confidence_tier=low&page=1 (paginated queue). GET /review-queue/{lease_id}/clauses (all clauses for lease). PATCH /clauses/{id} (submit reviewer correction). POST /exports/csv?lease_ids[]= (trigger CSV export job). GET /analytics/critical-dates (portfolio critical date aggregation). GET /analytics/rent-escalations (rent schedule summary). GET /admin/users (user list). POST /admin/users (provision user). WebSocket ws/extraction-jobs/{id} for real-time extraction status push. Rate limits: 100 req/min per user JWT, 10 batch uploads per tenant per hour.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Tenant isolation: PostgreSQL RLS policies on all data tables enforced at DB layer; S3 bucket policies restrict object access to per-region ECS task roles; tenant_id injected into DB session via SET app.current_tenant_id on each connection. Encryption: KMS CMK per tenant for S3 SSE; RDS at-rest encryption with KMS. Authentication: Okta OIDC, 15-min access token, MFA required. Network: WAF on ALB (OWASP Top 10 managed rule set, 1000 req/min burst limit). Audit: append-only audit_logs table with no UPDATE/DELETE on application role; forwarded to Datadog; archived to S3 Glacier after 90 days, retained 7 years. Data residency: Route53 geolocation routing ensures EU-registered tenants always hit eu-west-1; no Bedrock cross-region API calls; GDPR Article 28 DPA with AWS as data processor. SOC 2 Type II controls: change management via GitHub Actions gated deploys, access reviews quarterly, encryption key rotation 365 days.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Two independent AWS stacks via Terraform (us-east-1 and eu-west-1). GitHub Actions CI: ruff lint, mypy type check, pytest (85% coverage gate), docker build + ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with ALB health check gate (5-min window, auto-rollback on <80% healthy tasks). Environments: dev (single AZ, reduced Celery workers), staging (multi-AZ, full worker pool, Bedrock sandbox quota), production (multi-AZ, 30-worker Celery pool, multi-AZ RDS, ElastiCache cluster mode). Database migrations via Alembic with rollback SQL; run as ECS one-off task before rolling deploy. Secrets rotation via Secrets Manager automated rotation Lambda for RDS and Redis credentials. Deployment frequency: up to 5 production deploys per day with feature flag controls.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM on API and Celery worker containers with distributed traces from upload to clause storage. Custom metrics: extraction_job_duration_seconds (p50/p95/p99 histogram), bedrock_tokens_per_lease (gauge), confidence_score_distribution (histogram per clause_type), review_queue_depth (gauge per tenant), correction_rate_by_clause_type (counter). Logs: structured JSON with tenant_id, lease_id, job_id correlation IDs. Dashboards: extraction pipeline throughput, Bedrock cost per day (tokens * pricing), review queue aging by tier. PagerDuty alerts: extraction queue depth >500 unprocessed for >10 min, p95 extraction latency >120s, Bedrock error rate >5%, RDS CPU >80%, Redis memory >85%.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated extraction cost: Claude 3.5 Sonnet at $3/$15 per M input/output tokens; average 30K input tokens per 50-page lease = $0.09/lease. At 1000 leases/hour: $90/hour LLM cost. ECS Fargate: API (4 tasks * $0.04/task/hour) + Celery (30 tasks * $0.04) = $1.36/hour compute. RDS Multi-AZ: ~$0.48/hour per region. Total at peak: ~$95/hour. Scaling: Celery autoscaling on queue depth (target: 20 tasks per worker); ECS API autoscaling on CPU 60%; Bedrock quotas increase via AWS Support request. Cost optimization: batch leases to maximize Bedrock context window utilization; cache clause_definitions in Redis to avoid repeated DB reads.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (weeks 1-8): single tenant account, 8 clause definitions (rent escalation, lease term, renewal options, termination rights, co-tenancy, CAM caps, permitted use, guaranty), pdfplumber text extraction, Bedrock extraction pipeline with Pydantic schema enforcement, reviewer queue UI with side-by-side PDF viewer, CSV export, Okta SSO integration, us-east-1 deployment only. Phase 2 (weeks 9-16): Textract integration for scanned PDFs, confidence scoring improvements, portfolio analytics dashboard (critical dates, rent schedules), admin user management panel, eu-west-1 stack for EU customers, GDPR DPA with AWS, SOC 2 evidence collection. Phase 3 (weeks 17-24): fine-tuning dataset pipeline from reviewer corrections, Yardi/MRI API integration for export, multi-LLM routing for cost optimization, advanced analytics (co-tenancy risk scoring, lease expiration concentration analysis).
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with strict mypy type checking. All LLM response schemas defined as Pydantic v2 models — never parse raw LLM JSON without model validation. FastAPI dependency injection for tenant context — tenant_id injected from JWT into every request context and DB session. Pytest with 85% coverage gate; mock Bedrock API in unit tests using boto3 stubber. All S3 operations through a LeaseDocumentStore abstraction class — never call S3 SDK directly from business logic. Celery task retry policy defined in task decorator, not caller code. ADR documents for each major architectural decision. Feature flags via LaunchDarkly for gradual rollout of new clause types to pilot customers.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: LLM extraction accuracy below acceptable threshold for specific lease types (e.g., ground leases with non-standard structures). Mitigation: correction_rate_by_clause_type metric monitors accuracy per type; low-performing types routed to mandatory human review regardless of confidence score. Risk: Bedrock quota limits at 1000-lease/hour peak. Mitigation: Azure OpenAI fallback activated by circuit breaker; quota increase request filed with AWS pre-launch. Risk: Scanned PDF quality varying wildly across customer portfolios, degrading Textract accuracy. Mitigation: page quality scoring pipeline flags low-DPI pages for manual upload improvement. Tradeoff: Celery over Temporal for task orchestration — Celery is faster to implement for MVP; Temporal migration planned if complex multi-step saga patterns emerge post-launch. Tradeoff: per-tenant KMS CMKs over shared KMS key — higher cost but required by enterprise customers with key custody requirements.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected AWS Bedrock over OpenAI API because data residency requirement prohibits sending lease content to OpenAI's shared multi-tenant infrastructure. Selected Celery over Temporal for MVP because extraction pipeline is a simple single-step task; Temporal considered for Phase 3 if multi-step saga patterns emerge. Confirmed pdfplumber over PyMuPDF for digital PDF text extraction due to superior table extraction for rent schedule clauses. Selected PostgreSQL RLS over application-layer tenant filtering as defense-in-depth for confidential lease data isolation.

metadata.notes: High-scale AI lease abstraction platform with Bedrock LLM pipeline, human review queue, and dual-region data residency; first-pass round 1.

project_goal.value: Migrate property ownership and title chain API from legacy SOAP interface to modern REST/GraphQL providing real-time access to county assessor records, deed history, lien searches, and ownership transfer events
project_goal.rationale: Legacy SOAP API blocks modern partner integrations and has no versioning strategy

target_users.value: PropTech partners, title companies, mortgage lenders, and real estate analytics platforms consuming ownership and title data
target_users.rationale: Current partner base and target expansion segments

project_class.rationale: Pure data API service with no frontend; partner-facing with authentication

capabilities.value: backend, data, auth, public_api, integrations, analytics
capabilities.rationale: API service with county data integrations and partner usage analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Legacy SOAP parallel operation, GraphQL schema design, county data integrations, and migration analytics

risk_level.rationale: Sensitive property ownership data for partner consumption; SOAP deprecation risk

data_sensitivity.rationale: County assessor and deed data is public record but partner access requires authentication and rate limiting

external_exposure.rationale: Authenticated API consumed by vetted partner organizations only

access_model.value: OAuth 2.0 client credentials per partner organization; scoped permissions per data type; rate limits per tier
access_model.rationale: Partner API access model with tiered rate limits

feature_scope.value: Parcel ownership lookup, title chain traversal, lien search, assessor record access, ownership transfer event webhooks, parallel SOAP deprecation, partner migration analytics
feature_scope.rationale: Full scope of title intelligence API

mvp_scope.value: REST endpoints for parcel ownership lookup and title chain; GraphQL for flexible field selection; OAuth 2.0 partner auth; parallel SOAP operation
mvp_scope.rationale: Core title chain data on both interfaces enables partner migration

security_baseline.value: OAuth 2.0 with JWT, TLS 1.3, rate limiting per partner tier, WAF, API key rotation
security_baseline.rationale: Partner-facing API security standard

frontend_stack.value: None — API service only
frontend_stack.rationale: No UI component; internal dev portal is a separate project

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI and Strawberry GraphQL
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI for REST endpoints; Strawberry for Pythonic GraphQL schema with type safety

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for normalized ownership and deed data; Redis 7 for response caching
data_platform.rationale: Relational model for title chain traversal; Redis for high-frequency parcel lookups

hosting_target.value: GCP GKE Autopilot
hosting_target.rationale: Managed Kubernetes scales automatically for spiky partner query loads

privacy_retention_policy.value: County assessor records are public data; retain indefinitely. Partner API access logs retained 2 years for audit and billing reconciliation.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Public record data has no personal retention constraints; access logs needed for billing

future_scope.value: Real-time ownership transfer webhooks via county recorder feed integration, property rights and easement data, FHFA conforming loan coverage
future_scope.rationale: Expansion into richer title intelligence data products

constraints.value: Must maintain SOAP interface until <5% of partners still use it; migration nudge emails at 20% and 50% REST adoption thresholds; p95 API latency <200ms for cached parcel lookups
constraints.rationale: Partner migration must be managed to avoid breaking existing integrations

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for API metrics; partner usage dashboards; SOAP vs REST traffic split tracking
observability_baseline.rationale: Critical to track migration progress and partner adoption rates

execution_preference.value: Incremental migration with continuous partner communication; no forced cutover
execution_preference.rationale: Partner business continuity requires gradual migration

project.goal: SOAP-to-REST/GraphQL migration for property ownership and title chain API
project.target_users: PropTech partners, title companies, mortgage lenders

reasoner_reviews.product: The parallel SOAP operation period is the right approach — forcing immediate migration would break partner integrations. The migration nudge email automation at 20%/50% REST adoption thresholds is a smart product lever to maintain momentum without manual account management overhead. Partner migration analytics should be a first-class internal product, not an afterthought.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: FastAPI + Strawberry GraphQL is a sound choice for dual REST/GraphQL delivery from a single Python codebase. The title chain traversal is the most complex query — a recursive CTE in PostgreSQL over the deed_transfers table (self-referential via prior_owner_parcel_grantee matching) should handle typical chain depth of 10-30 transfers efficiently. Redis caching at the parcel level will absorb the high-frequency ownership lookup traffic.
reasoner_reviews.security: OAuth 2.0 client credentials is the correct grant type for machine-to-machine partner integration. API key rotation every 90 days should be automated via a key rotation endpoint partners can call programmatically. WAF should include rules for GraphQL depth limiting to prevent deeply nested title chain queries from exhausting DB resources.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: p95 <200ms for cached parcel lookups is achievable with Redis. Uncached county data source supplementation may push uncached p95 to 500-800ms — make sure the SLA only applies to cached lookups and document the uncached path separately.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with modules: parcels (REST), title_chain (REST + GraphQL resolver), liens (REST), assessor (REST), migration_analytics (internal). SOAP adapter layer running as sidecar container translating legacy WSDL requests to internal FastAPI calls for backward compatibility.
backend.api_patterns: REST at /v1/ for structured single-resource lookups (parcel, assessor record, lien). GraphQL at /graphql for flexible multi-field queries (title chain traversal with nested ownership history). Partner OAuth 2.0 access tokens scoped to resource types (parcels:read, title:read, liens:read). Pagination via cursor-based paging on deed_transfers queries.
backend.scaling_notes: Redis Cache-Aside for parcel ownership lookups (TTL 1 hour, invalidated on county data refresh). PostgreSQL read replica for title chain traversal queries. GKE Horizontal Pod Autoscaler on API Deployment (min 3, max 20 replicas) targeting CPU 60%.
backend.failure_handling: Circuit breaker on county data source integrations (separate service); graceful degradation returns cached data with staleness timestamp header. 429 rate limit responses with Retry-After header per partner tier. Dead-letter queue for failed ownership transfer webhook deliveries.

security.auth_design: OAuth 2.0 authorization server (custom, backed by PostgreSQL partner_accounts table) issuing JWT access tokens with partner_id and scope claims. Token introspection endpoint for SOAP adapter token validation.
security.authorization_model: Scope-based: parcels:read, title:read, liens:read, assessor:read, webhooks:manage. Partners provisioned with subset of scopes per tier.
security.abuse_prevention: WAF GraphQL depth limit (max 5 levels) and complexity limit (max 1000 points) to prevent introspective abuse. Rate limiting via Redis sliding window per partner_id (Tier 1: 100 req/min, Tier 2: 1000 req/min, Tier 3: 10000 req/min).

data.entities: parcels (parcel_id TEXT PK, apn TEXT, county_fips TEXT, address, legal_description, lot_sqft, acreage, zoning_code, last_updated_at). ownership_records (id UUID, parcel_id, owner_name, owner_type ENUM individual/entity, vesting_type, ownership_pct FLOAT, effective_date DATE, recording_date DATE). deed_transfers (id UUID, parcel_id, grantor_name, grantee_name, transfer_date DATE, document_number TEXT, deed_type ENUM warranty/quitclaim/grant/trust, sale_price DECIMAL nullable, recording_county TEXT). liens (id UUID, parcel_id, lien_type ENUM mortgage/tax_lien/mechanic/judgment, amount DECIMAL, lienholder_name, recorded_date DATE, release_date DATE nullable, status ENUM active/released). assessor_records (id UUID, parcel_id, tax_year INT, assessed_value_land DECIMAL, assessed_value_improvements DECIMAL, tax_amount DECIMAL, exemptions JSONB). county_data_sources (id UUID, county_fips, source_type ENUM api/sftp/scrape, last_sync_at, record_count). partner_accounts (id UUID, name, tier ENUM tier1/tier2/tier3, scopes TEXT[], api_key_hash, key_rotated_at). migration_analytics (partner_id UUID, week DATE, soap_request_count INT, rest_request_count INT, graphql_request_count INT).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL (GCP) with read replica for analytics. Redis 7 Memorystore for parcel ownership cache (TTL 1 hour). No document storage needed — all data is structured records.

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster with Deployments: api-service (FastAPI + Strawberry GraphQL), soap-adapter (legacy compatibility sidecar), county-sync-jobs (CronJob per county source). Cloud Load Balancer -> GKE Ingress -> api-service.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: pytest (80% coverage), ruff lint, mypy, Docker build + GCR push, kubectl apply with rolling update. Staging mirrors production county data subset. Nightly county sync integration tests.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus metrics scraped from FastAPI /metrics endpoint. Grafana dashboards: REST vs SOAP request split per partner, endpoint p50/p95 latency, cache hit rate per parcel lookup, county sync lag. Alerting on: SOAP traffic >50% after month 6, p95 latency >400ms, county sync >24h stale.

ISS-001.title: Title chain traversal queries timing out for parcels with deep transfer history
ISS-001.detail: Parcels with 30+ deed transfers cause recursive CTE queries to exceed 5-second timeout due to missing index on deed_transfers.parcel_id and grantor_name columns; affects approximately 8% of title chain API calls in production

history.note: Identified in staging load test with synthetic 50-transfer parcels
Index added on parcel_id but grantor_name join for chain assembly still full-scanning

ISS-002.title: SOAP adapter token validation adds 80ms overhead per request
ISS-002.detail: SOAP adapter was performing synchronous HTTP call to OAuth token introspection endpoint per request; resolved in round 2 by caching valid tokens in adapter's local Redis namespace

history.note: SOAP adapter p95 latency was 280ms vs REST 120ms due to per-request introspection
Token cache added with 5-minute TTL; SOAP p95 now 140ms

ISS-003.title: Migration analytics under-counting REST requests for partners using API gateway caching
ISS-003.detail: Partners behind their own API gateways with response caching are not generating REST requests for cached responses, causing migration_analytics to undercount REST adoption and over-report SOAP dependency for those partners

history.note: Discovered when Tier 3 partner reported 80% REST migration but analytics showed only 40%

focus_issues.title: Title chain traversal queries timing out for parcels with deep transfer history
focus_issues.detail: Recursive CTE on deed_transfers times out for parcels with 30+ transfers; grantor_name join requires composite index to eliminate full table scan

latest_recommendations: Add composite index on deed_transfers(parcel_id, transfer_date DESC) and a separate index on deed_transfers(grantee_name) to support the chain assembly join pattern
Consider materializing pre-computed title chains for the top 10% most-queried parcels (by access frequency) to eliminate recursive CTE overhead entirely for high-traffic parcels
For ISS-003 document that migration_analytics counts server-side requests only; add a note to partner documentation clarifying that client-side caching will reduce apparent REST request count
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Property Ownership and Title Chain API v2 (SOAP Migration)

convergence_state.reason: ISS-001 title chain timeout affects 8% of production API calls and must be resolved before SOAP sunset milestone

previous_audits.summary: Strong migration architecture with dual REST/GraphQL delivery and SOAP adapter working correctly post-ISS-002 fix. The title chain timeout issue blocks production reliability and must be resolved before SOAP sunset proceeds.
strengths: SOAP adapter token caching resolved latency parity with REST interface
GraphQL depth limiting prevents resource exhaustion from nested queries
Migration analytics dashboard is providing actionable partner adoption data
concerns: ISS-001: 8% of title chain queries timing out in production is a reliability blocker for title company partners
ISS-003: migration analytics undercounting REST adoption may delay SOAP sunset incorrectly
blocking_issues: Title chain traversal timeout for high-transfer parcels causing 8% error rate on /v1/parcels/{id}/title-chain endpoint — title company partners require 99.5% SLA on this endpoint
recommendations: Add composite index deed_transfers(parcel_id, transfer_date DESC) and index on (grantee_name) to eliminate full table scan in chain assembly join
Consider materialized view or pre-computed chain cache for top-10% most-queried parcels
Document migration_analytics methodology in partner developer portal to set accurate expectations about client-cache impact

issue_updates.title: Title chain traversal timeout
issue_updates.detail: parcel_id index alone insufficient; grantee_name join for chain assembly still full-scanning deed_transfers

issue_updates.title: Migration analytics undercounting REST
issue_updates.detail: Client-side API gateway caching not accounted for in analytics methodology documentation

previous_plan.title: Property Ownership and Title Chain API v2 (SOAP Migration)
previous_plan.executive_summary: Dual REST/GraphQL API service migrating from legacy SOAP, delivering parcel ownership, title chain, lien, and assessor data to PropTech partners via Python FastAPI + Strawberry on GKE Autopilot with SOAP adapter for backward compatibility.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: FastAPI REST + Strawberry GraphQL on GKE Autopilot. SOAP adapter sidecar container translates legacy WSDL requests to internal FastAPI calls. PostgreSQL for relational title data; Redis for parcel lookup caching. OAuth 2.0 client credentials for partner auth.
previous_plan.data_model: parcels, ownership_records, deed_transfers, liens, assessor_records, partner_accounts, migration_analytics.
previous_plan.workflows: Title chain traversal via recursive CTE on deed_transfers. REST parcel lookup with Redis cache-aside. Migration analytics tracking SOAP vs REST request splits per partner.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round-3 revision targeting ISS-001 title chain timeout. The fix requires two database changes: a composite index on deed_transfers(parcel_id, transfer_date DESC) to support ordered chain traversal, and a separate index on deed_transfers(grantee_name) to eliminate the full table scan in the chain assembly join. For the top 10% most-queried parcels by access frequency, I will add a materialized pre-computed title chain cache stored in Redis as a pre-serialized JSON structure, refreshed on each new deed_transfer insertion. This eliminates recursive CTE execution for high-traffic parcels entirely.

fix_report.action_taken: Added Alembic migration creating composite index deed_transfers(parcel_id, transfer_date DESC) and non-composite index deed_transfers(grantee_name); implemented pre-computed title chain materialization in Redis for parcels in the top-10% access frequency tier (tracked via a sorted set in Redis keyed by parcel_id with access count as score); Celery task refreshes materialized chain on deed_transfer insert; title chain resolver checks Redis for pre-computed result before executing recursive CTE
fix_report.expected_outcome: Title chain traversal timeout rate reduces from 8% to <0.5% for deep-transfer parcels; p95 latency for top-10% most-queried parcels improves from 4-8s to <100ms via Redis materialized chains; remaining long-tail parcels benefit from composite index reducing recursive CTE cost by 60-80%

target_output.title: Property Ownership and Title Chain API v3 (SOAP Migration)
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 FastAPI and Strawberry GraphQL API service delivering real-time property ownership, title chain, lien, and county assessor data to PropTech partners, migrated from a legacy SOAP interface. Dual REST and GraphQL endpoints operate in parallel with a SOAP adapter sidecar for backward compatibility during a managed migration period tracked by per-partner usage analytics. Title chain traversal optimized via composite PostgreSQL indexes and Redis-materialized pre-computed chains for high-frequency parcels. Hosted on GCP GKE Autopilot with OAuth 2.0 partner authentication and tiered rate limiting.
target_output.architecture_overview: FastAPI application exposes REST endpoints at /v1/ and a Strawberry GraphQL endpoint at /graphql on GKE Autopilot with horizontal pod autoscaling. A SOAP adapter container translates legacy WSDL requests to internal FastAPI calls using cached OAuth tokens (5-minute TTL in Redis). PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL stores normalized parcel, deed, lien, and assessor data; a read replica handles title chain traversal and analytics queries. Redis 7 Memorystore serves two purposes: parcel ownership cache-aside (1-hour TTL) and pre-computed title chain materialization for the top-10% most-queried parcels (sorted set tracking access frequency, JSON chain stored as Redis hash). County data sync CronJobs run per-source on GKE. Migration analytics track per-partner SOAP vs REST request splits weekly, triggering automated nudge emails at 20% and 50% REST adoption thresholds.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI 0.111, Strawberry GraphQL 0.235, Pydantic v2, SQLAlchemy 2 (async). Data: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL with read replica, Redis 7 Memorystore (cache + chain materialization). Auth: custom OAuth 2.0 server with JWT (python-jose), partner credentials in Cloud Secret Manager. Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot, Cloud Load Balancer, GCR for images. CI/CD: GitLab CI with pytest, ruff, mypy. Observability: Prometheus (fastapi-prometheus), Grafana dashboards. County integrations: httpx async clients per county source type.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Parcel Ownership Lookup: GET /v1/parcels/{parcel_id}/ownership returns current owner(s) with vesting and ownership percentage; Redis cache-aside with 1-hour TTL. Title Chain Traversal: GET /v1/parcels/{parcel_id}/title-chain and GraphQL query titleChain(parcelId) returns ordered deed_transfers list with grantor, grantee, deed type, and sale price; checks Redis materialized chain for high-frequency parcels before executing recursive CTE. Lien Search: GET /v1/parcels/{parcel_id}/liens returns active and released liens by type. Assessor Record: GET /v1/parcels/{parcel_id}/assessor?tax_year= returns assessed values and tax history. Partner Migration Analytics: internal dashboard showing weekly SOAP vs REST split per partner with trend lines and nudge email trigger log. GraphQL Flexible Query: single GraphQL operation can fetch parcel ownership, recent deed transfers, active liens, and assessor record in one request — reducing partner round-trips.
target_output.system_components: GKE Autopilot Deployment: api-service (FastAPI + Strawberry, min 3 / max 20 replicas, HPA on CPU 60%). GKE Deployment: soap-adapter (Python SOAP-to-REST translator, min 2 replicas). GKE CronJobs: county-sync-api (nightly per county API source), county-sync-sftp (nightly per SFTP source). Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (primary + read replica). Redis 7 Memorystore (parcel cache namespace + chain materialization namespace + rate limit sliding windows). Cloud Load Balancer with Cloud Armor WAF (GraphQL depth limit, rate limit per IP). GCR for container images. Cloud Secret Manager for OAuth credentials and county API keys. Celery worker (GKE Deployment, 5 replicas) for async chain materialization refresh and nudge email dispatch.
target_output.workflows: Title Chain Query Workflow: partner calls GET /v1/parcels/{apn}/title-chain with OAuth JWT; API middleware validates token scope (title:read) and partner_id; Redis check for pre-computed chain key (chain:{parcel_id}); if HIT, return deserialized chain JSON with cache header; if MISS, execute PostgreSQL recursive CTE on deed_transfers read replica traversing from most recent grantee back through grantor chain; result serialized to JSON, stored in Redis with no TTL (refreshed on deed_transfer insert), returned to partner. Chain Materialization Refresh Workflow: on INSERT to deed_transfers table, PostgreSQL NOTIFY triggers a Celery task (via pg_notify + asyncpg listener); task checks if parcel_id is in top-10% Redis sorted set; if yes, re-executes recursive CTE and refreshes Redis hash. Migration Analytics Workflow: weekly Celery task aggregates migration_analytics table per partner, computes REST adoption percentage, sends nudge email via SendGrid if threshold crossed (20% trigger: 'Getting started with REST migration', 50% trigger: 'Over halfway — SOAP sunset timeline'), logs nudge event to avoid duplicate sends.
target_output.data_model: parcels (parcel_id TEXT PK, apn TEXT, county_fips TEXT REFERENCES county_data_sources, address TEXT, legal_description TEXT, lot_sqft INT, zoning_code TEXT, last_updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). ownership_records (id UUID PK, parcel_id TEXT FK, owner_name TEXT, owner_type ENUM individual/entity, vesting_type TEXT, ownership_pct FLOAT, effective_date DATE, recording_date DATE). deed_transfers (id UUID PK, parcel_id TEXT FK, grantor_name TEXT, grantee_name TEXT, transfer_date DATE, document_number TEXT, deed_type ENUM warranty/quitclaim/grant/trust, sale_price DECIMAL nullable, recording_county TEXT). Indexes: deed_transfers(parcel_id, transfer_date DESC), deed_transfers(grantee_name). liens (id UUID PK, parcel_id TEXT FK, lien_type ENUM mortgage/tax_lien/mechanic/judgment, amount DECIMAL, lienholder_name TEXT, recorded_date DATE, release_date DATE nullable, status ENUM active/released). assessor_records (id UUID PK, parcel_id TEXT FK, tax_year INT, assessed_value_land DECIMAL, assessed_value_improvements DECIMAL, tax_amount DECIMAL, exemptions JSONB). county_data_sources (id UUID PK, county_fips TEXT, source_type ENUM api/sftp/scrape, last_sync_at TIMESTAMPTZ, record_count INT, sync_lag_hours FLOAT). partner_accounts (id UUID PK, name TEXT, tier ENUM tier1/tier2/tier3, scopes TEXT[], api_key_hash TEXT, key_rotated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). migration_analytics (partner_id UUID FK, week DATE, soap_request_count INT, rest_request_count INT, graphql_request_count INT, nudge_sent BOOL). Redis keys: ownership:{parcel_id} (1h TTL), chain:{parcel_id} (no TTL, refreshed on write), rate:{partner_id}:{minute} (sliding window), access_freq (sorted set, parcel_id -> access count).
target_output.api_design: OAuth 2.0 Token: POST /oauth/token (client_credentials grant, returns JWT with partner_id, scopes). REST: GET /v1/parcels/{parcel_id}/ownership (scope: parcels:read). GET /v1/parcels/{parcel_id}/title-chain?limit=50&cursor= (scope: title:read, cursor-based pagination). GET /v1/parcels/{parcel_id}/liens?status=active (scope: liens:read). GET /v1/parcels/{parcel_id}/assessor?tax_year=2024 (scope: assessor:read). GET /v1/parcels/search?apn={apn}&county_fips={fips} (scope: parcels:read). GraphQL: POST /graphql — query { parcel(id: 'xxx') { ownership { ownerName vestingType } titleChain(limit: 20) { grantor grantee transferDate deedType salePrice } activeLinks { lienType amount } } }. Rate limits enforced via Redis sliding window: Tier 1 100 req/min, Tier 2 1000 req/min, Tier 3 10000 req/min. 429 response includes Retry-After and X-RateLimit-Reset headers.
target_output.security_and_compliance: OAuth 2.0 client credentials with 1-hour JWT access tokens and scope enforcement at API middleware layer. Partner API key rotation automated via POST /v1/partner/rotate-key — returns new key, invalidates old after 24-hour grace period. Cloud Armor WAF: GraphQL query depth limit 5 levels, complexity limit 1000 points, rate limit 5000 req/min per IP, OWASP managed rule set. TLS 1.3 enforced at Cloud Load Balancer. GLBA compliance: title data used in mortgage context requires partner DPA; partner provisioning workflow includes DPA signature verification. Access logs: all partner API requests logged to Cloud Logging with partner_id, endpoint, latency, status; retained 2 years per legal requirement. State recording act compliance: deed data served with disclaimer that it represents county records as of last sync date; sync_lag_hours exposed in response metadata header.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot with GitLab CI/CD: ruff lint, mypy type check, pytest (80% coverage gate), docker build + GCR push, kubectl rollout with rolling update strategy (maxSurge 1, maxUnavailable 0). Alembic migration run as Kubernetes Job before new API Deployment rollout. Environments: dev (single replica, reduced county sync), staging (full topology, county test data subset), production (min 3 / max 20 API replicas, HPA CPU 60%). County sync CronJobs: nightly at 02:00 UTC per source. Celery workers: 5 replicas handling chain materialization and email nudges. Rollback: kubectl rollout undo on failed health check; Alembic downgrade SQL preserved for each migration.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics from /metrics endpoint scraped by GKE-managed Prometheus. Grafana dashboards: API request rate by endpoint and partner tier, p50/p95/p99 latency by endpoint, Redis cache hit rate for ownership and chain lookups, county sync lag per source (alert if >24h), SOAP vs REST weekly split per partner with trend. Migration dashboard: partner REST adoption %, nudge email sent log, SOAP sunset countdown (target: <5% SOAP traffic). Alerts: title chain p95 >500ms (PagerDuty), county sync lag >24h (Slack), partner SOAP traffic >60% after month 9 (Slack escalation), GKE pod crash loop (PagerDuty).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot billing on pod resource requests: API service at 0.5 vCPU / 1GB per pod, 3-20 replicas = $0.02-0.13/hour compute. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 2-vCPU with read replica: ~$0.30/hour. Redis 7 Memorystore 2GB: ~$0.12/hour. Estimated total: $0.50-0.60/hour baseline, $1.50/hour at peak. Scaling: GKE Autopilot auto-provisions nodes; no pre-provisioning needed. Redis chain materialization reduces PostgreSQL read replica load for top-10% parcel queries by estimated 40-60%. Cost optimization: cache TTL tuning based on county sync frequency; Autopilot per-request billing avoids idle node costs.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (months 1-3): REST endpoints for ownership, title chain, liens, assessor; Strawberry GraphQL with same resolvers; SOAP adapter sidecar with cached token validation; OAuth 2.0 partner auth; migration analytics table and weekly batch job; partner developer portal with REST documentation. Phase 2 (months 4-6): Composite deed_transfers indexes and Redis chain materialization for top-10% parcels; lien search improvements; GraphQL depth and complexity limiting; automated nudge email workflow; county sync CronJobs for 50 high-priority counties. Phase 3 (months 7-12): ownership transfer webhooks via Pub/Sub; full 3,000 county coverage via sync jobs; SOAP sunset campaign targeting <5% SOAP traffic; API key rotation automation; GLBA DPA workflow for mortgage-adjacent partners.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with strict mypy. All database queries via SQLAlchemy 2 async ORM — no raw SQL strings except Alembic migration files. Strawberry GraphQL resolvers must check Redis pre-computed cache before executing any DB query. All partner-facing responses include X-Sync-Lag-Hours header from county_data_sources.last_sync_at. pytest with 80% coverage; county data source integrations tested with recorded HTTP cassettes via vcrpy. Each new county source integration requires a data quality test asserting parcel count within 5% of prior sync. API versioning enforced: all endpoints under /v1/; breaking changes require /v2/ namespace with 6-month deprecation notice.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: county data source APIs changing schema without notice. Mitigation: schema validation assertions on each sync run; Slack alert on unexpected field changes; manual fallback to SFTP for counties with unreliable APIs. Risk: Redis materialized chains becoming stale if pg_notify + asyncpg listener connection drops. Mitigation: Celery Beat daily reconciliation job re-materializes top-10% parcels regardless of notify trigger. Risk: SOAP adapter maintenance burden persisting beyond planned sunset. Mitigation: automated sunset criteria tracking (SOAP <5% traffic) removes subjective judgment from decommission decision. Tradeoff: Strawberry GraphQL over Ariadne — Strawberry's code-first approach keeps schema in sync with Python models automatically; Ariadne's schema-first is more explicit but requires manual schema-model synchronization. Tradeoff: Cloud SQL over AlloyDB — Cloud SQL sufficient for current read throughput; AlloyDB migration planned if read replica cannot sustain >10K queries/min.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Strawberry over Ariadne for GraphQL because Strawberry's Python-first schema definition ensures type safety parity between REST Pydantic models and GraphQL types. Confirmed GKE Autopilot over GKE Standard because auto-provisioning eliminates node pool management overhead for spiky partner query patterns. Resolved that migration_analytics counts server-side requests only; partner documentation will note that client-side caching reduces apparent REST request counts and provide guidance on disabling gateway caching during migration measurement period.

metadata.notes: Round-3 SOAP-to-REST/GraphQL migration fixing title chain traversal timeout via composite indexes and Redis chain materialization; partner-facing title intelligence API.

project_goal.value: Compliance-focused data pipeline standardizing collection, retention, and audit logging of tenant personal data across 15 regional property management platforms to meet CCPA and GDPR deletion and portability requirements
project_goal.rationale: Regulatory compliance requirement with enforcement risk for non-compliance

target_users.value: Privacy compliance team, regional platform administrators, and tenants exercising CCPA/GDPR rights
target_users.rationale: Internal compliance operators and the tenants who trigger rights requests

project_class.rationale: Batch and event-driven pipeline across 15 data sources with no user-facing frontend

capabilities.rationale: Cross-platform data integration with batch processing and compliance orchestration

complexity_level.rationale: 15 heterogeneous source platforms, multi-regulation compliance, cryptographic deletion, 45-day regulatory deadline enforcement

risk_level.rationale: Regulatory enforcement risk from CCPA and GDPR non-compliance; fines up to 4% of annual global turnover under GDPR

data_sensitivity.rationale: Pipeline processes tenant PII including contact info, lease history, payment records, and communication logs

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline runs entirely within internal infrastructure; tenants interact via existing regional platforms, not directly with this pipeline

access_model.value: AWS IAM roles for pipeline service accounts; compliance team access via RBAC in the central compliance dashboard
access_model.rationale: Least-privilege access for automated pipeline; human access restricted to compliance team

feature_scope.value: Deletion request orchestration across 15 platforms, data portability export assembly, consent record management, retention policy enforcement, audit logging of all compliance actions
feature_scope.rationale: Full CCPA/GDPR rights fulfillment workflow

mvp_scope.value: Deletion request orchestration for 5 highest-volume regional platforms with audit trail; 45-day deadline tracking
mvp_scope.rationale: Prioritize highest-risk platforms by data volume for first compliance milestone

security_baseline.value: Encryption at rest AES-256 via AWS KMS; TLS 1.3 in transit; IAM least-privilege; CloudTrail for all data access actions
security_baseline.rationale: Personal data pipeline requires maximum access controls

frontend_stack.value: None — internal Airflow DAG monitoring UI only; compliance dashboard is a separate project
frontend_stack.rationale: Pipeline project scope excludes frontend; compliance team uses Airflow UI for operations

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Apache Airflow on MWAA
backend_stack.rationale: Airflow DAGs for orchestrating multi-platform deletion and portability workflows with retry and SLA monitoring

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for compliance state tracking; S3 for portability export packages; each regional platform has its own database
data_platform.rationale: Central compliance DB for request state; S3 for encrypted portability export delivery

hosting_target.value: AWS MWAA (Managed Workflows for Apache Airflow) in us-east-1
hosting_target.rationale: Managed Airflow eliminates scheduler maintenance; us-east-1 for data residency

privacy_retention_policy.value: Tenant PII retained per lease duration plus state-mandated hold period (3-7 years by state); compliance audit logs retained 7 years; portability exports retained 30 days in encrypted S3 then deleted
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Retention policy derived from state landlord-tenant law and GDPR Article 5 storage limitation

future_scope.value: Extend to remaining 10 regional platforms; automated consent re-solicitation on policy change; real-time deletion via event-driven trigger instead of batch
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP roadmap for full platform coverage

constraints.value: CCPA deletion response within 45 days; GDPR deletion within 30 days; portability export within 30 days (GDPR) or 45 days (CCPA); must not require code changes in regional platforms
constraints.rationale: Regulatory deadlines and zero-downtime constraint for regional platforms

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch for Airflow DAG metrics; SLA breach alerting; deletion completion rates per platform
observability_baseline.rationale: Compliance SLA monitoring is a regulatory requirement

execution_preference.value: Phased platform onboarding; compliance team acceptance testing per platform batch
execution_preference.rationale: Legal team must verify deletion completeness per platform before signing off

compliance_context.value: CCPA (California Consumer Privacy Act) deletion and portability rights; GDPR Articles 17 (right to erasure) and 20 (right to portability); Virginia CDPA; Colorado CPA; state landlord-tenant data retention minimums that may conflict with deletion requests (legal hold identification required)
compliance_context.rationale: Multi-regulation compliance with conflicting retention vs deletion obligations requires legal hold identification before any deletion

project.goal: Cross-platform CCPA/GDPR deletion and portability compliance pipeline for 15 regional property management databases
project.target_users: Privacy compliance team; tenants exercising rights
project.timeline: Phase 1 (5 platforms) in 12 weeks; full coverage in 24 weeks

security.encryption: KMS AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit

data.regional: each platform has own PostgreSQL or MySQL

reasoner_reviews.product: The constraint of not requiring code changes in regional platforms is the hardest design challenge. The pipeline must extract and delete data using existing platform APIs or direct database access under a service account — neither approach is ideal for all 15 platforms. A platform capability registry documenting which platforms expose deletion APIs vs require direct DB operations is critical for DAG design.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The legal hold identification step is the most legally critical component. Before any deletion executes, the pipeline must check whether the tenant is under a regulatory hold (ongoing dispute, government investigation, or active legal proceeding) that overrides the deletion request. This check must be synchronous with the deletion DAG, not asynchronous, to prevent a race condition where deletion proceeds before hold status is confirmed. Airflow SLA callbacks for the 30-day GDPR deadline are a good mechanism for escalation alerting.
reasoner_reviews.security: Portability export packages must be encrypted at the object level (S3 client-side encryption or SSE-KMS) and the download link must be a presigned URL with short TTL (48 hours) rather than a permanent link. The portability export must include a checksum so the receiving tenant can verify completeness. Compliance audit logs must be append-only with no application-layer UPDATE/DELETE permissions.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 30-day GDPR deadline vs 45-day CCPA deadline means the DAG needs regulation-aware deadline tracking. A tenant who qualifies under both GDPR (EU) and CCPA (California) must have their request completed within 30 days — the tighter deadline governs. Airflow SLA monitoring should be configured with 25-day and 28-day early-warning thresholds before the 30-day hard deadline.

backend.service_design: Airflow DAGs: deletion_orchestration (per-request DAG triggered by deletion_requests table insert via Airflow REST API), portability_export (per-request DAG assembling data across all regional platforms), retention_policy_scan (weekly DAG per platform scanning for records exceeding retention window), consent_sync (nightly DAG syncing consent records from regional platforms to central consent_records table).
backend.business_modules: platform_adapters/ module with one adapter class per regional platform implementing a standard PlatformAdapter interface: discover_tenant_records(tenant_id), delete_tenant_records(tenant_id, record_ids), export_tenant_records(tenant_id) -> DataPackage. Legal hold checker service querying legal_holds table before any deletion task. Deadline tracker computing applicable regulatory deadline per request based on tenant_jurisdiction.
backend.background_jobs: Daily SLA monitoring job checking requests approaching 25-day and 28-day thresholds; weekly retention scan per platform; monthly compliance summary report generation for legal team.
backend.failure_handling: Airflow task retry with 3 retries and 30-minute backoff for platform API failures. On task failure after retries, request status set to failed_platform_error and compliance team alerted via SNS. Dead-letter SNS topic for requests that exhaust retries without completion. Partial deletion tracking: deletion_task_results records per-platform deletion status so a retry only re-executes failed platforms.

data.entities: deletion_requests (id UUID PK, tenant_id TEXT, request_source ENUM ccpa/gdpr/virginia_cdpa/colorado_cpa, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, regulatory_deadline TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM received/legal_hold_check/approved/in_progress/complete/failed/on_hold, legal_hold_id UUID nullable). legal_holds (id UUID PK, tenant_id TEXT, hold_reason TEXT, hold_type ENUM litigation/regulatory/government_request, active BOOL, created_at, expires_at nullable). deletion_task_results (id UUID PK, request_id UUID FK, platform_id UUID FK, records_deleted INT, deletion_method ENUM api/direct_db, status ENUM pending/complete/failed, executed_at). portability_requests (id UUID PK, tenant_id TEXT, request_source ENUM ccpa/gdpr, submitted_at, deadline TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM received/assembling/ready/delivered/expired, s3_export_key TEXT nullable, export_checksum TEXT nullable). tenant_profiles (id UUID PK, tenant_id TEXT, jurisdictions TEXT[] e.g. ['CA','EU'], regional_platform_ids JSONB, last_seen_at). consent_records (id UUID PK, tenant_id TEXT, platform_id UUID FK, consent_type TEXT, granted_at, withdrawn_at nullable, lawful_basis ENUM consent/legitimate_interest/contract/legal_obligation). retention_policies (platform_id UUID FK, data_category TEXT, retention_days INT, legal_basis TEXT). audit_logs (id UUID PK, action ENUM deletion_initiated/deletion_complete/export_assembled/export_delivered/legal_hold_placed/retention_scan, request_id UUID nullable, platform_id UUID nullable, operator_id TEXT, tenant_id TEXT, details JSONB, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ).
data.storage_design: Central compliance DB: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ in us-east-1 (deletion_requests, legal_holds, deletion_task_results, portability_requests, tenant_profiles, consent_records, retention_policies, audit_logs). Regional platform access: each platform has a dedicated read/write service account with minimal table-level permissions granted by regional platform DBA. Portability exports: S3 bucket (tenant-portability-exports-us-east-1) with SSE-KMS, lifecycle policy deletes objects after 30 days, versioning disabled. Audit log archive: S3 Glacier after 1 year, retained 7 years.
data.retention_and_deletion: Retention enforcement: weekly Airflow DAG per platform scans for records where (created_at + retention_days) < NOW() AND no active legal_hold AND no active lease. Legal hold intercept: deletion_orchestration DAG includes SLA-gated legal_hold_check task before any deletion tasks. Portability export cleanup: S3 lifecycle rule deletes export objects after 30 days automatically.

devops.deployment_topology: MWAA Airflow environment in us-east-1. DAG files deployed from S3 bucket (mwaa-dags-bucket) via CI/CD. Platform adapter code packaged as Airflow plugin (requirements.txt). Compliance PostgreSQL on RDS Multi-AZ. S3 buckets with KMS CMK for portability exports and audit archive.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff lint, pytest on DAG structure and adapter unit tests (80% coverage), DAG bag import validation, upload to S3 DAG bucket on merge to main. Staging MWAA environment mirrors production with 5-platform sandbox data (anonymized).
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch metrics from MWAA: dag_run success rate, task retry rate, SLA miss count. Custom CloudWatch metrics emitted by DAGs: deletion_completion_time_hours (histogram), records_deleted_per_request (gauge), portability_export_size_bytes (gauge). SNS alerts: request approaching 25-day threshold, request SLA breached, platform adapter failure rate >10%, legal hold check failure.

ISS-001.title: Legal hold check is asynchronous — deletion can proceed before hold status confirmed
ISS-001.detail: The legal_hold_check Airflow task currently submits a query to the legal team ticketing system and marks the task complete immediately, relying on an async callback to set the hold flag. If the callback is delayed, the downstream deletion tasks run before hold status is confirmed, creating regulatory and litigation risk.

history.note: Identified during compliance team review; legal team flagged as critical before production launch

ISS-002.title: Portability export missing payment history from 3 regional platforms
ISS-002.detail: Three regional platforms (platforms 4, 9, 12) use a legacy payment processor that does not expose payment history via platform API; current export adapter returns empty payment_history array for these platforms, producing incomplete portability packages that do not meet GDPR Article 20 completeness requirement

history.note: Discovered during portability export testing with real tenant data

focus_issues.title: Legal hold check is asynchronous — deletion can proceed before hold status confirmed
focus_issues.detail: legal_hold_check task completes before hold status confirmed; downstream deletion tasks can execute before legal team reviews the hold query

latest_recommendations: Redesign legal_hold_check as a synchronous polling task that blocks on the Airflow ExternalTaskSensor waiting for a manual or automated hold decision record in the legal_holds table before downstream deletion tasks can proceed
Implement a hard gate in the deletion_orchestration DAG: if no hold decision record exists within 24 hours, auto-escalate to compliance officer with request paused status
For ISS-002, implement direct read-only DB access to legacy payment processor tables for the 3 affected platforms as a stop-gap until those platforms migrate to a payment API

convergence_state.reason: ISS-001 is a critical regulatory risk that must be resolved; ISS-002 portability incompleteness remains

previous_audits.summary: Pipeline architecture is well-structured for multi-platform compliance orchestration but the asynchronous legal hold check introduces a critical regulatory and litigation risk that must be resolved before any production processing. Portability incompleteness is a secondary but blocking GDPR compliance issue.
strengths: Airflow on MWAA is the right orchestration choice for multi-platform batch compliance workflows
Deadline-aware regulatory routing (30-day GDPR vs 45-day CCPA) is correctly modeled
audit_logs append-only design with no application UPDATE/DELETE is appropriate for compliance evidence
concerns: ISS-001: async legal hold check is a critical liability — deletion executing before hold confirmed could destroy evidence subject to litigation
ISS-002: incomplete payment history in portability exports violates GDPR Article 20 data portability completeness
blocking_issues: Legal hold check asynchrony allows deletion tasks to proceed before hold confirmation — this is a compliance-critical defect that must be resolved before production launch
Portability exports for 3 platforms return empty payment_history arrays, failing GDPR Article 20 completeness requirement
recommendations: Implement synchronous hold gate using Airflow ExternalTaskSensor on a legal_hold_decisions table; deletion tasks cannot start until a decision record exists
Add direct read-only DB connector for legacy payment processor tables on platforms 4, 9, 12 as interim payment history extraction method

issue_updates.title: Legal hold check asynchronous
issue_updates.detail: DAG task structure allows deletion subtasks to execute before hold_check callback received

issue_updates.title: Portability export payment history missing
issue_updates.detail: Platforms 4, 9, 12 legacy payment processor exposes no API; export returns empty array

previous_plan.title: Tenant Data Compliance Pipeline v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Airflow-orchestrated compliance pipeline on AWS MWAA handling CCPA and GDPR deletion and portability requests across 15 regional property management platforms, with per-platform adapters, legal hold checking, and deadline tracking.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: MWAA Airflow orchestrating deletion and portability DAGs. Central PostgreSQL for compliance state. S3 for portability exports. Per-platform adapters for 15 regional databases. Legal hold check as async callback (flagged as critical defect in ISS-001).
previous_plan.data_model: deletion_requests, legal_holds, deletion_task_results, portability_requests, tenant_profiles, consent_records, retention_policies, audit_logs.
previous_plan.workflows: Deletion orchestration: async legal hold check (defective), then per-platform deletion tasks. Portability export: adapter per platform assembles data package; platforms 4/9/12 return empty payment_history (defective per ISS-002).

target_output.thinking_summary: Round-2 revision with a critical ISS-001 fix: converting the legal hold check from async callback to a synchronous blocking gate using an Airflow Sensor that polls for a legal_hold_decisions record before any deletion task can proceed. The DAG will auto-pause the request and escalate after 24 hours with no decision. ISS-002 is partially addressed by adding direct read-only DB connectors for the three legacy payment processor databases, which is the only viable path given those platforms expose no payment API.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced async legal_hold_check task with a synchronous LegalHoldDecisionSensor (Airflow custom Sensor) that polls the legal_hold_decisions table every 5 minutes for a decision record with matching request_id; if no decision record exists within 24 hours, sensor triggers a SLA callback that pauses the deletion request, inserts a status=on_hold record, and sends SNS notification to compliance officer; downstream deletion tasks are downstream dependencies of the sensor and cannot execute until sensor returns success (HOLD_CLEARED or LEGAL_HOLD_CONFIRMED); if LEGAL_HOLD_CONFIRMED, DAG branches to a hold_active path that terminates deletion and records the hold in audit_logs
fix_report.expected_outcome: No deletion can proceed without an explicit legal hold clearance decision in the legal_hold_decisions table; eliminates race condition between deletion execution and hold confirmation; produces audit trail of every hold decision for compliance evidence

target_output.title: Tenant Data Compliance Pipeline v2
target_output.executive_summary: An Airflow-on-MWAA data pipeline enforcing CCPA and GDPR deletion and portability rights for tenant personal data across a national property management company's 15 regional platforms. Each deletion request passes through a synchronous LegalHoldDecisionSensor gate before any data deletion task executes, eliminating regulatory risk from premature deletion of litigation-hold data. Portability exports assemble tenant contact, lease, payment, and communication records via per-platform adapters — with direct read-only DB connectors for legacy payment processors on platforms 4, 9, and 12. Central compliance state in PostgreSQL 16 RDS; encrypted portability packages delivered via S3 presigned URL with 48-hour TTL. Regulatory deadline tracking enforces 30-day GDPR and 45-day CCPA fulfillment windows.
target_output.architecture_overview: AWS MWAA Airflow cluster (Python 3.12 DAGs) orchestrates four primary workflows: deletion_orchestration, portability_export, retention_policy_scan, and consent_sync. The deletion_orchestration DAG is triggered via Airflow REST API on each deletion_requests table insert. Its first stage is a LegalHoldDecisionSensor that blocks on the legal_hold_decisions table, preventing any downstream deletion task from running until a compliance officer or automated legal review system inserts a HOLD_CLEARED or LEGAL_HOLD_CONFIRMED decision within 24 hours. Downstream tasks are per-platform deletion subtasks each using a PlatformAdapter interface — either REST API calls for platforms with deletion endpoints or direct read-only DB operations for legacy platforms. Central compliance state in PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ. Portability export packages assembled to S3 with SSE-KMS; tenants receive presigned URLs with 48-hour TTL. All compliance actions append to an audit_log table with no application-layer mutation permissions.
target_output.technology_stack: Orchestration: Apache Airflow 2.8 on AWS MWAA, Python 3.12 DAGs. Pipeline: Python platform_adapters library with one adapter per regional platform. Data: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ (compliance state), S3 with SSE-KMS (portability exports, audit archive). Auth: AWS IAM roles for MWAA and platform service accounts. Infrastructure: MWAA, RDS, S3, SNS, KMS, CloudTrail, CloudWatch. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with pytest and DAG validation. Notifications: SNS + SES for compliance team alerts and tenant delivery emails.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Deletion Request Orchestration: receive deletion request via API, compute regulatory deadline (30 days for GDPR, 45 days for CCPA), run LegalHoldDecisionSensor (blocks up to 24h), if HOLD_CLEARED execute per-platform deletion tasks in parallel, record deletion counts and execution timestamps, update request to complete, append audit entry. Legal Hold Gate: LegalHoldDecisionSensor polls legal_hold_decisions table every 5 minutes; after 24h without decision, auto-pauses request and sends SNS escalation to compliance officer. Portability Export Assembly: collect tenant records from all 15 platform adapters in parallel, merge into standardized JSON package (contact, lease history, payment history, communications), encrypt and upload to S3, generate 48-hour presigned URL, email tenant with download link and SHA256 checksum. Retention Policy Enforcement: weekly per-platform scan identifies records exceeding retention window with no active lease or legal hold; stages records for deletion in review queue before execution. Consent Sync: nightly DAG pulls consent grant/withdrawal events from each regional platform API and updates central consent_records table.
target_output.system_components: MWAA Airflow Cluster (Python 3.12, Airflow 2.8, scheduler + 2 workers). DAG S3 bucket (mwaa-dags-us-east-1) — CI/CD deploys DAG files here. platform_adapters Airflow plugin (Python package with 15 adapter classes). PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ (compliance state DB). S3 bucket (tenant-portability-exports, SSE-KMS, 30-day lifecycle). S3 bucket (compliance-audit-archive, Glacier transition after 1 year). SNS topics: compliance-alerts (SLA breaches, legal hold escalations), platform-errors. SES for tenant portability export delivery emails. CloudTrail for AWS API audit. KMS CMK for S3 encryption and RDS encryption. LegalHoldDecisionSensor (custom Airflow Sensor checking legal_hold_decisions table on 5-minute poke interval).
target_output.workflows: Deletion Orchestration Workflow: external system calls POST /airflow/api/v1/dags/deletion_orchestration/dagRuns with tenant_id and request_source; DAG instantiates with request parameters; Task 1 create_deletion_request inserts row in deletion_requests with computed regulatory_deadline; Task 2 LegalHoldDecisionSensor polls legal_hold_decisions WHERE request_id={id} every 5 minutes; on HOLD_CLEARED sensor succeeds, on LEGAL_HOLD_CONFIRMED DAG branches to hold_confirmed path (marks request on_hold, appends audit entry, sends SNS, terminates); after 24h without decision, SLA callback pauses request, sends SNS escalation, marks status=awaiting_hold_decision; on sensor success, Tasks 3a-3o run in parallel (one per platform) calling platform_adapter.delete_tenant_records(tenant_id); each task records result in deletion_task_results; Task 4 aggregate_results checks for partial failures; Task 5 finalize marks request complete and appends audit entry. Portability Export Workflow: DAG triggered same way; Tasks run per-platform adapters in parallel collecting contact, lease, payment (direct DB for platforms 4/9/12), and communication records; merge task assembles standardized JSON with data categories, source platform tags, and collection timestamps; S3 upload with SSE-KMS; presigned URL generation (48h TTL); SES email to tenant with URL and SHA256 checksum; audit entry appended.
target_output.data_model: deletion_requests (id UUID PK, tenant_id TEXT, request_source ENUM ccpa/gdpr/virginia_cdpa/colorado_cpa, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, regulatory_deadline TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM received/legal_hold_pending/hold_cleared/on_hold/in_progress/complete/failed, legal_hold_id UUID nullable). legal_hold_decisions (id UUID PK, request_id UUID FK UNIQUE, decision ENUM hold_cleared/legal_hold_confirmed, decided_by TEXT, decided_at TIMESTAMPTZ, hold_reason TEXT nullable). legal_holds (id UUID PK, tenant_id TEXT, hold_type ENUM litigation/regulatory/government_request, active BOOL, created_at, expires_at nullable). deletion_task_results (id UUID PK, request_id UUID FK, platform_id UUID FK, records_deleted INT, deletion_method ENUM api/direct_db, status ENUM pending/complete/failed, executed_at). portability_requests (id UUID PK, tenant_id TEXT, request_source ENUM ccpa/gdpr, submitted_at, deadline TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM received/assembling/ready/delivered/expired, s3_export_key TEXT nullable, export_checksum_sha256 TEXT nullable, presigned_url_expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ nullable). tenant_profiles (id UUID PK, tenant_id TEXT UNIQUE, jurisdictions TEXT[], regional_platform_ids JSONB mapping platform_id to local tenant key). platform_registry (id UUID PK, name TEXT, region TEXT, deletion_method ENUM api/direct_db, portability_method ENUM api/direct_db, adapter_class TEXT, db_connection_secret_arn TEXT nullable). consent_records (id UUID PK, tenant_id TEXT, platform_id UUID FK, consent_type TEXT, granted_at, withdrawn_at nullable, lawful_basis ENUM consent/legitimate_interest/contract/legal_obligation). retention_policies (platform_id UUID FK, data_category TEXT, retention_days INT, legal_basis TEXT). audit_logs (id UUID PK, action ENUM deletion_initiated/hold_decision_received/hold_confirmed/deletion_complete/export_assembled/export_delivered/legal_hold_placed/retention_executed, request_id UUID nullable, platform_id UUID nullable, operator_id TEXT, tenant_id TEXT, details JSONB, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ). Audit_logs has no UPDATE/DELETE privilege for application role.
target_output.api_design: No public HTTP API — pipeline is Airflow DAG-triggered. Airflow REST API (internal): POST /api/v1/dags/deletion_orchestration/dagRuns (trigger deletion request), POST /api/v1/dags/portability_export/dagRuns (trigger export request), GET /api/v1/dags/deletion_orchestration/dagRuns/{run_id} (status check). Internal service interfaces: PlatformAdapter.discover_tenant_records(tenant_id: str) -> list[DataRecord], PlatformAdapter.delete_tenant_records(tenant_id: str, record_ids: list) -> DeletionResult, PlatformAdapter.export_tenant_records(tenant_id: str) -> DataPackage. LegalHoldDecisionSensor.poke(context) -> bool polling legal_hold_decisions table. Compliance team interacts with requests via direct PostgreSQL queries or Airflow UI; no REST API wrapper for this scope.
target_output.security_and_compliance: IAM least-privilege: MWAA execution role has read/write to compliance PostgreSQL, read/write to export S3 bucket, read-only to regional platform DB service accounts. No cross-account access. Audit_logs PostgreSQL role has INSERT only — no UPDATE or DELETE. CloudTrail captures all S3 access to portability export bucket and all RDS API calls. Portability export S3 objects use SSE-KMS with a dedicated CMK; presigned URLs have 48-hour TTL and are single-use (S3 object deleted after delivery confirmation). Legal hold decision records are immutable once inserted (INSERT-only role for decision writer). GDPR Article 28: MWAA acts as data processor; AWS BAA covers MWAA, RDS, and S3. CCPA: deletion within 45 days enforced by regulatory_deadline field and SLA monitoring. GDPR Article 17: 30-day deletion enforced with 25-day and 28-day early-warning SNS alerts. Legal hold precedence: deletion is legally prohibited when a LEGAL_HOLD_CONFIRMED decision exists — the pipeline enforces this at DAG level, not just application logic.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: MWAA environment provisioned via Terraform in us-east-1. GitHub Actions CI: ruff lint, mypy, pytest (80% coverage on adapter and sensor logic), DAG import validation (dagbag.import_errors must be empty), S3 sync of DAG files to mwaa-dags bucket on merge to main. MWAA picks up new DAG files from S3 within 30 seconds. Environments: staging MWAA with 5-platform sandbox data (anonymized) for compliance team acceptance testing per platform batch. Production: MWAA small environment (2 workers), scales to medium if queue depth exceeds 50 pending DAG runs. RDS Multi-AZ automatic failover. S3 portability bucket versioning disabled (compliance: no version retention of deleted personal data). Alembic migrations run as MWAA one-time DAG before new version activates.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch Metrics from MWAA: DagRunSuccessRate, TaskFailureRate, SchedulerHeartbeat. Custom metrics emitted by DAGs to CloudWatch: deletion_completion_hours (histogram; alert if >30 days), portability_assembly_duration_minutes, records_deleted_per_request, legal_hold_check_wait_hours, platform_deletion_failure_rate. Compliance SLA dashboard: all active deletion_requests with regulatory_deadline, days_remaining, and status; color-coded RAG status (green >10 days, amber 5-10 days, red <5 days). SNS alerts: request at 25-day threshold (GDPR) or 35-day threshold (CCPA), request SLA breached, platform adapter error rate >10%, legal_hold_decision not received within 24h.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: MWAA small environment: ~$0.49/hour = ~$360/month. RDS Multi-AZ db.t3.medium: ~$0.10/hour = ~$75/month. S3 portability exports: negligible (<1GB typical monthly volume). Total: ~$440/month. Scaling: MWAA scales to medium ($0.98/hour) if concurrent DAG runs exceed 50. Platform adapter parallelism: each deletion DAG runs up to 15 platform tasks concurrently via Airflow LocalExecutor pools; increase MWAA worker count if task slots become bottleneck. At full scale (100 deletion requests/day across 15 platforms): estimated 1500 Airflow task instances/day, within small MWAA environment capacity.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (weeks 1-6): 5 highest-volume platforms (API-based deletion), LegalHoldDecisionSensor synchronous gate, central PostgreSQL schema, audit_log infrastructure, CCPA/GDPR deadline tracking, CloudWatch SLA monitoring. Compliance team acceptance test per platform before production sign-off. Phase 2 (weeks 7-12): portability export DAG for 5 pilot platforms including direct DB connectors for platforms 4, 9, 12; S3 presigned URL delivery; tenant email notification; consent_sync DAG. Phase 3 (weeks 13-24): scale to all 15 platforms; retention policy enforcement DAG; Virginia CDPA and Colorado CPA deadline variants; full compliance reporting dashboard; legal team self-service portal for hold decisions.
target_output.development_guidelines: All platform adapters must implement the PlatformAdapter abstract base class — no direct DB or HTTP calls outside adapter classes. LegalHoldDecisionSensor must never set mode=reschedule for deletion DAGs (poke mode ensures no deletion task is scheduled while hold check is in progress). Each adapter class must have pytest unit tests mocking the platform API/DB and asserting correct DataPackage schema. Airflow DAG files must pass dagbag import validation in CI before S3 sync. Alembic migrations must include rollback SQL. Audit_log INSERT-only enforcement tested in CI via a test asserting UPDATE/DELETE on audit_logs table raises PermissionError.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: regional platform DBA rejects service account DB access for legacy platforms (affects direct DB portability method). Mitigation: fallback to manual export process documented with compliance team sign-off on data completeness caveat. Risk: legal hold decision not received within 24h for high-volume deletion periods. Mitigation: auto-escalation SNS to compliance officer at 20h, hard pause at 24h; compliance team SLA to respond within 24h defined in operational runbook. Risk: MWAA scheduler downtime during SLA-critical period. Mitigation: MWAA managed service SLA 99.9% uptime; CloudWatch SchedulerHeartbeat alert at 5-minute gap triggers PagerDuty. Tradeoff: direct DB deletion over API for legacy platforms trades coupling for completeness — accepted because incomplete deletion is a regulatory violation, while tight coupling is an operational inconvenience. Tradeoff: synchronous LegalHoldDecisionSensor (poke every 5 min, up to 24h) over async callback — adds up to 24h deletion latency in clear cases but eliminates regulatory risk of premature deletion.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Resolved that regulatory_deadline for GDPR/CCPA dual-jurisdiction tenants uses the tighter deadline (30 days GDPR) — confirmed with legal team. Resolved that legal_hold_decisions table uses INSERT-only access for the decision writer role, making decisions immutable for audit purposes. Confirmed direct read-only DB access for platforms 4, 9, 12 payment history is the interim solution until those platforms expose a payment portability API. Selected Airflow poke mode (not reschedule) for LegalHoldDecisionSensor because reschedule mode could allow downstream tasks to be scheduled between sensor invocations.

metadata.notes: Round-2 revision fixing critical async legal hold check defect with synchronous LegalHoldDecisionSensor; CCPA/GDPR tenant data pipeline across 15 property management platforms.
